text: Eric Records is an American record company that produced singles from 1968 to 1996 mainly with reissues of successful oldies, continuing today with compact disc compilations.
Nina Edith Coote (23 September 1883 – 6 January 1945) was an Irish croquet player.
Hillhead High School is a day school in Glasgow, Scotland, on Oakfield Avenue, neighbouring the University of Glasgow.
DeForest is a village in Dane County, Wisconsin, United States. The population was 10,811 at the 2020 census. The village was named for Isaac De Forest, who bought the land in 1856.
Satannish is a fictional demon appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
Ari is a given name with varied origins and meanings. It appears in Badaga, Finnic languages, Greek, Hebrew (meaning "lion"), and Scandinavian languages (meaning "eagle").

Faerie Tale Theatre (also known as Shelley Duvall's Faerie Tale Theatre) is an American live-action fairytale fantasy drama anthology television series of 27 episodes, that originally aired on Showtime from September 11, 1982 until November 14, 1987. It is a retelling of 26 fairy tales, particularly those written by The Brothers Grimm, Charles Perrault and Hans Christian Andersen, an additional episode is based on the poem "The Pied Piper of Hamelin".

The 27th episode was a reunion episode of cast and crew titled "Grimm Party", in which in Fairytale style, celebrating the series they attended a gala in fancy dress.

The series follow a similar style to an earlier series narrated by Shirley Temple, titled Shirley Temple's Storybook, that ran from 1958 and 1961 and also featured numerous Hollywood stars in roles.

Kathryn Michelle "Katy" Kinard (born September 16, 1980) is an American Christian pop and worship singer-songwriter and pianist. She began her music career in 2005, releasing the studio albums *Headed Back* (2005), *You're Still Better* (2007), *Lullaby Hymns: The Weary Soul* (2010), and *God of Fireflies* (2016).

*The Art of Self-Defense* is a 2019 American dark comedy film written and directed by Riley Stearns. It stars Jesse Eisenberg, Alessandro Nivola, and Imogen Poots. The film premiered at South by Southwest on March 10, 2019, and was released in the United States on July 12, 2019, by Bleecker Street.

This article is about the phonology of Bernese German. It deals with current phonology and phonetics, including geographical variants. Like other High Alemannic varieties, it has a two-way contrast in plosives and fricatives that is not based on voicing, but on length. The absence of voice in plosives and fricatives is typical for all High German varieties, but many of them have no two-way contrast due to general lenition.

Aquinas Walter Richard Sipe (December 11, 1932 – August 8, 2018) was an American Benedictine monk-priest for 18 years (1952–1970 at Saint John's Abbey, Collegeville, Minnesota), a psychotherapist and the author of six books about Catholicism, the clerical sexual abuse in the Catholic Church, and clerical celibacy.

Hambleden Lock is a lock on the River Thames in Berkshire, England, near the village of Hambleden. First built in 1773, it was rebuilt in 1870 and again in 1994. The lock has a length of 61 meters (200 ft 2 in), a width of 7.7 meters (25 ft 3 in), and a fall of 1.44 meters (4 ft 9 in).

The lock is accessible from Aston on one side and Mill End via walkways across the weirs on the other. The reach above the lock includes Greenlands, Temple Island (the start of the Henley Royal Regatta course), and the town of Henley-on-Thames.

Nicole Bilderback (born June 10, 1975) is a Korean-born American actress, known for her recurring guest roles on the television programs Dark Angel and Dawson's Creek, and the films Bring It On and Bad Girls From Valley High (aka A Fate Totally Worse than Death). She also was one of the Cordettes, Cordelia Chase's friends, on the Buffy the Vampire Slayer episode "The Wish" and the Unaired Buffy pilot. She also starred in a few episodes in season 6 of The Fresh Prince of Bel Air as Ashley's friend.
Lars Eric Mattsson is a guitarist, songwriter and producer living in Åland, Finland.

*Oxalis latifolia* is a perennial herb in the wood-sorrel family, Oxalidaceae. Native to Mexico, it is known on most other continents as an introduced and invasive species. It spreads via bulbs and stolons and infests agricultural crops.

*Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee v. FEC*, 518 U.S. 604 (1996), was a case in which the Supreme Court of the United States held that independent expenditures by political parties, made without coordination with a candidate, are protected by the First Amendment. The case arose after the Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee purchased radio ads attacking a Democratic candidate. The Federal Election Commission (FEC) argued this violated the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 (FECA).

The Court's plurality opinion, delivered by Justice Breyer, found the ads were independent expenditures and thus protected. Justices Kennedy and Thomas concurred, arguing for broader First Amendment protections. Justice Stevens dissented, arguing for the government's interest in preventing corruption and leveling the electoral playing field. The case was later revisited in 2001, with the Court then ruling that coordinated expenditures by parties could be restricted.

"Ambush" is the season premiere of the fourth season of the American medical drama *ER*. Directed by Thomas Schlamme and written by Carol Flint, it originally aired on NBC on September 25, 1997. The episode is presented as a PBS documentary crew filming at County General Hospital, capturing various medical cases and the personal dramas of the staff. Key storylines include the arrival of British surgeon Elizabeth Corday (Alex Kingston), John Carter (Noah Wyle) repeating his internship, and Chief of Surgery Dr. Morgenstern (William H. Macy) suffering a heart attack.

"Ambush" was filmed live twice, once for the East Coast and again for the West Coast broadcasts. The episode achieved record-breaking viewership, becoming the highest-rated television episode of the week with 42.71 million viewers and the most-watched season premiere of a drama series in television history.

Critical reception to "Ambush" was mixed, with praise for its technical achievement and minimal errors during the live broadcast, but criticism for its writing and contrived plot. Despite initial mixed reviews, it has since been recognized as a significant episode in the series, earning a Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Technical Direction, Camerawork, Video Control for a Series.

The 2009 AFL season was the 113th season of the Australian Football League (AFL), the highest level senior Australian rules football competition in Australia, which was known as the Victorian Football League until 1989. The season featured sixteen clubs, ran from 26 March until 26 September, and comprised a 22-game home-and-away season followed by a finals series featuring the top eight clubs.

The premiership was won by the Geelong Football Club for the eighth time, after it defeated St Kilda by twelve points in the 2009 AFL Grand Final.

Blackwell Farm is an estate in Surrey, England with a history dating back to the 17th century. Once owned by railway engineer William Frederick Faviell and later by John Cawsey Dennis, it was used by the military during World War II. Today, it is owned by the University of Surrey and is the subject of controversy due to proposed development plans.
Hugues Alexandre Joseph Meunier (born 1759, Mont-Louis, died 1831, Poitiers) was a French general. He joined the army as a child and rose through the ranks.

Hopanoids are a diverse class of pentacyclic triterpenoids produced by many bacteria. Analogous to sterols in eukaryotes, hopanoids modulate the fluidity and permeability of bacterial cell membranes, aiding adaptation to extreme environments. They are synthesized from squalene via squalene-hopene cyclase, and are often further modified with functional groups. These modifications influence their packing within the membrane and their specific functions, such as stress tolerance and nitrogen fixation.

Hopanoids are among the most abundant natural products on Earth and are preserved in sediments over geological timescales as hopanes, making them valuable biomarkers for studying the evolution of life. While 2-methylhopanes were initially proposed as biomarkers for oxygenic photosynthesis by cyanobacteria, this has been disputed, with research suggesting other bacterial sources. Similarly, 3-methylhopanoids were thought to be markers of aerobic methanotrophy, but are now known to be produced by a wider range of bacteria. Hopanoids have potential applications in industry, agriculture as biofertilizers, and medicine by influencing multidrug transport.
Lugazi University (LU), was a private university in Uganda. The university was founded in 2007. It closed in December 2010.

Antasena-class combat boat is a combat boat built by North Sea Boats and designed by LOMOcean Design for the Indonesian armed forces. The manufacturer designated it as Combat Boat X-18 / CB X-18. Initially, this class of ships was developed from a tank boat program from a consortium consisting of PT Lundin (North Sea Boats), PT Pindad, PT LEN, and PT Hariff Daya Tunggal Engineering, with John Cockerill as the turret supplier.

VENUE is a brand of live sound digital mixing consoles introduced by Digidesign in February 2005. The family now includes 5 different consoles and a number of ways they can be configured. They can all be connected to Pro Tools, the audio editing software also created by Avid/Digidesign, to provide recording and 'Virtual Soundcheck' facilities. One of the system's key marketing points is its use of the same AAX DSP/TDM plugins as Pro Tools, an industry standard digital audio workstation (DAW). This is designed to enable the sounds recorded by the artist in the studio to be easily recreated on stage, and to allow for greater flexibility in signal processing without heavy and mechanical-shock-sensitive racks of external processors. There is also a PC-based offline editor for creation and editing of show files, although there is no audio processing in the editor.

Digidesign was acquired by Avid Technology in 1995, but its products continued to be branded Digidesign until the brand was phased out in 2010, and VENUE systems are now branded Avid.

Karli Coburger (born 4 October 1929) is a former Major general in the East German Ministry for State Security (MfS / "Stasi"). In 1984 he became, as matters turned out, the last head of the service's Central Department for surveillance, fact finding and arrests ("Hauptabteilung / Section VIII").

Rajkumar Singhajit Singh (born May 1, 1931) is an Indian classical dancer, choreographer, and scholar of Manipuri dance. Born into a family of dancers and musicians in Manipur, he received training from several gurus, including Ojha Iboton Singh, Amubi Singh, Thambal Angou Singh, Chauba Singh, and Ojha Gaura Singh.

In 1954, Singh joined Triveni Kala Sangam in New Delhi as the Head of the Manipur Dance Section. He later founded the Triveni Ballet in 1962, serving as its Director and Principal Dancer. Singh and his troupe have toured extensively, performing in numerous countries across Europe, the Americas, Asia, and Africa. He is a recipient of the Sangeet Natak Akademi Award and the Tagore Award.

Władysław Gizbert-Studnicki, a Polish politician and publicist, was born on 15 November 1867 in Dünaburg, Vitebsk Governorate, Russian Empire (current Latvia), into a Polish szlachta family of the Kresy region. Both his parents fought in the January Uprising. Throughout his life, Studnicki was famous for his strongly pro-German stance, and in the Polish People's Republic all his books were banned from publication. He was the older brother of historian Wacław Studnicki. He died on 10 January 1953 in London.

In July 2022, the Islamist militant group al-Shabaab launched an invasion from Somalia into Ethiopia's Somali Region. Following attacks on the Somali side of the border, the rebel militants initially attacked Ethiopia's Afder Zone on 21 July and occupied the town of Hulhul before being driven back by Somali Region paramilitary forces. On 25 July, the militants launched a second incursion at Ferfer which was also defeated. Further cross-border attacks continued in the following days, while Ethiopia launched counter-attacks in response. Clashes between the Somali rebels and security forces inside Ethiopia extended into early August, and at least one small al-Shabaab contingent succeeded in evading the Ethiopian force and reached its main target, the Bale Mountains.

The invasion was the largest attack by al-Shabaab in Ethiopian territory to date.

A Zirkel (German for "circle", as in "circle of friends") is a monogram used in European student societies in countries such as Germany, Austria, Switzerland, Belgium, Hungary, Latvia, Estonia (Studentenverbindungen).

The Dornier Do 11 was a German heavy bomber, developed in secret in the early 1930s. It was originally called the Dornier F before being renamed by the Reichsluftfahrtministerium (RLM) in 1933, and was considered a heavy bomber at the time. It came into service in 1932, a continuation of a line of bomber designs from the Dornier Do P in 1930, and the Dornier Do Y in 1931. The line would continue to develop with the Dornier Do 13 and Dornier Do 23.
*Madonna and Child Kissing* is an oil-on-panel painting by Quentin Matsys, dating from circa 1525–1530. It is currently located in the Mauritshuis in The Hague.

Kate Walsh (née Shimmin; born 13 February 1992) is a former Australian and English netball player. Primarily a goal defense and goal keeper, Walsh played for the Adelaide Thunderbirds (2011-2016, 2018-2020), Queensland Firebirds (2017), and Sunshine Coast Lightning (2021-2022) in the Suncorp Super Netball league. She was part of the Thunderbirds team that won the premiership in 2013. Walsh represented Australia in the Fast5 Netball World Series from 2012 to 2017, securing three runner-up finishes. She also played for the England national team beginning in 2019. Walsh retired from elite netball in November 2022.

Hazel Susan Hawke, AO (née Masterson, 20 July 1929 – 23 May 2013) was the first wife of Bob Hawke, the 23rd Prime Minister of Australia. She married him in 1956, and supported him throughout his prime ministership (1983–1991); they divorced in 1995. She worked in social policy areas, and was an amateur pianist and a patron of the arts. After she was diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease, she made public appearances in order to raise awareness of the disease.

Christopher van Wyk (1957–2014) was a South African author and poet. He is known for his autobiographical novel, *Shirley, Goodness & Mercy*, and his poem, *In Detention*. Van Wyk also edited *Staffrider* magazine and abridged Nelson Mandela's *Long Walk to Freedom* for children.

The Department of Social Security (DSS) was an Australian government agency formed in 1972 and dissolved in 1998. It followed the Department of Social Services, taking on its functions and some from the Department of Health. In 1997, its service delivery functions were taken over by Centrelink.

Ribadesella (Asturian: Ribeseya) is a small 84-square-kilometre (32 sq mi) municipality in the Autonomous Community of the Principality of Asturias, Spain. Known for its location on the Cantabrian Sea, at the outlet of the River Sella, Ribadesella is a town that forms part of the Picos de Europa (Peaks of Europe). It is bordered on the east by Llanes, on the south by Cangas de Onís and Parres, and on the west by Caravia. Ribadesella is the home town of Queen Letizia of Spain.

On the first weekend of August, the Descenso Internacional del Sella (International Descent of the Sella) takes place; kayakers from all over the world gather to attempt the final 20 km of the Sella River in record time. The town is also known for its prehistoric cave, known as the cave of Tito Bustillo, which is open to visitors all year round. Ribadesella is also visited for its history, sport activities, scenery, natural amenities, and food.

The 297th Infantry Division was a German infantry division active from February 1940 to January 1943, and again from April 1943 to May 1945. It fought in Operation Barbarossa and the Battle of Stalingrad, where it was destroyed. It was later reformed and engaged in anti-partisan activities in Yugoslavia.

Leigh Diffey (born 3 March 1971) is an Australian American auto racing commentator. His career began calling motorcycle races in his home country before moving to the United Kingdom to cover other forms of motorsport. Diffey then moved to the United States to join Speed Channel, while simultaneously working for Network Ten in Australia. Since 2013, he has served as a play-by-play announcer and studio host with NBC Sports in the United States.
John Doyley (1602–1660) was an English politician who sat in the House of Commons from 1646 to 1648.

Hugh Alan Leslie OBE (17 April 1900 – 2 September 1974) was an Australian politician. He was a member of the Country Party and served terms in the Western Australian Legislative Assembly (1943–1949) and House of Representatives (1949–1958, 1961–1963). He was a newspaper editor before entering politics.
Matthew Tindal (baptised 12 May 1657 – 16 August 1733) was an English deist writer whose work *Christianity as Old as the Creation* (1730) became a central text of deism.

Lord Forbes was launched at Chester in 1803 as a West Indiaman. She soon became an "armed defense ship", but by 1805 had returned to being a West Indiaman. She made two voyages as an "extra" ship for the British East India Company (EIC). She continued trading with India until 1817 when she sustained damage on her way to Bengal. There she was surveyed, condemned and sold.

Ian Simmonds (born 1966), also known as Juryman, is a Welsh-born electronic musician. He is one of a few talents "to successfully breach the chasm that keeps DJ culture from more tangible, organic realms." His complex musical landscapes are often accompanied by his deep calm voice, making poetic, political and social statements at the same time. He is a self-taught musician, singing and playing trumpet, bass guitar, piano and is a producer with numerous releases, contributions and co-productions.
Ruby is a color that is a vivid red. The first recorded use of *ruby* as a color name in English was in 1572.
Heber Springs is a city in Cleburne County, Arkansas, United States. The population was 6,969 at the 2020 census. The city is near Greers Ferry Lake.

Ultraman Powered is a fighting game developed by Tose and published by Bandai for the 3DO Interactive Multiplayer. The player takes on the role of the extraterrestrial superhero Ultraman, tasked with protecting the Earth from destructive aliens and monsters. Gameplay primarily consists of one-on-one battles where the player must deplete an adversary's health meter using both basic and special fighting techniques. The game also contains 3D rail shooter sections and a two-player versus option.

The game is based on the 1993 television series Ultraman: The Ultimate Hero, known in Japan as Ultraman Powered. The game was created using digitized backgrounds and character sprites from this series and uses clips from it as full-motion video (FMV) cutscenes. Ultraman Powered was released exclusively in Japan on March 20, 1994 as a launch title for the 3DO in that region. Global critical reception for the game has been mixed with many reviewers complimenting its graphics but panning its slow gameplay and controls.

Health Canada (French: Santé Canada) is the department of the Government of Canada responsible for national health policy. It is responsible for upholding the Canada Health Act. The department's stated goal is for "Canadians to be among the healthiest people in the world." The department has approximately 11,223 employees and an annual budget of $3.9 billion (2021–22). The current Minister of Health is the Hon. Jean-Yves Duclos, with the Hon. Dr. Carolyn Bennett serving as Associate Minister of Health and Minister of Mental Health and Addictions.

Health Canada works with other governmental departments and agencies, provincial and territorial governments, and international organizations to achieve its mandate. It is supported by the Health Portfolio, which includes the Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC), the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR), and others.

The department's Compliance and Enforcement Directorate ensures adherence to regulations concerning various products, including drugs, medical devices, and natural health products. Health Canada also operates a Special Access Programme, allowing healthcare providers to request medications unavailable for sale in Canada. The department's drug approval process has faced criticism for delays, with calls for more efficient review mechanisms.
Jean Garrabé (1931–2020) was a French psychiatrist, psychoanalyst, and director of clinical teaching.

The **red flour beetle** (*Tribolium castaneum*) is a species of beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, that attacks stored grain and other food products. It is estimated to be one of the two most common secondary pests of plant commodities in store throughout the world.
Howard Minion Radzely (born 1970) is an American lawyer who served as the United States Deputy Secretary of Labor from December 19, 2007 through February 2, 2009.

Vahida Maglajlić (17 April 1907 – 1 April 1943) was a Yugoslav Partisan recognized as a People's Hero of Yugoslavia for her part in the struggle against the Axis powers during World War II. She was the only Bosnian Muslim woman to receive the order.

Born to a Banja Luka qadi, Maglajlić was denied a higher education by her father. She took up various social and humanitarian causes, including women's rights. After Yugoslavia was invaded by the Axis powers in April 1941, Bosnia became part of the Independent State of Croatia, a fascist puppet state. Maglajlić entered the Communist Party of Yugoslavia in May and joined the growing Partisan resistance movement. Her high social status allowed her to inconspicuously shelter and equip local Partisans for several months, but she was eventually discovered by the authorities and sent to prison. After months of torture, Maglajlić escaped and joined the Partisans. She was an influential political figure in Bosanska Krajina until her death in combat with German troops in Mala Krupska Rujiška.

After the war, her remains were exhumed from Mala Krupska Rujiška and reburied at the Partisan cemetery in Banja Luka. Her image has been displayed on the cover of leaflets distributed by the Palestine Liberation Organization, which has sought to promote her as an example for Palestinian women.

Augustin Schubert (born František Schubert; November 8, 1902 – July 28, 1942) was a Czech Catholic priest, an Augustinian monk, and prior of the St. Thomas monastery in Prague. He was an active member of the Catholic Gymnastics Organization Orel. A popular preacher, he was also known for his opposition to Nazism. Arrested in 1940, he was imprisoned in several concentration camps, including Dachau, where he died. He is currently in the process of beatification.
Harry Ruilton Watson (August 31, 1921 – June 8, 2001) was an American child actor, a U.S. Coast Guard combat photographer in World War II, and a pioneer in television journalism.

William Methwold (sometimes spelled Methold), (baptised 1590 in South Pickenham, near Swaffham, Norfolk; died 5 March 1653, Kensington), was an English merchant and colonial administrator in India. He is credited with identifying the site of Bombay as a strategic port.

A fictional character in the novel Midnight's Children by author Salman Rushdie bears the same name and is described as a direct descendant. (See List of Midnight's Children characters). He himself appears as a character in 1637: The Peacock Throne by Eric Flint and Griffin Barber.

1939 Battle of Westerplatte, originally titled in Poland as Tajemnica Westerplatte ("The Secret of Westerplatte"), is a 2013 Polish-Lithuanian war film written and directed by Paweł Chochlew. It portrays the Battle of Westerplatte between the Polish Army and Nazi German forces at the start of World War II in 1939. The film focuses on the conflict between Westerplatte commander Henryk Sucharski (Michał Żebrowski) and his deputy Franciszek Dąbrowski (Robert Żołędziewski).

Dinkar D. Patil (1915–2005) was an Indian film director and screenwriter, primarily in Marathi cinema. Active from 1948 to 1993, he directed and wrote scripts for over 60 Marathi films, often advocating for socialism and depicting rural life. Patil is credited with introducing Lavani folk dance to Marathi films.

Fight Sports is a Grappling and Brazilian jiu-jitsu academy and team based out of Miami, Florida. It was founded in 2011 by multiple time world champion Roberto "Cyborg" Abreu. Fight Sport's competition team includes a number of jiu-jitsu's top competitors, including many ADCC qualifiers. Fight Sports is also an internationally known Jiu Jitsu affiliation with 32 schools worldwide.

Marco Pinotti (born February 25, 1976) is a retired Italian road cyclist. A time trial specialist, he won two individual stages of the Giro d'Italia (2008, 2012) and the National Time Trial Championships six times (2005, 2007–2010, 2013). He wore the leader's pink jersey in the 2007 and 2011 Giro d'Italia.

MAZON: A Jewish Response to Hunger is an American nonprofit working to end hunger among people of all faiths and backgrounds in the United States and Israel.

MAZON, which means "food" or "sustenance" in Hebrew, practices and promotes a multifaceted approach to hunger relief, recognizing the importance of responding to hungry peoples' immediate need for nutrition and sustenance while also working to advance long-term solutions. This holistic approach - executed through three interrelated strategies of education & advocacy, partnership grant-making and strategic initiatives - symbolizes its desire to embody twin Jewish ideals: tzedakah and tikkun olam.

MAZON has offices in Los Angeles, California (headquarters) and Washington, D.C.

Suzanne M. Levine (born June 28, 1957) is an American podiatric physician known for inventing aesthetic podiatric treatments. She is the founder of Institute Beauté in New York City. Levine is a frequent foot care expert on NBC's The Today Show and WNBC's Today in New York and has appeared on other television programs. She is the author of *My Feet Are Killing Me!*, *50 Ways To Ease Foot Pain*, and other books.

USCGC *Robert Yered* (WPC-1104) is the fourth Sentinel-class cutter to be delivered. Like her sister ships, she is equipped for coastal security patrols, interdiction of drug and people smugglers, and search and rescue. She is named for Robert J. Yered, who was awarded a Silver Star for his heroism in the Vietnam War.

The mountain ground squirrel (Geosciurus princeps) is a rodent that is native to southwestern Angola, western Namibia, and western South Africa. It is also known as the Kaoko ground squirrel or the Damara ground squirrel.

It is the closest relative of the Cape ground squirrel (Latin name Geosciurus inauris), which is so similar in appearance that the two are difficult to distinguish in the field. Both species have long bushy black and white tails with a white stripe from the shoulder towards the rump. Geosciurus princeps is slightly larger, on average, than G. inauris, although there is considerable overlap in body size. Differences in skull morphology also distinguish the two species, and the incisors are yellow to orange rather than white as in G. inauris.
*Cinéaste* is a quarterly American film magazine established in 1967.

Design studies is an interdisciplinary field examining design's internal practices and its external effects on society, culture, and the environment. It encompasses design history, theory, and criticism, exploring design's role in shaping values and its connections to various disciplines.
Geometrics are an English indie rock band formed in 2008.
The Indian River is a river in Lanark County, Ontario, Canada. It begins at an unnamed lake in Lanark Highlands and flows into the Mississippi River in Mississippi Mills.

The Redan Football Netball Club, nicknamed the Lions, is an Australian rules football and netball club based in Redan, a suburb of Ballarat, Victoria. Founded in 1871, the club is one of the oldest in the Ballarat Football League (BFL). The club's senior football team plays its home games at the City Oval, while the junior and female football teams play at the Western Oval.

Redan has won 11 BFL premierships. Their premiership years are 1946, 1952, 1975, 1976, 1977, 2002, 2003, 2006, 2007, 2009 and 2011. They defeated Sunbury in the grand final for the 2002, 2003, 2007 and 2011 premierships, and East Point in 2006 and 2009.

The Henderson Medal is awarded to the best and fairest player in the Ballarat Football League. Redan players have won the medal 15 times, including Stan Webb in 1946, Keith Rawle in 1951, David Jenkins in 1976, Michael Phyland in 1992, Michael Smith in 1993 and 1994, Nathan Horbury in 2017 and Jarrod Edwards in 2006, 2007, 2008 and 2009.

The Tony Lockett Medal is awarded to the leading goal-kicker in the Ballarat Football League. Redan players have won the medal four times, including W. Wells in 1952 (73 goals) and 1953 (116 goals), D. Atkinson in 1975 (67 goals), and G. Cahir in 1982 (84 goals).

Princess Jane Mpologoma Nabanakulya (born April 12, 1964) is a Ugandan princess, nurse, and pastor. She is the daughter of Mutesa II of Buganda and Naome Nanyonga. She studied nursing in Uganda, Greece, and Sweden, and currently co-pastors a church in London with her husband.

The Young Plan, or 楊慕琦計劃, was a set of proposed constitutional reforms for Hong Kong introduced by Governor Sir Mark Young in 1946. Prompted by the collapse of British power in East Asia during World War II and the Chinese Nationalist government's calls for Hong Kong's retrocession, the plan aimed to foster a sense of belonging among Hong Kong residents and to introduce self-government, as pledged by the new Labour government in the UK.

The plan centered on establishing a Municipal Council with greater power than its English counterparts, effectively creating a representative government. It also proposed changes to the Legislative Council, increasing the proportion of unofficial members. The Municipal Council was to have an elected majority, open to permanent residents literate in Chinese or English, divided equally between Chinese and non-Chinese members.

However, the Young Plan faced opposition from within the government, British business elites, and some unofficial members of the Legislative Council, who criticized the plan and proposed alternative reforms. Alexander Grantham, Young's successor, was unenthusiastic about the reforms and delayed their progress. Public opinion was divided, with some supporting the plan and others demanding greater representation for the Chinese population. Ultimately, the plan was shelved in 1949 and abandoned in 1952, with the British government citing the Cold War, the Korean War, and the influx of refugees as reasons to prioritize stability over political reform.
Eddy L. Harris (born January 26, 1956) is an American writer of creative nonfiction and a filmmaker.

Uckfield Town Council governs the five wards of Uckfield town. The council appoints one councillor who is then known as the Mayor of Uckfield. The current Mayor of Uckfield is Councillor Jackie Love.

Uckfield town council comprises five wards – North, East, West, New Town, and Ridgewood – electing between two and four town councillors each.

Clockwise, from the north, it borders the communities of Maresfield, Buxted, Framfield and Little Horsted.
Beatrijs: Katholiek weekblad voor de vrouw was a Dutch Catholic weekly magazine for women. Founded in 1939, it was taken over by Libelle in 1967.
Enrico Morin (1841–1910) was an Italian admiral and politician who held several cabinet posts.

Robert "Bobby" James Voelker (born April 26, 1979) is an American professional mixed martial artist competing in their Welterweight division. A professional competitor since 2006, Voelker has formerly competed for the UFC, Bellator MMA, Strikeforce, M-1 Global, and Titan FC.

The Antasena-class combat boat is a catamaran-hull combat boat designed and built by North Sea Boats. The first prototype, named "Antasena", began sea trials in April 2021. It is equipped with a Cockerill 3030 unmanned turret, two 12.7 mm machine guns, and can carry up to 60 troops. The boat is intended for use by the Indonesian Army.

Marcq-en-Barœul is a commune in the Nord department in northern France, located within the Hauts-de-France region. It is part of the European Metropolis of Lille. In 2022, the population was 39,943. The current mayor, elected in 2020, is Bernard Gérard.

The head offices of Groupe Holder and its subsidiaries, Ladurée and Paul, are located in Marcq-en-Barœul. The town has a racecourse, two golf courses, and a rugby club called Olympique Marcquois Rugby, founded in 1971. The rugby club is affiliated with the Stade Toulousain since 1999.

Bajrang Punia (born 26 February 1994) is an Indian freestyle wrestler. He has won medals at the Olympic Games, World Championships, Asian Games, and Commonwealth Games. Punia began wrestling at age seven and was trained at Chattarsal Stadium in 2008. In 2015, his family moved to Sonepat so that he could attend a regional center of the Sports Authority of India.

The National Premier League, also known for sponsorship purposes as the Red Stripe Premier League, is sanctioned by the Jamaican Football Federation as the top division for men's association football in Jamaica. Contested between 12 clubs, it operates on a system of promotion and relegation with Jamaica's regional Super Leagues where the two lowest placed teams are relegated to their corresponding local second division league.

Josef Kiefel (2 October 1909 – 11 March 1988) was a German communist who participated in the German Resistance against Hitler and fought with Soviet partisans against the Nazis. After the creation of East Germany in 1949, Kiefel served as a Stasi officer.

The Hoosiers are an English pop rock band formed in Indianapolis, Indiana, United States. The band consists of members Irwin Sparkes (lead vocals, lead guitar) and Alan Sharland (drums, percussion, vocals).

Their first single "Worried About Ray" reached number 5 on the UK Singles Chart in July 2007. In October 2007, they released their debut album The Trick to Life, which reached number one in the album charts. The band released their second top ten album The Illusion of Safety on 16 August 2010 and was preceded by the lead single "Choices", released on 1 August 2010, and which reached the UK top 20.

In July 2015, bassist Martin Skarendahl left the band in what was described by the remaining members as "one of the most amicable decisions in music history".
Bent Sæther (born 18 February 1969 in Oslo, Norway) is the bass guitarist and lead vocalist of the Norwegian psychedelic rock band Motorpsycho from the start in 1989.

Mieczyslaw "Mickey Doyle" Kuzik, most commonly known as Mickey Doyle, is a fictional character in the HBO television series *Boardwalk Empire*, portrayed by Paul Sparks. Mickey is a Polish-American gangster and bootlegger who attempts to establish himself in the illegal alcohol trade. He works with and against various major characters throughout the series, including Nucky Thompson, Jimmy Darmody, and Al Capone. Doyle is loosely based on Philadelphia mobster Mickey Duffy.

The Complete Beat is a 5-CD box set by British ska/new wave band The Beat (known as The English Beat in the US) released on 10 July 2012 only in the US and Canada. The box set contains The Beat's three studio albums remastered and two CDs of bonus content: the first contains extended and dub versions, and the second contains all the tracks from their three Peel Sessions and several tracks live from the Boston Opera House.

The studio albums are the US release versions, as "Tears of a Clown", "Ranking Full Stop", "Too Nice to Talk To" and "Hit It" weren't included on the original UK releases of their respective albums. Also, both Wha'ppen and Special Beat Service CDs include bonus tracks.

On the same day of release, Shout! Factory also released the single-CD compilation album Keep The Beat: The Very Best of The English Beat and a live album, Live at the US Festival, '82 &'83.

*TV Sound and Image* is a compilation album of library music released by Soul Jazz Records on July 3, 2012. Compiled by Stuart Baker, Pete Reilly, and Steve Platt, the album received positive reviews, with critics noting its funky breaks and nostalgic appeal. It was released on compact disc and as two separate vinyl releases.

Richard Franz Stöhr (11 June 1874 – 11 December 1967) was an Austrian composer, music author and teacher.

Born in Vienna, Stöhr studied composition with Robert Fuchs at the Vienna Conservatory. After working there as a repetiteur and choral instructor from 1900, he taught music (theory of harmony, counterpoint, form) from 1903 to 1938, being professor from 1915. Among his students were Herbert von Karajan, Rudolf Serkin, Erich Leinsdorf, Samuel Barber, Erich Zeisl, Louis Horst, Marlene Dietrich, Alois Hába, Hellmut Federhofer, and Mimi Wagensonner. Fired from the Vienna Conservatory due to his Jewish heritage in 1938, he emigrated to the US in 1939 and taught at the Curtis Institute of Music in Philadelphia. His students there included Leonard Bernstein and Eugene Bossart. From 1941 to 1950 he taught at St. Michael's College in Vermont, where he maintained emeritus status until 1960. He died in Montpelier, Vermont in the United States.

May Song Vang (February 5, 1951 – August 5, 2013) was an American Hmong community leader and activist. She was the widow of General Vang Pao, a former member of the Royal Lao Army and prominent Hmong American leader, who died in 2011. May Song Vang became a more prominent symbol of the Hmong American community in California and the rest of the United States after the death of her husband.

Caguas is a city and municipality in the central region of Puerto Rico, located in the Valle del Turabo (Turabo Valley). Founded in 1775, it's known as "La Ciudad Criolla" (Creole City) and "La Cuna de los Trovadores" (Cradle of Trovadores). As of 2020, its population was 127,244, making it the fifth-largest city in Puerto Rico.

Historically, the Caguas Valley was first inhabited by the Saladoid and later by Taíno cultures. The Spanish established agriculture and mines in the area. Sugarcane became a primary crop in the 19th century. After the Spanish-American War, Caguas became a part of the United States.

Caguas experienced significant growth in the 1970s, becoming a pharmaceutical manufacturing center and a commuter town for San Juan. It is part of the San Juan Metropolitan Area. The city lies along the Great Northern Puerto Rico fault zone. Caguas is home to the Criollos de Caguas, a celebrated baseball team, and numerous landmarks, parks, and festivals.

Dance of the Tiger (Swedish: Den Svarta Tigern) is a novel by Finnish palaeontologist Björn Kurtén, published in 1978 and English translation in 1980. It is a prehistoric novel dealing with the interaction between Neanderthals and Cro-Magnons. A sequel, Singletusk, published in 1982, continues the story of the family.

Ticagrelor, sold under the brand names Brilinta and Brilique among others, is a medication used to reduce the risk of stroke in people with acute ischemic stroke or high-risk transient ischemic attack. In the EU, it is used with aspirin to prevent atherothrombotic events in adults with acute coronary syndromes or a history of myocardial infarction.

Common adverse effects include an increased risk of bleeding and shortness of breath. Ticagrelor blocks adenosine diphosphate (ADP) receptors, preventing platelet activation. It is a nucleoside analogue.

Helmut Zilk (1927–2008) was an Austrian politician and Mayor of Vienna from 1984 to 1994. In 1993, he was severely injured by a letter bomb sent by Franz Fuchs. After his death, documents revealed he had collaborated with Czechoslovak secret police during the 1960s.

Chenani is a town and tehsil in the Udhampur district, in the Indian union territory of Jammu and Kashmir. It is governed by a notified area council (city council). Before 1947, it was an internal jagir ruled by a Chandel (Rajput) ruler in the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir.
Petra Kronberger (born February 21, 1969) is a former Austrian alpine skier. She won two gold medals at the 1992 Winter Olympics and the World Cup overall title three times (1990, 1991, 1992).

The **Gonzaga Bulldogs** are the athletic teams that represent Gonzaga University, located in Spokane, Washington. They compete in the West Coast Conference (WCC) of the NCAA Division I. While the official mascot is a bulldog, the teams are often referred to as the "Zags".

*The Divine Wings of Tragedy* is the third studio album by the American progressive metal band Symphony X, released in 1996. It is a concept album whose title track contains excerpts from classical compositions and whose lyrical theme was inspired by works of literature. It has since been included on several lists of best metal albums.

Muttrah, (Lang-ar) administratively a wilayah (province), is located in the Muscat Governorate of Oman. Before the discovery of oil in Oman, Muttrah was the center of commerce in Oman (Muscat). It is still a center of commerce as one of the largest sea ports of the region is located there. Other landmarks include Souq Muttrah, a traditional bazaar and Sour Al-Lawatiah, a small community of houses surrounded by an old wall. To the south lies Muscat District.

Patillas is a town and municipality of Puerto Rico located on the southeastern coast. Founded in 1811, it is known as "The Emerald of the South" and "The Lions". As of 2020, the population was 15,985. Patillas is subdivided into 16 barrios and is known for its beaches and the Lago Patillas reservoir.

Skimlinks is a marketing and advertising company founded in London in 2007 by Alicia Navarro and Joe Stepniewski. Originally a social recommendation tool called Skimbit.com, the company pivoted to focus on affiliate marketing technology. Skimlinks aggregates merchant programs and provides tools for online publishers to monetize their content through in-text advertising.

The company has received investment from firms including The Accelerator Group and Greycroft. Skimlinks acquired Atma Links in 2011 and InvisibleHand in 2013. In February 2012, it was revealed that Pinterest was using Skimlinks to monetize user content. Skimlinks was acquired by Taboola on September 1, 2021.

The Faculty of Social and Human Sciences (FCSH/NOVA) is a public faculty of the NOVA University Lisbon, in Portugal, established in 1977. It is located in central Lisbon and is organized into 12 departments and 16 research centers. As of 2016, FCSH/NOVA has over 5,000 students in undergraduate, master's, and doctoral programs. The faculty's library system includes several special collections.

Alfred Webb (1834 – 1908) was an Irish politician and writer. Born into a Quaker family in Dublin, he became involved in Irish politics in 1865, inspired by the Fenians despite his commitment to non-violence. He was elected to the House of Commons for West Waterford in 1890 and 1892. A friend of Dadabhai Naoroji, Webb presided over the Indian National Congress in 1894. He also supported Anti-Caste, an anti-racism journal.

John William Goode Jr. (1923–1994) was a lawyer from San Antonio, Texas. A Republican, he unsuccessfully ran for Congress in 1961 against Henry B. Gonzalez. He was a World War II veteran, receiving the Silver Star and Bronze Star.
Eric Records is a record label founded in 1968 in Philadelphia by Bill Buster. It re-released old pop songs and switched to CDs in 1996.

Road Rules: Down Under is the sixth season of the MTV reality television series Road Rules. It took place exclusively in Hawaii for part of the first episode, with the rest of the season exclusively in Oceania. This season is often noted as Road Rules: Australia.

Hadith: A Re-evaluation is a book by Kassim Ahmad (or Ahmed). In it he writes that the hadith books in Shia and Sunni Islam were introduced without any authoritative basis. It claims that the hadith books were not endorsed by Muhammad, nor by the first four caliphs, nor by the Quran. The book makes the case for Quranism, a minority sect in Islam.

Annie Buller (1895–1973) was a Ukrainian-Canadian communist activist and union organizer. She co-founded the Montreal Labour College and the Communist Party of Canada. She was married to Harry Guralnick and had one son.

PKP class ET22 is a Polish six-axle electric freight locomotive built by Pafawag from 1969 to 1989. Factory designation is 201E. With 1183 units, it is one of the most numerous standard gauge electric locomotive built in Europe. Further 23 units were built for Morocco as E-1000 class.

*NBA Street Vol. 2* is a 3-on-3 basketball sports game developed by NuFX and EA Canada, published by EA Sports BIG, and released in 2003. It is a sequel to *NBA Street*. The game features 29 NBA teams from the 2002-03 season, as well as street and NBA legends. Players can compete in modes like Pick Up Game, NBA Challenge, Be a Legend, and Street School. The game includes new trick moves, dunks, and a "gamebreaker" feature. The soundtrack features artists like Nelly, Nate Dogg, and Just Blaze. Upon release, the game received positive reviews.

The Lipetsk fighter-pilot school was a secret training facility operated by the German Reichswehr in the Soviet Union from 1926 to 1933. Germany was prohibited from operating an air force by the Treaty of Versailles, so it circumvented the treaty by training pilots and developing aircraft in the Soviet Union. The school trained 120 fighter pilots and hundreds of ground personnel who later served in the Luftwaffe.
John Mortimer Boudreaux, Jr. (December 10, 1936, New Roads, Louisiana – January 14, 2017, Los Angeles) was an American drummer who was active in jazz, soul, and rhythm & blues idioms.

Vlașca County (Romanian: Județul Vlașca) was an administrative division of Romania, located in the historic region of Wallachia. The county was established before 1601 and existed until the administrative reform of 1950. Its county seat was Giurgiu.

In 1930, the county had a population of 296,412, with a majority of Romanians (97.1%) and smaller populations of Romanies (2.2%) and Hungarians (0.2%). The predominant religion was Eastern Orthodox (99.4%). In 1938, it was incorporated into Ținutul Argeș, but was re-established in 1940, only to be abolished by the Communist regime ten years later.

Sheila Elias is an American artist and art historian. Her work, which spans painting, mixed media, sculpture, installation, and performance, explores the intersection of art aesthetics and social consciousness. She lives and works in Miami and New York City.

Trochilinae is a subfamily of hummingbirds (family Trochilidae). A molecular phylogenetic study divided the hummingbird family into multiple subfamilies and redefined the Trochilinae to contain three tribes: Lampornithini, Mellisugini, and Trochilini.

Saint Vincent Martyr School, located in Madison, New Jersey, is a Roman Catholic elementary school serving students in pre-kindergarten through eighth grade. Established in 1848, the school has an enrollment of approximately 400 students. Saint Vincent Martyr School has received accreditation from the Blue Ribbon Schools Program and the Middle States Association of Colleges and Schools. The school offers a variety of sports programs, including basketball, baseball, softball, track and field, cross country, and cheerleading. A scout program is also available.

Hannah Mary Bardell (born 1 June 1983) is a Scottish politician who has served as Member of Parliament (MP) for Livingston since 2015. A member of the Scottish National Party (SNP), she served as its spokesperson for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport from 2018 to 2019.

None but One is a studio album released by American singer Jean Ritchie in 1977 on Sire LP record SA-7530. The album was a stylistic departure for Ritchie in that it featured "modern" instruments and production values. It resulted in significant critical acclaim, but the album suffered serious distribution problems. A song from the album, "Now is the Cool of the Day" became an anthem for environmentalists opposed to mountaintop removal.

In homological algebra, the Tor functor is a derived functor that measures the failure of the tensor product to be exact. Given a ring *R*, the Tor functor, denoted TorRi(A, B), is defined for *R*-modules *A* and *B* and non-negative integers *i*. It can be computed by taking a projective resolution of either *A* or *B*, tensoring with the other module, and then taking the homology of the resulting complex.

The Tor functor is zero for negative *i*, and TorR0(A, B) is isomorphic to A ⊗R B. The higher Tor functors, TorRi(A, B) for *i* > 0, capture the torsion elements that are "lost" when tensoring. For a commutative ring *R*, TorRi(A, B) is an *R*-module. For a non-commutative ring *R*, it is an abelian group.

The Schillinger System of Musical Composition, named after Joseph Schillinger (1895–1943) is a method of musical composition based on mathematical processes. It comprises theories of rhythm, harmony, melody, counterpoint, form and semantics, purporting to offer a systematic and non-genre approach to music analysis and composition; a descriptive rather than prescriptive grammar of music.

While it influenced some prominent figures, such as Lawrence Berk (founder of the Berklee College of Music) and George Gershwin (likely influencing the piece "I Got Rhythm Variations"), it began to fall out of favor in the 1960s after receiving criticisms for being over-complicated and pseudo-scientific, and was removed from the Berklee curriculum.

Swedish tourists Sven Urban Höglin, aged 23, and his fiancée Heidi Birgitta Paakkonen, aged 21, disappeared while tramping on the Coromandel Peninsula of New Zealand in 1989. Police, residents, and military personnel conducted the largest land-based search undertaken in New Zealand, attempting to find the couple. In December 1990, David Wayne Tamihere (born 1953) was convicted of murdering Höglin and Paakkonen, and sentenced to life imprisonment based largely on the testimony of three prison inmates.

Höglin's body was discovered in 1991, revealing evidence which contradicted the police case against Tamihere, who has always maintained his innocence and filed a series of unsuccessful appeals during the 1990s. Tamihere was released on parole in November 2010 after serving twenty years. In 2017, Secret Witness C, one of the former prisoners who had testified against Tamihere at his murder trial, was found guilty of perjury. On 26 April 2018, the identity of Witness C was revealed as Robert Conchie Harris, who had originally been convicted of the double murder of a couple in 1983.

Caguas (IPA-es, IPA-es) is a city and municipality of Puerto Rico located in the Central Mountain Range of Puerto Rico, south of San Juan and Trujillo Alto, west of Gurabo and San Lorenzo, and east of Aguas Buenas, Cidra, and Cayey. Caguas was founded in 1775. The municipality had a population of 127,244 at the 2020 census.

Caguas is located 20 miles (32 km) from San Juan. It is located in the Valle de Caguas or Caguas Valley (also known as Valle del Turabo or the Turabo Valley), at the eastern ranges of the Central Mountain Range. It is known as La Ciudad Criolla (Criollo City), Valle del Turabo (the Turabo Valley) and La Cuna de los Trovadores (The Cradle of the Trovadores). Its name originates from the Taíno cacique Caguax, who was a regional chief. Caguas is a principal city of both the San Juan-Caguas-Guaynabo Metropolitan Statistical Area and the San Juan-Caguas-Fajardo Combined Statistical Area.

Titus Haterius Nepos was an eques who held a number of imperial Roman positions during the reigns of Trajan and Hadrian. The two most important posts were praefectus vigilum or commander of the vigiles or nightwatch, and praefectus or governor of Egypt (120-124). According to Fergus Millar, Nepos was the first eques who was promoted from regular procuratorial posts into a proper secretarial appointment.

Donald Clarke Sweeney (born August 17, 1966) is a Canadian former ice hockey defenceman who played over 1,100 games in the National Hockey League (NHL), mostly with the Boston Bruins. He ranks among the top ten in many Bruins team statistics, including fourth overall in total games played. After retiring from hockey following the 2003–04 season, he worked briefly as a broadcaster before rejoining the Bruins as a team executive in 2006.

He is the current general manager of the Bruins, a position he has held since May 2015 after replacing Peter Chiarelli.

Bellevue Plantation is a historic plantation in Leon County, Florida, near Tallahassee. It was added to the U.S. National Register of Historic Places on March 11, 1971. The plantation was named after the Hotel de Belle-Vue in Brussels.

The house has been restored with period furnishings and is now part of the Tallahassee Museum, where visitors can learn about plantation communities. Adjacent to the house are reconstructed buildings, including a plantation kitchen and a slave cabin. Catherine Murat, one of the owners, was involved in the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association.

The 2014 WNBA Finals was the championship series of the 2014 WNBA season. The Phoenix Mercury, who finished with a league-best 29–5 record, defeated the Chicago Sky, who made their first finals appearance in franchise history. Phoenix won the series 3–0, and Diana Taurasi was named the WNBA Finals MVP. The Mercury had won the regular-season series against the Sky 2-0.

David Loud (born November 28, 1961) is an American music supervisor, music director, conductor, vocal and dance arranger, pianist, and actor. He is known for his work with Kander and Ebb and Stephen Sondheim, including *Ragtime*, *Steel Pier*, and *The Scottsboro Boys*.

Otto Eisenschiml (June 16, 1880 – December 7, 1963) was an Austrian-born chemist and industrial executive in the American oil industry, and a controversial author. He may be best known for his provocative 1937 book on the assassination of Abraham Lincoln in which he proposed that a senior member of Lincoln's Cabinet orchestrated the plot to kill the president.

Elizabeth Marian Swaney (born July 30, 1984) is an American freestyle skier who represented Hungary at the 2018 Winter Olympics in the halfpipe event. She qualified by strategically entering competitions with fewer participants and avoiding falls. Her performance, described as "perfectly mediocre," sparked debate about Olympic qualification standards and prompted the Hungarian Olympic Committee to reevaluate its selection process.

Melukote in Pandavapura taluk of Mandya district, Karnataka, in southern India, is one of the sacred places in Karnataka.It is built on rocky hills, known as Yadugiri, Yaadavagiri and Yadushailadeepa, overlooking the Cauvery valley. Melukote is about 51 km (32 miles) from Mysore and 133 km (83 mi) from Bangalore.

Melukote is the location of the Cheluvanarayana Swamy Temple, with a collection of crowns and jewels which are brought to the temple for the annual celebration. On the top of the hill is the temple of Yoganarasimha. Many more shrines and ponds are located in the town. Melukote is home to the Academy of Sanskrit Research, which has collected thousands of Vedic and Sanskrit manuscripts.

Early in the 12th century, the famous Srivaishnava saint Sri Ramanujacharya, who hailed from Tamil Nadu, stayed at Melukote for about 12 years. It has thus become a prominent centre of the Srivaishnava sect.

An antifuse is a type of electrical component that, when triggered, permanently changes from a non-conducting state to a conducting one. They are known for their use in miniature Christmas tree lights, where they short out a blown bulb to keep the string lit. Antifuses are also used to program integrated circuits (ICs), including programmable logic devices (PLDs) and programmable read-only memory (PROM).

SLINEX (Sri Lanka India Naval Exercise) are a series of naval exercises between the Indian Navy and the Sri Lanka Navy. The first SLINEX exercise took place in 2005. The eighth edition took place in 2020.

CBS Playhouse is an American anthology drama television series that aired on CBS from 1967 to 1970. Airing twelve plays over the course of its run, the series won ten Primetime Emmy Awards and featured many noteworthy actors and playwrights.

The *General der Nachrichtenaufklärung Training Referat* (GDNA Training Section) was a German military signals intelligence training unit during World War II. It trained cryptanalysts in methods of decipherment, substitution, transposition, and machine ciphers. The unit was part of *In 7/VI* and later *Referat 5* of Group IV of the GDNA.

Four Lane Ends is a Tyne and Wear Metro station, serving the suburbs of Benton and Longbenton, North Tyneside in Tyne and Wear, England. It joined the network on 11 August 1980, following the opening of the first phase of the network, between Haymarket and Tynemouth via Four Lane Ends.

68th Siege Battery was a unit of Britain's Royal Garrison Artillery (RGA) formed in Wales during World War I. It served on the Western Front, including the Battles of the Somme and Passchendaele, defended against the German Spring Offensive and took part in the crushing victories of the Allied Hundred Days Offensive in 1918.

The Wrocław Global Forum is an annual international conference held in Wrocław, Poland, since 2010. It brings together politicians, diplomats, military officers, journalists, academics, and business leaders to discuss transatlantic cooperation and global challenges. The Atlantic Council's Freedom Awards are presented at the Forum.
Cary Castle stood on Lodge Hill overlooking the town of Castle Cary, Somerset, England. It is a Scheduled Ancient Monument.

Building officials of developed countries are generally the jurisdictional administrator of building and construction codes, engineering calculation supervision, permits, facilities management, and accepted construction procedures.
Ethelbert Watts (1846–1919) was an American diplomat who served as a consul-general in various countries, including Russia during the Russo-Japanese War and Belgium during World War I.

William Henry Seaman (November 15, 1842 – March 8, 1915) was an American lawyer and judge from Wisconsin. He served as a United States federal judge for 22 years, first in the Eastern District of Wisconsin, then on the Seventh Circuit.

The Port of Gaza is a small port located in the Gaza Strip, State of Palestine. Historically, the area was the site of the ancient port of Maiuma. The modern port was constructed by the Palestinian National Authority (PNA). Since 2007, following Hamas' takeover of Gaza, Israel has imposed a naval blockade, restricting access to the port. Several attempts have been made to break the blockade. Plans for a larger seaport in Gaza have been proposed since 1993 but have not materialized due to the Israeli–Palestinian conflict.

The 9th Marine Regiment was an infantry regiment of the United States Marine Corps. Formed during World War II, it served until 1994, when it was deactivated during the post Cold War drawdown. Battalions of the Ninth Marine Regiment, but not the regiment itself, were reactivated from 2007 to 2014 as part of the Marine Corps' growth during the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. The battalions have subsequently been deactivated again.

The mountain ground squirrel (Geosciurus princeps) is a rodent found in the southwest arid region of Africa, from southern Angola to southern Namibia and as far south as Richtersveld National Park. It is classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List.

This large-bodied squirrel has small ears, with a head and body length of 23 to 29 cm, a tail length of 21 to 28 cm, and a weight of 490 to 710 grams. Its body is covered in short, pale cinnamon brown hair, with white fur on its belly, around the eyes, and on the front of its face. A white stripe extends from its shoulders to its hips. Mountain ground squirrels are diurnal, with females living alone or in small family groups, while males are mostly solitary.

The Yalta International Economic Forum is an annual business event held in Yalta, Crimea. It was established in August 2015 by the Yalta International Economic Forum Foundation, with support from the Republic of Crimea's Ministry of Justice and Council of Ministers, as well as the Russian Presidential Administration. Following a successful inaugural event in 2015, the forum was made an annual occurrence. The 2017 forum saw participation from 2,200 individuals representing 46 countries. The 2018 forum was expected to draw over 3,000 participants from more than 60 countries and included topics such as cryptocurrencies.

Dehraadun Diary is a Hindi mystery thriller film released in January 2013. This was the debut film for Ragini Nandwani who made the jump from TV to film industry. The film is based on a real life murder.

Charles Van Dell Johnson (August 25, 1916 – December 12, 2008) was an American film, television, theatre and radio actor. He was a major star at Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer during and after World War II.

Johnson was described as the embodiment of the "boy-next-door wholesomeness" which made him a popular Hollywood star in the 1940s and 1950s, playing "the red-haired, freckle-faced soldier, sailor, or bomber pilot who used to live down the street" in MGM films during the war years, with such films as Thirty Seconds Over Tokyo, A Guy Named Joe, and The Human Comedy. He made occasional World War II films through the end of the 1960s, and he played a military officer in one of his final feature films in 1992. At the time of his death in 2008, he was one of the last surviving matinee idols of Hollywood's "golden age".

The terpene synthase C-terminal domain (Terpene_synth_C) is a protein domain involved in terpene synthesis. It catalyzes the cyclization of geranyl diphosphate, a key step in producing terpenoids. This domain is found in a large family of terpene synthases (Tps) and is thought to have at least two alpha helices.

Crystina Poncher is a play-by-play broadcaster for Top Rank, currently the only female in that role in boxing. She has also worked for the NFL Network, NFL.com, Fox Sports West, and Prime Ticket. She graduated from Long Beach State.
*Crossing the Red Sea with The Adverts* is the 1978 debut album by the English punk rock band The Adverts, recorded at Abbey Road Studios. It reached number 38 on the UK Albums Chart.

Helene P. Foley is an American classical scholar. She is Professor of Classical Studies at Barnard College, Columbia University and a member of the Institute for Research on Women, Gender and Sexuality at Columbia. She specialises in ancient Greek literature (epic poetry, tragedy, and comedy), women and gender in antiquity, and the reception of classical drama.

An acoustic torpedo is a torpedo that uses sonar to detect, classify, and home in on its target. These torpedoes are equipped with acoustic transducers that create acoustic beams to sense sound originating from the target (passive sonar) or from reflections of sonar pulses emitted by the torpedo (active sonar). Once launched, the torpedo uses a fire control system to search for the target within a set depth range, initially employing passive sonar and then switching to active sonar for tracking.

During World War II, the introduction of acoustic torpedoes by German U-boats in the Battle of the Atlantic initially posed a significant threat to Allied escorts and merchant ships. However, the Allies developed countermeasures such as the Foxer noise maker, which generated louder cavitation noises to decoy the torpedoes. Other countermeasures included the "Step-Aside" maneuver. Post-war, towed decoys like the AN/SLQ-25 Nixie and AN/SLQ-61 Lightweight Tow (LWT) Torpedo Defense Mission Module (TDMM) have been developed to defend against acoustic homing torpedoes. The capture of German U-boats, such as U-505 and U-250, provided Allied forces and the Soviet Union with access to the technology, furthering the development of countermeasures.

Cine City was a cinema located at 494 Wilmslow Road, Withington, Manchester. It opened around 1912 as The Scala, later becoming the Scala Electric Palace and Scala Palace Cinema. It was the third cinema to open in Britain, and by its closure in 2001, it was the third longest-running cinema in the country. Originally a single-screen auditorium with 675 seats, it was a popular venue, even hosting performances by future celebrities like John Barbirolli and Violet Carson.

Despite minor damage during the Manchester Blitz in 1940, the cinema continued operating. In the 1970s, it was renamed Cine City and divided into three screens. It closed in July 2001 due to competition from a new multiplex. After closure, several redevelopment plans were proposed but faced rejection. The building was demolished in 2008, though its Grade II listed stone piers were retained. After 15 years, a block of flats called the Scala Apartments, named after the original picture house, was completed in 2018.

Buck Gurley (born April 7, 1978) is an American football coach and former player. He played college football for the Florida Gators, and professionally for the Tampa Bay Buccaneers, winning Super Bowl XXXVII. He is currently a coach at Lake Mary Preparatory School.

The Love of Hypnosis (Chinese: 南烟斋笔录; pinyin: Nán Yānzhāi Bǐlù; lit. 'Southern Mist House Records') is an upcoming Chinese television series based on the eponymous manhua Nan Yanzhai Bilu (南烟斋笔录) by Ke Xiaosha and Zuo Xiaoling. It stars Liu Yifei and Jing Boran.
Philipp Jakob Wilhelm von Henke (19 June 1834 in Jena – 17 May 1896 in Tübingen) was a German anatomist.

*Vishwa Thulasi* is a 2004 Indian Tamil-language film directed by Sumathy Ram, starring Mammootty and Nandita Das. Set in the 1940s and 1960s, the film revolves around Thulasi, who returns to her village to become a dance teacher and reunites with her past love, Vishwa.
Building officials ensure construction projects comply with building codes and regulations. Qualifications and specific roles vary by jurisdiction.
Police impersonation involves individuals falsely presenting themselves as law enforcement officers to commit crimes.

Jack Ross is a New Zealand writer and lecturer at Massey University. He specializes in academic and creative writing. Ross has edited several literary magazines, including *brief*, *The Pander*, *Spin*, *Landfall*, and *Poetry NZ*. His work is known within New Zealand avant-garde literary circles.

Suzanne M. Levine, DPM, PC, is a clinical podiatrist and a foot surgeon at the NewYork–Presbyterian Hospital, Lenox Hill Hospital and The Center for Specialty Care in New York City, New York, United States. She is best known for her book My Feet Are Killing Me!, and for her contributions to several notable newspapers, magazines and TV shows. Levine practices at her New York City podiatry clinic and medical-spa, Institute Beauté.

The instruction cycle (also known as the fetch–decode–execute cycle, or simply the fetch-execute cycle) is the cycle that the central processing unit (CPU) follows from boot-up until the computer has shut down in order to process instructions. It is composed of three main stages: the fetch stage, the decode stage, and the execute stage.

In simpler CPUs, the instruction cycle is executed sequentially, each instruction being processed before the next one is started. In most modern CPUs, the instruction cycles are instead executed concurrently, and often in parallel, through an instruction pipeline: the next instruction starts being processed before the previous instruction has finished, which is possible because the cycle is broken up into separate steps.
Dale Albert Kimball (born November 28, 1939) is a Senior Judge of the United States District Court for the District of Utah, assuming office in 2009.
Ham Green Pottery was produced near Bristol, England, from the 12th to 13th centuries. The pottery was traded throughout Britain and is now used to date archaeological sites.

Vincent is a census-designated place (CDP) in Los Angeles County, California, United States. The population was 15,922 at the 2010 census. Vincent is located at an elevation of 3,225 feet (983 m). According to the United States Census Bureau, the CDP has a total area of 1.5 square miles (3.9 km2), all of it land.
Recep Burak Yılmaz (born 27 November 1995), is a Turkish professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Hatayspor.

Walton Castle was a Saxon Shore fort built in the late 3rd century near Felixstowe in Suffolk, England. It may be the *Portus Adurni* listed in the *Notitia Dignitatum*. The fort was reused by the Normans, who built a castle within its walls. Coastal erosion led to its collapse into the sea by the late 18th century. Remnants are visible during exceptionally low tides.

Potamia (Greek: Ποταμιά, Turkish: Potamya) is a village in the Nicosia District of Cyprus. In 2001, it had a population of 415. Historically, the village had a Turkish-Cypriot majority, but now Greek-Cypriots form the majority. Potamia is known for its bi-communal character, with a history of co-existence and cooperation between Greek and Turkish Cypriots. The village was also the site of a Royal residence.

In ecology, rarefaction is a technique to assess species richness from the results of sampling. Rarefaction allows the calculation of species richness for a given number of individual samples, based on the construction of so-called rarefaction curves. This curve is a plot of the number of species as a function of the number of samples. Rarefaction curves generally grow rapidly at first, as the most common species are found, but the curves plateau as only the rarest species remain to be sampled.

The issue that occurs when sampling various species in a community is that the larger the number of individuals sampled, the more species that will be found. Rarefaction curves are created by randomly re-sampling the pool of N samples multiple times and then plotting the average number of species found in each sample (1,2,. N). "Thus rarefaction generates the expected number of species in a small collection of n individuals (or n samples) drawn at random from the large pool of N samples.".
North Bengal State Library is the largest library in the North Bengal region of India. It is situated in the Cooch Behar city area.

The Mad Dog Gang Meets Rotten Fred and Ratsguts is a 1978 New Zealand children's television drama. It was written by Ian Mune and Arthur Baysting and directed by Ross Jennings. It was filmed in Dunedin and the surrounding Otago country district.

Club Deportivo Corporación Arturo Fernández Vial is a Chilean football club based in Concepción, Chile, founded on June 3, 1903. Known as *La Maquina Aurinegra*, they play their home games at the Estadio Municipal de Concepción, which has a capacity of 30,448. In 1982, the club won the Second Division title, achieving promotion to the Chilean First Division. Currently, they compete in the Primera B.

Stefi Baum (born December 11, 1958 in Chicago, Illinois) is an American astronomer. The American Astronomical Society honored her work by awarding her the Annie J. Cannon Prize in 1993. Baum helped to develop the Hubble Space Telescope and, starting in 2004, was the director of Rochester Institute of Technology’s Chester F. Carlson Center for Imaging Science.

GABA transporter 2 (GAT2; SLC6A13) also known as sodium- and chloride-dependent GABA transporter 2 is one of four GABA transporters, GAT1 (SLC6A1), GAT2 (SLC6A13), GAT3 (SLC6A11) and BGT1 (SLC6A12). Note that GAT2 is different from BGT1 despite the fact that the latter transporter is sometimes referred at as (mouse) GAT-2.

All these transporters are highly hydrophobic proteins with 12 transmembrane segments, extracellular glycosylation sites, and intracellular consensus sites for phosphorylation, and there is over 50% amino acid homology between each of them. Each binds GABA with varying affinities with BGT1 having the lowest affinity and GAT3 the highest. GAT2 (SLC6A13) is predominantly expressed in hepatocytes in the liver, but is also found in proximal tubules in the kidney as well as in the leptomeninges and in some blood vessels in the brain.

*Marvel Super Heroes: The Heroic Role-Playing Game* is a superhero role-playing game published by TSR in 1984. It was written by Jeff Grubb and Steve Winter, with a scenario by Bruce Nesmith. The game uses a simple "Universal Table" to determine the success of actions.

An eruption column or eruption plume is a cloud of super-heated ash and tephra suspended in gases emitted during an explosive volcanic eruption. The volcanic materials form a vertical column or plume that may rise many kilometers into the air above the vent of the volcano. In the most explosive eruptions, the eruption column may rise over 40 km (25 mi), penetrating the stratosphere. Stratospheric injection of aerosols by volcanoes is a major cause of short-term climate change.

A common occurrence in explosive eruptions is column collapse when the eruption column is or becomes too dense to be lifted high into the sky by air convection, and instead falls down the slopes of the volcano to form pyroclastic flows or surges (although the latter is less dense). On some occasions, if the material is not dense enough to fall, it may create pyrocumulonimbus clouds.
George Ware, Ph.D. (1924–2010) was an American dendrologist and former research director of the Morton Arboretum Illinois who specialized in the evaluation of Asiatic species of elm as urban trees.

Stone Brothers Racing (SBR) was an Australian motor racing team that competed in the International V8 Supercars Championship between 1998 and 2012. The team was formed in 1998 when Ross and Jim Stone bought Alan Jones's shares in Alan Jones Racing and renamed it Stone Brothers Racing.

The team won the Bathurst 1000 in its debut year with Jason Bright and Steven Richards. It won the 2003 and 2004 championships with Marcos Ambrose and the 2005 championship with Russell Ingall. SBR won the Teams Championship (combined points of both cars) in 2003, 2004 and 2005, the 2004 V8 Supercars Series being a 1–2 finish.

The team was based in Yatala on the Gold Coast. Their nominated test track was initially Lakeside Raceway and later Queensland Raceway.

At the end of 2012, the team was sold to Erebus Motorsport.

The Mississippi Wing of Civil Air Patrol (CAP) is the highest echelon of CAP in the state of Mississippi. It has over 500 members as of 2018. The wing performs three missions: emergency services, cadet programs, and aerospace education. In 2014, the wing saved six lives through search and rescue missions.
François Picquet (1708–1781) was a French Sulpician missionary to New France, where he ministered to the First Nations.

Bygdøy Royal Estate (Lang-no), also known as the Bygdø Royal Farm, is a Kongsgård estate and manor house that occupies a large part of the northwestern part of the Bygdøy peninsula in Oslo, Norway. It is the official summer residence of the King of Norway.

St. Mary's High School is a coeducational school located in Belagavi, Karnataka, India. Established in 1851, the school serves students from LKG through the 10th grade. Students participate in a variety of co-curricular activities, including sports, clubs, and talent exams. The school also offers a number of scholarships.

Germinated wheat, known as *samani* (سمنی) in Azerbaijan, holds significant cultural importance as a symbol of renewal and prosperity. The cultivation of *samani*, which refers to green sprouting wheat, is a central and sacred tradition during Novruz, the celebration of the spring equinox. The sprouting wheat represents sowing, a bountiful harvest, grain, bread, increase, and abundance. It is seen as a pledge of life and a vital necessity.

The practice involves growing wheat, barley, peas, lentils, or other grains in copper dishes, reflecting a deep reverence for the process of germination. The Azerbaijani proverb "Səməni, ay səməni, hər il göyərdərəm səni" ("samani, o samani, I try to make you grow every year") encapsulates the annual renewal of nature that *samani* embodies.

Oak was a programming language developed by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems in 1992 for smart appliances. It was later renamed Java in 1994 due to a trademark conflict. Oak served as the foundation for Java 1.0, which was released in 1996.
Machi is a Taiwanese hip-hop group formed in 2003 and still active as of 2024. They are signed to Warner Music Group and Machi Entertainment.

Benton Township is one of ten townships in Adair County, Missouri, United States. As of the 2010 census, its population was 19,700. It is named for Thomas Hart Benton, one of the inaugural pair of U.S. senators from Missouri.

A limiting factor restricts the size a population can reach or the rate at which a process can occur. In ecology, it regulates population equilibrium, often based on the scarcest resource, as described by Liebig's Law of the Minimum. The concept also applies to business, technology, and chemistry, where it constrains production, sales, or reaction rates.
The Insurance Act, 1938 is an Indian law consolidating and amending regulations for the insurance industry. It was enacted by the Imperial Legislative Council and has been amended since.
Joan Maie Freeman AO (7 January 1918 – 18 March 1998) was an Australian physicist.

Cephaloridine is a first-generation cephalosporin antibiotic introduced in 1962. It was briefly popular for its high blood levels after intramuscular administration, but its poor oral absorption and the introduction of second-generation cephalosporins led to its decline in human use. It is still used in veterinary medicine. Cephaloridine can cause kidney damage.
HMS Rover was a 16-gun sloop-of-war that the Royal Navy purchased in 1796, commissioned in 1798, and that was wrecked in early 1798. In her brief career she captured one French privateer.

Severn FM is an internet radio station in the United Kingdom, which started broadcasting online in June 2011, and officially launched from its studio in Hardwicke on 3 December 2011 with the Severn Bore Breakfast Show, the launch coincided with its first 28-day RSL broadcast on 87.9 FM. The brainchild of Andy Clarke (Managing Director of South Gloucester Media CIC and Ringtone.net Ltd) who used his skills gained as a mobile network consultant, it initially followed the standard community radio model but has evolved along the way under the leadership of Andy and his Board of Directors (Barry Kirby, Caro Kingsnorth and Fr Andrew James) along with the presenters and other volunteers who form part of this growing and evolving South Gloucester "Community Project".

Severn FM is a 24/7 Internet Radio Station broadcasting on the internet.
Cipher runes, or cryptic runes, are the cryptographical replacement of the letters of the runic alphabet.
