The Maas–Hoffman model describes the relationship between crop yield and soil salinity using two linear equations. It is defined by the maximum yield (C), the slope (A) and intercept (B) of a descending line, and the salinity level (X). The model is used to assess crop tolerance to seawater. An inverted version of the model can be used for growth factors that negatively affect crop production at low values.
Michael N. Delagi (April 22, 1879 – October 7, 1957) was an American lawyer, politician, and judge from New York.

The Zec de la Rivière-Blanche is a controlled harvesting zone (ZEC) in the Portneuf Regional County Municipality, Quebec, Canada, established in 1978. It is administered by L'Association sportive Miguick, Inc. and is known for hunting, fishing, camping, and water sports. The ZEC features 240 lakes and 130 km of motorable roads.

*Code R* is an American action-adventure television series that aired on CBS from January 21 to June 10, 1977. Produced by Warner Bros., the show starred James Houghton, Martin Kove, and Tom Simcox as heads of emergency services on an island. It was cancelled after 13 episodes due to low ratings.

The Leader of Alderney is the civil leader of Alderney. Alderney is a dependency of the Bailiwick of Guernsey. Its leader has traditionally been appointed by the British Crown and has been known by various titles including Lord of Alderney, Governor of Alderney, and the current President of the States of Alderney. The President of the States of Alderney is directly elected every four years and there is no constitutional limit to the number of terms served. The current president, William Tate has held the post since 2019.

Hugh Alan Leslie OBE (17 April 1900 – 2 September 1974) was an Australian politician. He was a member of the Australian Parliament for Moore from 1949 to 1958, and again from 1961 to 1963. Prior to federal politics, Leslie served in the Western Australian Legislative Assembly.

KNBN (channel 21) is a television station in Rapid City, South Dakota, United States, affiliated with NBC and MyNetworkTV. Owned by Jim Simpson's Rapid Broadcasting, the station maintains studios on South Plaza Drive in Rapid City, and its transmitter is located on Cowboy Hill west of downtown.

KNBN can also be seen on low-power digital translators KWBH-LD (channel 27) in Rapid City; K22AD-D (channel 22) in Gillette, Wyoming; and K35MW-D (channel 35) in Lead, South Dakota (also serving the Black Hills region).

Jacob Blankenship is an American pole vaulter. While at Gahanna Lincoln High School, he set Ohio state records indoors and outdoors. He won a bronze medal at the 2011 World Youth Championships.

As a freshman at the University of Tennessee in 2012, he placed fourth at the NCAA Men's Indoor Track and Field Championships. He won the Penn Relays in both 2013 and 2014. Blankenship placed third at the 2014 NCAA Outdoor Championships and won a gold medal at the 2014 NACAC Under-23 Championships. In 2015, he improved his personal best to 5.80 m.

Angelino Dulcert (fl. 1325–1339) is the name associated with two major portolan charts, one dated 1339 held in Paris, and another formerly dated 1325, now revised to 1330, held privately in Florence. Virtually nothing is known of Dulcert's life. It is commonly assumed he was an Italian from Liguria who emigrated to Majorca. The 1339 map is considered the founding piece of the Majorcan cartographic school, notable for its detailed illustrations. A third map, unsigned but attributed to Dulcert, is held in London.

The Maas–Hoffman model is a mathematical tool to characterize the relation between crop production and soil salinity. It describes the crop response by a broken line of which the first part is horizontal and the second is sloping downward. The breakpoint (Pb) or threshold is also called tolerance because up to that point the yield is unaffected by the salinity, so the salt is tolerated, while at greater salinity values the crops are affected negatively and the yield goes down.

The Bridge for Kids, officially named the Orting Emergency Evacuation Bridge, is a suspension bridge in Orting, Washington. It is designed to facilitate the rapid evacuation of residents, particularly students, in the event of a lahar from Mount Rainier. The bridge is 540 feet (160 m) long and is designed to accommodate 12,000 people in 30 minutes. Orting is estimated to have a 40 minute evacuation window after the Mount Rainier Volcano Lahar Warning System is activated.

Sajek Tripuri Valley is one of the most popular tourist spots in Bangladesh situated among the hills of the Kasalong range of mountains in Sajek union, Baghaichhari Upazila in Rangamati District. The valley is 2,000 feet (610 m) above sea level. Sajek Tripuri Valley is known as the Queen of Hills & Roof of Rangamati.
Scott Campbell (born December 28, 1973) is an American production designer, concept artist, and art director. He is known for his work at LucasArts and Double Fine Productions.

"Iced Out Audemars" is a song by American rapper Pop Smoke, featuring rapper Dafi Woo, from the deluxe version of the former's posthumous debut studio album, Shoot for the Stars, Aim for the Moon (2020). The song was written by Pop Smoke, who is credited under his legal name of Bashar Jackson, alongside John Stevens, Khadafi Julio, and its lead producer Rico Beats. A drill track, the lyrics see Pop Smoke and Dafi Woo celebrating over the finer things and expressing their love for Dior, among other designer jewelry.

A remix of the song that features Lil Wayne was released on October 15, 2020. For the lyrics of the remix, Lil Wayne replaces Dafi Woo and pays homage to Pop Smoke after he died in a home invasion on February 19, 2020. The remix of "Iced Out Audemars" received positive reviews from music critics, with a number of them praising Lil Wayne's verse.

Mount Kineo is a prominent geological feature located on a 1,150-acre (470 ha) peninsula that extends from the easterly shore of Moosehead Lake in the northern forest of Maine. With 700-foot (210 m) cliffs rising straight up from the water, it is the central feature of Mount Kineo State Park, a protected area of 800 acres (320 ha) managed by the Maine Department of Agriculture, Conservation and Forestry.
Henry-Louis de La Grange (1924–2017) was a French musicologist and biographer, best known for his work on Gustav Mahler.

Sir John Dinham (1359–1428) was a knight from Devonshire, England. His principal seats were at Hartland in North Devon, Kingskerswell and Nutwell in South Devon, Buckland Dinham in Somerset and Cardinham in Cornwall. He killed one of the murderers of his father in Exeter Cathedral, for which he was pardoned by the king. He later broke into Hartland Abbey and assaulted the Abbot over a long-standing disagreement, and also performed other acts of violence. He married three times; his heir was John Dinham (1406–1458). His monument survives in Kingskerswell parish church.
Marko Čakarević (Serbian Cyrillic: Марко Чакаревић, born May 13, 1988) is a Serbian professional basketball player for Beograd (3x3) team.

San José Castle -also known as Castillo de San José in Spanish- was opened to the public on 25 May 1846 on the «Buena Vista Hill» on the southeast of Guatemala City by general Rafael Carrera government; it was also known as «Carrera Castle». San José Fort was located where later Guatemalan administrations built a new city Hall, a National Theater and part of the Bolivar Avenue. The fort had a shooting range for the soldiers, ammunition warehouses, horses, diners and a small pond that was called "Soldier's Pond".

The Togliattiazot affair involves allegations of fraud and misappropriation surrounding the privatization of Togliattiazot (Toaz) and the Transammiak pipeline in Russia. Investigations and legal battles ensued, including claims of tax evasion, anti-monopoly violations, and the violation of minority shareholder rights. In July 2019, five individuals, including Vladimir and Sergei Makhlai, were convicted in absentia for their roles in the scheme. The court also awarded Uralchem billions of rubles in damages.

Vijenac (English: The Wreath) is a biweekly magazine for literature, art and science, established in December 1993 and published by Matica hrvatska, the central national cultural institution in Croatia.
MercyMe, It's Christmas is the second Christmas album by MercyMe, released on October 9, 2015, through Fair Trade Services. The album was produced by Brown Bannister and Ben Shive.

In polymer chemistry, condensation polymers are any kind of polymers whose process of polymerization involves a condensation reaction (i.e. a small molecule, such as water or methanol, is produced as a byproduct). Condensation polymers are formed by polycondensation, when the polymer is formed by condensation reactions between species of all degrees of polymerization, or by condensative chain polymerization, when the polymer is formed by sequential addition of monomers to an active site in a chain reaction. The main alternative forms of polymerization are chain polymerization and polyaddition, both of which give addition polymers.

Polycondensation A polymerization in which the growth of polymer chains proceeds by condensation reactions between molecules of all degrees of polymerization. Notes: 1. The growth steps are expressed by: Px+Py→Px+y+L {x}∈{1,2,.∞};{y}∈{1,2,.∞}

where Px and Py denote chains of degrees of polymerization x and y, respectively, and L a low-molar-mass by-product. 2. The earlier term 'polycondensation' was synonymous with 'condensation polymerization'. The current definitions of polycondensation and condensative chain polymerization were both embraced by the earlier term 'polycondensation'.

Condensation polymerization is a form of step-growth polymerization. Linear polymers are produced from bifunctional monomers, i.e. compounds with two reactive end-groups. Common condensation polymers include polyamides, polyacetals, and proteins.
Madonna and Child Kissing is a 1520s oil on panel painting by the Flemish renaissance artist Quentin Matsys in the collection of the Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam, on loan to the Mauritshuis.
Soviet Union military aircraft designation systems evolved from a pre-war system to one introduced in 1940. NATO also developed a reporting name system.

Ticagrelor, sold under the brand name Brilinta among others, is a medication used for the prevention of stroke, heart attack and other events in people with acute coronary syndrome, meaning problems with blood supply in the coronary arteries. It acts as a platelet aggregation inhibitor by antagonising the P2Y12 receptor. The drug is produced by AstraZeneca.

The most common side effects include dyspnea (difficulty breathing), bleeding and raised uric acid level in the blood.

It was approved for medical use in the European Union in December 2010, and in the United States in July 2011. In 2020, it was the 247th most commonly prescribed medication in the United States, with more than 1 million prescriptions.

The 2009 AFL season saw the Geelong Cats defeat the St Kilda Saints in the Grand Final. St Kilda finished as minor premiers. Geelong also won the pre-season cup. Gary Ablett, Jr. won the Brownlow Medal, and Brendan Fevola won the Coleman Medal.

The pre-season draft saw Richmond select Ben Cousins. Daniel Rich won the AFL Rising Star award. The season also saw rule changes and issues with players contacting umpires.

The Reading Municipal Building is a historic civic building at 49 Pleasant Street in Reading, Massachusetts. Built in 1883, it was designed by Horace G. Wadlin in the Renaissance Revival and Stick/Eastlake styles. The building originally housed town offices, the fire station, and the local jail. It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1984 and now serves as a senior center.

Sanan Suleymanov (born in Dmanisi) is an Azerbaijani Greco-Roman wrestler. He won a bronze medal at the 2021 European Wrestling Championships and a silver medal at the 2019 World U23 Wrestling Championship. He also won a bronze medal at the 2022 European Wrestling Championships and a silver medal at the 2021 Islamic Solidarity Games.

The Sleeping Tiger is a 1954 British film noir directed by Joseph Losey and starring Alexis Smith, Dirk Bogarde and Alexander Knox. It was Losey's first British feature, which he directed under the pseudonym of Victor Hanbury due to being blacklisted in the McCarthy Era. It was shot at Walton Studios and on location in London. The film's sets were designed by the art director John Stoll. It was released by Anglo-Amalgamated while in America it was distributed by Astor Pictures.

Andrée Geulen (6 September 1921 – 31 May 2022) was a Belgian teacher and member of the Committee for the Defence of Jews (CDJ) during the Holocaust in Belgium. As one of the few non-Jewish members of the CDJ, she persuaded Jewish families to give their children to the organization, which then placed them with non-Jewish families, schools, and religious institutions under false identities. She was personally involved in the arrangements made for 300 Jewish children. After the war, she remained involved with the Jewish community. Geulen was recognized as Righteous Among the Nations in 1989 and granted honorary Israeli citizenship in 2007.

Stepan Petrovich Krasheninnikov (Lang-ru; November 11 [O.S. October 31] 1711 – March 8 [O.S. February 25] 1755) was a Russian explorer of Siberia, naturalist and geographer who gave the first full description of Kamchatka in the early 18th century. He was elected to the Russian Academy of Sciences in 1745. The Krasheninnikov Volcano on Kamchatka is named in his honour.

Cuck is a 2019 American thriller film directed by Rob Lambert from a screenplay by Lambert and Joe Varkle. It stars Zachary Ray Sherman, Timothy V. Murphy, Sally Kirkland and Monique Parent.

The film had its world premiere at the Cleveland International Film Festival on March 28, 2019 and it was released on October 4, 2019.

Springfield Central Railway Station is the terminus station of the Springfield Line in Queensland, Australia. It serves Springfield Central (the central suburb of Greater Springfield) and other nearby suburbs in the City of Ipswich. Brisbane-bound services depart half-hourly during off-peak times, with most travelling to Kippa-Ring. The station was opened in December 2013 after the line was extended from Richlands.

Walhalla-orden was a secret society founded in the early part of 1783 in the Sveaborg (today, in Finnish: Suomenlinna) fortress outside Helsinki, Finland by Johan Anders Jägerhorn along with Gustaf Adolf Reuterholm. It is thought to have been instrumental in setting in motion forces that eventually caused Finnish independence.

The Holy Saviour Monastery of Julfa (Armenian: Սուրբ Ամենափրկիչ վանք, Surb Amenaprkich Vank) was an Armenian Apostolic monastery near Jugha, Nakhichevan. Thought to be founded in the 9th or 10th century, it was reportedly destroyed between 1997 and 2003. The monastery also had a cemetery with 47 tombstones, which was also destroyed.

The Gonzaga Bulldogs (/ɡənˈzæɡə/) (also known unofficially as the Zags) are the intercollegiate athletic teams representing Gonzaga University, located in Spokane, Washington, United States. Gonzaga competes in the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) Division I as a member of the West Coast Conference.
Archibald Cameron Corbett, 1st Baron Rowallan (23 May 1856 – 19 March 1933), was a Liberal Party Member of Parliament and Liberal Unionist Party politician.

Boane District is a district of Maputo Province in southern Mozambique. The principal town is Boane. The district is located in the center of the province, and borders with Moamba District in the north, the city of Matola in the northeast, Matutuíne District in the south, and with Namaacha District in the west. In the east, it is bounded by the Indian Ocean. The area of the district is 815 square kilometres (315 sq mi). It has a population of 98,964 as of 2007.

*Dutchman* is a one-act play by Amiri Baraka, first presented in March 1964. It focuses on a chance encounter between Lula, a white woman, and Clay, a young Black man, on a New York City Subway train. The play explores themes of race, identity, gender, and social power dynamics in America.

The plot follows Lula and Clay's increasingly tense interaction, filled with flirtation, manipulation, and racial provocation. Lula confronts Clay with stereotypes and alludes to intimate details of his past. Clay eventually erupts in a monologue expressing the suppressed rage of Black Americans. The play culminates in a violent act, highlighting the destructive forces of racial prejudice. The title *Dutchman* alludes to the slave ships that brought Africans to America, and the subway car symbolizes the inescapable "Course of History."

Nagorik Natya Sampradaya is a Bangladeshi theatre company. Established in 1968, it was the first theatre group in Dhaka and started performing plays on stage in 1972. The chairperson of the group is the actor and director Aly Zaker and his wife, actress Sara Zaker, is the vice-chairperson. As of December 2018, Nagorik had performed 46 different productions.
Gamawan Fauzi (born 9 November 1957) is an Indonesian politician.

*Wizzard Brew* is the 1973 debut album by the British rock band Wizzard. Produced by Roy Wood, the album was recorded at Phonogram and EMI Studios in London. The album is considered to be in the genre of art rock and rock and roll. It was reissued on CD in 1999, and again in 2006 with additional tracks. The album was met with mixed critical and popular reaction.
The Insurance Act, 1938 is a law originally passed in 1938 in British India to regulate the insurance sector. It provides the broad legal framework within which the industry operates.

Janet Haworth is a Welsh Conservative politician. She was a Member of the Welsh Assembly (AM) for the North Wales region from May 2015 to April 2016, succeeding Antoinette Sandbach. Haworth was third on the Conservative list for North Wales in the 2011 election. She resigned as a Councillor in October 2015.

Tamara Bowie (born June 3, 1981) is an American former professional basketball player. In 2007, she was named the Úrvalsdeild kvenna Foreign Player of the Year after leading the league in scoring and rebounds. She played professionally in Iceland, Latvia, Israel, Greece and Bulgaria, where she won the national championship in 2011.

Patillas (IPA-es, IPA-es) is a beach town and municipality of Puerto Rico located in the southeastern coast, south of San Lorenzo; west of Yabucoa and Maunabo; and east of Guayama and Arroyo. It is spread over 15 barrios and Patillas Pueblo (the downtown area and the administrative center of the city). It is part of the Guayama Metropolitan Statistical Area.
Hillhead High School is a co-educational secondary school in Glasgow, Scotland, established in 1885 and comprehensive since 1972.
The former Roman Catholic Diocese of Ypres, in present-day Belgium, existed from 1559 to 1801. Its seat was Saint Martin's Cathedral in Ypres. In 1969 it was reconstituted as a titular see.

*MercyMe, It's Christmas!* is a 2015 Christmas album by MercyMe. It received positive reviews, with critics praising its broad appeal and unique, spirit-filled sound, calling it a success and a spectacular addition to any Christmas music collection.
Villa Park is a neighborhood in West Denver, Colorado, bounded by Lakewood Gulch, Federal Boulevard, the 6th Avenue Freeway, and Sheridan Boulevard. The population in 2017 was 9,400.
Christa Peters-Lidard is an American hydrologist known for her work on integrating land surface modeling and data assimilation, particularly with remotely sensed measurements of precipitation.

Jagadishpur is a census town in the Howrah district of West Bengal, India. In 2011, it had a population of 25,360. It is located at 22°39′N 88°17′E. Benaras Road (State Highway 15) is the town's main thoroughfare.

*Aussie Gold Hunters* is an Australian factual television series that premiered on the Discovery Channel on September 15, 2016. Narrated by Matt Dickson, the series follows various gold miners in Western Australia, Victoria, and Queensland as they strive to meet their seasonal gold targets using diverse mining techniques. Produced by Electric Pictures, the show has been a ratings success in Australia and the United Kingdom, and has been broadcast in 125 countries.

Yemodish Bekele (born 1960) is a published author of more than seven books, including poetry, short stories, and a novella, and was the first Ethiopian woman to publish her own collection of short stories. A crime journalist, Yemedish was also the first woman to become the head editor for Polis Ena Ermijaw (‘Police and Future Journeys’) the newspaper of the Federal Police in Ethiopia.

An activist for women's rights, she works with and serves on board for organisations devoted to women's advancement. She is currently the president of the Ethiopian Women Writers’ Association, and serves as the chair of the board of the Network of Ethiopian Women's Associations.

Cavaliere Modesto Marini (born in Lake Garda, Italy) is an Italian chef, restaurateur and entrepreneur. He is the Founder of The Marini's Group which includes Marini's on 57 (Malaysia's highest rooftop bar, lounge and contemporary Italian restaurant), Marble 8 (Kuala Lumpur's premier steakhouse specializing in producing its own dry-aged steak); and M Marini Caffè, Malaysia's first dedicated champagne and caviar cafe.

Cavaliere Modesto Marini was awarded the prestigious Ordine Della Stella D’Italia (Knight of the Order of the Star of Italy) award in October 2016, which carries the title “Cavaliere” and is one of the highest civilian honors of the Italian government.

It recognizes Italians living abroad or foreigners who have been instrumental in the preservation and promotion of national prestige abroad, who have made important contributions to relations between their country and Italy. It is awarded in many areas, including philanthropic activities, scientific and technological research, commercial enterprises and the promotion of Italian food and wine.

GLtron is a 3D snake game based on the light cycle portion of the film Tron. The game is free and open-source software and has been ported to many mobile and non-mobile operating systems such as Windows, MacOS, Symbian and Android over the years.
**Secours populaire français** (SPF) is a French non-profit organization dedicated to fighting poverty and discrimination in France and worldwide.

The Medieval University of Dublin was a short-lived institution established in 1320 by Archbishop Alexander de Bicknor, by authority of a papal brief granted in 1311. It was closely connected with St Patrick's Cathedral, Dublin. Despite efforts to revive it, the university suffered from a lack of funds and political instability. It effectively ceased to exist by 1475 and disappeared altogether during the Irish Reformation of 1534–41.
Ludvík Podéšť (1921–1998) was a Czech composer of classical music, film scores, and popular songs, some of which promoted the Czechoslovak communist regime.

Matthijs van Nieuwkerk (IPA-nl; born 8 September 1960) is a Dutch journalist and television presenter. He is best known for hosting the daily De Wereld Draait Door (DWDD) television show from 2005 to 2020.

In the Heart of Kunoichi Tsubaki (Japanese: くノ一ツバキの胸の内, Hepburn: Kunoichi Tsubaki no Mune no Uchi) is a Japanese manga series written and illustrated by Sōichirō Yamamoto. It has been serialized in Shogakukan's shōnen manga magazine Monthly Shōnen Sunday since January 2018. An anime television series adaptation by CloverWorks aired from April to July 2022.

CollabNet VersionOne is a software firm headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, United States. CollabNet VersionOne products and services belong to the industry categories of value stream management, devops, agile management, application lifecycle management (ALM), and enterprise version control. These products are used by companies and government organizations to reduce the time it takes to create and release software.

Kimbolton is a village and civil parish in Cambridgeshire, England with a population of 1,477 (including Stonely, per the 2011 census). Kimbolton is of Anglo-Saxon origin, meaning "Cenebald's Ton" (or estate). Kimbolton Castle is in the centre of the town, which is now Kimbolton School. Catherine of Aragon was imprisoned there after her divorce from Henry VIII, and died there in 1536.

Jefferson Joseph DeBlanc (February 15, 1921 – November 22, 2007) was an American World War II Marine Corps fighter pilot and flying ace, credited with shooting down nine Japanese aircraft during two tours of duty in the Pacific at Guadalcanal and Okinawa. On January 31, 1943, despite mechanical problems with his aircraft, he pressed forward with a mission and shot down five Japanese aircraft in minutes before being shot down himself. DeBlanc parachuted to safety and was rescued by native islanders, traded for a sack of rice, and picked up by a Navy seaplane. He received the Medal of Honor for his actions that day. He served in the Marine Corps Reserve until 1972. In that time, he also taught school and completed graduate and doctoral degrees in math, physics and education.

Baguma Crescent, popularly known as Beenie Gunter, is a Ugandan reggae dancehall artist, commonly known for his hit songs Pon Mi and Olina Work, the latter, a collaboration with Nigerian artist Skales. He released his 25 track debut studio album No Fear in 2019 that featured artists like Skales and Lydia Jazmine.

The 1979 Memorial Cup occurred May 6–13 at the Palais des Sports in Sherbrooke, Quebec, the Colisée de Trois-Rivières in Trois-Rivières, Quebec and at the Verdun Auditorium in Verdun, Quebec. It was the 61st annual Memorial Cup competition and determined the major junior ice hockey champion of the Canadian Hockey League (CHL). Participating teams were the winners of the Ontario Major Junior Hockey League, Quebec Major Junior Hockey League and Western Hockey League which were the Peterborough Petes, Trois-Rivières Draveurs and Brandon Wheat Kings. Peterborough won their first Memorial Cup, defeating Brandon in the final game.

*Cuck* is a 2019 American drama film directed by Rob Lambert, starring Zachary Ray Sherman as Ronnie, a mentally unstable loner who retreats into online extremist groups. The film explores themes of isolation, online radicalization, and masculinity. It received mixed reviews from critics.
Europe – Simone Veil (IPA-fr) is a station on Line 3 of the Paris Métro located in the 8th arrondissement of Paris.

"D.A.N.C.E." is the second single by Justice and the first from their album †. It includes edited and extended versions of "D.A.N.C.E", a rougher mix in the style of their earlier releases, "B.E.A.T", and the track "Phantom" which was previously issued in limited quantities twice on 12"vinyl preceding the release of"D.A.N.C.E.".
Joseph Frederick "Joe" Cotton (1922–2016) was a U.S. Air Force Colonel and test pilot.

The Borscht Corporation is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit that creates short films and videos in and about the city of Miami, Florida. In addition to hosting a quasi-annual film festival that screens their work, Borscht Corp. provides financial and technical support to its commissioned filmmakers.

Since receiving a challenge grant from the John S. and James L. Knight Foundation in 2010, Borscht Corp. has spearheaded productions by untested moviemakers and provided a creative platform for underrepresented (often Latin American) identities in film.

The original Citroën Rosalie was a light-weight racing car that established a succession of records at the Montlhéry racing circuit. More generally the Rosalie was a range of three models/sizes of automobile that comprised the core of Citroën's model range between 1932 and 1938. The three models were originally designated respectively the 8CV, the 10CV and the 15CV, the numbers defining the cars' fiscal horsepower which in turn defined the approximate engine size of each model. After the introduction of the new Traction Avant, the lineup was modified and at the 1934 Paris Salon the two smaller models became the 7UA and the 11UA, now with the overhead-valve engines from the Traction. Starting with the 1933 model year Citroen offered the 1,766 cc 11UD diesel engine option.

"7empest" (pronounced "tempest") is a song by American rock band Tool. Clocking in at over 15 minutes in length, it is the final song on the band's fifth studio album, Fear Inoculum. The song peaked at number 6 on the Billboard Hot Rock Songs chart and was cited by critics as a standout track from the album. It later won the Grammy Award for Best Metal Performance.

In mathematics, the Tor functors are the derived functors of the tensor product of modules over a ring. Along with the Ext functor, Tor is one of the central concepts of homological algebra, in which ideas from algebraic topology are used to construct invariants of algebraic structures. The homology of groups, Lie algebras, and associative algebras can all be defined in terms of Tor. The name comes from a relation between the first Tor group Tor1 and the torsion subgroup of an abelian group.

In the special case of abelian groups, Tor was introduced by Eduard Čech (1935) and named by Samuel Eilenberg around 1950. It was first applied to the Künneth theorem and universal coefficient theorem in topology. For modules over any ring, Tor was defined by Henri Cartan and Eilenberg in their 1956 book Homological Algebra.

The SS William G. Mather (Official Number 224850) is a retired Great Lakes bulk freighter now restored as a museum ship in Cleveland, Ohio, one of five in the Great Lakes region. She transported cargo such as ore, coal, stone, and grain to ports throughout the Great Lakes, and was nicknamed "The Ship That Built Cleveland" because Cleveland's steel mills were a frequent destination.
Domingos Sousa Coutinho Meneses Duarte (born 10 March 1995) is a Portuguese professional footballer who plays as a central defender for La Liga club Getafe and the Portugal national team.

Muttrah is a district of the Muscat Governorate in Oman, with a population of 234,226 in 2016. It is the most densely populated district in the nation. Muttrah is home to Port Sultan Qaboos, a major commercial port, and Souq Muttrah, a marketplace that is approximately 200 years old.

*Nactus kunan* is a species of gecko found only on Manus Island in the Admiralty Islands. It is brightly colored with alternating dark and golden bands, and a unique head pattern. It appears to be bisexual, unlike the related *Nactus pelagicus*. Its conservation status is Data Deficient.

Bajrang Punia (born 26 February 1994) is an Indian freestyle wrestler, who competes in the 65-kg weight category. At the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, Punia won a bronze medal by defeating Kazakhstan's Daulet Niyazbekov with a wide 8-0 margin. Punia is the only Indian wrestler to win 4 medals at the World Wrestling Championships.

HMS *Rover* was a Royal Navy *Swan*-class sloop-of-war launched in Bermuda in 1796. In 1798, she captured the French privateer *Jean Bart*. *Rover* was wrecked on 23 June 1798 in the Gulf of St. Lawrence.

The 1975 Council of Ministers was the first cabinet of East Timor, formed by Fretilin on December 1, 1975, following a unilateral declaration of independence. It was superseded by the Indonesian invasion later that month.

Cécile Rol-Tanguy (10 April 1919 – 8 May 2020) was a French communist who was a Resistance fighter during World War II. She participated in the liberation of Paris serving as secretariat and a liaison officer, conducting clandestine operations and relaying confidential communications.
R (Corner House Research) v Director of the Serious Fraud Office UKHL 60 is a UK constitutional law case, concerning the rule of law.

*Satellite in the Sky* is a 1956 British science fiction film directed by Paul Dickson and starring Kieron Moore, Lois Maxwell and Donald Wolfit. The plot concerns the maiden voyage of a British spaceship carrying a nuclear bomb, intended to force global nuclear disarmament. Complications arise when a stowaway reporter and a propulsion malfunction threaten the mission and the crew's lives. It was the first British science fiction film shot in Cinemascope and WarnerColor.

Ultraman Powered is a 1994 fighting game developed by Tose and published by Bandai for the 3DO. Based on the 1993 home video series *Ultraman: The Ultimate Hero* (known as *Ultraman Powered* in Japan), the game sees players control Ultraman to defend Earth from alien monsters. Gameplay consists of one-on-one battles featuring punches, kicks, and special abilities fueled by a replenishing power meter.

The game offers single-player modes like Visual Mode, featuring FMV clips from the series, and Battle Mode, which includes 3D rail shooter segments. A Versus Mode allows two players to fight, with one controlling Ultraman and the other choosing from a roster of monsters. Released exclusively in Japan as a 3DO launch title, *Ultraman Powered* utilizes digitized landscapes and character sprites from the show.

Reception to *Ultraman Powered* has been mixed. Some critics praised its graphics, drawing comparisons to SNK's *Art of Fighting*, while others criticized its slow gameplay and poor controls.

Swedish cuisine is centered around dairy products, breads, berries, fruits, beef, chicken, pork, eggs, and seafood. Potatoes are a common side dish. A wide variety of rye, wheat, and oat breads are popular, including crispbreads. Meat dishes, like meatballs, are often served with lingonberry jam. Fruit soups are also typical.

Historically, fish preservation through salting was important. Long winters led to a reliance on root vegetables like rutabaga and potatoes. While everyday food was once bland, local herbs have been used since ancient times. *Husmanskost* denotes traditional Swedish dishes with local ingredients, emphasizing pork, fish, cereals, milk, and root vegetables. The *smörgåsbord* and *julbord* are internationally recognized Swedish culinary traditions.

Raymond Cannon (born Ulises Tildmann Cannon; September 1, 1892 – June 7, 1977) was an American actor, film director, screenwriter, journalist, and author. He was active in film from 1913 to 1945, and later wrote books about sport fishing.

The Cóndores UNAM (English: UNAM Condors), was one of the three American football teams that represented the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM) from 1970 to 1997. The Cóndores competed in the ONEFA and won 10 National Championships, including four in a row from 1983 to 1986.

The bullace (Prunus domestica subsp. insititia var. nigra) is a variety of plum. Its name likely derives from the Old French word for sloe. It may have originated from hybrids between the sloe and cherry plum, or developed from the sloe alone. Though once cultivated, it declined in popularity as other plum varieties emerged.

Four main varieties are recognized in England: White, Black, Shepherd's, and Langley. They can grow as small trees or bushes, ripening later than other plums, typically in October–November. Bullaces are often found wild, especially near old farmhouses. While often used for cooking and preserves, some varieties are palatable raw when ripe.

Adolph Ernst Knoch (1874–1965) was a Bible translator and theologian known for his *Concordant Literal New Testament*. He was associated with the Brethren Movement and published the magazine *Unsearchable Riches*.
Ivaylo Brusowski (Lang-bg) is a movie director, actor, screen and playwright and composer.
Unison is an unincorporated community village in Loudoun County, Virginia. It is located approximately five miles from Middleburg in the Loudoun Valley close to the Fauquier County border.

Kazimieras Jaunius (1848–1908) was a Lithuanian Catholic priest and linguist. While Jaunius published very little, his major achievements include a well regarded Lithuanian grammar, systematization and classification of the Lithuanian dialects, and descriptions of Lithuanian accentuation. Though most of his conclusions on etymology and comparative linguistics were proven to be incorrect, his works remain valuable for vast observational data.

Jaunius studied at the Kaunas Priest Seminary and Saint Petersburg Roman Catholic Theological Academy. He was ordained a priest in 1875 and earned his Master of Theology in 1879. He taught several subjects, including moral theology and homiletics, at the Kaunas Priest Seminary from 1880 to 1892. His class notes on the Lithuanian language became a well regarded Lithuanian grammar book first published in 1897. After disagreements with Bishop Mečislovas Leonardas Paliulionis, Jaunius became a dean in Kazan in 1893. However, he experienced severe mental health issues and returned to Lithuania to recuperate in 1895. He obtained a teaching position at the Saint Petersburg Roman Catholic Theological Academy in 1898. He was of poor health and developed graphophobia (fear of writing). In 1902, Kazimieras Būga was hired as Jaunius'personal secretary to help collect and publish Jaunius' work. Jaunius retired from teaching in 1906 and died in 1908. Būga published two of his major works already after Jaunius' death.

The *gens Tettidia* was a minor Roman family. Its *nomen*, Tettidius, was derived from the *nomen Tettius*, thought to be of Oscan origin. Most inscriptions of the *gens* are from towns in Samnium. They used a variety of common *praenomina*.

John Armand Lafore Jr. (May 25, 1905 – January 24, 1993) was an American politician who was a Republican member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Pennsylvania. He was also president of the American Kennel Club.

Benedicto “Bong” Ducat (born May 6, 1957) is a Filipino impressionist painter. He is now teaching at the University of Santo Tomas College of Fine Arts and Design. He is noted for his renderings of plants, flowers and nature in droplet-like brush strokes and bright colors. In college, Ducat won the University of Santo Tomas Benavides Civic Award.

William Ross Bond (December 4, 1918 – April 1, 1970) was a Brigadier General in the United States Army. He served from 1940 to 1970, participating in World War II, the Korean War, and the Vietnam War. In 1970, Bond was killed in action by a sniper in South Vietnam.

"I Am Real" is the debut single by Australian hotelier Simon van Kempen. It was released on 21 April 2011, through JSM Music, during his wife Alex McCord's tenure on the American reality television series The Real Housewives of New York City. The song was written by Allison Beth Simon, Jon Jason Appleton, and Koki Saito, while production was handled by Joel Simon, Ross Hopman, and Saito. During its development, van Kempen and McCord met with the team of writers to ensure that the song's lyrics would represent them accurately. A dance-pop and EDM track, "I Am Real" contains tongue-in-cheek lyrics that address van Kempen's critics. One lyric references a comment McCord used to describe fellow cast member Luann de Lesseps.

"I Am Real" received mostly unfavourable reviews from music critics, who dismissed van Kempen as a television personality who tried launching a singing career. It also received comparisons to "Money Can't Buy You Class" (2010) by de Lesseps. The song was unsuccessful, selling just 1,000 copies in the United States, as of September 2011. van Kempen has since performed "I Am Real" on Watch What Happens Live with Andy Cohen and at Manhattan's annual "Fashion Night Out" event in 2011.

*Nippu Ravva* is a 1993 Indian Telugu-language action film directed by A. Kodandarami Reddy, produced by M. V. Srinivas Prasad under Yuvarathna Arts, and written by the Paruchuri Brothers. The film stars Nandamuri Balakrishna and Vijayashanti. Bappi Lahiri composed the film's soundtrack, with one song composed by Raj–Koti. A. R. Rahman provided background score. The film follows Suryam, a trade union leader who fights against the injustices of a mine owner.

Kalwaria Zebrzydowska (IPA-pl) is a town in southern Poland with 4,429 inhabitants (2007 estimate). As of 1999, it is situated in Lesser Poland or Małopolska (in Polish). Previously, the town was administered within the Voivodeship of Bielsko-Biała (1975–1998).

Bellevue Plantation was the U.S. home of Catherine Willis Gray Murat, located in Tallahassee, Florida. It was purchased in 1854 after Catherine's second husband Prince Achille Murat (son of Joachim Murat, Napoleon's brother-in-law and King of Naples from 1808 to 1815) died in 1847. Murat was the great-grandniece of George Washington. She was a daughter of Colonel Byrd C. Willis (August 29, 1781 – 1846) and his wife Mary Lewis. Mary Lewis was the granddaughter of Fielding Lewis, George Washington's brother-in-law. Through the Lewis family, she was also a relative of explorer Meriwether Lewis.

The *New York World* was a daily newspaper published in New York City from 1860 until February 27, 1931. Joseph Pulitzer bought the paper in 1883 and began an aggressive era of circulation building, hiring reporter Nellie Bly and building the New York World Building. The paper was known for its sensationalism and circulation battles with the *New York Journal-American*, giving rise to the term yellow journalism. After Pulitzer's death, his heirs sold the *World* to Scripps-Howard, who closed it down.
Stevie Anderson (born May 12, 1970) is a former American football wide receiver who played in the National Football League.

Give Notice (foaled 18 May 1997 – after 2004) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse. He showed no discernible promise as a juvenile in 1999 but over the next two seasons he made steady progress, winning five times in handicap races over extended distances. He emerged as a top-class stayer in 2002 when he won the Sagaro Stakes and the Prix du Cadran. Apart from his victories he finished second in the Goodwood Cup and third in the Cesarewitch

William Ross Bond (December 4, 1918 – April 1, 1970) was a United States Army brigadier general who was killed by an enemy sniper in 1970 while commanding the 199th Infantry Brigade in South Vietnam. General Bond also served in World War II with the Army Rangers and was a prisoner of war.

Kingman is a city in Mohave County, Arizona, United States. Incorporated in 1952, it serves as the county seat. As of 2020, the population was 32,689. Kingman is located along Interstate 40 and U.S. Route 93 and its motto is "The Heart of Historic Route 66".

A Zirkel is a symbol used by German student fraternities (Studentenverbindungen). It consists of intertwined lines representing the fraternity's name, followed by an exclamation mark to indicate activity. Members use it on paraphernalia and after signatures.

William Beau Mirchoff (/ˈmɜːrtʃɒf/; born January 13, 1989) is an Canadian-American actor best known for his role in the MTV series Awkward, his role as Jamie Hunter in Good Trouble, and his role in Now Apocalypse on Starz.

The giant skippers are butterflies in the disputed subfamily Megathyminae, which is part of the skipper family. Some authorities treat this as a distinct and separate subfamily, but more modern classifications tend to place them within the subfamily Hesperiinae. However, some works, such as the Integrated Taxonomic Information System, still treat is as a valid subfamily. The giant skippers include two tribes, Aegialini and Megathymini. There are three genera and about eighteen species in this subfamily. These butterflies typically live in the southwest United States and Mexico in desert areas.
Recep Burak Yılmaz (born 27 November 1995) is a Turkish professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Hatayspor.

Nerima Wako-Ojiwa (born c. 1989) is a Kenyan political analyst and corporate executive. She is the Executive Director of Siasa Place, a youth-led organization she co-founded in 2015. She holds degrees from Jacksonville State University. Her husband and uncle are also involved in Kenyan politics.

VENUE is a series of digital mixing consoles and live sound systems manufactured by Avid Technology. The product line includes a range of consoles with varying sizes, features, and input/output capabilities.

The original VENUE system, D-Show, was discontinued in 2015. Profile, a smaller console, became an industry standard for touring and festivals. The SC48 is an all-in-one console, while the S3L-X features a compact design and Ethernet AVB architecture. The flagship S6L system, released in 2015, offers enhanced processing power, touch screen control, and high-performance I/O racks.

All VENUE consoles create inter-compatible show files, though some editing may be required when moving between systems. While earlier consoles run Windows XP Embedded and use TDM plugins, newer systems like S3L-X and S6L utilize HDX chipsets and AAX DSP plugins. All consoles are compatible with Pro Tools for recording and virtual soundchecks.

The National League of Families POW/MIA flag, often referred to as the POW/MIA flag, was adopted in 1972 and consists of the official emblem of the National League of Families of American Prisoners and Missing in Southeast Asia in white on a black background. In 2019 the National POW/MIA Flag Act was signed into law, requiring the POW/MIA flag to be flown on certain federal properties, including the U.S. Capitol Building, on all days the U.S. flag is flown.
Personal and Vehicular Basic Loads and The Morrow Project Role Playing Expansion is a 1983 role-playing game supplement published by Timeline for The Morrow Project.
Diether Pascual Ocampo (born July 19, 1974) is a Filipino actor, singer, and model.

The Highwomen is the debut studio album by country music supergroup the Highwomen, made up of band members Brandi Carlile, Natalie Hemby, Maren Morris, and Amanda Shires. It was released on September 6, 2019, by Elektra Records.

Herald Scholarly Open Access is a publisher active since at least 2015, using an open access model that charges authors. It publishes around 73 journals in clinical, medical, life sciences, and pharmaceutical fields. It has been listed as a potential predatory publisher and criticized for spam and mimicking journal titles.

The 2014 Mountain Province bus accident occurred on February 7 in Bontoc, Mountain Province, Philippines. A GV Florida Transport bus fell off a mountain, resulting in 14 deaths and 32 injuries. Among the deceased were two foreign nationals and several Filipino personalities. An investigation suggested mechanical problems as a possible cause. The LTFRB initially suspended GV Florida Transport, but the suspension was later lifted by the Court of Appeals.

The Finse Tunnel (Lang-no) is a 10,300-metre-long (6.4 mi; 11,300 yd) railway tunnel west of the village of Finse in Eidfjord Municipality in Vestland county, Norway. The tunnel is part of the Bergen Line between the cities of Oslo and Bergen. It is the longest tunnel on the line, and the third longest railway tunnel in the kingdom. Inside the tunnel, at 1,237 metres (4,058 ft) elevation, is the highest point on the Norwegian railway network.

The tunnel was constructed to increase the regularity of the line past the Finse peak which was often blocked by snow. Planning started during the 1980s to remove this section that was regularly snowed down, and at the same time reduce the length and increase the speed of the railway. Construction started in 1990, and it was opened by King Harald V on 16 May 1993. Following the construction of the tunnel, 32 kilometres (20 mi) of railway around this area was also rebuilt to achieve higher train speeds, a shorter overall distance, and to maximize the natural protection from the weather. This was built in five stages, opening between 1995 and 1998.

The Great Lakes Depression is a major freshwater basin in Mongolia, encompassing approximately 100,000 square kilometers (39,000 sq mi). Located at 49°18′39′′N 92°38′15′′E, the depression is an important wetland area in Central Asia, characterized by interconnected shallow lakes and wide reed belts within a desert steppe environment.

These wetlands provide crucial habitat for several rare and migrating bird species, including the Eurasian spoonbill, black stork, osprey, white-tailed eagle, swan goose, and bar-headed goose. The area also supports a small population of great white pelicans. While the region has a relatively low diversity of fish species, a significant proportion of those present are endemic or near-endemic, particularly within the genera Oreoleuciscus, Thymallus, and Triplophysa.

The Sun Fast 1/2 Ton is a French racing keelboat designed by Tony Castro and built by Jeanneau from 1984 to 1986. Only ten examples of the limited edition were completed. It is constructed of fiberglass, with hull and deck factory options of carbon fiber or Kevlar reinforcement. It has a fractional sloop rig, a fin keel and an internally mounted spade-type rudder. The design is supported by the Half Ton Class.
The Merchant Street Historic District is located in Honolulu, Hawaii, and listed on NRHP.
Satannish is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.

John Hull Campbell (October 10, 1800 – January 19, 1868) was an American politician from Pennsylvania who served as an American Party member in the U.S. House of Representatives for Pennsylvania's 3rd congressional district from 1845 to 1847.

Meri (Lang-he, an acronym for Mahaneh Radikali Yisraeli (Hebrew: מחנה רדיקלי ישראלי‎), lit. Israeli Radical Camp) was a small radical left-wing political party in Israel. It was founded in the 1960s as HaOlam HaZeh – Koah Hadash by Uri Avnery, editor of HaOlam HaZeh.
Veniamin Lvovich Beylin (Lang-ru; 25 August 1904 – 16 March 1982) was a Red Army major general.

*Hadith: A Re-evaluation* is a book by Kassim Ahmad (1933- ) arguing that the Quran is complete and the hadith is a human invention, a product of human politics, and contradictory to itself, the Quran, and science. He argues the Quran is the sole source of legislation and use of hadith is a sinful innovation. His lectures were cancelled and the book banned in Malaysia.
Patrizio Bianchi (born 28 May 1952) is an Italian economist and academic. He has been minister of education in the Draghi Cabinet from 2021 to 2022.

Royalty Exchange is an American company that operates an online platform for buying and selling royalty assets of any type, mostly music, where royalty owners can sell their future payments to investors as alternative assets. The company hosts a centralized marketplace and online auction platform that connects a community of over 22,500+ investors with owners of royalty-based assets.

All types of assets can be monetized and invested in including music royalties, film royalties, and tax credits, patent and intellectual property royalties, trade secret royalties, renewable energy land leases, technology licensing royalties, book publishing royalties, franchise royalties, copyright royalties and agriculture royalties.

The Cello Concerto is a concerto for cello and orchestra by Esa-Pekka Salonen. Salonen's fourth concerto, it is in three movements and has a duration of approximately 38 minutes. The work is scored for solo cello, a large orchestra, and live electronics. It premiered in 2017, with Yo-Yo Ma as the soloist.

"Kashmiri Song" or "Pale Hands I Loved" is a 1902 song by Amy Woodforde-Finden based on a poem by Laurence Hope, pseudonym of Violet Nicolson.

The poem first appeared in Hope's first collection of poems, The Garden of Kama (1901), also known as India's Love Lyrics.

The following year, when Amy Woodforde-Finden set to music Four Indian Love Lyrics, "Kashmiri Song" emerged as the most popular, quickly becoming a drawing room standard and remaining popular until the Second World War.

The Alligator Rivers region is located in the Northern Territory of Australia, containing the East, West and South Alligator Rivers. Despite their name, these rivers are inhabited by crocodiles, not alligators, a mistake made by explorer Phillip Parker King in 1818. The rivers flow into the Van Diemen Gulf and have created extensive alluvial plains and mangrove swamps over the past 20,000 years.

The region experiences a monsoon climate with distinct wet and dry seasons. It is known for its diverse wildlife, including a wide variety of birdlife and a significant population of waterbirds. The area has been continuously inhabited by Aboriginal people for 50,000 years, and contains numerous Aboriginal cultural sites, including cave paintings and rock carvings. European settlement was slow, but the discovery of uranium in the 1950s led to the development of a mining industry in the region.

Alfred John Webb (10 June 1834 – 30 July 1908) was an Irish Quaker from a family of activist printers. He became an Irish Parliamentary Party politician and Member of Parliament (MP), as well as a participant in nationalist movements around the world. He supported Butt's Home Government Association and the United Irish League. At Madras in 1894, he became the third non-Indian (after George Yule and William Wedderburn) to preside over the Indian National Congress.
John William Goode Jr. (March 10, 1923 – February 5, 1994) was a lawyer in San Antonio who was a figure in the 1950s and 1960s rebirth of the Republican Party in Texas.
Odostomia farallonensis is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusc in the family Pyramidellidae, the pyrams and their allies.

Lieutenant Colonel Thomas Bernard Hackett (1836 – 1880) was an Irish recipient of the Victoria Cross. As a lieutenant in the 23rd Regiment of Foot during the Indian Mutiny, he was awarded the VC for rescuing a wounded corporal under heavy fire and preventing a fire. He later died in a firearm accident.
John Doyley (c. 1602 – c. 1660) was an English politician who sat in the House of Commons in 1646.

Substance Church is a nondenominational, Evangelical Christian, megachurch in Minneapolis–Saint Paul, Minnesota, United States. In 2010, Outreach Magazine ranked the church as the 21st fastest growing congregation in the United States (8th in terms of growth percentages). Because the church has integrated ultra-contemporary worship, cell church, and cafe church methods into a singular megachurch setting, Substance is often cited as being "trend-setting".

The 297th Infantry Division (Lang-de) was an Infantry Division within the German Army, active during the Second World War. It was one of the components of the 6th Army during its failed attack on Stalingrad.

Cardea is a goddess in ancient Roman religion associated with doorways, hinges, and thresholds. In Christian polemics, she is linked to Forculus and Limentinus, deities of doors and thresholds. Her powers include opening and closing things and averting vampiric *striges*.

Ovid's *Fasti* recounts the rape of the nymph Cranaë by Janus, who then gives her the whitethorn, a symbol of Cardea's power to protect doorways. Carna, sometimes conflated with Cardea, was a goddess of health whose feast day, the Bean-Kalends, involved offerings of beans and lard.
Deerfoot (1828 – 18 January 1896, Cattaraugus Reservation) was an American Seneca runner. His most noted achievements took place in England.

The three-point turn (sometimes called a Y-turn, K-turn, or broken U-turn) is the standard method of turning a vehicle around to face the opposite direction in a limited space, using forward and reverse gears. This is typically done when the road is too narrow for a U-turn.

This manoeuvre is a common requirement in driving tests.

Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee v. FEC, 518 U.S. 604 (1996), was a case heard by the Supreme Court of the United States in which the Colorado Republican Party challenged the Federal Election Commission (FEC) as to whether the "Party Expenditure Provision" of the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 (FECA) violated the First Amendment right to free speech. This provision put a limit on the amount of money a national party could spend on a congressional candidate's campaign. The FEC argued that the Committee violated this provision when purchasing a radio advertisement that attacked the likely candidate of the Colorado Democratic Party. The court held that since the expenditures by the Committee were made independently from a specific candidate, they did not violate the campaign contribution limitations established by the FECA, and were protected under the First Amendment.
*The Time* is a studio album by Xu Weizhou, released in 2017 and 2018. It consists of four quarters of music.

Testamentum Domini ("Testament of Our Lord") is a Christian treatise which belongs to the genre of the ancient church orders. The work can be dated about the 5th-century CE even if a 4th-century date is sometimes proposed. The provenience is regarded as Syria, even if also Egypt or Asia Minor are possible origins.

Bakhtyuras Shampekuly Besikbayev (Lang-kk; 1920 – 26 June 1941) was a gunner and radio operator in the 207th Long-Range Bomber Aviation Regiment during the Second World War. He was posthumously declared both a Hero of the Russian Federation and a Hero of Kazakhstan after the dissolution of the Soviet Union.

John A. Lafore Jr. (May 25, 1905 – January 24, 1993) was a Republican member of the U.S. House of Representatives representing Pennsylvania's 13th district from 1957 to 1961 and the Pennsylvania House of Representatives from 1950 to 1957.

"Take Me for a Ride" is a song by British group Steps, released on July 29, 2021, as the second single from their seventh studio album, *What the Future Holds Pt. 2*. The music video, directed by Jordan Rossi, was released on August 26, 2021.
Joseph Frederick Cotton (January 21, 1922 – May 5, 2016) was an American military test pilot.

Joanne M. Garvey (1935–2014) was an attorney in San Francisco, California, and was a nationally recognized expert in field of taxation. She was the first woman to serve on the California State Bar Board of Governors from 1971–1974, and was one of the founders of the California Women Lawyers organization in 1974. Garvey also was the first female president of the San Francisco Barristers, and in 1981 became the first female president of the Bar Association of San Francisco since the Association’s founding in 1872. In addition, she served in a number of leadership positions with different sections and committees of the American Bar Association, and as a member of its Board of Governors and House of Delegates. Moreover, Garvey helped form the State Bar of California's Taxation Section, from which she also received the Section’s lifetime achievement award in 1994—the Joanne M. Garvey Award—which was named for her. In 2003, Garvey received the American Bar Association Commission on Women's 2003 Margaret Brent Women Lawyers of Achievement Award. After Garvey’s death in 2014, Charles Rettig—prior to becoming Commissioner of the Internal Revenue Service—stated that: "From every perspective, Joanne Garvey was a legend. She was among the most highly respected lawyers in the United States (tax or otherwise)".

The Autumn Exhibition of Leningrad artists of 1978 was held at the Leningrad Union of Soviet Artists Exhibition Halls. Almost 1100 works were displayed by over 650 Leningrad artists, including painters, sculptors, and graphic artists. A catalog of the exhibition was published.

Benjamin Franklin Grauer (June 2, 1908 – May 31, 1977) was a US radio and TV personality, following a career during the 1920s as a child actor in films and on Broadway. He began his career as a child in David Warfield's production of The Return of Peter Grimm. Among his early credits were roles in films directed by D.W. Griffith.

Grauer was born in Staten Island, New York. After graduating from Townsend Harris High School, he received his B.A. from the City College of New York in 1930. Grauer started in radio as an actor but soon became part of the broadcasting staff at the National Broadcasting Company. He was one of the four narrators, along with Burgess Meredith, of NBC's public affairs series The Big Story, which focused on courageous journalists.

In 1954, he married interior designer Melanie Kahane.

*Que viva la lucha!* is a 2007 Mexican documentary film directed by Gustavo Vazquez. The film explores the cultural significance of lucha libre in Tijuana, Mexico, by following the lives of several professional wrestlers. It focuses on the dual identities of these luchadores, who lead working-class lives outside the ring but transform into local heroes when they don their masks.

The documentary examines the archetypes within lucha libre, such as the heroic *técnicos* (good guys) and the villainous *rudos* (bad guys), and the importance of the mask (*la máscara*) in creating these characters. It also highlights the participation of women, gay, and midget wrestlers, as well as the passionate fans who contribute to the vibrant atmosphere of lucha libre events. Vazquez captures the unique space that lucha libre provides for spectators to express their emotions and engage with the spectacle.

Uzel Holding was a privately held Dutch company with roots in the Ottoman Empire. Founded in 1937 by İbrahim Uzel, the company initially produced leaf springs before expanding into agricultural machinery, automotive parts, and venture capital. In 2005, Uzel relocated its headquarters to Amsterdam. By 2007, it was among the world's ten largest tractor companies. Financial difficulties and family disputes led to its collapse in 2011 and bankruptcy in 2012.

James Bouknight (born September 18, 2000) is an American professional basketball player for the Charlotte Hornets of the National Basketball Association (NBA). He played college basketball for the UConn Huskies.

Akhtarul Wasey (born September 1, 1951) is the president of Maulana Azad University, Jodhpur, India, and a former professor of Islamic Studies. He taught at Jamia Millia Islamia (Central University) in New Delhi, where he remains professor emeritus in the Department of Islamic Studies.

*In the Heart of Kunoichi Tsubaki* (くノ一ツバキの胸の内, *Kunoichi Tsubaki no Mune no Uchi*) is a Japanese comedy manga series written and illustrated by Sōichirō Yamamoto. It began serialization in *Monthly Shōnen Sunday* in 2018. An anime television series adaptation by CloverWorks aired from April to July 2022. The story follows Tsubaki, a kunoichi who is curious about men, who are forbidden from contact.
South Venice is a census-designated place (CDP) in Sarasota County, Florida, United States. The population was 15,619 at the 2020 census.

Fatal Deviation is a low-budget cult film produced and set in Trim, County Meath, Ireland. Produced in 1998, it enjoys the distinction of being Ireland's first full-length martial arts film. The film stars real-life martial arts enthusiast James Bennett. The film was conceived by James Bennett. The film was also panned by critics and many have considered this film to be one of the worst films ever made. The movie went straight to video without a theatrical release.

La Vinia Delois Jennings is an American literary scholar and critic of twentieth-century American literature and culture, currently a Distinguished Humanities Professor at the University of Tennessee, and also formerly a Lindsay Young Professor and a 1998 Fulbright Senior Lecturer appointed to the University of Málaga in Spain.

Pierre Marinovitch (Lang-sr; 1 August 1898 – 2 October 1919) was a French World War I flying ace credited with 21 confirmed and 3 probable aerial victories. He was the youngest French flying ace of the war, scoring his 5th victory at age 19 years and 169 days. He was killed in an air accident following the war.
Cipher runes are runic alphabets with substitutions to conceal the meaning of the text.

An antifuse is an electrical device that performs the opposite function to a fuse. Whereas a fuse starts with a low resistance and is designed to permanently break an electrically conductive path (typically when the current through the path exceeds a specified limit), an antifuse starts with a high resistance, and programming it converts it into a permanent electrically conductive path (typically when the voltage across the antifuse exceeds a certain level). This technology has many applications.
The Downs are an area of public open limestone downland in Bristol, England. They consist of Durdham Down to the north and east and Clifton Down to the south, separated by Stoke Road.

Janet Elizabeth Haworth is a Welsh Conservative politician who was a Member of the Welsh Assembly (AM) for the North Wales Region between 2015 and 2016.

Haworth ran a guest house from 2005 with her partner, Dennis Oliver. She worked for Shell in Aberdeenshire until she retired to Llandudno in about 2003, and became a town and county councillor.
This article lists the squads of the women's hockey competition at the 2010 Commonwealth Games held in New Delhi, India from 4 to 13 October 2010.
Michelle Stephenson (born 3 January 1977) is a British pop singer and television presenter. At the age of 17, she was briefly a member of the girl group that would go on to become the Spice Girls.

Parasol was a British Thoroughbred racehorse foaled in 1800. Bred by the 3rd Duke of Grafton, she was sired by Pot-8-Os, out of the mare Prunella. Parasol won several major races, including the Town Plate (1803), the Newmarket October King's Plate (1804, 1805), and the Jockey-Club Plate (1805). As a broodmare, she produced twelve foals. Parasol was shot in the autumn of 1826, having been covered by the stallion Sam in the spring.

Margaret Bernadine Hall (1863–1910) was a British artist. She studied in Paris in a time when there were few female artists in the city. Hall traveled extensively between 1888 and 1894, and exhibited her paintings in Vienna, Chicago, London and Manchester in 1887. Her painting *Fantine* is held at the Walker Art Gallery.

Casey Cizikas (born February 27, 1991) is a Canadian professional ice hockey centre currently playing for the New York Islanders of the National Hockey League (NHL). He was drafted by the Islanders in the fourth round, 92nd overall, of the 2009 NHL Entry Draft, and made his NHL debut in 2012. Cizikas is commonly considered to be one of the best fourth line players in the NHL. In 2009, Cizikas was found guilty of manslaughter and was given a sentence of one year probation and 100 hours of community service.

Four Freedoms Plaza is a fictional building appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics. It first appeared in *Fantastic Four* #296 (November 1986) as the new headquarters for the Fantastic Four after the destruction of the original Baxter Building.

Designed to Reed Richards' specifications, the 100-story building was constructed of advanced composites, concrete, and glass. The top 50 floors were occupied by the team, while the bottom 50 were leased to the former tenants of the Baxter Building. The building was later destroyed by the Masters of Evil. The name "Four Freedoms Plaza" is sometimes cited as an alternate address for the Baxter Building.

Romy Simpkins (born 2 November 1993) is a British actress, model, Mental health ambassador and beauty pageant titleholder who competed in several beauty pageants. She has represented her country & the United Kingdom internationally in beauty pageants. She competed at the Miss International 2016 pageant in Tokyo, Japan. Romy competed in the Miss Supranational 2018 pageant in Krynica-Zdrój, Poland.

Asia Aluminum was a private aluminum extrusion company founded in 1992, with headquarters in Hong Kong, China. In 2009, the company began liquidation proceedings in Hong Kong. Later that year, the Hong Kong Court approved the purchase of Asia Aluminum by its management team for US$475 million.

Urbanus is a Flemish celebrity comic strip created by Willy Linthout and loosely based on Flemish comedian and singer Urbanus. The stories are written by Willy Linthout, Urbanus and Ann Smets, and drawn by Linthout. The first story was published in 1982 and is still in syndication as of this day, being the longest-running and most successful Flemish comic strip based on a celebrity. It is also the longest-running celebrity comic in the world made by the same writer/artist team. The series sells well in the Netherlands too, due to Urbanus' popularity there.
Ä (lower case ä) is a character that represents either a letter from several extended Latin alphabets, or the letter A with an umlaut mark or diaeresis.
Roger Shah (IPA-de; born 29 November 1972), also known as DJ Shah and Sunlounger, is a German electronic music composer and producer.

Mindy Lubber is an American business strategist focused on sustainability. She was a Regional Administrator for the EPA under President Clinton. Lubber founded Green Century Capital Management and is an Expert Consultant to The B Team.

*NBC Nightside* was an American late-night news program that aired on NBC from November 4, 1991, to September 20, 1998. It was NBC's second attempt at a late-night news program, following *NBC News Overnight*. Unlike its competitors, *Nightside* was broadcast from Charlotte, North Carolina, and aired seven days a week. Several of its anchors, including Antonio Mora and Campbell Brown, achieved national recognition. Despite its profitability, the show was canceled in 1998 and replaced with a block of repeats.

Clotilde Margarete Anna Edle von der Planitz (5 November 1892 – 11 January 1974), known professionally as Clotilde von Derp, was a German expressionist dancer, an early exponent of modern dance. Her career was spent essentially dancing together with her husband Alexander Sakharoff with whom she enjoyed a long-lasting relationship.

Angelino Dulcert (fl. 1339), probably the same person known as Angelino de Dalorto (fl. 1320s), and whose real name was probably Angelino de Dulceto or Dulceti or possibly Angelí Dolcet, was an Italian-Majorcan cartographer.

He is responsible for two notable 14th-century portolan charts, the "Dalorto" chart of 1325 and the "Dulcert" chart of 1339. The latter is the first portolan known to have been produced in Palma, and considered the founding piece of the Majorcan cartographic school. He is also believed to be the author of a third undated and unsigned chart held in London.

Woodall Services is a motorway service station on the M1 motorway in South Yorkshire, England. Opened in 1968 and operated by Welcome Break, it is located close to the Derbyshire border. The Northbound and Southbound sides are linked by a footbridge. It is noted for its popularity with coaches and for murals reflecting the local area.

A limiting factor is a variable of a system that causes a noticeable change in output or another measure of a type of system. The limiting factor is in a pyramid shape of organisms going up from the producers to consumers and so on. A factor not limiting over a certain domain of starting conditions may yet be limiting over another domain of starting conditions, including that of the factor.

E. S. Rose Park is a 25-acre park in Nashville, Tennessee, owned by the Nashville Metro Parks & Recreation Board. Renovated in 2010–2011, it is home to Belmont University's baseball, softball, soccer, and track & field teams, as well as Hume-Fogg High School's baseball team. Local sports leagues also use the park. Its construction followed discussions with local residents, who were given assurances of shared access.

*Sea of Love* is the fourth studio album by South Korean R&B duo Fly to the Sky, released on April 26, 2002. The album spawned two singles: "Sea of Love", a poppy ballad, and "Condition of My Heart", a ballad written by Brian McKnight that cemented the duo's signature style.

WTRF-TV (channel 7) is a television station licensed to Wheeling, West Virginia, United States, serving the Wheeling, West Virginia–Steubenville, Ohio market as an affiliate of CBS, MyNetworkTV, and ABC. The station is owned by Nexstar Media Group and maintains studios on 16th Street in downtown Wheeling; its transmitter is located in Bridgeport, Ohio.

WTRF-TV was the first station to specifically serve Wheeling and Steubenville. It was originally an NBC affiliate before switching to CBS in January 1980. In the 2000s, it expanded to add two new subchannel services.

Barbara Keiler (born April 7, 1953 in New York City, United States), known more widely by her pseudonym Judith Arnold, is a best-selling American author of crime fiction and over eighty-five romance novels. She has been writing since 1983, and has also been published under the pen names Ariel Berk and Thea Frederick.

The Western Tapes, 1983 is an EP by American country rock band Lone Justice, released on 23 November 2018 by Omnivore Recordings. It consists of demo recordings made in 1983 prior to their self-titled debut release in 1985 and was recorded by the original Lone Justice lineup consisting of Maria McKee, Ryan Hedgecock, Dave Harrington and Don Willens. All tracks are previously unissued, except "Drugstore Cowboy" which was included on the compilation album This World Is Not My Home in 1999. David Mansfield, who has toured with Bob Dylan and later became a member of Bruce Hornsby and the Range, guests on pedal steel guitar and fiddle.
Ethelbert Watts (February 25, 1846 – July 13, 1919) a United States diplomat for over twenty-four years, played important roles in the Spanish–American War, Russo-Japanese War, and World War I.

Pierre Marinovitch (1 August 1898 – 2 October 1919) was a French World War I flying ace. Credited with 21 aerial victories, he was known as "The Youngest Ace". Marinovitch was awarded the Légion d'honneur and Croix de Guerre. He died in a flying accident in Brussels.

The second USS Housatonic was the Southern Pacific Steamship Company freighter El Rio. The ship was one of four company ships temporarily converted for planting the World War I North Sea Mine Barrage.

El Rio was built for the Morgan Line in 1899 and served as a freighter until the United States Shipping Board took control of the vessel in 1917 for conversion to wartime naval use. After return to commercial service the ship resumed normal freight operations. In 1925 the ship was sold to the Clyde-Mallory Lines and renamed Brazos. In 1945 the vessel was sold to Atlantic, Gulf & West Indies Lines (Agwilines) continuing freight service until sunk in a collision in 1942.

Commonwealth v. Pullis, 3 Doc. Hist. 59 (1806) was a US labor law case, and the first reported case arising from a labor strike in the United States. It decided that striking workers were illegal conspirators.

Stadtallendorf is a town in the Marburg-Biedenkopf district of Hesse, Germany. It is known for being a secret munitions center during World War II and for its successful economy. The population was 21,552 on December 31, 2022.

Amy Yamada (山田詠美, Yamada Eimi, born February 8, 1959) is the pen name of Yamada Futaba, a Japanese novelist, short-story writer, and essayist. She achieved widespread recognition in 1985 when her novel *Bedtime Eyes* won the Bungei Prize. Yamada's work often explores themes of separation and bullying, drawing from her own experiences. Her notable works include *Soul Music Lovers Only*, *Trash*, *Animal Logic*, and *A2Z*.
The gens Tettidia, occasionally found as Tettiedia, was an obscure plebeian family at ancient Rome. No members of this gens are mentioned by Roman writers, but several are known from inscriptions.

The Störtebeker Festival is an annual open-air theatre festival in Ralswiek on the island of Rügen, Germany. Since 1993, it has told the legend of the pirate Klaus Störtebeker, who sailed the Baltic Sea in the 14th century. The festival employs over 140 extras, 20 actors, and 30 horses. By the end of the 2016 season, it had attracted over 7.3 million visitors.

Janice Charette is a Canadian public servant serving as the clerk of the Privy Council and secretary to the Cabinet. She previously held the role from 2014 to 2016, before being appointed as the Canadian high commissioner to the United Kingdom in 2016.

She has been recognized as one of Canada’s Top 100 Most Powerful Women in 2013, 2014, and 2015.

Benjamin Franklin "Ben" Grauer (June 2, 1908 – May 31, 1977) was an American broadcaster best known for his 40-year career in radio with NBC. He joined NBC in 1930 and became a senior commentator and reporter. Grauer was the announcer for Walter Winchell's Jergens Journal and, most notably, for the NBC Symphony Orchestra at the request of Arturo Toscanini, who called him his favorite announcer.

Grauer covered numerous major events, including the Olympic Games, presidential inaugurations, and New Year's celebrations in Times Square. He was also part of NBC Radio's Monitor and narrated the first installment of "The First Fabulous Fifty," a documentary series on NBC's history. In television, he provided commentary for NBC's first TV special and hosted The Ben Grauer Show. Grauer died of a heart attack in 1977.

Kimbolton is a town and civil parish in England. Kimbolton is about 9 miles (14 km) west of Huntingdon and 14 miles (23 km) north of Bedford. Kimbolton is administered as part of Cambridgeshire; however it is situated within Huntingdonshire, which is an historic county of England and is now a non-metropolitan district of Cambridgeshire. The parish includes the hamlet of Stonely.

Catherine of Aragon, after her divorce from Henry VIII, died at Kimbolton Castle in 1536.

Prince Sports is an American sporting goods company based in Atlanta, Georgia. Founded in 1970 by Robert H. McClure, Prince manufactures rackets, footwear, apparel, tennis balls, and accessories. Authentic Brands Group owns the Prince brand.
PKP class Pm36 is a class of two express passenger (P) 4-6-2 (m) steam locomotives ordered in 1936 for the Polskie Koleje Państwowe (Polish State Railways).

Svalikhita-jivani is an autobiography of Bhaktivinoda Thakur written in 1896 at the request of his son, Lalita Prasad Thakur. The book covers 56 years of Bhaktivinoda's life, recounting his experiences with financial struggle, health issues, internal doubts, and introspection. It portrays his path to accepting Caitanya Mahaprabhu's teachings as his ultimate goal. The candid narrative depicts Bhaktivinoda as a humble and modest man, without claims of spiritual acumen or saintliness. Svalikhita-jivani was published posthumously in 1916 by Lalita Prasad.

Grace Yun Yi is a Chinese-Canadian statistician. She obtained a Ph.D. from the University of Toronto in 2000, under the supervision of Donald A. S. Fraser. Yi was a professor at the University of Waterloo from 2000 to 2019 and is currently a professor at the University of Western Ontario. She won the CRM–SSC Prize in 2010 and is a Fellow of the American Statistical Association and the Institute of Mathematical Statistics.

*Planet Earth* is a British nature documentary franchise produced by the BBC Natural History Unit. It began with *The Blue Planet* in 2001, and continued with the *Planet Earth* television series in 2006, as well as numerous books, films, and spin-off series.
Nicolas Marroc (born 5 December 1986) is a French racing driver.

Supersingular Isogeny Key Exchange (SIDH) is a post-quantum cryptographic algorithm for secure key exchange based on the difficulty of finding isogenies between supersingular elliptic curves. Created in 2011 by De Feo, Jao, and Plut, SIDH aimed to provide forward secrecy and resist attacks from quantum computers, which pose a threat to widely used public-key algorithms like RSA and Diffie-Hellman.
