The protocol involves two parties, Alice and Bob, each constructing secret isogenies from a common elliptic curve. They exchange information about the target curves of their isogenies, allowing them to compute a shared secret, the j-invariant of isomorphic curves.

While initially considered secure, a 2022 attack by Castryck and Decru demonstrated a practical key-recovery attack on SIKE, a NIST post-quantum cryptography submission based on SIDH. The attack exploited auxiliary points in SIDH public keys. Despite this, SIDH's small key size makes it applicable to contexts with strict space requirements.

Chandler School is an independent, co-educational day school in Pasadena, California, serving students in grades K–8. It was founded in 1950 by Thomas and Catherine Chandler in Altadena, California. The school moved to its current location in 1958. John Finch is the current Head of School.

I Feel Pretty is a 2018 American comedy film written and directed by Abby Kohn and Marc Silverstein. The film stars Amy Schumer, Michelle Williams, Emily Ratajkowski, Rory Scovel, Aidy Bryant, Busy Philipps, Tom Hopper, Naomi Campbell, and Lauren Hutton. The plot involves an insecure woman who, after suffering a head injury, gains extreme self-confidence in her appearance.

The film was released in the United States on April 20, 2018, by STX Entertainment, and grossed over $94 million worldwide. It received mixed reviews from critics, with some saying it did not fully commit to its premise or tone, although the performances of both Schumer and Williams were praised.
The 1994–95 season was a season for Atlante F.C. They finished 10th in the Primera División and were eliminated in the first round of the Copa México.

A. Richard Sipe (1932 – August 8, 2018) was an American clinical mental health counselor known for his research on celibacy and sexuality of Catholic priests. His work was featured in the film *Spotlight*, about the *Boston Globe*'s investigation into the Catholic Archdiocese of Boston.

*Slaughter Studios* is a 2002 American horror film directed by Brian Katkin. The film centers on Steve (Peter Stanovich), an egotistical film student who convinces his friends to help him shoot a horror movie titled *Naughty Sex Kittens vs. the Giant Praying Mantis* in an abandoned studio. The studio has a history: twenty years prior, an actor was accidentally killed during a film shoot, leading to its closure.

The group breaks into the studio with only nine hours to complete the film before it is torn down. As they begin shooting, members of the cast and crew start disappearing and turning up dead. Steve, increasingly agitated and fueled by stress medication, pushes forward, oblivious to the danger. Madigan (Amy Shelton-White), the production assistant, and Kevin (Nicolas Read), the actor playing the monster, discover a hidden camera that reveals the identity of the killer. They realize that they are trapped in the studio with a murderer, who is revealed to be a homeless man seeking revenge for being disturbed.

The Ma Tante River is a 5.4 km (3.4 mi) river in the unorganized territory of Rivière-aux-Outardes and the municipality of Franquelin, in the Côte-Nord region of Quebec, Canada. It originates from Ma Tante Lake at an elevation of 197 m (646 ft) and flows into the Rivière aux Anglais at 151 m (495 ft). The river's name, meaning "My Aunt River," is linked to the nearby "Rivière Mon Oncle" ("My Uncle River") and was formalized in 1974.

Sir George Lisle (baptised 10 July 1615 – 28 August 1648) was a professional soldier from London who briefly served in the later stages of the Eighty and Thirty Years War, then fought for the Royalists during the Wars of the Three Kingdoms. Captured at Colchester in August 1648, he was condemned to death by a Parliamentarian court martial and executed by firing squad along with his colleague Charles Lucas.

Son of a successful publisher with connections to the powerful Villiers family, Lisle began his military career in Europe before returning to England. Quickly proving a brave and competent leader, he was promoted to command of a brigade in the Royalist field army until it was destroyed at Naseby in June 1645. He surrendered at Oxford in June 1646 and made terms with Parliament.

When the Second English Civil War began in 1648, he joined the Royalist uprising in Kent before retreating to Colchester. After capitulating in August, he and Lucas were executed and later enshrined as Royalist martyrs.

Muhammad bin Saud Al Muqrin (Lang-ar Muḥammad bin Suʿūd Āl Muqrin; 1687–1765), also known as Ibn Saud, was the emir of Diriyah and is considered the founder of the First Saudi State and the Saud dynasty, which are named for his father, Saud bin Muhammad Al Muqrin. His reign lasted between 1727 and 1765.

Pre-revolutionary Imperial Russia (before 1917) did not have a single national unified system but instead relied on those provided by the manufacturers of the aircraft, like Sikorsky Ilya Muromets or Anatra Anasal.

George Washington's reception at Trenton was a celebration on April 21, 1789, when he passed through Trenton, New Jersey, on his way to New York City for his presidential inauguration. He was greeted at the Assunpink Creek bridge by the "Ladies of Trenton," who sang and spread flowers before him. A triumphal arch was erected, and a dinner was held at the City Tavern. The event has been depicted in numerous artworks and was reenacted in 1989.

The Anatolian diagonal is a biogeographical barrier in Turkey that separates the flora of Western and Eastern Anatolia. First proposed in 1971, the diagonal is characterized by a high number of endemic plant species found nowhere else. Ecological and climatological differences are thought to contribute to the barrier.

Stone Brothers Racing (SBR) was an Australian motor racing team that competed in the V8 Supercars Championship from 1998 to 2012. Founded by New Zealand-born brothers Ross and Jim Stone, the team initially raced as Alan Jones Racing in 1996, with Alan Jones as a co-owner. The Stone brothers bought out Jones' stake at the end of 1997.

SBR achieved significant success, including winning the Bathurst 1000 in its first year as Stone Brothers Racing with drivers Jason Bright and Steven Richards. The team won the V8 Supercars Championship in 2003 with Marcos Ambrose, in 2004 again with Ambrose, and in 2005 with Russell Ingall. The team recorded 23 round wins and 27 pole positions. At the end of 2012, Stone Brothers Racing was sold to Erebus Motorsport.

The Queen Sirikit Museum of Textiles is located in the Grand Palace in Bangkok Thailand. The museum replaced the 1873 Ratsadakorn-bhibhathana Building (Thai: Hor Ratsadakorn Pipat) of the Royal Treasury Ministry by a request of Queen Sirikit in 2003. The building was the original organization of the current Crown Property Bureau.

The Mizoram Peace Accord, 1986 was an official agreement between the Government of India and the Mizo National Front (MNF) to end insurgency and violence in Mizoram, India, that started in 1966. The Mizo National Front was an organisation of Mizo secessionists led by Laldenga to fight for independence from India. The movement was basically due to lack of support from the government during the great famine (called Mautam) in Mizoram in the late 1950s. Political insurgency and social unrest ensued in the next decades. After a number of negotiations, the document titled Mizoram Accord, 1986: Memorandum of Settlement was finally signed on 30 June 1986. It was signed by Laldenga for MNF, R.D. Pradhan, Home Secretary, Government of India, and Lalkhama, Chief Secretary, Government of Mizoram. It is remarked as the most and only successful peace agreement in India after its independence from British Empire in 1947.

Going Blank Again is the second studio album by English rock band Ride, released on 9 March 1992 on Creation Records. It was produced by Alan Moulder, and peaked at No. 5 in the UK Albums Chart. In October 2009 the album was certified gold by the British Phonographic Industry for sales of over 100,000 units.

Latino literature is literature written by people of Latin American ancestry, often but not always in English, most notably by Mexican Americans, Puerto Ricans, Cuban Americans, and Dominican Americans, many of whom were born in the United States.

Notable writers include: Elizabeth Acevedo, Julia Alvarez, Gloria Anzaldua, Rudolfo Anaya, Giannina Braschi, Julia de Burgos, Ana Castillo, Sandra Cisneros, Junot Diaz, Cristina García, Oscar Hijuelos, Judith Ortiz Cofer, Piri Thomas, Rudy Ruiz, Denise Chavez, Cherrie Moraga, Kathleen Alcalá, Carmen Maria Machado, and Edmundo Paz Soldan, among others.
Ronald Horvath is an American-born geographer and academic. He taught at the University of Sydney from 1976 to 1996 before retiring.
Other Voices is a Canadian current affairs television series which aired on CBC Television from 1964 to 1965.

Gracefield is a city in western Quebec, Canada, in the La Vallée-de-la-Gatineau Regional County Municipality. The city is located along the Gatineau River. The first settler arrived in 1840, and the community grew as Northfield and Wright merged to form Wright-Gracefield-Northfield in 2002, which was renamed Gracefield in 2003. The city experienced a major flood in 1974 and damage from an earthquake in 2010. Mining was once a major industry. The population in 2021 was 2,376.

Sokratis Kokkalis (Greek: Σωκράτης Κόκκαλης; 1939) is a Greek businessman. His father, Petros Kokkalis, was a communist politician and Greek Resistance member, living in exile in East Germany after the end of the Greek Civil War.

The 1975 Council of Ministers (Lang-pt, Lang-tet) was the Council of Ministers formed by the Fretilin political party in 1975 as the inaugural administration or cabinet of the Democratic Republic of East Timor proclaimed in November 1975.

Ibex Peak, is a 2,039-metre (6,690-foot) granitic horn located in the North Cascades of southwestern British Columbia, Canada. It is situated 13 km (8 mi) west-northwest of Coquihalla Summit, and 1 km (1 mi) northwest of Steinbok Peak. Its nearest higher peak is Alpaca Peak, 6.2 km (4 mi) to the east. Precipitation runoff from the peak drains into tributaries of the Anderson River. The mountain was named for the ibex, as part of the ungulate theme for several other nearby peaks that were submitted by Philip Kubik of Vancouver. The mountain's name was officially adopted on February 5, 1976, by the Geographical Names Board of Canada.
*Black Noise: Rap Music and Black Culture in Contemporary America* is a 1994 book by Tricia Rose, examining rap music, black culture, and racial politics. It received a 1995 American Book Award.

The tie is a symbol in the shape of an arc similar to a large breve, used in Greek, phonetic alphabets, and Z notation. It can be used between two characters with spacing as punctuation, non-spacing as a diacritic, or (underneath) as a proofreading mark. It can be above or below, and reversed. Its forms are called tie, double breve, enotikon or papyrological hyphen, ligature tie, and undertie.

Medelpad (IPA-sv or IPA-sv) is a historical province or landskap in the north of Sweden. It borders Hälsingland, Härjedalen, Jämtland, Ångermanland and the Gulf of Bothnia.

The province is a part of Norrland and as such considered to be Northern Sweden, although the province geographically is located in the middle of Sweden. It is a common misconception that the name Medelpad ("middle land" or "middle ground") reflects this, but the name actually refers to the fact that most of the province lies between its two rivers Ljungan and Indalsälven.

Hopanoids are a diverse subclass of triterpenoids with the same hydrocarbon skeleton as the compound hopane. This group of pentacyclic molecules therefore refers to simple hopenes, hopanols and hopanes, but also to extensively functionalized derivatives such as bacteriohopanepolyols (BHPs) and hopanoids covalently attached to lipid A.

The first known hopanoid, hydroxyhopanone, was isolated by two chemists at The National Gallery, London working on the chemistry of dammar gum, a natural resin used as a varnish for paintings. While hopanoids are often assumed to be made only in bacteria, their name actually comes from the abundance of hopanoid compounds in the resin of plants from the genus Hopea. In turn, this genus is named after John Hope, the first Regius Keeper of the Royal Botanic Garden, Edinburgh.

Since their initial discovery in an angiosperm, hopanoids have been found in plasma membranes of bacteria, lichens, bryophytes, ferns, tropical trees and fungi. Hopanoids have stable polycyclic structures that are well-preserved in petroleum reservoirs, rocks and sediment, allowing the diagenetic products of these molecules to be interpreted as biomarkers for the presence of specific microbes and potentially for chemical or physical conditions at the time of deposition. Hopanoids have not been detected in archaea.

Systems of Survival: A Dialogue on the Moral Foundations of Commerce and Politics is a 1992 book written by American urban activist Jane Jacobs.

It describes two fundamental and distinct ethical systems, or syndromes as she calls them: that of the Guardian and that of Commerce. She argues that these supply direction for the conduct of human life within societies, and understanding the tension between them can help us with public policy and personal choices.

The Mizoram Peace Accord was a peace agreement signed between the Mizo National Front (MNF), the Government of India, and the Government of Mizoram in New Delhi on June 30, 1986. It officially ended years of unrest and insurgency in Mizoram.

The conflict stemmed from the incorporation of the Mizo people into British India in the 1870s and subsequent governance under Assam after India's independence in 1947, exacerbated by the Mautam famine in 1959. The MNF, formed in 1961, sought a "Greater Mizoram," leading to armed conflict from 1966. Negotiations, primarily led by church leaders, eventually led to the accord.

Under the agreement, the MNF ceased violent activities. Mizoram was granted statehood in 1987, becoming the 23rd state of India. Laldenga, the leader of the MNF, became the Chief Minister. The accord also led to the establishment of Mizoram University and the declaration of "Remna Ni" (Peace Day) on June 30.
Aleksei Fyodorovich Losev (1893–1988) was a Russian philosopher, classical philologist, and culturologist. He worked at multiple institutions, including Moscow University and the Moscow Conservatory.

Ambient techno is a genre of electronic music that emerged in the late 1980s and early 1990s in the United Kingdom and United States. It blends elements of techno, ambient house, and other genres to create atmospheric "electronic listening music".

Filip Ingebrigtsen (born 20 April 1993) is a Norwegian middle-distance runner who specializes in the 1500 meters and mile. He won a bronze medal at the 2017 World Championships in the 1500m. At the 2016 European Athletics Championships, he won gold in the 1500 meters, with his older brother Henrik taking third place.

Ingebrigtsen has achieved personal bests of 3:30.01 in the 1500 meters and 3:49.60 in the mile, the former being a national record. He represented Norway at the 2016 Rio Olympics, but was disqualified in the 1500 meters trial heat. Some of his performances during the 2019 season were controversial, including a disqualification at the 2019 European Athletics Indoor Championships.

Save Our Waterways Now (SOWN) is a community-based organisation rehabilitating Enoggera catchment in Brisbane, the largest city in Queensland, Australia. The principal waterway in the Enoggera catchment is Enoggera Creek with two main tributaries, Fish Creek and Ithaca Creek.

SOWN was incorporated on 18 February 1998 as Save Our Waterways - Now Inc., organisation number IA19057, an association under the jurisdiction of the Office of Fair Trading, Queensland. SOWN was formed as a response to habitat loss and environmental degradation. Enoggera Creek and its tributaries had been degraded by past agricultural land-uses and by the dumping of refuse and garden waste from the homes and developments in the catchment.

SOWN operates a volunteer-run plant nursery which propagates more than 30,000 locally native species every year and provides them free to SOWN members.

Melanie Susan Oxley is an Australian musician, singer-songwriter and primary school teacher. Oxley was a member of the dance pop group, The Sparklers (from 1985 to 1989), which in October 1988 released their debut album, Persuasion.

Since 1989 Oxley teamed with keyboardist, Chris Abrahams to perform and record as a soul pop duo, Melanie Oxley & Chris Abrahams. They have released four studio albums, Welcome to Violet, Coal, Jerusalem Bay and Blood Oranges. At the ARIA Music Awards of 1993 Welcome to Violet was nominated for ARIA Award for Best Independent Release.

Kurihara (栗原市, Kurihara-shi) is a city located in Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. As of 30 April 2020, the city had an estimated population of 66,565, and a population density of 83 persons per km2 in 24,994 households. The total area of the city is 804.97 square kilometres (310.80 sq mi). Parts of the city are within the borders of the Kurikoma Quasi-National Park.
Rodney Bullock Jr. (born 3 June 1994) is an American professional basketball player for Prishtina of the Kosovo Basketball Superleague. He competed in college basketball for Providence.

Meri (מר"י) was a left-wing Israeli political party founded in the 1960s by Uri Avnery. It surprisingly passed the electoral threshold in the 1965 election. In 1977, the party merged with other parties to form the Left Camp of Israel.

Levi General (March 15, 1873 – June 27, 1925), commonly known as Deskaheh, was a Haudenosaunee hereditary chief and appointed speaker noted for his persistent efforts to get recognition for his people. He is most famous for bringing Iroquois concerns before the League of Nations in the 1920s.

Barry Ono (1876–1941) was a British variety theatre performer, collector of penny dreadfuls and benefactor of the British Library. His collection of songbooks was bequeathed to the British Library in 1941.
Drammen Museum of Art and Cultural History (Drammens Museum for kunst og kulturhistorie) is a museum in Drammen in Viken county, Norway.

Multiple complex developmental disorder (MCDD) is a debated diagnosis due to its absence from the DSM-V and ICD-10. Diagnosis requires the presence of symptoms from at least three categories: psychotic, affective/behavioral, autistic, and neurological. These symptoms must not be better explained by another disorder. The exact diagnostic criteria remain unclear, and the term may be considered vague. MCDD is likely caused by various genetic factors, with each individual displaying unique symptoms. Neuropsychological disorders can also be found in family members of people with MCDD.
Doon Valley is one of the nine electoral wards of East Ayrshire Council. Created in 2007, the ward elects three councillors using the single transferable vote electoral system.

The 1983 Barcelona City Council election was held on May 8, 1983. All 43 seats in the City Council of Barcelona were up for election, with 22 seats needed for a majority. The mayor before the election was Pasqual Maragall of the PSC, who was re-elected as mayor. The election saw a turnout of 67.4%.
The Time is the second album and first full-length studio album of Chinese singer-songwriter Xu Weizhou. It is divided into four parts.

Helene P. Foley is an American classicist. She received a PhD from Harvard University in 1975. Foley taught at Swarthmore College until 1979, when she joined Barnard College, Columbia University. She was President of the American Philological Association in 1998 and the Sather Professor of Classical Literature at the University of California, Berkeley in 2008.

The red flour beetle (Tribolium castaneum) is a species of beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, the darkling beetles. It is a worldwide pest of stored products, particularly food grains, and a model organism for ethological and food safety research.

Dragon Ball Xenoverse is an action role-playing fighting game based on the Dragon Ball franchise developed by Dimps and published by Bandai Namco Games. It was released in February 2015 for PlayStation 3, PlayStation 4, Xbox 360, Xbox One, and Microsoft Windows.

A sequel, Dragon Ball Xenoverse 2 was released in 2016.
Maria Silva Cruz (20 April 1915 – 23 August 1936) was a Spanish anarchist and a hero of the Casas Viejas Uprising in Spain. She was also known as "La Libertaria."

Sheikh Ahmad bin Ali bin Abdullah bin Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani (Lang-ar; 1922 – 25 November 1977) was the Emir of Qatar who ruled from 1960 to 1972. Qatar's financial status witnessed significant improvements during his reign as a result of the enrichment and discovery of several new oil fields. Qatar also gained its independence as a sovereign state in September 1971 under his rule. He was deposed in February 1972 by his cousin, Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani.

The Bridge for Kids is a proposed bridge across the Carbon River in Orting, Washington, about a mile upstream of where it joins the Puyallup River. It would provide an emergency evacuation route for school children to escape a future lahar flow from Mount Rainier, consisting of an up to 10-meter (33 ft) high flood of mud, rock and boulders. As of 2016, the $40 million bridge was still in the planning phase.
Sina Karimian (born 23 January 1988) is an Iranian kickboxer, fighting out of Power of Dream Gym. He is first and current K-1 WORLD GP Cruiserweight Champion.

In photography, filmmaking, and other imaging disciplines, color correction is the process of altering the color of an image. The goal of color correction is to render colors as faithfully as possible.

One aspect of color correction involves adjusting the correlated color temperature (CCT) of a light source, which simplifies the color of white light. Higher CCTs appear bluer, while lower CCTs appear more orange. Color temperature filters, such as color temperature blue (CTB) and color temperature orange (CTO) gels, are commonly used to convert light from one color temperature to another. Other gels, like minus green and plus green, are used to correct for green casts often found in fluorescent lights.

Color correction can be achieved through physical filters placed over a camera lens or through digital manipulation of the image. In digital video systems, white balance can be adjusted electronically, but using physical filters can maximize the dynamic range of the captured image. In film cameras, color correction filters are essential for adjusting white balance during shooting. Software like Photoshop also offers various tools for color correction and manipulation.

*Euprymna tasmanica*, also known as the southern dumpling squid, is a species of bobtail squid native to southern Australia. It is a member of the *Euprymna* genus within the Sepiolidae family.

Like other bobtail squid, *E. tasmanica* possesses a light organ in its mantle cavity that houses symbiotic bioluminescent bacteria. This organ emits light to eliminate the squid's silhouette. They typically display iridescent green or yellow coloration with brown spots, though they can change color.

The species reproduces sexually, with females laying multiple batches of creamy orange eggs. The incubation period can last up to four and a half months, and hatchlings are well-developed and capable of capturing relatively large prey. The species is currently classified as Data Deficient by the IUCN.

Abubakar Umar Tutare (born February 13, 1965) is a Nigerian politician who served as the Senator for Taraba Central from 2011 to 2015. Before becoming a senator, he was a member of the Taraba State House of Assembly and a state commissioner.

The disappearance of Sven Höglin and Heidi Paakkonen refers to the unsolved case of a Swedish couple who went missing on April 8, 1989, while backpacking near Thames, on the Coromandel Peninsula of New Zealand's North Island. Their disappearance sparked a large-scale search operation and intense media interest.

David Tamihere, who had a prior conviction for manslaughter and was on the run from rape charges, was later convicted of their murder in December 1990. The trial relied heavily on circumstantial evidence and testimony from prison inmates, one of whom later recanted his statement, claiming police coercion. Höglin's remains were discovered in 1991, but Paakkonen's body has never been found.

Tamihere's conviction was controversial, leading to appeals and ongoing debate about the fairness of the trial. He was eventually released on parole in 2010, maintaining his innocence. In 2020, he was granted a royal prerogative of mercy, referring the case back to the Court of Appeal. The case has been the subject of documentaries, books, and podcasts, continuing to generate public interest and scrutiny.

"O RLY?" is an internet slang phrase often paired with an image of a snowy owl. The phrase, short for "Oh, really?", is often followed by "YA RLY!", meaning "Yes, really!".

The phrase has been referenced in video games such as *World of Warcraft*. In 2006, a computer worm called "W32/Hoots-A" displayed an image of a snowy owl with the letters "O RLY?" on infected computers. The phrase has also been used to parody O'Reilly Media's book covers.

The Pulmonary Vascular Research Institute (PVRI) is a UK registered medical research charity. It is registered Charity in the United Kingdom (Charity No: 1127115) and a private limited company by guarantee. The company registration number in the United Kingdom is 5780068. The PVRI is a professional membership organisation for doctors and scientists from around the world who have a special interest in pulmonary hypertension (PH) or pulmonary vascular diseases (PVD).

Violet Dandridge, was the pseudonym for Serena Katherine Dandridge (1878–1956), she was an American scientific illustrator, painter, naturalist, and suffragist. She was the Smithsonian Institution’s first female scientific illustrator.

Alligator Rivers is the name of an area in an Arnhem Land region of the Northern Territory of Australia, containing three rivers, the East, West, and South Alligator Rivers. It is regarded as one of the richest biological regions in Australia, with part of the region in the Kakadu National Park. It is an Important Bird Area (IBA), lying to the east of the Adelaide and Mary River Floodplains IBA. It also contains mineral deposits, especially uranium, and the Ranger Uranium Mine is located there. The area is also rich in Australian Aboriginal art, with 1500 sites. The Kakadu National Park is one of the few World Heritage sites on the list because of both its natural and human heritage values. They were explored by Lieutenant Phillip Parker King in 1820, who named them in the mistaken belief that the crocodiles in the estuaries were alligators.
Robbie Thorpe is an Aboriginal Australian activist from the Gunnai Nation, known for his advocacy of Indigenous solutions and sovereignty.

The 2011–12 Kategoria Superiore was the 73rd season of top-tier football in Albania. It began on September 10, 2011, and concluded on May 12, 2012. Skënderbeu won their 3rd Albanian title. Teams relegated were Kamza, Pogradeci and Dinamo. The top goalscorer was Roland Dervishi with 20 goals. Teams promoted to the league were Pogradeci, Tomori, Kamza and Apolonia. Shkumbini and Dinamo retained their spots in the league.

PRNP (prion protein) is the human gene encoding for the major prion protein PrP (prion protein, Pr for prion, and P for protein), also known as CD230 (cluster of differentiation 230). Expression of the protein is most predominant in the nervous system but occurs in many other tissues throughout the body.

The protein can exist in multiple isoforms: the normal PrPC form, and the protease-resistant form designated PrPRes such as the disease-causing PrPSc(scrapie) and an isoform located in mitochondria. The misfolded version PrPSc is associated with a variety of cognitive disorders and neurodegenerative diseases such as in animals: ovine scrapie, bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE, mad cow disease), feline spongiform encephalopathy, transmissible mink encephalopathy (TME), exotic ungulate encephalopathy, chronic wasting disease (CWD) which affects cervids; and in humans: Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (CJD), fatal familial insomnia (FFI), Gerstmann–Sträussler–Scheinker syndrome (GSS), kuru, and variant Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (vCJD). Similarities exist between kuru, thought to be due to human ingestion of diseased individuals, and vCJD, thought to be due to human ingestion of BSE-tainted cattle products.

Paterson is a New Jersey Transit commuter railway station located on an elevated viaduct above Market Street in downtown Paterson, New Jersey. The railway through the station is double tracked, for north and south traffic on the NJT Main Line.

Vishwa Thulasi is a 2004 Tamil-language drama film directed by Sumathy Ram, starring Mammooty and Nandita Das. This film's score and soundtrack were composed by Ilayaraaja and M. S. Viswanathan. The film was notable for having a female director.
Park Sol-mi (born January 3, 1978) is a South Korean actress and pianist.

The Holy Science is a book written by Swami Sri Yukteswar Giri in 1894 under the title Kaivalya Darsanam. Sri Yukteswar states that he wrote The Holy Science at the request of Mahavatar Babaji. The book compares parallel passages from the Bible and Upanishads in order to show the unity of all religions.

Klagenfurt Athletic Sports Club or EC KAC (Lang-de) is a professional ice hockey team in the ICE Hockey League. The team plays their home games in Klagenfurt, Carinthia, Austria at Stadthalle Klagenfurt. EC KAC has won the most Austrian ice hockey Championships, a total of 32 times including 11 consecutive titles from 1964 to 1974, and four consecutive from 1985 to 1988. Most recently they won it in 2021.

Anthony Minghella CBE (6 January 1954 – 18 March 2008) was a British playwright, director, and screenwriter. He gained notice for *Whale Music* (1985) and mainstream success with *Made in Bangkok* (1986). Minghella won the Academy Award for Best Director for *The English Patient* (1996). He received an Adapted Screenplay nomination for *The Talented Mr. Ripley* (1999). His last work was the screenplay for *Nine* (2009). Minghella died in 2008 following an operation for cancer.

Charles Percy Abbott (born 10 June 1939) is a former Australian rules footballer who played with Hawthorn in the Victorian Football League (VFL), a polo player, and the recipient of the Australian Sports Medal.

Milnrow Cricket Club is a cricket club notable for having attracted several prominent Test and county cricket players as professionals, despite its modest record in the Central Lancashire League. These professionals have included Australian all-rounder George Tribe, county spinners Albert Rhodes and John McMahon, former Lancashire captain Ken Grieves, West Indies Test player Derick Parry, Zimbabwean Kevin Curran, Indian bowler Chetan Sharma, and New Zealand off-spin bowler Paul Wiseman. George Tribe once took 148 wickets in a single season, equalling the league record at the time.

Princess Jane Mpologoma Nabanakulya is a daughter of Uganda's first President Mutesa II of Buganda. She was born in Sunga Village, Buyaga County, Bunyoro-Kitara Kingdom, on April 12, 1964 by Omuzaana Naome Nanyonga, a midwife of Major General Sir Mutesa II of Buganda.

"Remember a Day" is a song by the British rock band Pink Floyd, written and sung by their keyboardist Richard Wright, appearing on their second album, A Saucerful of Secrets (1968). It was performed by Pink Floyd only once, as an encore in May 1968; it was subsequently performed by David Gilmour in September 2008 in memory of Wright, who had recently died of cancer, on Later. with Jools Holland, and by Nick Mason during his Saucerful of Secrets tour. The dreamy, poetic lyrics are about nostalgia for the lost paradise of early childhood.

*I Feel Pretty* is a 2018 American comedy film written and directed by Abby Kohn and Marc Silverstein. The film stars Amy Schumer as Renee Bennett, a woman with low self-esteem who, after hitting her head, suddenly believes she is beautiful. The film also stars Michelle Williams, Rory Scovel, Emily Ratajkowski, and Aidy Bryant.

*I Feel Pretty* was released in the United States on April 20, 2018, by STX Entertainment. It grossed $94.5 million worldwide against a budget of $32 million. While commercially successful, the film received mixed reviews, with some critics criticizing its perceived mixed messages about body positivity. A French remake, *Belle, belle, belle*, was released in 2021.

Mafdet was a goddess in ancient Egyptian religion during the First Dynasty (c. 3100 – c. 2900 BCE). She protected against snakes and scorpions, and she was often associated with royal protection and justice.

Mafdet was said to defend the sun god Ra from threats during his nightly voyage and ensure the coming of dawn. She also protected the dismembered god Osiris. In royal tombs, her symbol is associated with Anubis, suggesting she accompanied the gods as a hunter or executioner.

In art, Mafdet was depicted as a feline or mongoose, or as a woman with the head of such an animal. She was also shown running up the side of an executioner's staff, ripping out the hearts of wrongdoers and delivering them to the pharaoh. During the New Kingdom, she was seen as ruling over the judgment hall in Duat.
Harrisburg is a town in Lewis County, New York, United States. The population was 437 at the 2010 census. The town was formed in 1803 from part of the towns of Lowville, Champion, and Mexico.

*Unleashed* (released as *Danny the Dog* in France) is a 2005 action film directed by Louis Leterrier, written by Luc Besson, and starring Jet Li, Morgan Freeman, and Bob Hoskins. The film follows Danny (Li), a man raised as a weapon, as he learns to be human.

The Swedish Society for Anthropology and Geography (SSAG) is a learned society that promotes research and knowledge in anthropology and geography. Founded in 1878, the society is known for awarding the Vega Medal to physical geographers and the SSAG Medal (formerly the Anders Retzius Medal) to geographers or anthropologists. The awards are presented by the King of Sweden on April 24, the anniversary of Adolf Erik Nordenskiöld's return from the Vega expedition. The society publishes *Kritisk Etnografi* (Swedish Journal of Anthropology), a biannual, peer-reviewed, open-access journal in collaboration with DiVA, an online archive maintained by Uppsala University.
Thomas Anthony Lawton (born Darwin, 1 November 1962) is a former Australian rugby union player. He played as a hooker.

Educational robotics uses robots as educational tools in schools and camps to raise interest in programming, AI, and robotics. Robots can be physically coded or screen coded. Robotics programs are increasingly common in post-secondary education, with some universities now offering degrees in robotic engineering.

Up the Elephant and Round the Castle is a British television sitcom, which aired from 1983 to 1985, and was produced by Thames Television for the ITV network. Starring comedian Jim Davidson, who played the role of Jim London, the show spawned a sequel, Home James!, which was also made by Thames. Home James ran from 1987 to 1990.

In the Night Room is a 2004 horror-thriller novel by American author Peter Straub, a sequel to his 2003 book Lost Boy, Lost Girl. The work was first published in hardback on October 26, 2004 through Random House and it won the 2004 Bram Stoker Award for Novel. Straub encountered some difficulties while writing In the Night Room and had written several different passages for the work before growing bored with each version before writing it using the same technique he used for its predecessor in which he "reached down inside the book and turned it inside out."

Raymond Cannon (born Ulises Tildmann Cannon;: 270 September 1, 1892 – June 7, 1977) was an American actor, film director, screenwriter, journalist, and author known for his work with D. W. Griffith and Buster Keaton.
Frederick William Fairholt (1814 – 1866) was a British draughtsman, engraver, and antiquary.
James J. Carrigan (born April 25, 1941) is an American attorney and politician who served in the Massachusetts General Court.

Irving Trust was an American Commercial bank headquartered in New York City that operated between 1851 and 1988 when it was acquired by Bank of New York. From 1965 the bank was the principal subsidiary of the Irving Bank Corporation.

Between 1913 and 1931, its headquarters was in the Woolworth Building; after 1931, until it was acquired by Bank of New York, its headquarters was located at One Wall Street, at what is now known as the BNY Mellon Building.

Anne Ebbs (born 1940) is an Irish table tennis player and sports administrator. She won silver medals at the 1972 and 1984 Summer Paralympics and a bronze in 1980. Ebbs created the Paralympic Council of Ireland in 1987 and was honored with the Paralympic Order in 2008.

Scott Alan Mendes (born July 4, 1969) is an American former professional bull rider. He won the 1997 Professional Rodeo Cowboys Association (PRCA) World Championship. Mendes co-founded the Professional Bull Riders (PBR). After becoming a born-again Christian, he founded Western Harvest Ministries. He is also a producer of Christian films and a motivational speaker.

The February 2016 Ankara bombing occurred on February 17, 2016, at 18:31 local time in Ankara, Turkey. A car bomb detonated while army buses carrying military personnel were waiting at traffic lights, killing 30 people and injuring 60. The attack targeted military personnel and took place next to a residential block for high-ranking military personnel.

The Kurdistan Freedom Hawks (TAK) claimed responsibility for the bombing, identifying the suicide bomber as Abdulbaki Sönmez, a Turkish citizen. Initially, the Turkish government declared the assailant to be Salih Necar, a Syrian Kurd, but DNA reports later confirmed Sönmez as the perpetrator. Turkish authorities asserted that Sönmez was trained by the YPG and that the attack was planned by the YPG, PKK, PYD, and the Syrian government. The bombing was widely condemned both domestically and internationally.

Joanne M. Garvey is an American attorney. She grew up in Oakland, California, and received a B.A. from the University of California at Berkeley and a J.D. from UC Berkeley School of Law, where she was one of only five women in her graduating class.

Garvey began her legal career in Santa Barbara, California, in 1962. She then spent 25 years at the law firm Jordan, Keeler and Seligman in San Francisco. In 1988, she joined Heller Ehrman, also in San Francisco, and subsequently moved to Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton after Heller Ehrman dissolved in 2008.

Garvey has received several notable awards, including the State Bar of California Taxation Section Joanne M. Garvey Award, named in her honor, and the American Bar Association Commission on Women's 2003 Margaret Brent Women Lawyers of Achievement Award.

*In the Night Room* is a 2004 horror novel by Peter Straub. The story follows author Timothy Underhill as he struggles with the loss of his sister and receives mysterious emails from deceased individuals. Simultaneously, author Willy Patrick fears for her sanity, believing her deceased daughter is being held captive. As their stories intertwine, Timothy and Willy must uncover the truth to prevent further misfortunes. The novel explores themes of trauma, narrative reality, and the power of fiction. Critical reception was mostly positive, with praise for its exploration of dark fantasy and the correlation between personal demons and fictional horrors.
Sangita Patel (born January 2, 1979) is a Canadian television personality, currently the host of HGTV's Home to Win and an on-air personality of Entertainment Tonight Canada.

Persia is a town in Cattaraugus County, New York, United States. The population was 2,203 at the 2020 census. It is in the northwest part of the county. The village of Gowanda is partially in the town.

The Petaluma and Santa Rosa Railroad was an interurban railway in Sonoma County, California, formed in 1903 through the consolidation of four horse car lines. Electrification enabled service between Petaluma and Santa Rosa, with extensions to Sebastopol and Forestville. A notable event in its history was the "Battle of Sebastopol Road" in 1905, a conflict with a steam railroad over a crossing. Expansion plans were disrupted by the 1906 San Francisco earthquake. Passenger service declined, ceasing entirely in 1932 after being purchased by Northwestern Pacific Railroad. The line was gradually dismantled between 1931 and 1989.
Dame Thea King DBE FRCM FGSM (26 December 1925 – 26 June 2007) was a British clarinettist.

Crown Prince Hyoryeong (born 4 June 1149), personal name Wang Gi was a Goryeo Royal Prince as the first and oldest son of King Uijong and Queen Janggyeong who later deposed from his position alongside his father and his only son by Jeong Jung-bu and Yi Ui-bang in 1170.

Argentina was one of the seventeen nations that competed at the inaugural Summer Paralympic Games in 1960 held in Rome, Italy from September 19 to 24, 1968. Preparations for the Games began two years prior in 1958 to stage what was at the time called the 9th Annual International Stoke Mandeville Games. The team finished tenth in the medal table with a total of six medals, two gold, three silver and one bronze. The Argentinian team consisted of five athletes, one man and four women.

"Casa Tomada" ("House Taken Over") is a short story by Argentine writer Julio Cortázar, written in 1946 and first published in 1951 in the magazine *Los Anales de Buenos Aires*. It was later included in Cortázar's collection *Bestiario*.

The story follows a brother and sister in their early forties who live together in their ancestral home, passing their days in quiet routine. Their peaceful existence is disrupted when they begin to hear unexplained noises within the house. Gradually, these noises encroach upon their living space, forcing them to retreat further and further until they are finally driven out altogether. They abandon the house, throwing the key into the sewer.

The source of the noises is never revealed, leaving the reader to interpret the nature of the "takeover." The story has been interpreted as an allegory for various themes, including the loss of personal space, the encroachment of the unknown, and, notably, as a critique of Peronism, given Cortázar's own experiences with political persecution.
Women's suffrage in Mexico began in the late 19th century, achieved some gains during the Revolution, and culminated in 1953 with the vote.

Erik Rauch (May 15, 1974 – July 13, 2005) was an American biophysicist and theoretical ecologist. He received a Ph.D. from MIT and worked at Princeton University. Rauch founded MetaCarta and proposed car-free neighborhoods. He died in a hiking accident at age 31.

Mongolia competed at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, Greece, from 13 to 29 August 2004. This was the nation's tenth appearance at the Olympics, except the 1984 Summer Olympics in Los Angeles, because of its partial support to the Soviet boycott.

The Mongolian National Olympic Committee sent a total of 20 athletes, 13 men and 7 women, to compete in 7 different sports, tying its delegation count with Sydney four years earlier. In the wake of Munkhbayar Dorjsuren's transfer to the German team, three Mongolian athletes had previously competed in Sydney, including shooter Otryadyn Gündegmaa in the women's pistol events. Judoka and world champion Damdinsürengiin Nyamkhüü was appointed by the committee to become Mongolia's flag bearer in the opening ceremony, dressed in a traditional costume.

After failing to achieve a single Olympic medal from Sydney, Mongolia left Athens with only a bronze from twenty-year-old judoka Khashbaataryn Tsagaanbaatar on the first day of the Games.
Gustavo Osvaldo Parraguez Galarce (born 24 May 1943) is the municipal secretary of Pichilemu, and was the interim Mayor of the commune of Pichilemu three different times, in 1992, 2007, and 2008.

*No Gravity* is a 3D space shooter developed by realtech VR, where players complete missions from first- or third-person perspectives. Originating as a BeOS game called *SpaceGirl*, *No Gravity* was open-sourced in 2005 and ported to multiple platforms. An extended PSP port was released in 2009.

Farma 12 - City vs Village is the twelfth season of the Slovak version of The Farm reality television show based on the Swedish television series of the same name. The show began filming in August 2020 and premiered on August 31, 2020 on Markíza.

Future of the Left are a Welsh alternative rock band based in Cardiff. The group consists of former Mclusky members Andrew Falkous (vocals, guitar) and Jack Egglestone (drums) and former Million Dead bassist Julia Ruzicka.

In linguistics, the pluperfect, also called past perfect, is a verb form that combines past tense and perfect aspect. It refers to an event that was completed before a point in the past. For example: "It had already been raining for a week when the big storm started."

Languages form the pluperfect in different ways. Some, like Latin, inflect the verb. Most modern European languages use auxiliary verbs with past participles. Examples of pluperfect forms are found in Ancient Greek, French (plus-que-parfait), Italian (trapassato prossimo and trapassato remoto), Spanish (pluscuamperfecto and pretérito anterior), Portuguese/Galician (mais-que-perfeito or antepretérito), Judeo-Spanish (pluskuamperfekto), Romanian (mai mult ca perfect), English (had jumped), German (Plusquamperfekt), Dutch (Voltooid verleden tijd), Swedish (pluskvamperfekt), Ukrainian and Belarusian (давньоминулий час or запрошлы час), Slovenian (predpreteklik), Korean, Welsh, Irish, and Finnish (pluskvamperfekti).

"Winter" is a song by U2, released on February 27, 2009. It was written for the 2009 war film *Brothers*, directed by Jim Sheridan. The lyrics, written by Bono, were inspired by Tobey Maguire's character in the film.

U2 created two versions of the song: a rock arrangement that appeared in the film *Linear*, and an acoustic version for *Brothers*. An electronic version was also developed. Although the band considered including "Winter" on their album *No Line on the Horizon*, it was ultimately omitted. "Winter" was nominated for the Golden Globe Award for Best Original Song in 2010.

An avulsion injury is the separation of tissue from its attachment point. Skin avulsions range in severity and may require grafts or reconstructive surgery. In rock climbing, skin avulsions called "flappers" can occur on the fingers. Avulsions can also affect the ear, eyelid, nail, brachial plexus, and teeth. Treatment varies depending on the location and severity of the injury, and may include suturing, microvascular surgery, nerve grafts, or tooth re-implantation. In some cases, avulsion is performed surgically to treat certain conditions.

Koforidua Senior High Technical School, is one of the best secondary technical schools in Ghana. The school is located at Koforidua, the capital of Eastern Region in southern Ghana. Koforidua Secondary technical also called Sec-Tech, is a Senior High School located in Koforidua in the Eastern Region of Ghana. As of December 2011, the school has over 2,200 students.

The Space Force Association (SFA) is an independent, 501(c)(3) non-profit organization that serves as a professional military association, space advocacy group, and space education association for the United States Space Force and space professionals at large.

Tamara Bowie (born June 3, 1981) is an American basketball player. She played college basketball for Ball State, where she was twice named MAC Player of the Year. Professionally, she played in the WNBA for the Washington Mystics and in several international leagues, winning a Bulgarian League championship in 2011.

The Yalta International Economic Forum (shortened as YIEF) is an annual business event held in Yalta on the Crimea Peninsula since 2015. It is considered the fourth largest Russian economic forum, after the Petersburg International Economic Forum, the Eastern Economic Forum and the Sochi Investment Forum. The forum is sponsored by the local Government of the Republic of Crimea and the Yalta International Economic Forum Fund, all Russian sources.

The YIEF sees itself as an international venue for discussing socio-economic development issues, for Russia and the world economy as a whole.

According to the organizers, the forum is attended by Russian government officials, international experts, Russian business people and economists, as well as representatives of business organisations and other thinkers. One theme of the forums discussions, is the focus on diversification from Energy and boosting competitiveness of the Russian economy.

Attabad Lake (also known as Gojal Lake) is a lake in the Hunza Valley of Gilgit−Baltistan, Pakistan, that was formed in January 2010. A landslide dammed the Hunza River, creating the lake, which submerged portions of the Karakoram Highway and displaced thousands of people.

The Nieuport-Macchi Parasol was an Italian parasol wing monoplane designed and manufactured by Nieuport-Macchi, entering service in 1913. It was derived from the Nieuport IV, and was primarily constructed of wood and canvas. The Royal Italian Army employed the Parasol for artillery spotting during World War I, before retiring it from service in November 1915 due to a high accident rate.

"Rollin' in my 5.0" is a hip hop song written by American rapper Vanilla Ice. The song samples "Fly Like an Eagle" by the Steve Miller Band. The studio version of the song was only found on the single as it was originally intended to be featured on Mind Blowin. It is about Vanilla Ice's 5.0 Liter Foxbody Mustang (Ford Mustang (third generation)).

Attabad Lake (Lang-ur) is a lake located in the Gojal region of Hunza Valley in Gilgit−Baltistan, Pakistan. It was created in January 2010 as the result of a major landslide in Attabad. The lake has become one of the biggest tourist attractions in Gilgit−Baltistan, offering activities like boating, jet-skiing, fishing and other recreational activities.
Wodan Timbur Coaster is a wooden roller coaster at Europa-Park in Germany. Built by Great Coasters International, it opened in 2012.

Harewood House is a stately home in Harewood, West Yorkshire, England. Built between 1759 and 1771 for Edwin Lascelles, 1st Baron Harewood, it is a Grade I listed building designed in the Palladian style by John Carr and Robert Adam. The estate was formed by merging the Harewood Castle and Gawthorpe estates, later acquired by the Lascelles family in the 18th century, who derived wealth from plantations in the West Indies.

The house has been modified over time, notably by Sir Charles Barry in the 1840s. In the 20th century, it became the family seat of the Lascelles family, including Princess Mary. Today, Harewood House is managed by the Harewood House Trust and is open to the public, featuring art collections, gardens, and a bird garden. It has also served as a filming location for productions such as *Downton Abbey* and the *Emmerdale* television series.
DeForest is a village in Dane County, Wisconsin, United States, along the Yahara River. The population was 10,811 at the 2020 census. It is part of the Madison Metropolitan Statistical Area.
Grantham and Stamford is a county constituency in Lincolnshire, England created in 1997. The current MP is Gareth Davies, a Conservative.
James Adedokun Adegoke (1934–2017) was a teacher and football referee who became Apetumodu of Ipetumodu in 1992. He is from Asalu compound of Fagbemokun ruling house of Ipetumodu.
Nina Edith Coote (1883–1945) was an Irish croquet player, winning several championships.

"7empest" is a song by the American rock band Tool, released on August 30, 2019, as part of their album *Fear Inoculum*. The 15-minute track is known for its complex structure and transitions. It received critical acclaim, with many publications citing it as the best song on the album. "7empest" won the Grammy Award for Best Metal Performance.

William David Arnett (born 1940) is a Regents Professor of Astrophysics at Steward Observatory, University of Arizona, known for his research on supernova explosions, the formation of neutron stars or black holes by gravitational collapse, and the synthesis of elements in stars; he is author of the monograph Supernovae and Nucleosynthesis which deals with these topics. Arnett pioneered the application of supercomputers to astrophysical problems, including neutrino radiation hydrodynamics, nuclear reaction networks, instabilities and explosions, supernova light curves, and turbulent convective flow in two and three dimensions.

Villa Park is a neighborhood in Denver, Colorado. Its hilly topography provides panoramic views of downtown Denver and of the Rocky Mountains, particularly from Lakewood / Dry Gulch Park, which divides the neighborhood diagonally.
Freienfeld (IPA-de; Lang-it IPA-it) is a comune (municipality) in the province of South Tyrol in northern Italy, located about 45 kilometres (28 mi) north of the city of Bolzano.

The Swedish Society for Anthropology and Geography (SSAG; Lang-sv) is a scientific learned society founded in December 1877. It was established after a rearrangement of various sections of the Anthropological Society, which was formed in 1873 by Hjalmar Stolpe, Hans Hildebrand, Oscar Montelius, and Gustaf Retzius.

The society functions as a link between science and the public, especially in the subjects of anthropology and geography. It awards research fellowships, organizes excursions and lectures, and hands out awards including the Vega Medal and Retzius Medal. In 1880, the society published the first edition of the Swedish yearbook Ymer, and it has published the international journal Geografiska Annaler since 1919, a publication that is divided between physical geography and human geography.

In 2018, it established Kritisk Etnografi, an academic journal of ethnography.

HMS Fairy was a three-funnel, 30-knot destroyer of the First World War. One of three similar ships built by Fairfields for the Royal Navy, she was ordered under the 1896–1897 Naval Estimates and the sixth Royal Navy ship to carry this name. She was classified, along with other similar ships, as a C-class destroyer in 1913. She sank in 1918 from damage inflicted by ramming and sinking the German submarine UC-75.

Joshua Oigara (born 1975) is a Kenyan businessman and accountant. He is the former Group Chief Executive Officer of Kenya Commercial Bank Group (KCB), overseeing operations in several East African countries.

Oigara holds a Bachelor of Commerce degree from the University of Nairobi and an MBA from Edith Cowan University. He is also a certified public accountant. Before joining KCB in 2011, he worked at PricewaterhouseCoopers, Bidco Africa, and Bamburi Cement, where he rose to become Group Finance Director. He stepped down as CEO of KCB in May 2022.

Kentucky Route 8 is a 114.202-mile-long (183.790 km) east–west state highway divided into two distinct segments across northern Kentucky. The western terminus of the route is at KY 237 near Francisville. The eastern terminus is at U.S. Route 23 in South Portsmouth. The two distinct segments of this route were not meant to be connected together. KY 8 from its west end in Boone County to Augusta in Bracken County is designated as a part of an identified corridor for bikes named the Ramblin' River Tour by the American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials (AASHTO).

KY 8 is named the Mary Ingles Highway for part of its length. It is rumored that she was the first white woman in Kentucky. Captured by Indians in Virginia in 1755 and taken to Ohio, she later escaped a salt-making party at Big Bone Lick and made her way across the Kentucky wilderness back home to Virginia.

Lis Shoshi (born June 29, 1994) is a Kosovan professional basketball player who last played for Maccabi Rishon LeZion in the Israeli Basketball Premier League. He played college basketball for Howard College and University of Arkansas at Little Rock before playing professionally in Kosovo, Lithuania and Israel.

Quick couplers (also called quick hitches) are used with construction machines to allow the rapid change of buckets and attachments on the machine. They remove the need to use hammers to manually drive out and insert the mounting pins for attachments. They also bring with them additional safety risks that must be overcome by careful design and manufacture, and proper use.

The qualifying competitions for the 2017 CONCACAF U-20 Championship were handled by two regional of CONCACAF's bodies; the Caribbean Football Union (Caribbean zone) and the Central American Football Union (Central American zone).

Representative teams from Canada, Mexico and the United States automatically qualified for the final competition.

General der Nachrichtenaufklärung Training Referat was the training organization within the General der Nachrichtenaufklärung (GDNA), the military signals intelligence agency of the Wehrmacht, before and during World War II. Until 1942, the work of the Training Referat was not fully exploited and only a small beginners course was in progress.

Michael McClune (born August 22, 1989) is an American professional tennis player.

He turned professional in 2007, was represented by IMG and signed a lucrative contract with Nike.

"Loti" is a song by Albanian singer and songwriter Elvana Gjata, released on June 10, 2021, through East Music Matters and Loudcom. Gjata wrote the song, while Jumpa and Lex Barkey handled the production and arrangement, respectively. Musically, "Loti" is an Albanian pop, R&B urban song, incorporating elements of folk, trap, and rock. Lyrically, it tells the story of a woman losing intimacy with her partner.

"Loti" was met with positive reviews from music critics, who praised its fresh sound, Gjata's vocals, and its blend of modern and traditional elements. Commercially, the song reached number one in Albania and charted in Switzerland.

The accompanying music video, filmed at Divjakë-Karavasta National Park and directed by Flicka Elisa and Vasjen Katro, premiered on June 10, 2021. It trended on YouTube in several European countries and received praise for its visuals and styling.

Sir John Wallace Pringle (born in Madras, India, to Scottish and English parents; died July 16, 1938) was an officer in the Royal Engineers. He surveyed the Uganda Railway and later became an inspecting officer for the Board of Trade and Ministry of Transport, investigating railway accidents.
Crossing the Red Sea with The Adverts is the debut studio album by English punk rock band the Adverts. It was released on 17 February 1978 by record label Bright.

Gracefield is a city in La Vallée-de-la-Gatineau Regional County Municipality in the Outaouais region of Quebec, Canada. It was reorganized on 13 March 2002 when three former municipalities (Gracefield, Northfield, and Wright) were merged into one.

Its name comes from Patrick Grace who opened there the first business and was mayor of Wright from 1885 to 1890.

Gracefield is located along the Gatineau River and Picanoc River. Its area is dotted with many lakes, most of which are lined with numerous summer camps and cottages.
James Adedokun Adegoke Akunraaledoye II (1934 – 2017) was the Apetumodu of Ipetumodu from 1992 to 2017. Before his kingship, he was a teacher and headmaster.

On July 25, 1988, Lieth Von Stein was murdered in his home in Washington, North Carolina. His wife, Bonnie Von Stein, was seriously injured in the attack. Suspicion fell on Bonnie's children, Chris and Angela Pritchard. The investigation revealed that Chris Pritchard had orchestrated the murder with his friends, James Upchurch and Neal Henderson, to obtain his inheritance.

In January 1990, Upchurch was found guilty of first-degree murder and sentenced to life imprisonment after his death sentence was set aside. Pritchard and Henderson pleaded guilty to reduced charges and testified against Upchurch. Pritchard was convicted of aiding and abetting in second-degree murder and paroled in 2007. Henderson was also convicted of second-degree murder and paroled in 2000. The case has been the subject of books and television adaptations, including *Cruel Doubt* and *Blood Games*.
Kate Addo (born 23 July 1972) is a Ghanaian journalist, broadcaster and public relations practitioner. She is currently the Director of Public Affairs of the Parliament of Ghana.

Mothers against decapentaplegic homolog 7, or SMAD7, is a protein that inhibits TGF-β signaling by preventing the formation of Smad2/Smad4 complexes. It interacts with activated TGF-β type I receptor, blocking the association, phosphorylation, and activation of Smad2. SMAD7 also plays a role in negative feedback for Activin and bone morphogenetic protein pathways. It is induced by stimuli such as epidermal growth factor, interferon-γ, and tumor necrosis factor-α, providing cross-talk between TGF-β signaling and other cellular signaling pathways. A mutation in the SMAD7 gene is linked to susceptibility to colorectal cancer.

Mindy Lubber is President of Ceres, a non-profit sustainability advocacy organization based in Boston, Massachusetts. Lubber became President of Ceres in January 2014 and was a speaker at the Sustainable Innovation Forum of the 2015 United Nations Climate Change Conference.

Agnieszka Brustman (born 31 July 1962) is a Polish chess player holding the title of woman grandmaster. She has been the Polish women's champion four times and competed in the Candidates' tournament for the Women's World Championship twice.

Margaret Ransone Murray (1901–1986) was an American anatomist and cell biologist. She is known for her research in surgical pathology and tissue culture, particularly her work on myelination and synaptogenesis. She was also a founder of the Tissue Culture Association and the American Society for Cell Biology.

Ambient techno is a subgenre of techno that incorporates the atmospheric textures of ambient music with the rhythmic elements and production of techno. It was pioneered by 1990s electronic artists such as Aphex Twin, Carl Craig, the Black Dog, Pete Namlook and Biosphere.

*Up the Elephant and Round the Castle* is a British comedy series that aired on ITV from 1983 to 1985. Starring Jim Davidson, the show follows Jim London, who inherits a run-down property in the Elephant and Castle area of London. The theme music was composed by Keith Emerson. The complete series was released on DVD in 2008.

Bathothauma lyromma, the lyre cranch squid, is a highly modified species of glass squid from the family Cranchiidae with a distribution which encompasses most of the world's oceans from the tropics to high latitudes.

Paul Thomson (June 29, 1916 – May 31, 2008) was an American exotic fruit enthusiast, self-taught horticulturist and botanist, fruit farmer, and the co-founder of the California Rare Fruit Growers Association, a group of amateur horticulturists which now has more than 3,000 members in approximately 35 countries. Thomson is credited with helping to expand the farming of exotic fruits in California – everything from cherimoyas to longans to pitahayas. Thomson also wrote one of the few literary treatments of the genus Dudleya, and was an avid collector of the plants.

Leigh Diffey (born March 3, 1971) is an Australian-American sports commentator. He is employed by NBC, where he commentates on IndyCar, IMSA, and Supercross. Diffey has also covered NASCAR, Global Rallycross, and the Dakar Rally. He has covered multiple Olympic Games, commentating on events such as luge, rowing, and track and field. Prior to joining NBC, Diffey worked for Network Ten, the BBC, and Speed Channel.

Nakae Chōmin (中江 兆民, December 8, 1847 – December 13, 1901) was the pen-name of a journalist, political theorist and statesman in Meiji-period Japan. His real name was Nakae Tokusuke (中江 篤助). His major contribution was the popularization of the egalitarian doctrines of the French philosopher Jean-Jacques Rousseau in Japan. As a result, Nakae is thought to have been a major force in the development of liberalism in early Japanese politics.

Klinga Church (Lang-no) is a parish church of the Church of Norway in Namsos municipality in Trøndelag county, Norway. It is located in the village of Klinga. It is the church for the Klinga parish which is part of the Namdal prosti (deanery) in the Diocese of Nidaros. The white, wooden church was built in a long church style in 1866 using plans drawn up by the architects Ernst Kulaas and Jacob Wilhelm Nordan. The church seats about 270 people.

WUFO is an AM radio station broadcasting at 1080 kHz in Amherst, New York, serving the Buffalo metropolitan area. Owned by Sheila L. Brown's Visions Multi Media Group, it airs an Urban AC and Classic Hip Hop format, branded as "Power 96.5". The station's programming is simulcast on FM translator W243DX at 96.5 MHz.

WUFO's history traces back to 1927 with the founding of WKEN. In 1961, the station adopted the WUFO call sign and became the first radio station programmed for Buffalo's African American community. Many prominent African American radio personalities have worked at WUFO. In 2013, Sheila Brown became the first African American woman to own a radio station in Western New York.

Barley yellow mosaic virus is plant pathogenic virus that causes the yellow mosaic disease of barley. Its shape is categorized as being flexuous filamentous, with lengths of 275 and 550 nanometers. The virus has a limited host range, and barley appears to be the only known susceptible host. The virus is transmitted via Polymyxa graminis, which is a plasmodiophorid protist, through the resting spores that survive in the soil, and eventually zoospores. Eastern Asia is the most affected region, but the virus can be found worldwide. Current agricultural practices have been ineffective at eliminating the virus, but breeding resistance appears to be the only way to help reduce the disease.

Otto Eisenschiml (1880–1963) was an Austrian-American industrial chemist and author. He is known for his 1937 book, *Why Was Lincoln Murdered?*, which proposed that Secretary of War Edwin Stanton orchestrated Abraham Lincoln's assassination. Eisenschiml's theory, though controversial and largely discredited, has been referenced in popular culture.
Franziska Junge (born 1981) is a German actor and singer. She has performed in film, television, and theatre since 2001.

The 2009 FIA GT3 European Championship season was the fourth season of the FIA GT3 European Championship. The season began on 2 May at Silverstone and ended on 14 November 2009 at Zolder. The season featured six double-header rounds, with each race lasting for a duration of 60 minutes. Most of the events were support races to the 2009 FIA GT Championship season, the 2009 GT4 European Cup and the 2009 Lamborghini Super Trofeo.

Phoenix Racing pairing Christopher Haase and Christopher Mies claimed the overall title, with a fourth place in the final race of the season at Zolder. In the Teams Championship, Hexis Racing were champions ahead of Matech Racing and Team Rosberg.
*In Search of Sunrise* is a series of trance music compilation albums that began in 1999, initially mixed by Tiësto, then Richard Durand, and later Markus Schulz.

USCGC Robert Yered (WPC-1104) is a Sentinel-class cutter based in Miami, Florida. She was launched on November 23, 2012, and was commissioned on February 15, 2012. Debbie Wasserman Schultz, the Congressional Representative for the district containing the vessel's base, met the ship when she arrived in Miami on January 27, 2013.

The kidnapping of Alexandra Măceșanu and Luiza Melencu happened in Caracal, Olt County, Romania on 25 July 2019. Romanian police were heavily criticized for taking 19 hours to locate and search the building where she was held captive. The handling of the case met widespread outrage and protests, which led to the dismissal of Interior Minister Nicolae Moga and other officials.

Stonestown Galleria station is a light rail station on the M Ocean View line of the San Francisco Municipal Railway (Muni). It is located on 19th Avenue at Winston Drive, adjacent to the Stonestown Galleria shopping mall. The station originally opened in 1925 and was rebuilt in 1993 with a high-level island platform.
*The Bottle & Fresh Horses* is the second and final studio album by The Refreshments, released September 16, 1997. It was produced by Paul Leary.

CubETH is a Swiss satellite project. As of 2015, it is being developed by several Swiss academic and industrial partners. The spacecraft is a single unit CubeSat and therefore 10 x 10 x 10 cm (3.9 x 3.9 x 3.9 in) in size. Its main goal is to prove the feasibility of attitude and orbit determination with low cost commercial off-the-shelf GNSS-receivers.

Marcus Bontempelli (born 24 November 1995) is a professional Australian rules footballer playing for the Western Bulldogs in the Australian Football League (AFL). He has served as Western Bulldogs captain since the 2020 season, and was previously the vice-captain from 2018 to 2019.

Bontempelli was drafted with the fourth selection in the 2013 AFL draft. He made his AFL debut in the 2014 AFL season. He received a nomination for the 2014 AFL Rising Star award in round 13 of the 2014 season, placing second in the overall count at the conclusion of the season. Since then, he has been an AFL Premiership player, three-time All-Australian, three-time Charles Sutton Medallist, and a recipient of the AFLCA Champion Player of the Year Award. He has polled a total of 97 career Brownlow Medal votes.
Apollonius Dyscolus (Lang-el; reached his maturity sometime around 130 CE) is considered one of the greatest of the Greek grammarians.

Gwrgi and Peredur were 6th-century brothers and warriors in the Hen Ogledd (now Northern England and southern Scotland). They are best known for fighting in the Battle of Arfderydd in 573. Later, Geoffrey of Monmouth wrote of a Peredur in both his *Vita Merlini* and *Historia Regum Britanniae*. A Peredur is also the hero of the Welsh romance *Peredur son of Efrawg*.

Jerry Collins (4 November 1980 – 5 June 2015) was a Samoan – New Zealand rugby union player. He played for New Zealand, for whom he was capped 48 times, as well as for the Wellington Hurricanes in New Zealand, Toulon and Narbonne in the Rugby Pro D2, Ospreys in the UK, and Yamaha Júbilo in Japan. He played as a flanker and number eight, and was considered to be one of the hardest tacklers in rugby history, along with Brian Lima.

Collins and his partner, Alana Madill, were killed in a car accident in southern France in June 2015.

Yana Peel (born June 1974) is a Canadian executive, businesswoman, children's author and philanthropist who is currently global head of arts and culture at Chanel. She was CEO of the Serpentine Galleries from 2016 to 2019, and was previously a board member.

Peel is a co-founder of the Outset Contemporary Art Fund (with Candida Gertler), and Intelligence Squared Asia, and was CEO of Intelligence Squared Group from 2013 to 2016.

Peel has several advisory positions including the Tate International Council, V-A-C Foundation, and the NSPCC therapeutic board. She has been an advisor to the British Fashion Council, Asia Art Archive, Lincoln Center, Para Site and the Victoria and Albert Museum, where she founded the design fund.

*Faerie Tale Theatre* is an American anthology fairytale fantasy television series created and hosted by Shelley Duvall, which originally aired on Showtime from 1982 to 1987. Each of the 27 episodes is an adaptation of a different classic fairy tale, featuring well-known Hollywood actors in costume. Duvall also served as executive producer, occasional star, narrator, and voice talent for the series.

The series was one of the first examples of original cable programming. Episodes were directed by filmmakers such as Francis Ford Coppola and Tim Burton. *Faerie Tale Theatre* was followed by three other less successful anthologies also produced by Duvall: *Tall Tales & Legends*, *Nightmare Classics*, and *Bedtime Stories*. The series has been released on VHS, Laserdisc and DVD. *Faerie Tale Theatre* won a Peabody Award, a TCA Award, and a Golden CableACE Award.
Child Mortality in Ghana describes the child mortality in the country of Ghana. Like in other parts of the world, child mortality is declining in Ghana.
Andrey Viktorovich Zayakin (Lang-ru; born 23 February 1981) is a Russian physicist, political activist and journalist. One of the founders of the volunteer community network Dissernet.
San Miguel High School is a private, coeducational Catholic high school in Tucson, Arizona, established in 2004. It is part of the Cristo Rey Network, offering a work-study program for students.

Vagif Mustafazadeh (16 March 1940 – 16 December 1979) was an Azerbaijani jazz pianist and composer. Mustafazadeh was a pioneer of jazz mugham, a fusion of jazz and traditional Azerbaijani music. He was named Honored Artist of Azerbaijan SSR and was posthumously awarded the Azerbaijan State Prize.
Ronald J. Horvath (born 1937) has made contributions to the study of colonialism, African development, and urban geography of Sydney, Los Angeles, Detroit and Addis Ababa.

In the United Kingdom and the Isle of Man, the term "institute" is a protected word with specific legal restrictions on its use. Only organizations engaged in high-level research or professional bodies of the highest standing are permitted to use the term. Companies operating under a business name different from their registered company name must also adhere to the Business Names Act. Using the title "institute" necessitates approval from the Secretary of State. Failure to obtain this approval constitutes a criminal offense.

Oxalis latifolia is a species of flowering plant in the woodsorrel family known by the common names garden pink-sorrel and broadleaf woodsorrel. It is native to Mexico and parts of Central and South America.
Samuel Lord (1803-1889) was a British-born American retail millionaire who founded Lord & Taylor. It was the oldest luxury department store in the United States.
Borough Green & Wrotham is a railway station in Borough Green, Kent, England, on the Maidstone Line. Opened as Wrotham in 1874, it was renamed in 1962. The station is managed by Southeastern.

Nestor Labastilla Principe, also called "Ka Wadi" (March 15, 1945 - May 7, 1973) was a Filipino activist, writer, and martial arts instructor best known for his student activism at the Lyceum of the Philippines, his community mobilization work among the poor communities of Manila's port ara, and for his martyrdom on May 7, 1973, when he was killed and beheaded by elements of the Philippine Constabulary during Ferdinand Marcos' Martial Law regime.

On November 30, 2018, Principe's name was engraved on the Wall of Remembrance of the Bantayog ng mga Bayani, which honors the martyrs and heroes that fought against the Martial Law regime of Ferdinand Marcos.

Give Notice was a British-bred bay gelding foaled in 1997. Trained by John Dunlop, he was owned by Ian Stewart-Brown and Michael Meacock. Give Notice is notable for winning the Stubbs Stakes in 2001, the Sagaro Stakes in 2002, and the Prix du Cadran in 2002. He was sired by Warning and his dam was Princess Genista.

Dan Beach Bradley (1804–1873) was an American medical missionary to Siam (modern Thailand). Active from 1835 to his death, he is known for introducing smallpox vaccination to Siam. Bradley also brought the first printing press to Siam, and started the first newspaper in the country, *The Bangkok Recorder*.

Prince Global Sports, LLC is an American sporting goods manufacturing company based in Atlanta, Georgia. Founded in 1970, Prince's range of products includes rackets, footwear, apparel, tennis balls, pickleball paddles, stringing machines, hats and bags.

Prince is the leading global manufacturer of tennis rackets.

Euprymna tasmanica, also known as the southern dumpling squid or southern bobtail squid, is a bobtail squid that lives in the shallow (0.5 m to at least 80 m) temperate coastal waters of southern Australia's continental shelf. It lives for between 5 and 8 months and the adults can grow up to 6 or 7 cm long with a mantle length of 3 to 4 cm. They are found in seagrass beds or areas with soft silty or muddy bottoms from Brisbane on the east coast to Shark Bay on the west, as well as around Tasmania. Southern dumpling squid are nocturnal and during the day hide in sand or mud covered in a mucus-lined coat of sediment. If disturbed acid glans can quickly remove this coat as an additional decoy to ink squirting.

The 2015–16 Pittsburgh Penguins season was the 49th season for the National Hockey League franchise that was established on June 5, 1967. Their regular season games began on October 8, 2015 against the Dallas Stars. On December 12, the team had a record of 15–10–3. The organization then fired head coach Mike Johnston and replaced him with Mike Sullivan, head coach of the organization's American Hockey League affiliate in Wilkes-Barre.

The Penguins qualified for the playoffs for the tenth consecutive season. They earned second place in the Metropolitan Division with 104 points. They began the 2016 Stanley Cup playoffs on April 13 against the New York Rangers, the team they were eliminated by in the Stanley Cup playoffs both of the previous two seasons. They went on to beat the Rangers in five games. In the second round the Pens defeated the Washington Capitals, the winners of the Presidents'Trophy, in six games. The team then played in their first Eastern Conference Final since 2013 against the Tampa Bay Lightning. The Pens defeated the Lightning in seven games, despite trailing the series 3–2 after Game 5, to earn the franchise's fifth berth in the Stanley Cup Final. In the Final, the Penguins defeated the San Jose Sharks in six games to win the franchise's fourth league title.

KNBN, virtual channel 21 (UHF digital channel 21), is an NBC-affiliated television station licensed to Rapid City, South Dakota, United States. It also carries MyNetworkTV on its second subchannel. The station is owned by Jim Simpson of Rapid Broadcasting Company. KNBN first went on the air as a low-power station on July 15, 1996, before launching its full-power signal on May 14, 2000. KNBN's branding is NewsCenter 1.

*None but One* is a 1977 folk/folk rock album by Jean Ritchie, produced by Al Steckler and Ron Frangipane and released on Sire Records. It departs from Ritchie's traditional sound by incorporating electric instruments and more elaborate production. The album includes social commentaries and became Ritchie's most critically acclaimed and widely recognized work, receiving high praise from *Rolling Stone* and *Melody Maker*. Distribution problems led Ritchie to form her own record label.

*Spider-Man: India* is a Marvel Comics limited series that ran from November 2004 to February 2005.
