Anthony Minghella, CBE (6 January 1954 – 18 March 2008) was a British film director, playwright and screenwriter. He was chairman of the board of Governors at the British Film Institute between 2003 and 2007.

He won the Academy Award for Best Director for The English Patient (1996). In addition, he received three more Academy Award nominations; he was nominated for Best Adapted Screenplay for both The English Patient and The Talented Mr. Ripley (1999), and was posthumously nominated for Best Picture for The Reader (2008), as a producer.

The 2011–12 Kategoria Superiore was the 73rd official season, or 76th season of top-tier football in Albania (including three unofficial championships of WW2) and the fourteenth season under the name Kategoria superiore. The season began on 10 September 2011 and ended on 12 May 2012. The defending champions were Skënderbeu, who won their second Albanian league championship last season and third in their history.

The size of the league was expanded from twelve to fourteen teams this season.

The year 1945 saw the selection of All-America teams in college football. The NCAA recognizes 10 published All-American teams as "official" designations for consensus determinations.

During the 1940s, African-American players were often excluded from major college football programs and played at Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCUs). While major All-America selectors did not include players from HBCUs, *The Pittsburgh Courier* selected its own All-America team from players at those institutions.

Henry "Hank" Foldberg (March 12, 1923 – March 7, 2001) was an American football player and coach. He played college football at Texas A&M and Army, earning All-American honors in 1946. Foldberg played professionally for the Brooklyn Dodgers and Chicago Hornets. He then coached at Purdue, Texas A&M, and Florida before becoming the head coach at Wichita State from 1960 to 1961, winning two conference titles. He served as head coach and athletic director at Texas A&M from 1962 to 1965.
Muthoot Microfin is an Indian microfinance institution and a subsidiary of Muthoot Pappachan Group. It was established in 2012 and granted NBFC-MFI status by the Reserve Bank of India in 2015.
Extracts from Music for White Cube, London 1997 is the seventeenth solo studio album from British musician Brian Eno, released in 1997.

Muthoot Microfin Limited, the microfinance arm of Muthoot Pappachan Group, is an Indian microfinance institution focused on providing microloans to women entrepreneurs with a focus on rural regions of India.

The Virginia Convention of 1864 was an assembly of sixteen loyal Unionists during the American Civil War meeting under the auspices of Virginia's Restored Government. It abolished slavery in the state of Virginia, and framed the fundamental civil law that served Virginia government for six years through Appomattox, Presidential Reconstruction and Congressional Reconstruction.

"Winter" is a song by rock band U2. The track was originally planned to be included on the band's 2009 album No Line on the Horizon, but it was cut at the end of the recording sessions as it did not fit the album's theme. The song does appear on the accompanying film Linear. "Winter" was written for the 2009 war film Brothers at the request of director Jim Sheridan, and it plays over the closing credits. Several different versions of the song have been recorded, including an uptempo rock arrangement on Linear and a slower acoustic version in Brothers. The song was nominated for the Golden Globe Award for Best Original Song.

The 1910 Deperdussin monoplane, is a general term for a variety of Deperdussin aircraft models built between 1910 and 1911. Initially released in August 1910, it was the first aircraft to be built in significant quantities by Aéroplanes Deperdussin. Many variations of the aircraft were produced, differing in size, engine type and horsepower and tail configuration. These were loosely labelled as the "Deperdussin monoplane", but were sometimes more specifically classified either by function or by seating-type. A number of Deperdussin monoplanes were flown successfully in air races and gained several speed and endurance records during 1911–1912. Several have survived, including an airworthy example in the Shuttleworth Collection in England.

Lieth Peter Von Stein (March 16, 1946 – July 25, 1988) was an American businessman whose murder launched a high-profile trial in North Carolina. The case became the subject of two books and two television films.

An executive at National Spinning, a textile factory in Washington, North Carolina, Von Stein was stabbed and bludgeoned to death in his home as the result of a plot devised by his stepson, Christopher Wayne Pritchard. Von Stein's wife, Bonnie (Pritchard's mother), was also attacked, but survived. Pritchard's friend James Bartlett Upchurch III was convicted of first-degree murder, while Pritchard and another co-conspirator, Gerald Neal Henderson, were convicted of aiding and abetting the murder. The trial revealed that Pritchard's motive was his desire to inherit his stepfather's fortune, derived from his family's dry cleaning chain, Camel City Dry Cleaners in Winston-Salem, North Carolina.

Following the termination of hostilities in World War II, the Allies were in control of the defeated Axis countries. Anticipating the defeat of Germany and Japan, they had already set up the European Advisory Commission and a proposed Far Eastern Advisory Commission to make recommendations for the post war period. Accordingly, they managed their control of the defeated countries through Allied Commissions, often referred to as Allied Control Commissions (ACC), consisting of representatives of the major Allies.

The City Gala is an annual black-tie gala and awards ceremony that takes place on the same evening as either the Grammy Awards or the Academy Awards. The event features keynote speakers, honorees, and musical performances. Past speakers and honorees include James Cameron, Richard Branson, Buzz Aldrin, John Travolta, Ashton Kutcher, and Matthew McConaughey. The event supports various philanthropic causes.
Hanna Yussuf Atik (born February 7, 1959) is a Lebanese politician and former commander in the Lebanese Forces.

Rajmohan's Wife (1864) is the debut novella of the Indian author Bankim Chandra Chatterjee. The English-language novella, claimed by many to be the very first Indian novella, was published by a lesser-known periodical named Indian Field edited by Kishori Chand Mitra. The novella abruptly ended for unknown reasons, and later grew more obscure, until it was rediscovered and republished.

The Nieuport-Macchi Parasol was a single-engine observation aircraft produced by the Italian aeronautical company Nieuport-Macchi in the early 1910s. It was the first aircraft designed independently by Macchi, which until then had only produced aircraft under license. The Parasol operated in front-line service in 1915 during the early stages of Italy's participation in World War I.

Lis Shoshi (born June 29, 1994) is a Kosovan professional basketball player. He played college basketball for Howard College and Little Rock. Shoshi has played professionally in Kosovo, Lithuania, Israel and Germany. He won the Baltic League Championship in 2018. Shoshi is also a member of the Kosovo national basketball team.

Muhammad Said al-Attar (Lang-ar) (26 November 1927 – 20 November 2005) was the acting Prime Minister of Yemen for five months in 1994. During his career, he held various ministerial positions in Yemen and represented his country at the United Nations. From 1974 to 1985, Muhammad Said Al-Attar was Executive Secretary of the United Nations Economic Commission for Western Asia (currently ESCWA).

The Black Country derby is the local derby between English football teams West Bromwich Albion and Wolverhampton Wanderers, eleven miles (18 km) apart in the Black Country of the West Midlands.

The most successful side in the Black Country derby is West Bromwich Albion, losing just three of the past 23 matches between the two sides. Wolves are currently in the Premier League, and West Brom in the EFL Championship following their relegation in 2021.

The Lipetsk fighter-pilot school (Lang-de), also known as WIWUPAL from its German codename Wissenschaftliche Versuchs- und Personalausbildungsstation "Scientific Experimental and Personnel Training Station", was a secret training school for fighter pilots operated by the German Reichswehr at Lipetsk, Soviet Union, because Germany was prohibited by the Treaty of Versailles from operating an air force and sought alternative means to continue training and development for the future Luftwaffe. It is now the site of Lipetsk Air Base.

The Young Plan was a constitutional reform proposal carried out in 1946 attempting to introduce representative democracy in Colonial Hong Kong. Named after the then Governor, Mark Young, it was the first major reform proposal to give Hong Kong inhabitants a greater share of managing their own affairs by widening the base of Hong Kong's political system through the creation of a new Municipal Council. The proposed Council was to consist of an elected majority based on a fairly wide franchise, with powers and autonomy over all urban services, education, social welfare, town planning and other functions. It even allowed for indirect election of two Unofficial Members of the Legislative Council (LegCo) by the new Council.

Unofficial Members of the Legislative Council were opposed to the transfer of power to the new body and Young's successor, Governor Alexander Grantham who was opposed to the Young Plan, did not press this issue. Discussion dragged on but the continued opposition of Unofficials in LegCo in addition to the fear of the potential of penetration by Communist China finally killed the plan in 1952. This was the last move towards any form of major electorally-based government until in the 1980s during the Sino-British negotiations on Hong Kong's sovereignty.

A phantom border is a former political border that continues to structure the present-day world, with historical spaces persisting or re-emerging. The concept, stemming from the Phantomgrenzen project, highlights how past borders, understood as social constructs, leave lasting marks on societies.

Examples of phantom borders can be seen in Germany, where historical divisions between Prussia/East Germany and the rest of Germany influence modern political preferences. In Poland, the borders of historical partitions between the German, Habsburg, and Russian empires have led to political divisions between "liberal Poland" in the west and "solidarity Poland" in the center and south. In Romania, the historical border between Habsburg Transylvania and Ottoman Wallachia/Moldavia influences political attitudes and ethnic diversity. In Ukraine, historical divisions between Russia, the Habsburg Empire, and other states have led to regional political differences.

Martina Carraro (born 21 June 1993) is an Italian swimmer. She competed at the 2020 Summer Olympics, in 100 m breaststroke and 200 m breaststroke. She was European champion in short course in the 100 m breaststroke at the 2019 European Short Course Swimming Championships in Glasgow, Scotland.
Stevie Anderson (born May 12, 1970) is a former American football wide receiver who played in the NFL from 1993 to 1997.
Hastings is a city in Mills County, Iowa, United States. The population was 152 at the time of the 2020 census.

¡Que viva la lucha! is a 2007 film directed and produced by Gustavo Vazquez about Lucha libre in Tijuana, Mexico, considered to be a form of extreme lucha libre. The film follows Extreme Tiger, an up-and-coming professional luchador from Tijuana, on a journey that places him in a mask vs. hair match against Joe Líder. In addition, he follows other new talent, Angel Negro Jr. and Pancho Cachondo. In the process, the film also interviews other luchadores, promoters, commissioners, families, and fans who discuss the cultural significance of lucha libre as sport, ritual, and spectacle. The film includes music by Carne Cruda, Marziano, White Pine, and Nortec Collective. ¡Que viva la lucha! premiered at the 30th annual Mill Valley Film Festival on Saturday, Oct. 13, 2007, and has won two awards; at the Latino Film Festival and at the San Francisco International Film Festival.

Ruslan Sayd-Khusseinovich Edelgeriev (born December 4, 1974) is a Russian politician. He served in the Russian Armed Forces and Interior Ministry before entering Chechen politics in 2007. He was the Minister of Agriculture of the Chechen Republic from 2008 to 2012, before becoming Prime Minister of the Chechen Republic on May 24, 2012. Since June 22, 2018, he has been Adviser to the President of the Russian Federation on climate change issues and Special Representative of the President of the Russian Federation on climate issues since July 18, 2018.

Rabbi Menachem Mendel Monsohn (Hebrew: מנחם מענדל מאנזאהן‎; October 13, 1895 – September 3, 1953) was a member of the Monsohn family of Jerusalem, born in the Old City of Jerusalem. He was a great-grandson of Abraham-Leib Monsohn, one of the founders of the Ashkenazi Old Yishuv of Jerusalem in the early nineteenth century, and a son of Abraham-Leib Monsohn II, a founder of the A.L. Monsohn Lithography in Jerusalem. After marrying, Monsohn lived with his family in the Batei Broide section of Nachlaot, Jerusalem, which provided housing for rabbis and their families. In 1924 Monsohn immigrated to the United States with a group of rabbis from Eretz Israel, settling in Brooklyn, New York, where he served as rabbi of Congregation Ezrath Israel on Gates Avenue, in the Bedford-Stuyvesant section, until his death in 1953. His book, Mi-Peninei Ha-Rambam: Bi’ur ‘al ha-Torah, a compendium of Maimonides’ commentaries on the Pentateuch, arranged by the compiler in order of the Torah chapters, first appeared in Brooklyn c.1925 and was reprinted there several times in the early 1930s. In 2006 it was re-released by Mossad Harav Kook of Jerusalem, which also published an English translation, Pearls of the Rambam (tr. Avraham Berkovits) c. 2008. Some of the early editions included a Yiddish introduction to the life of Maimonides.
Matthew Kantakouzenos (c. 1325 – 1383/1391) was a Byzantine emperor from 1353 to 1357, as well as Despot of the Morea from 1380 until he resigned the position to his son in 1383.

Hatton Castle stands on the lower part of Hatton Hill, the most easterly of the Sidlaw Hills, to the south of Newtyle in Angus, Scotland. The castle overlooks the wooded Den of Newtyle, and its views extend across Strathmore and include Ben Lawers and Schiehallion as well as the Angus and Glenshee hills. The 16th-century castle was originally built in a typical Scottish "Z plan" tower house design, as a fortified country house or château. There was an earlier castle called Balcraig Castle which stood less than half a mile from the present building, also on Hatton Hill.

Annie Buller (9 December 1895 – 19 January 1973), also known as Annie Buller-Guralnick, was a union organizer as well as co-founder of the Communist Party of Canada (CPC) and manager of many CPC publications.

Henry Christian Foldberg Sr. (March 12, 1923 – March 7, 2001) was an American college and professional football player who became a college football coach. Foldberg played college football for Texas A&M University and the United States Military Academy, and thereafter, he played professionally for Brooklyn Dodgers and the Chicago Hornets of the All-America Football Conference (AAFC). He later served as the head football coach of Wichita State University and Texas A&M University.

Asia Aluminum is the largest aluminum extrusion group in Asia, with an annual designed capacity of 350,000 metric tons. It employed, directly and indirectly, in excess of 10,000 people, predominantly in the Asia Aluminum Industrial City which housed its primary manufacturing facilities, located in the Zhaoqing region of the People's Republic of China. Asia Aluminum made its debut on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange in 1998.
The women's 1500 metres event at the 2016 Summer Olympics took place between 12 and 16 August 2016 at the Olympic Stadium.
Come is an American alternative rock band, formed in Boston by Thalia Zedek (vocals, guitar), Chris Brokaw (guitar, vocals), Arthur Johnson (drums), and Sean O'Brien (bass).

Sea of Love is the third studio album by South Korean R&B duo Fly to the Sky. It was released via SM Entertainment on April 26, 2022. Commercially, the album peaked at number two on the monthly RIAK album chart and sold almost 250,000 copies by the end of the year.

Grimethorpe Hall is a manor house in Grimethorpe, South Yorkshire, England. Built circa 1670 for Robert Seaton (also spelled Seton), it is thought to be in the style of York architect Robert Trollope. Around 1800 the hall passed to John Farrar Crookes of Tunbridge Wells. It was last used as a house in the 1960s and afterwards was purchased by the National Coal Board. The National Coal Board applied to demolish it in 1981 but, after a campaign by the Ancient Monuments Society, this was unsuccessful. The structure received statutory protection as a grade II* listed building in 1985.

Proposals by later owners to convert the hall into a nursing home, restaurant or social housing came to naught and the hall fell into the ownership of the Crown Estate in 2016 after the company owning it was dissolved. Concerns have since been raised over the structure and a local campaign has been launched in an attempt to restore it.

Parasol (1800–1826) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse. In total she won twenty of her thirty-five races, including two Newmarket First October King's Plates, the Jockey-Club Plate and a match race against Derby winner Cardinal Beaufort. Her only race away from Newmarket was for her début, in the Oaks Stakes in 1803. She was bred and owned by Augustus FitzRoy, 3rd Duke of Grafton. As a broodmare she foaled the stallion Partisan, 2000 Guineas winner Pindarrie and Pastille, who won both the 2000 Guineas and the Oaks.

"Charles" is a short story by Shirley Jackson, first published in *Mademoiselle* in 1948. The story, told from the perspective of a mother, follows a boy named Laurie as he begins kindergarten and tells stories about a classmate named Charles.
SLINEX (Sri Lanka India Naval Exercise) is a series of naval exercises conducted between the navies of India and Sri Lanka. Editions of the exercise took place in 2013, 2015, 2018 and 2020.

Circumstance (Lang-fa; Lang-fr) is a 2011 French-Iranian-American dramatic film written and directed by Maryam Keshavarz starring Nikohl Boosheri, Sarah Kazemy, Reza Sixo Safai and Keon Alexander. It explores homosexuality in modern Iran, among other subjects.
Hasan Zati Sungur (March 10, 1898 – July 6, 1984) was an internationally recognized Turkish stage magician.
Feliksas Vaitkus (1907–1956) was a Lithuanian American pilot who in 1935 flew the Lituanica II across the Atlantic Ocean.
The 1994–95 Atlante F.C. season was the 4th season since the team's last promotion to Primera División. Atlante competed in Primera División and Copa México

Baltoceratidae is an extinct family of orthoconic cephalopods belonging to the subclass Nautiloidea endemic to what would be Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America during the Ordovician living from about 480–460 mya, existing for approximately 20 million years.

*Money Jungle* is a 1963 studio album by Duke Ellington, accompanied by Charles Mingus and Max Roach. It was recorded on September 17, 1962, in New York City and released in February 1963 on United Artists. The album, produced by Alan Douglas, is considered a post-bop classic.

The recording session brought together three generations of jazz musicians, with Ellington as a guiding figure for Mingus and Roach. The album features seven tracks on the original LP, six composed by Ellington and one by Juan Tizol. CD releases include additional compositions and alternative takes. Despite rumors of tension during the session, *Money Jungle* received favorable reviews and has inspired numerous musicians. Critics have praised Ellington's playing and the modernity of his ideas, while also noting the album's sound quality.
In Search of Sunrise (abbreviated ISOS) is a trance music mix-compilation music CD series released on SongBird, a subdivision of Black Hole Recordings originally founded by Tiësto.

Trochilinae is one of the six subfamilies that make up the hummingbird family Trochilidae.

The subfamily is divided into three tribes: Lampornithini (mountain gems) containing 18 species, Mellisugini (bees) containing 37 species and Trochilini (emeralds) containing 115 species.
Alexandra Pringle, Hon. FRSL (born 1952/53), is a British publisher. She was a founding director of Virago Press and the editor-in-chief of Bloomsbury Publishing for 18 years as of March 2018.
The Dartmouth Jack-O-Lantern (also known as the Jacko) is a college humor magazine, founded at Dartmouth College in 1908.

Ruaraka is an industrial and residential suburb in the city of Nairobi. Located within the larger sub-county of Kasarani, it is approximately 8 kilometres (5.0 mi) northeast of Nairobi's central business district off Thika Road.

Old Street is a 1-mile (1.6 km) street in inner north-east Central London that runs west to east from Goswell Road in Clerkenwell, in the London Borough of Islington, via St Luke's and Old Street Roundabout, to the crossroads where it meets Shoreditch High Street (south), Kingsland Road (north) and Hackney Road (east) in Shoreditch in the London Borough of Hackney.

It has London Underground station Old Street on the Northern line which has other platforms for National Rail's Northern City Line.

Its west half is inside London's Congestion Charging Zone; a short part of its eastern half is the inner ring marking its limit.

Georgy Vasilyevich Pigulevsky (1888–1964) was a Russian chemist and biochemist. He studied terpenes, essential oils, and vegetable fats, and contributed to the production of medicines during the siege of Leningrad. He was a professor at Leningrad State University.
Gilad Efrat, born in Beersheba in 1969, is an Israeli painter and professor known for his works that explore landscape representation and the language of painting.

*Systems of Survival: A Dialogue on the Moral Foundations of Commerce and Politics* is a 1992 non-fiction book by Jane Jacobs. Written as a Platonic dialogue, it explores two distinct "ethical syndromes": the Guardian Moral Syndrome and the Commercial Moral Syndrome. Jacobs argues that conflicts arise when precepts from one syndrome are inappropriately applied to the other, leading to "monstrous moral hybrids."

Ports North, officially the Far North Queensland Ports Corporation Limited, is a statutory corporation owned by the Queensland Government in Australia. Headquartered in Cairns, it superseded the Cairns Port Authority and is responsible for managing ports within the Far North Queensland region.

The Cairns Seaport, located on Trinity Inlet, is a key component of Ports North's operations. It handles both cargo and passenger vessels, playing a vital role in the region's tourism and supply chains, particularly for communities in Cape York Peninsula, Torres Strait, and the Gulf of Carpentaria. The Cairns Marlin Marina, with its numerous berths, caters to private and commercial vessels, supporting tourism to the Great Barrier Reef. Cityport is an urban renewal project adjacent to the seaport.

WNSN (101.5 MHz) is a commercial FM radio station in South Bend, Indiana. It is owned by Mid-West Family Broadcasting and airs an adult contemporary radio format known as Sunny 101.5. WNSN is usually the market's top rated radio station according to the Nielsen ratings. Sunny 101.5 carries Delilah's syndicated love songs show in the evening from Premiere Networks. Most weekends it features 1980s hits. From mid-November to Dec. 26, WNSN switches to all Christmas music.

The studios and offices are on East Douglas Road in Mishawaka. The transmitter is off Ironwood Road, south of downtown South Bend. It is co-located with sister station 960 WSBT's tower.

East Rennell, located on Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands, is the world's largest raised coral atoll. Designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1998, it encompasses Lake Tegano, surrounding land, and adjacent marine areas. The site is a natural laboratory for scientific study. In 2013, it was added to the List of World Heritage in Danger due to logging threats to its bird population.

Publius Sulpicius Quirinius (c. 51 BC – AD 21), also translated as Cyrenius, was a Roman aristocrat. After the banishment of the ethnarch Herod Archelaus from the tetrarchy of Judea in AD 6, Quirinius was appointed legate governor of Syria, to which the province of Judaea had been added for the purpose of a census.

The 68th Siege Battery, Royal Garrison Artillery (RGA) was a British Army unit formed in October 1915 during World War I. It served on the Western Front, including the Somme, Passchendaele, and the Spring Offensive. The battery was disbanded in 1919.
Livia Orestilla was a Roman noblewoman who was briefly empress as the wife of Emperor Caligula in 37 or 38 AD, after he forced her to divorce her husband.
*Odostomia farallonensis* is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusk in the family Pyramidellidae. It is found off the Farallon Islands.

Nomads R.F.C. are a Hong Kong rugby union club, who play in G4S League Divisions 2,5 and 7 and are based in the Tai Hang Tung area in New Kowloon. Between 2008/09 the club has won over 6 different league and 7's trophies.

Pottawattamie Park is a town in Michigan Township, LaPorte County, Indiana, United States. The population was 235 at the 2010 census. It is included in the Michigan City, Indiana-La Porte, Indiana Metropolitan Statistical Area.

Leslie Quirk RBV (30 September 1914 – 20 October 2004), also known as Y Kione Jiarg (IPA-gv, "the red head") was a Manx language activist and teacher who was involved with the language's revival on the Isle of Man in the 20th century. His work recording the last native speakers of the language with the Irish Folklore Commission and the Manx Museum helped to ensure that a spoken record of the Manx language survived.

Dale Van Sickel (1907–1977) was an American college football player and stuntman. A graduate of the University of Florida, he was the first Gator to be named a first-team All-American in 1928. Van Sickel was inducted into the College Football Hall of Fame in 1975. After coaching, he moved to Hollywood and became a stuntman, appearing in over 280 films.

Kooralbyn International School is a co-educational, non-denominational school in Kooralbyn, Queensland, Australia. Established in 1985, it closed in 2002 due to funding issues, but reopened in 2004. In 2017, the school had an enrollment of 285 students and a staff of 34 teachers.

Marcus Bontempelli (born 24 November 1995) is an Australian rules footballer playing for the Western Bulldogs in the Australian Football League (AFL). Drafted in 2013, he made his debut in 2014 and quickly became a key player, earning a Rising Star nomination in his first season.

Bontempelli has achieved significant accolades, including the 2016 AFL premiership, the Leigh Matthews Trophy in 2021, and four All-Australian selections. He has also won the Charles Sutton Medal four times as the Western Bulldogs' best and fairest player.

Bontempelli was appointed captain of the Western Bulldogs in 2020. Off the field, he is known for his activism on social issues and involvement in charitable work.
Ikhtiyar al-Din Ai-Taq was an influential amir in western Khurasan following the decline of the Seljuks, and the ruler of Gurgan and Dihistan from 1161 until 1165.

A wireless intrusion prevention system (WIPS) prevents unauthorized wireless network access. It often overlays existing wireless LANs, detecting and preventing threats by analyzing wireless traffic against defined policies. WIPS can be implemented as a network or hosted solution.

Kate Walsh (née Shimmin; born in 1992) is an Australian netball player in the Suncorp Super Netball league, playing for the Sunshine Coast Lightning. In August 2019, Jess Thirlby the new England Roses Coach announced Shimmin would join the full time England Roses programme.

Shimmin grew up in South Australia and was a part of the South Australian Institute of Sport Volleyball program before being offered a SASI Netball scholarship which she chose over volleyball. When she finished High School she moved to Canberra where she developed her skills at the Australian Institute of Sport.

Richard Mellon Scaife (/skeɪf/; July 3, 1932 – July 4, 2014) was an American billionaire, a principal heir to the Mellon banking, oil, and aluminum fortune, and the owner and publisher of the Pittsburgh Tribune-Review. In 2005, Scaife was number 238 on the Forbes 400, with a personal fortune of $1.2 billion. By 2013, Scaife had dropped to number 371 on the listing, with a personal fortune of $1.4 billion.

During his life, Scaife was known for his financial support of conservative public policy organizations over the past four decades. He provided support for conservative and libertarian causes in the United States, mostly through the private, nonprofit foundations he controlled: the Sarah Scaife Foundation, Carthage Foundation, and Allegheny Foundation, and until 2001, the Scaife Family Foundation, now controlled by son David.

Harry Porter Deuel (December 11, 1836 – November 23, 1914) was a prominent railroad man in Omaha, Nebraska. Over the course of his career Deuel was employed throughout the transportation sector in Omaha, working for the foremost Missouri River shipping company in Omaha during its early years. Later he worked for the Union Pacific and the Chicago, Burlington and Quincy railroads, among others. He is the namesake of Deuel County, Nebraska. When he resigned from his last railroad job to become the Douglas County Registrar of Deeds, Deuel was the oldest railroad man in continuous service in Omaha, and was said to be one of the best known passenger men in the Western United States.

The Zhdanov Doctrine (also called Zhdanovism or Zhdanovshchina; Lang-ru) was a Soviet cultural doctrine developed by Central Committee secretary Andrei Zhdanov in 1946. It proposed that the world was divided into two camps: the "imperialistic", headed by the United States; and "democratic", headed by the Soviet Union. The main principle of the Zhdanov Doctrine was often summarized by the phrase "The only conflict that is possible in Soviet culture is the conflict between good and best". Zhdanovism soon became a Soviet cultural policy, meaning that Soviet artists, writers and intelligentsia in general had to conform to the party line in their creative works. Under this policy, artists who failed to comply with the government's wishes risked persecution. The policy remained in effect until the death of Joseph Stalin in 1953.

The SS *William G. Mather* is a Great Lakes freighter built in 1925. It served as the flagship for Cleveland-Cliffs until 1952 and remained active until 1980. It was the first American vessel with an automated boiler system. Since 1987, it has been a museum ship in Cleveland, Ohio, and is now part of the Great Lakes Science Center.

The Society for the Rise of Kurdistan (SAK) was a Kurdish organization that aimed to promote Kurdish culture, language, and political rights in the aftermath of World War I. Founded in 1918, it sought self-government for Kurds, advocating for their rights at the Paris Peace Conference. The SAK was led by prominent figures like Abdulkadir Ubeydullah and supported the Koçkiri rebellion in 1921. Internal divisions and Turkish suppression led to its dissolution and the execution of several leaders in 1925.
Cary Castle in Somerset, England, was a motte and bailey castle built in the 12th century, of which only earthworks remain.

In computational geometry, a bitonic tour of a set of point sites in the Euclidean plane is a closed polygonal chain that has each site as one of its vertices, such that any vertical line crosses the chain at most twice.

Horse pulling is a sport that evolved from farmers challenging each other to see whose horse or team could pull the most weight. It has since become an organized sport where horses are trained to build strength and prevent injuries.

Dynamometers, first conceived after World War I, are used to measure the pulling power of horse teams. Competitions involve teams pulling a dynamometer loaded with increasing weights. Teams that successfully pull the weight advance to the next round until only one team remains. Organizations like the Eastern Draft Horse Association enforce rules to ensure the safety of the horses during competitions.

*The Complete Beat* is a box set by the English ska revival band The English Beat, released on July 10, 2012, by Shout! Factory. It compiles recordings from 1979 to 1982, showcasing the band's blend of ska, 2 Tone, new wave, and dub influences.

The box set received critical acclaim, earning a score of 90/100 on Metacritic. Critics lauded the inclusion of extended mixes, dub versions, and live recordings, which highlight the band's adventurous spirit and energetic performances. Reviewers at AllMusic and Rolling Stone gave it a rating of 4.5 out of 5. Paste magazine gave the box set 9.1/10, concluding that the live tracks are the "most compelling". The set includes a booklet with a history of the band and many rarities receiving their first CD release.

Robert Mackenzie Beverley (born c. 1797) was a British writer known for his religious and political commentary. Originally a Quaker, he resigned during the Beaconite Controversy and joined the Plymouth Brethren. Beverley authored books, satires, and poems on religious and political themes. He was a staunch opponent of Darwin's theory of evolution, writing "The Darwinian Theory of the Transmutation of Species" in 1867. He died in Scarborough on November 3, 1868.

"The Crocodile" is the fourth episode of the second season of the American fantasy drama series *Once Upon a Time*, which aired on October 21, 2012. In the episode, Belle confronts Mr. Gold about his activities, while in the Enchanted Forest, Rumplestiltskin confronts Captain Hook, resulting in Hook becoming his nemesis. The episode was well-received by critics and garnered a 3.3/8 rating among adults 18-49, with 9.89 million viewers.

The Alliance française is an international organization that aims to promote French language and culture around the world. It was founded in Paris on July 21, 1883, by figures such as Louis Pasteur, Ferdinand de Lesseps, and Jules Verne. The organization is financed by course fees, rental income, and a subsidy from the French government.

The Alliance comprises a network of independently run local chapters. The Alliance française Paris Ile-de-France, located in Paris, is a private higher education institute and the oldest school in the network.

The Deperdussin 1910 monoplane was a successful early aircraft designed by Louis Béchereau and produced by Aéroplanes Deperdussin. First flown in October 1910, the aircraft was a mid-wing monoplane of tractor configuration, with wing warping for lateral control. The initial production model, the Type A, was powered by a 35 hp Anzani engine, while later variants used Gnome rotary engines. The Type B, introduced in 1911, featured an improved design and more powerful engines. Deperdussin monoplanes were used in racing and military roles, and the company operated flying schools in France and England.

The Stockade Building System was designed by Richard Buckminster Fuller and his father-in-law, James Monroe Hewlett, and was patented in 1927. Both of them had previously formed a company, in 1922, which made bricks out of compressed wood shavings with vertical holes cast in them.

The Bethlehem Atlantic Works was a shipyard in East Boston formed in 1928 by Bethlehem Steel's purchase of Atlantic Works and Simpson Dry Dock Company. The yard repaired naval vessels through World War II. Racial violence in the late 1970s and 1980s led to the Navy pulling out, and the yard closed in 1984.

John Zachary Young (1907–1997) was a British zoologist, anatomist, and physiologist. He is known for his research on the giant axon of the squid, which inspired Nobel Prize-winning work on signal transmission in nerves.

Spider-Man: India is a superhero comic book series originally published in India by Gotham Entertainment Group in 2004, retelling the story of Marvel Comics'Spider-Man in an Indian setting. It ran for four issues, which were later also published in the United States in 2005 and collected into a trade paperback (ISBN 0-7851-1640-0). The series was created by Sharad Devarajan, Suresh Seetharaman, and Jeevan J. Kang with Marvel Comics. The Indian version was conceptualized by Indian director Satyajit Ray, who had once met Stan Lee in New York to discuss creating an Indian version of the superhero. However, this was not done during Ray's lifetime.
The Museum of Russian Impressionism is an art museum in Moscow, Russia, founded by Boris Mints and opened in 2016.
Rodney Bullock (born June 3, 1994) is an American professional basketball player for KB Prishtina of the Kosovo Basketball Superleague. He played college basketball for the Providence Friars.

The second season of the American reality competition show Top Chef Masters was announced on October 22, 2009. In addition, it was announced that Gail Simmons would be added as a critic. The season premiered on April 7, 2010 with 22 chefs competing against each other in weekly challenges. In the season finale that premiered on June 9, 2010, Marcus Samuelsson was crowned Top Chef Master.

"Tight Connection to My Heart (Has Anybody Seen My Love)" is a song by Bob Dylan that was released on his 1985 album Empire Burlesque. As a single, it was a Top 40 Hit in New Zealand and Belgium. An earlier version of the song, entitled "Someone's Got a Hold of My Heart", was recorded for Dylan's 1983 LP Infidels, but was not included on that album; it later appeared on The Bootleg Series Volumes 1–3 (Rare & Unreleased) 1961–1991.
**Hasan Zati Sungur** (1898–1984) was a Turkish stage magician, known as "King of the Magicians".

Somalia has participated in thirteen Summer Olympic Games. The Somali Olympic Committee was founded in 1959, but it wasn't until shortly prior to the 1972 Summer Olympics that it was recognised by the International Olympic Committee (IOC), allowing it to compete.

Somalia debuted at the 1972 Games in Munich. They boycotted the 1976 and 1980 Games. No athlete representing Somalia has ever won a medal at the Olympics, nor have they taken part in the Winter Games. Due to the ongoing Somali Civil War, many athletes have left to represent other nations.

A mercy rule is a regulation in various sports that ends a game prematurely if one team has a lead that is deemed insurmountable. It is designed to prevent humiliation, reduce the risk of injury, and save time. The specific conditions for invoking a mercy rule vary widely depending on the sport, level of play, and governing body.

In American football, many states implement a "continuous clock" rule in middle and high school games when one team achieves a significant lead, typically in the second half. This keeps the clock running even when it would normally stop, such as after incomplete passes or out-of-bounds plays. College football allows for shortened quarters by mutual agreement of the coaches and referee, though a running clock is generally not permitted.

Other sports with mercy rules include association football (soccer), where a large goal difference can end a match, and baseball/softball, where a significant run differential after a certain number of innings triggers the rule. Basketball often employs a continuous clock rule similar to football. In amateur boxing, a significant point deficit can lead to a referee stopping the contest. Even sports like curling, goalball, and wrestling have variations of the mercy rule to end matches early under specific circumstances. Cricket is a notable exception, as teams aim to maximize their net run rate, but it does have the follow-on rule and declaration.

Shochiku Studio Co., Ltd. (株式会社松竹撮影所, Kabushiki gaisha Shōchiku Satsueijo) is a Japanese film and production studio company of Shochiku Group, which has been producing movies and dramas for roughly a century, being the second-oldest motion picture company in Japan.

The company has production bases in Kyoto and Tokyo since its inception in the 1920s, and the long-established Shochiku Kyoto Studio (松竹京都撮影所, Shōchiku Kyōto Satsueijo) is equipped with a studio facility that is deeply rooted in tradition.

It has also worked on the production of Hollywood films such as "The Last Samurai".

Shochiku Studio is known for productions of many Japanese period dramas, movies, TV and commercials.

Contour crafting is a construction automation technology that 3D prints large-scale structures. Behrokh Khoshnevis of the University of Southern California's Viterbi School of Engineering invented the technology. Contour crafting has the potential to revolutionize the construction industry by building structures faster and more efficiently, and with less waste.

NASA has explored using contour crafting to construct habitats on the Moon and Mars using in-situ resources. In 2017, the Contour Crafting Corporation announced a partnership with Doka Ventures, stating that they would begin delivering printers in early 2018.

Skimlinks is a content monetization platform for online publishers (including editorial sites, forums, bloggers, social networks, and app developers). It specializes in technology that automatically affiliates product links from publishers' commerce content.

The company was founded in London (2007) by Australian co-founders Alicia Navarro and Joe Stepniewski. It has over 62 employees, and has raised $24 million in funding as of August 2013. It has offices in London and New York City. On October 17, 2013, Skimlinks was announced as a member of Tech City UK's Future Fifty Programme, a government programme that supports fast-growing companies with the aim of potentially floating in the London Stock Exchange. In 2014, Skimlinks drove $625 million in sales for over 20,000 vendors.

*Kachche Heere* is a 1982 Indian Hindi-language action film directed by Narender Bedi. It stars Feroz Khan, Reena Roy, Danny Denzongpa, Aruna Irani, and Shakti Kapoor. The film revolves around a man named Ajnabi (Feroz Khan) who seeks revenge on those who wronged his family. Five petty thieves, hiding from the police, undergo a reformation after witnessing the plight of villagers under the thumb of dacoits. Together, Ajnabi and the five friends save the village from the dacoit Lukka Singh.

Great Wishford is a village and civil parish in Wiltshire, England. It lies in the Wylye Valley, about four miles north of Wilton and five miles northwest of Salisbury. The parish has a population of 368. The village is known for its annual celebration of Oak Apple Day, where villagers claim their ancient rights to collect wood from Grovely Wood.

The Michigan Dogman is a cryptid said to inhabit the state of Michigan. The first reported encounter occurred in 1887 in Wexford County, with other reports surfacing in the following decades.

The creature gained widespread attention in 1987 when disc jockey Steve Cook recorded a song titled "The Legend" as an April Fool's Day joke. Listeners who heard the song contacted Cook with their own encounter stories. Cook maintains skepticism about the dogman's existence. The dogman has been featured on television shows such as MonsterQuest and Monsters and Mysteries in America. In 2011, a film titled *Dogman* was released.

Victor Lenard Shapiro (16 October 1924, Chicago – 1 March 2013, Riverside, California) was an American mathematician, specializing in trigonometric series and differential equations. He is known for his two theorems (published in 1957) on the uniqueness of multiple Fourier series.

Adolph Ernst Knoch (December 19, 1874 – March 28, 1965) was the author of numerous theological writings and a Bible publisher.

Knoch founded the Concordant Publishing Concern and translated the Concordant Version of the Bible.

Donkeyboy (occasionally stylised as donkeyboy) is a synthpop band from Drammen, Norway, formed in 2005. The band consists of Cato Sundberg (vocals, rhythm guitar), Kent Sundberg (synth, vocals), Peter Michelsen (guitar, backing vocals) and Thomas Drabløs (drums)

*The Western Tapes, 1983* is an EP by the band Lone Justice, released on November 23, 2018, by Omnivore Recordings. The EP consists of recordings made in 1983, prior to the band signing with Geffen Records. Five of the six tracks were produced by Marvin Etzioni at the Record Plant in Los Angeles, while the sixth was produced by Ryan Hedgecock at Mystic Sound. Critics have noted the "raw exuberance" and "freshness" of the recordings, with some considering them superior to the band's later work. Three songs were re-recorded for their debut album *Lone Justice*.

The 2013 Stadium Super Trucks (SST) Series was the inaugural season of the Robby Gordon-organized stadium truck racing series. An exhibition race was held at Crandon International Off-Road Raceway in 2012 to test equipment. The season consisted of races held at stadiums and street circuits such as the Grand Prix of Long Beach and Honda Indy Toronto. Robby Gordon won the championship by seven points over Rob MacCachren.

*Hallelujah, Baby!* is a musical with music by Jule Styne, lyrics by Adolph Green and Betty Comden, and a book by Arthur Laurents, that premiered on Broadway in 1967. The story follows Georgina, an ambitious African American woman navigating racism and pursuing stardom from the Great Depression through the Civil Rights Movement. The musical won the Tony Award for Best Musical.
Brad Davis (born 30 April 1982) is a rugby league footballer who previously played for the Gold Coast Titans in the National Rugby League. He played as a half-back.

Ben James Herbert Burridge (11 March 1898 – 22 December 1977) was an English footballer who made 124 appearances in the Football League for Darlington, Sheffield Wednesday and Oldham Athletic in the 1920s and 1930s. He played as a half-back – at wing half in the early part of his career, and at centre half later on – and could also play as a forward.

Severn FM is an internet radio station broadcasting from Hardwicke, Gloucestershire, UK. Owned and operated by Ringtone.net Ltd since its inception in 2011, Severn FM offers a diverse range of programming, including news, sports coverage, interviews, talk shows, and music.

The station broadcasts 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, featuring programs such as "The Mystery Train," "Keepin it Country," and "The Severn Bore Breakfast Show." Severn FM also provides live match commentaries for Gloucester City AFC. In June 2014, the station began simulcasting "Extraños en la noche con Luis Cebrian" on 22 FM stations throughout Spain. Severn FM offers two online streams and a mobile portal.

Qin Shengxiang (born February 1957) is a retired Chinese admiral. He served as Director of the General Office of the Central Military Commission from 2012 to 2017 and Political Commissar of the People's Liberation Army Navy from 2017 to 2022.

The slender-billed gull (*Chroicocephalus genei*) is a species of gull in the family Laridae. It is found around the Mediterranean Sea and the northwestern Indian Ocean.

Measuring 37 to 40 cm in length, it is similar in appearance to the black-headed gull, but lacks the black hood in summer. It has a pale grey body, white head and breast, and a long, dark red bill. The species breeds in colonies, nesting on the ground near lagoons and lakes. Its diet consists mainly of fish, marine invertebrates, and insects. The IUCN lists it as Least Concern.

*Red Obsession* is a 2013 Australian documentary film co-directed and co-written by David Roach and Warwick Ross, narrated by Russell Crowe. The film received positive reviews, with praise for its cinematography and enlightening nature.

Bernese German phonology has several distinctive features. Vowel length is distinctive except for [ə], without interdependence of length and quality. Seven diphthong phonemes exist, with more phonetic diphthongs and triphthongs due to vocalized /l/ and combinations with [j]. Obstruents occur in fortis and lenis pairs, differing in length and possibly tenseness, not voicedness. Long /lː/ is pronounced [wː], and /l/ at the end of a syllable is [w].
Cuba–Suriname relations refers to the diplomatic, economic, and cultural ties between Cuba and Suriname. Cuba has provided medical support to Suriname. Trade between the two countries is negligible.

A mercy rule, slaughter rule, knockout rule, or skunk rule ends a two-competitor sports competition earlier than the scheduled endpoint if one competitor has a very large and presumably insurmountable scoring lead over the other. It is called the mercy rule because it spares further humiliation for the loser. It is common in youth sports in North America, where running up the score is considered unsporting. It is especially common in baseball and softball in which there is no game clock and a dominant team could in theory continue an inning endlessly.

The rules vary widely, depending on the level of competition, but nearly all youth sports leagues and high school sports associations and many college sports associations in the United States have mercy rules for sports including baseball, softball, American football and association football.

However, mercy rules usually do not take effect until a prescribed point in the game (like the second half of an association football game). Thus, one team, particularly if it is decidedly better than a weaker opponent, can still "run up the score" before the rule takes effect. For instance, in American football, one team could be ahead by 70 points with three minutes left in the first half; in baseball, the better team could have a 20-run lead in the second inning, but the game would still continue.
Kristine Jensen (born 1956), is a Danish architect who has specialized in landscape architecture.

Edith Gertrude Clements (1874–1971), also known as Edith S. Clements and Edith Schwartz Clements, was an American botanist and pioneer of botanical ecology who was the first woman to be awarded a Ph.D. by the University of Nebraska. She was married to botanist Frederic Clements, with whom she collaborated throughout her professional life. Together they founded the Alpine Laboratory, a research station at Pikes Peak, Colorado. Clements was also a botanical artist who illustrated her own books as well as joint publications with Frederic.

Both Clementses were involved with the study of phytogeography, especially those factors determining the ecology of vegetation in particular regions, and they would be praised as "the most illustrious husband-wife team since the Curies." It is impossible to entirely disentangle the work of each Clementses as they worked together during their noteworthy years.

Christian Malford is a village and civil parish in the county of Wiltshire, England. The village lies about 4+1⁄2 miles (7 km) northeast of the town of Chippenham. The Bristol Avon forms most of the northern and eastern boundaries of the parish. The hamlets of Thornend and Upper Town lie within the parish.

The unusual name is evidently a corruption of Christ mal Ford, Old English moel, mal being a mark: "Christ’s mal" is Christ's mark or sign, the cross. Thus the name signified "Cross Ford". Deeds from Glastonbury Abbey cartulary relate to Christmalford Manor: in AD 940 King Edmund granted Christmalford to St Dunstan, Abbot of Glastonbury. In 1086 the Abbey of Glastonbury remained tenant-in-chief of Cristemeleforee in the ancient Domesday hundred of Sterchelai (Startley). Christian Malford and the other parishes of Startley hundred were amalgamated with additional parishes to form the hundred of Malmesbury.

Hallelujah, Baby! is a musical with music by Jule Styne, lyrics by Adolph Green and Betty Comden, and a book by Arthur Laurents. The show is "a chronicle of the African American struggle for equality during the [first half of the] 20th century."

The musical premiered on Broadway in 1967 and made a young Leslie Uggams a star. It won the Tony Award for Best Musical.

Govind Prasad Kukreti (25 August 1932 – 31 August 2016), popularly known as Dabral Baba, was an Indian yogi and a disciple of Vikrant Bhairav and Mahavatar Babaji. He was also known as Baba and Shri Dabral Baba.

Dabral Baba hailed from Ujjain in the state of Madhya Pradesh. He adopted his surname (Dabral) after his maternal uncle's who virtually brought him up at Dhar and later to Ujjain. He was also unusual among Indian yogis who was a householder - he got married, raised a family, worked in Vikram University in Ujjain. Dabral Baba lived with his family at his home (Bhairav Niwas) located in Rishinagar in Ujjain. Dabral Baba attainted substantial enlightenment and earned popularity among highest ranks of yogis in 20th and 21st century period.

In the later part of his life, he was visited by many great saints both physically and spiritually primarily Mahavatar Babaji who took him to his gupha as well. Dabral Baba unveiled Vikrant Bhairav temple in Bhairavgarh, Madhya Pradesh the idol of Vikrant Bhairav was deep buried in mud for years, Dabral Baba was guided to unveil this temple and he later attained enlightenment with the guidance of his guru.

American Zombie is a 2007 American mockumentary horror film directed by Grace Lee, written by Rebecca Sonnenshine and Lee, and starring Lee and John Solomon as documentary filmmakers who investigate a fictional subculture of real-life zombies living in Los Angeles.

TV Sound and Image, British Television, Film and Library Composers 1956-80 is a 2012 compilation album released by Soul Jazz Records. The album consists of a style of music known as Library music, music developed by production houses with the intent that the recordings would be licensed to various film, television, and radio programs.

Swedish cuisine (Lang-sv) is the traditional food of Sweden. Due to Sweden's large north-to-south expanse, there are regional differences between the cuisine of North and South Sweden.

Historically, in the far north, meats such as reindeer, and other (semi-)game dishes were eaten, some of which have their roots in the Sami culture, while fresh vegetables have played a larger role in the South. Many traditional dishes employ simple, contrasting flavours, such as the traditional dish of meatballs and brown cream sauce with tart, pungent lingonberry jam (slightly similar in taste to cranberry sauce).

Swedes have traditionally been very open to foreign influences, ranging from French cuisine during the 17th and 18th centuries, to the sushi and caffé latte of today.

Holy Saviour Monastery of Julfa (Lang-hy) was an Armenian Apostolic monastery, located north-west of Jugha cemetery at the end of the gorge, 3 km away from the cemetery, on that slope of a high mountain. In the southern part of the monastery, at the foot of the Magharda mountain range, the Araks River flows.
Doon Valley is one of the nine wards used to elect members to the East Ayrshire Council. It elects three councillors.

Kalwaria Zebrzydowska is a town in Lesser Poland, known for its 17th-century monastery and the trails of the Passion of Christ. The town was established in 1617 to house pilgrims visiting the monastery, and its name comes from the founder, Mikołaj Zebrzydowski.
John Birkbeck (6 July 1817 – 31 July 1890) was a Yorkshireman, banker, alpinist, and pioneer potholer.

The men's horizontal bar competition at the 2016 Summer Olympics was held at the HSBC Arena on 6 and 16 August. There were 71 competitors from 34 nations. The event was won by Fabian Hambüchen of Germany, the nation's first victory in the horizontal bar since 1996 and third overall. Danell Leyva won the United States'third silver medal in four Games in the event; Nile Wilson's bronze was Great Britain's first-ever medal in the horizontal bar.

The medals were presented by Claudia Bokel IOC member, Germany and Naomi Valenzo, FIG Executive Committee Member.

Shikantaza (只管打坐) is a Japanese term, derived from the Chinese Zhǐguǎn dǎzuò, meaning "just sitting." It is a practice that involves sitting meditation (zazen). Some have traced the root of the word to the Japanese pronunciation of Sanskrit vipassana, though this is uncertain. The phrase "just sitting" was used by Dōgen's teacher Tiantong Rujing for silent illumination. Dōgen used shikantaza to mean doing zazen wholeheartedly. It is described as quiet sitting in open awareness.
Fatma Nur Serter (born 1948) is a Turkish politician, academic, and writer.

The IRT Third Avenue Line, also known as the Third Avenue Elevated, was an elevated railway line in New York City. Originally operated by the New York Elevated Railway Company, it later came under the control of the Interborough Rapid Transit Company (IRT). The line ran along Third Avenue in Manhattan and the Bronx, with its southern terminal at South Ferry and its northern terminals at Bronx Park Terminal and Gun Hill Road.

The Third Avenue El opened in stages between 1878 and 1920. As part of the Dual Contracts, the line was triple-tracked to allow for express service. However, as subway lines were built, the elevated lines became viewed as obsolete.

The Third Avenue El was closed in sections between 1950 and 1973. The portion in Manhattan was closed in 1955, and the remaining section in the Bronx was closed in 1973. The demolition of the El spurred new construction in Manhattan, but also led to decline in parts of the Bronx. After the El's closure, the Bx55 bus route replaced it, offering free transfers to the subway. The Bx55 was later replaced by the Bx41 Select Bus Service and the Bx15 Limited.

Satellite in the Sky is a 1956 British CinemaScope science fiction film in Warner Color, produced by Edward J. Danziger and Harry Lee Danziger, directed by Paul Dickson, and starring Kieron Moore, Lois Maxwell, Donald Wolfit, and Bryan Forbes. The film was distributed by Warner Bros. Pictures. Special effects were by Wally Veevers, who would later work on Stanley Kubrick's 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968).

The medieval University of Dublin (Lang-la) was an early but generally unsuccessful attempt to establish a university in Dublin, the capital city of the Lordship of Ireland. Founded in 1320, it maintained an intermittent existence for the next two centuries, but it never flourished, and disappeared for good at the Reformation in Ireland (1534–41). It was located in Saint Patrick's Cathedral, Dublin. It had no connection with the present University of Dublin, better known as Trinity College Dublin, which was founded in 1592.

A condensation polymer is a type of polymer formed through a condensation reaction, where molecules join together—losing small molecules as byproducts such as water or methanol. This is in contrast to addition polymers, which involve the joining of monomers without the loss of any molecules. Two major classes of condensation polymers are polyamides and polyesters.

Polyamides, such as nylons and proteins, are formed from the reaction between a carboxylic acid and an amine. The polymerization of amino-carboxylic acids results in the co-formation of water. When prepared from diamines and dicarboxylic acids, the polymerization produces two molecules of water per repeat unit.

Polyesters are formed from the reaction between a carboxylic acid and an alcohol, with polyethyleneterephthalate (PET) being a common example.

Condensation polymers are often more biodegradable than addition polymers due to the presence of hydrolyzable bonds, such as peptide or ester bonds, between the monomers. These bonds can be broken down through hydrolysis, especially with the aid of catalysts or bacterial enzymes.

Anula Karunathilaka (Lang-si, born 23 January 1946) is a Sri Lankan film actress and theatrical performer. Her work is noted during the 70s film and cinema history in Sri Lanka. One of the most popular actresses in Sinhala cinema in 1960s, she is best known for the popular role 'Dammi' in the blockbuster film Golu Hadawatha.

Helmut Rahn (16 August 1929 – 14 August 2003) was a German footballer. He played as a wing forward. Rahn was nicknamed "Der Boss". He is best known for scoring the winning goal in the 1954 FIFA World Cup Final against Hungary. He also played in the 1958 World Cup, finishing with 21 goals in 40 international matches.

David John Lee (born 26 November 1969) is an English former professional footballer who played as a central defender.

Lee began his career as a youth player at Chelsea in 1983, making his senior debut in 1988. He spent ten years with the club, making 196 appearances, but had loan spells with Reading, Plymouth Argyle, Portsmouth, and Sheffield United. In 1998, he joined Bristol Rovers, followed by Crystal Palace, Colchester United, Exeter City, Parramatta Power, Havant & Waterlooville, Forest Green Rovers, Mangotsfield United and Weston-super-Mare.

After retiring from playing, Lee became chief scout with Bristol City and later assistant manager at Northampton Town. He was briefly in temporary charge of Northampton Town.

William Le Baron Jenney (September 25, 1832 – June 14, 1907) was an American architect and engineer who is known for building the first skyscraper in 1884.

In 1998, Jenney was ranked number 89 in the book 1,000 Years, 1,000 People: Ranking the Men and Women Who Shaped the Millennium.

Shochiku Studio (株式会社松竹撮影所, Kabushiki gaisha Shōchiku Satsueijo) is a Japanese film studio and filmmaking company. It was founded on October 15, 2008, with headquarters in Kyoto. It has two divisions: Kyoto Studio and Tokyo Studio.

Shochiku built Shochiku Kamata Studio in Tokyo in 1920, but it was heavily damaged in 1923, leading to the establishment of Shochiku Kyoto Studio. In 1936, Shochiku relocated the studio to Ofuna. Shochiku purchased a studio in Uzumasa, Kyoto in 1940. In 1995, Shochiku Ofuna Studio transformed into a theme park. In 2008, Shochiku Kyoto Studio Co., Ltd. was founded. The company's name was changed to Shochiku Studio in 2011.

*Ro-45* was a Kaichū type submarine of the K6 sub-class built for the Imperial Japanese Navy during World War II. Laid down in October 1942, she was commissioned in January 1944 and sunk near Truk on April 30, 1944.

Govind Prasad Kukreti (25 August 1932 – 31 August 2016), known as Dabral Baba, was a Hindu religious figure from Uttarakhand, India. He is known for rediscovering the Vikrant Bhairav Temple in Ujjain in 1960, an event that led to him being honored with the title "Baba."

Dabral Baba's spiritual journey is characterized by his devotion to Vikrant Bhairav and his claimed communion with Mahavatar Babaji, the guru of Lahiri Mahasaya. He spoke of Mahavatar Babaji's guidance and visits, and there were instances reported during the Kumbh Mela in Ujjain in 2016 where Dabral Baba identified Mahavatar Babaji in different forms.

Dabral Baba was revered by many, including spiritual leaders from the Himalayas, who acknowledged his connection to Mahavatar Babaji. He was known for his selfless service, meeting with hundreds of people daily to offer blessings and guidance, often attributing his insights to his spiritual master. His childlike demeanor, compassion, and lack of personal gain attracted devotees from across India and abroad. He never accepted anything from visitors, and donations to his temple were used to serve devotees.

An amnesty bin or amnesty box is a receptacle into which items can be placed without incurring consequences related to those items. Amnesty bins have been used for various items, including drugs, weapons, fruit, invasive species, and animals. A version of an amnesty bin is also used at Amazon warehouses for damaged items.

Donald "Don" Sweeney (born August 17, 1966) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey defenceman and current general manager of the Boston Bruins of the National Hockey League (NHL).

Sweeney played for the Boston Bruins (1988–2003) and Dallas Stars (2003-2004), amassing 273 points in 1,115 regular season games. He was drafted out of St. Paul's School by the Bruins in 1984, later playing hockey at Harvard University. Sweeney was named general manager of the Bruins in May 2015, and won the NHL General Manager of the Year Award in 2019.

Kizzuwatna was an ancient kingdom in southeastern Anatolia, present-day Turkey, during the 2nd millennium BC. The area was rich in resources like silver and fertile land. Several ethnic groups, including Hurrians, Hittites, and Luwians, coexisted there. Kizzuwatna played a strategic role in the power struggles between the Hittites and Mitanni, sometimes allied with one or the other. Its pantheon was integrated into the Hittite one.
Charles Harrie Gooden (ca.1867 – 10 November 1905) was an artist in South Australia.
Oliver Samuels (born 9 November 1948) is a Jamaican comedian and actor. He is often described as the Jamaican "King of Comedy", performing both stand-up and comic theatre.

*Ceiling Unlimited* was an American anthology series broadcast on CBS from November 9, 1942, to April 30, 1944. Created by Orson Welles, the program was a morale-boosting collection of stories about heroic tales of aviation, sponsored by Lockheed-Vega Corporation. Welles hosted and directed the first 13 episodes, also writing them with Arthur Miller and others. Bernard Herrmann composed the music.

The show featured many actors from Welles's Mercury Theatre company, including Ray Collins, Joseph Cotten, Agnes Moorehead, and Everett Sloane. After Welles's departure in February 1943, celebrity guest hosts such as Ronald Colman, Marlene Dietrich, and Cary Grant took over. From June to August 1943, author James Hilton hosted the program.

The second season, beginning in August 1943, was retitled *America — Ceiling Unlimited* and adopted a musical/variety format hosted by Joseph Cotten. The series presented a John Steinbeck short story, "With Your Wings," which was later published in 2014. While most episodes are believed to be missing, some recordings and scripts are preserved in collections like the Lilly Library at Indiana University Bloomington.

The 2023 BAL qualification, also known as Road to BAL 2023, was a basketball tournament organized by FIBA Africa from October 10 to November 27, 2022. Twenty-one teams participated, competing for slots in the 2023 Basketball Africa League (BAL).

The 2017 CONCACAF U-20 Championship qualifying took place between June and October 2016. The Caribbean zone was contested in group stages hosted by five nations, while the Central American zone was a single round-robin stage. The final round was hosted by Curaçao.

Primitive Love is the ninth studio album and second English-language record by the Miami Sound Machine, released in August 1985, by Epic Records.

A 2CD remastered version of the album was scheduled for March 31, 2017 by Vibe on Records, but it was postponed because Estefan is working on her new album. To date there is no set date for the re-release; however, the track listing was revealed to the public: it will include five songs that were previously unreleased on CD, sixteen remixes, two soundtrack songs, one demo and the Spanish version of "Words Get in the Way". The album sold over 6 million copies worldwide.
Claude Frederic T'Serclaes, Count of Tilly, was a soldier and later general in the Dutch States Army.

The 500-series format is a modular design for audio equipment, particularly signal processors. It was invented by Saul Walker in 1967 for a mixing console at Apostolic Recording Studio. Walker and Lou Lindauer co-founded Automated Processes, Inc. (API) in 1969.

Originally, engineers repurposed modules from API consoles. API later produced its own chassis, including the Lunchbox. The format's popularity grew, and by 2020, over 500 modules were available from various manufacturers. In 2006, API established the VPR Alliance to standardize 500-series specifications, ensuring compatibility across different brands.

Koforidua Senior High Technical School is a secondary school located in Koforidua, Ghana. Established in 1967 by Kwame Nkrumah, it was the second technical school in Ghana. Originally an all-boys school, it began admitting girls in 1991. The school is considered a Class A school in Ghana.
Li Shuai (born August 18, 1982) is a Chinese former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He played for Qingdao Jonoon, Guangzhou Evergrande, and Shanghai Shenhua.
Sofya Andreyevna Arzhakovskaya (Lang-ru; born 12 August 1987), known professionally as Sofya Skya (Lang-ru), is a Russian actress, ballet dancer and acting coach.

Horse pulling is a draft horse competition where horses in harness, usually one or two animals, pull a stone-boat or weighted sled and the winner is the team or animal that can pull the most weight for a short distance.

There are different weight classes and strict rules to avoid animal cruelty.

Draft breeds are commonly used, but here are also competitions for ponies, where they pull significant weight relative to their size, but usually for shorter distances than full-sized horses.

Related sports are tractor pulling and Canine Weight pulling. However, horses pull less weight than tractors, but much more than dogs.

In Michigan folklore, the Michigan Dogman was allegedly witnessed in 1887 in Wexford County, Michigan. The creature is described as a seven-foot tall, blue-eyed, or amber-eyed bipedal canine-like animal with the torso of a man and a fearsome howl that sounds like a human scream. According to legends, the Michigan Dogman appears in a ten-year cycle that falls on years ending in 7. Sightings have been reported in several locations throughout Michigan, primarily in the northwestern quadrant of the Lower Peninsula. In 1987, the legend of the Michigan Dogman gained popularity when disc jockey Steve Cook at WTCM-FM recorded a song about the creature and its reported sightings.

MAZON: A Jewish Response to Hunger is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization founded in 1985. Headquartered in Los Angeles, California, it combats hunger through partnership grantmaking, education, outreach, advocacy, and strategic initiatives. Inspired by the Ethiopian famine of 1985 and conceived by Leonard Fein, MAZON seeks to connect the Jewish community with efforts to alleviate hunger worldwide. The organization's name, meaning "food" or "sustenance" in Hebrew, reflects its holistic approach to ensuring access to nutritious food for those in need and developing long-term solutions to end hunger. Abby J. Leibman is the current President and CEO, and Rabbi Joel Pitkowsky is the Chair of the Board of Directors.

*The Mad Dog Gang Meets Rotten Fred and Ratsguts* is a 1979 New Zealand children's film about three children who form a gang and befriend a local vagrant who is wrongly accused of killing sheep. It won the 1979 Feltex award for best drama.

Millennium Fantasy for Piano and Orchestra is a piano concerto written by the American composer Ellen Taaffe Zwilich. The work was commissioned by the pianist Jeffrey Biegel in association with the Adele Marcus Foundation, the Abraham J. & Phyllis Katz Foundation, the South Florida Council of the Chopin Foundation of the United States, Isa and Marvin Leibowitz, the American Music Center, and a consortium of 27 American orchestras. It was first performed by Jeffrey Biegel and the Cincinnati Symphony Orchestra under the direction of Jesús López Cobos in Cincinnati on September 22, 2000.
Giorgos Fountas (Lang-el; February 13, 1922 – November 28, 2010) was a Greek actor in film and television.
Doris Mary Stenton (1894–1971) was an English medieval historian, university lecturer, and writer.

Michael Cobbins (born August 9, 1992) is an American professional basketball player for Basket Brescia Leonessa of the Italian Lega Basket Serie A (LBA). He played college basketball for Oklahoma State.

Joseph Lawson Edward Howze (born Lawson Edward Howze, August 30, 1923 – January 9, 2019) was an African-American prelate of the Catholic Church. He served as the first Bishop of Biloxi from 1977 to 2001, and was the first openly Black Catholic bishop of a US diocese.
Civil Hospital, Aizawl is a 300-bed public hospital owned by the government of Mizoram in Aizawl, India. Established in 1896, it offers various medical specialties and is a Regional Cancer Centre.

Brazil is a predominantly Christian country with Islam being a minority religion, first brought by African slaves and then by Lebanese and Syrian immigrants. Due to secular nature of the Brazil's constitution, Muslims are free to proselytize and build places of worship in the country. However, Islam isn't independently included in charts and graphics representing religions in Brazil due to its very small size, being grouped in "other religions", which generally represent about 1% of the country's population. The number of Muslims in Brazil, according to the 2010 census, was 35,207 out of a population of approximately 191 million people. This correspond to 0.018% of the Brazilian population.

Konanakunte is a neighborhood in the Bangalore Urban district of Karnataka, India, with a population of 13,262 as of 2001. Once a secluded, rural area, it has undergone significant development and urbanization over the last two decades.

Tobin David Stokes (born 1966) is a Canadian composer and theatre creator, notable for his work in opera, theatre, choral music and television. His works have been performed by New York City Opera, Long Beach Opera, City Opera of Vancouver, the Moscow Symphony Orchestra, and the Victoria Symphony. He also composes for television, including scores for documentaries by the BBC, ABC, and PBS.

*Force Majeure* is a 2014 Swedish comedy-drama film directed by Ruben Östlund. It stars Johannes Bah Kuhnke and Lisa Loven Kongsli as a married couple on a ski trip in the French Alps whose relationship is tested after Tomas (the husband) abandons his family during an apparent avalanche.

The film premiered at the 2014 Cannes Film Festival, where it won the Jury Prize in the Un Certain Regard section. It was also Sweden's entry for Best Foreign Language Film at the 87th Academy Awards, though it was not nominated. An English-language remake, *Downhill*, was released in 2020.

CNN Pipeline was an internet streaming television network owned by Time Warner that launched on December 5, 2005. It offered subscribers four simultaneous live video feeds, known as "pipes," and access to CNN's archives. Anchors included Veronica De La Cruz, Nicole Lapin, Melissa Long, and Richard Lui. CNN discontinued the subscription-based service on June 27, 2007, replacing it with a free, ad-supported live video stream. In September 2014, CNN announced CNNgo, an online service that effectively replaced Pipeline.

Walter Gilbert Alexander I (December 3, 1880 – February 5, 1953) was an American physician and Republican Party politician from New Jersey. He was president of the National Medical Association and the first African American to serve in the New Jersey Legislature.
Boussens is a municipality in the Gros-de-Vaud district in the canton of Vaud, Switzerland.
John Patrick (born February 29, 1968) is an American professional basketball coach and former professional basketball player.

Mount Hope is a mountain in Victoria, Australia, first ascended by Thomas Livingstone Mitchell in 1836. Mitchell, a Scottish-born explorer, sighted the mountain four days prior and named it Mount Hope. He discovered two new plant species on its summit. In 1860, members of the Burke and Wills expedition, including Ludwig Becker, ascended the mountain. Mount Hope Station was established in 1845. An unsubstantiated gold rush occurred in 1857. A quarry operated at Mount Hope from the 1940s until 1958.
Wilhelm von Henke (1834–1896) was a German anatomist and professor known for his research and the eponymous "Henke's space".

Vladimir Nikolaevich Nemukhin (Lang-ru; February 12, 1925 in village Priluki, Kaluga Oblast, RSFSR, USSR – April 18, 2016 Moscow, Russia) was a Russian artist. Nemukhin was associated with the 'Lianozovo Group' along with Oscar Rabin, Valentina Kropivnitskaya [ru] (1924-2008) and Lidiya Masterkova.

Mount Hope is a granite outcrop located to the north of Pyramid Hill in northern Victoria, Australia. The outcrop lies within the 126 hectare Mount Hope Nature Conservation Reserve which is managed by Parks Victoria.

The reserve has limited facilities, including picnic tables and an unsealed parking area at the foot of the outcrop which is accessed from the unsealed Old Leitchville Road. Activities include rock climbing and bird observing.

Communications facilities for Ambulance Victoria, Goulburn Murray Water and the Department of Sustainability and Environment are located near the mountain summit.

Władysław Studnicki (1867–1953) was a Polish politician, publicist, and political activist. Initially a socialist, he later embraced nationalism and advocated for Polish-German cooperation. He envisioned a Central European bloc against the Soviet Union. In 1939, he opposed the alliance with Britain, warning of dire consequences for Poland. During World War II, he attempted to influence German policy and was imprisoned. After the war, he lived in exile, defending his controversial views.
Karl Hermann Heinrich Benda (also Carl Benda), (baptised 2 May 1748 – 15 March 1836) was a German violinist and composer of Bohemian origin.
Eric Reed (born 1963 or 1964) is an American thoroughbred horse trainer. He won his first Kentucky Derby in 2022 with Rich Strike.
Persia is a town in Cattaraugus County, New York, United States. The population was 2,203 at the 2020 census. The town was founded in 1835 from part of the town of Perrysburg.

Scott Mendes (born July 4, 1969 in Visalia, California) is an American former professional rodeo cowboy who specialized in bull riding. He won the 1997 Professional Rodeo Cowboys Association (PRCA) Bull Riding World Championship, and is the inventor of Rodeo Judge, the original rodeo card game. He was also a co-founder of the Professional Bull Riders (PBR). In 2022, he was inducted into the Bull Riding Hall of Fame.
Feliksas Vaitkus (1907–1956), also known as Felix Waitkus, was an American-born Lithuanian pilot and the sixth pilot to fly solo across the Atlantic.
Lilian Staple Mead (born c. 1864) was an Australian suffragist, temperance advocate, and author of children's books. She was a signatory on the 1894 Women's Suffrage Petition in South Australia.

The University of St. Thomas School of Law is one of the professional graduate schools of University of St. Thomas in Minnesota, United States. It is one of three law schools in Minneapolis–Saint Paul. It currently enrolls 434 students. St. Thomas Law is accredited by the American Bar Association. It is also a member of The Association of American Law Schools. The University of St. Thomas School of Law is the second highest ranked law school in Minnesota amongst the state's three law schools. Founded in 1999, the School of Law graduated its first class in 2004.
