*Formula One Grand Prix*, also known as *World Circuit*, is a racing simulator developed and published by MicroProse in 1991. Designed and programmed by Geoff Crammond, it was released on the Amiga, Atari ST, and DOS platforms. The game puts players in a Formula One season, featuring all 16 circuits from the 1991 season.

*Formula One Grand Prix* is considered a milestone, offering accurate track modeling, realistic physics, and car handling. Players could tune gear ratios, tire compounds, and wing settings, significantly impacting performance. It also featured functional rearview mirrors and an instant replay system with adjustable cameras. Despite limitations like a fixed frame rate and collision-detection flaws, its blend of graphics and physics created an immersive experience. The game's learning curve, enabled by driving assistance features, made it accessible to both novice and advanced players.

The game fostered a vibrant online community that organized competitions and created modifications. Modern-day community efforts focus on updates, including open-source game editors like "Chequered Flag" and "ArgEditor".

Unleashed (Lang-fr) is a 2005 action thriller film directed by Louis Leterrier, written by Luc Besson, and co-produced by Jet Li and Besson. It stars Jet Li, Morgan Freeman, Bob Hoskins and Kerry Condon, with action choreography by Yuen Woo-Ping. The film's setting and shooting location are Glasgow.

Nestor Principe (March 15, 1945 – May 7, 1973) was a Filipino activist and martial arts instructor. He is best known for introducing the Filipino martial art of Sikaran to Malaysia in 1965. Principe traveled the world before returning to the Philippines to study journalism and become an activist. He was killed in 1973 by the Philippine Constabulary in Kabayan, Benguet. In 2018, he was honored at the Bantayog ng mga Bayani, a memorial dedicated to the heroes and martyrs who fought against the martial law regime of Ferdinand Marcos.

Republic Township is a civil township of Marquette County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 1,060 at the 2010 census. The unincorporated community of Republic is located within the township.

Dinkar D. Patil (Marathi: दिनकर द. पाटील) (1915–2005) was a prominent Marathi film director, scriptwriter, and dialogue writer during the Golden Era (1950–1990) of the Marathi Film Industry. He directed, wrote scripts and dialogue for more than 60 Marathi films. He also directed two Hindi Films-Mandir and Gharbar. He wrote his famous autobiography titled as Patlache Por.

The Swallow Music Awards (Armenian: Ծիծեռնակ երաժշտական մրցանակաբաշխություն) are presented by the Ministry of Culture of Armenia to recognize excellence and outstanding achievement in the music industry. The awards are held annually at the Armenian National Academic Theatre of Opera and Ballet named after Alexander Spendiaryan in Yerevan, with the first ceremony taking place in 2017. Unlike some other Armenian music awards, the Swallow Music Awards feature nominees based on applications submitted by the artists. Award categories are determined based on the number of applications received. The 2017 edition included a special prize for Stephan Shakaryan, while the 2018 edition named Iveta Mukuchyan and Erik as the best female and male singers, respectively. The application process has faced some criticism from artists.

Kampung Siam (also Kampong Siam) is a historic Thai enclave within George Town, Penang, Malaysia. Established in the early 19th century, it is located in Pulau Tikus. In 1845, the British authorities granted the land to Siamese settlers, who then built Wat Chaiyamangkalaram, a prominent Thai Buddhist temple, next to the village.

More recently, Kampung Siam has faced threats of redevelopment. The land was sold to a developer in 2014 without the knowledge of the trust holders, leading to a legal dispute and fears of eviction for the residents. A lawsuit filed by the residents was dismissed in 2015.
Dennis Mallonee (born 1955) is an American comic book writer, known for Champions.
**Fred Crawford** (1910–1974) was an All-American college football player for Duke University and briefly played professionally.

The Art of Self-Defense is a 2019 black comedy martial arts film written and directed by Riley Stearns and starring Jesse Eisenberg, Alessandro Nivola and Imogen Poots. It had its world premiere at South by Southwest on March 10, 2019, and was released in the United States on July 12, 2019, by Bleecker Street.

Dardanella was a touring theatre company from the Dutch East Indies (modern-day Indonesia) established by Willy A. Piedro in 1926. Arising from a background of musical theatre, the troupe focused on realistic stories, both adaptations of foreign works and original stage plays about life in the Indies. Starring Dewi Dja' and Tan Tjeng Bok, the troupe performed original works by Piedro and Andjar Asmara. Popular both in the Indies and abroad, Dardanella dissolved during an international tour after 1936. Several of its members later went into film.

E. S. Rose Park is a baseball, softball, soccer, and track & field venue in Nashville, Tennessee, United States. It is home to Nashville Union FC and Belmont Bruins baseball, softball, men's and women's soccer, and men's and women's track & field teams of the NCAA Division I Ohio Valley Conference. It is located approximately five minutes from Belmont University's main campus. The city-owned park was renovated extensively in 2010–2011.

A Walk in the Sun is a 1945 American war film based on the novel by Harry Brown, who was a writer for Yank, the Army Weekly based in England. The book was serialized in Liberty Magazine in October 1944.

The film was directed by Lewis Milestone, stars Dana Andrews and features Richard Conte, George Tyne, John Ireland, Lloyd Bridges, Sterling Holloway, Norman Lloyd, Herbert Rudley and Richard Benedict, with narration by Burgess Meredith.

In 2016, the film was deemed "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant" by the United States Library of Congress, and selected for preservation in its National Film Registry.
Auguste-Henri-Victor Grandjean de Montigny (1776–1850) was a French and Brazilian architect. He was known for his classical buildings in Rio de Janeiro.

Emerich (Imre) Vogl (born 12 August 1905 in Temesvár, Austria-Hungary (now Romania) – died 29 October 1971 in Bucharest, Romania) was a Romanian football player and coach of Banat Swabian ethnicity who was a member of Romanian team which participated at the 1930 FIFA World Cup in Uruguay and 1934 FIFA World Cup in Italy.

Dan Carpenter (born November 25, 1985) is an American former placekicker. He played college football at Montana, and professionally for the Miami Dolphins (2008-2012) and Buffalo Bills (2013-2016). Carpenter was named to the Pro Bowl in 2009.
Nicolas Marroc (born 5 December 1986 in La-Roche-sur-Yon) is a French racing driver.

The karbovanets or karbovanet (Lang-uk, plural: карбованці, karbovantsi for 2–4, or карбованців, karbovantsiv for 5 or more), also known as kupon (Lang-uk, plural: купони, kupony) or coupon, has been a distinct unit of currency in Ukraine during three separate periods of the 20th century. It is also a predecessor currency of today's Ukrainian hryvnia.

The karbovanets was subdivided into one hundred kopiykas, but no denominations in kopiykas have ever been issued.

In the ISO 4217 standard, the official name is spelled as karbovanet, while English version of the National Bank of Ukraine's website refers to it as karbovanets.

Henrique Honoré Dumont (July 20, 1832 in Diamantina – August 30, 1892 in Rio de Janeiro) was a Brazilian engineer, coffee farmer and father of Alberto Santos-Dumont. A son of French immigrants, he is considered one of the three kings of the coffee of his time, introducing modern methods to coffee farming.

The Farman F.220 was a French high-wing bomber and airliner developed in the 1930s. It saw service with the French Air Force (Armée de l'Air) and Air France. Several variants were produced, including the F.221, F.222, F.223, and F.224. A modified F.223 was the first Allied bomber to raid Berlin during World War II.

Harry Watson (August 31, 1921 – June 8, 2001) was an American child actor. He was a member of the Watson Family, a family of child actors in early Hollywood. He later became a combat photographer and television journalist.

*Urbanus* is a Belgian comic strip created by Willy Linthout. It stars a younger version of the comedian Urbanus, who frequently tries to trick the local villagers and gets into trouble. The comedy style is absurd and often vulgar.

The series was conceived in 1982, with Linthout writing and drawing the first two albums. Since 1996, Standaard Uitgeverij has published the series. *Urbanus* was discontinued on October 26, 2022, after 201 stories. An animated film adaptation was released in 1992, and the characters have appeared in crossovers with other comic series.

VirusTotal is a website dedicated to analyzing files and URLs for potential malware and other security threats. Launched in June 2004, it is currently owned by Chronicle, a subsidiary of Google LLC. VirusTotal aggregates results from numerous antivirus engines, website scanning engines, and file characterization tools to provide users with a comprehensive analysis of uploaded files or submitted URLs.

The service is available in multiple languages and offers optional registration. Users can submit files and URLs through the website or via various tools, including a Windows Uploader (discontinued in 2017), uploaders for Mac OS X and Linux, browser extensions, and a mobile app for Android. VirusTotal also provides a public API that allows developers to automate scans and access reports.

Files uploaded to VirusTotal are stored and may be shared with anti-malware companies to improve detection rates. The website offers a range of antivirus products, website/domain scanning engines, and file characterization tools and datasets.

Old Street is a road in London that may have ancient origins, possibly as part of a pre-Roman track. The road skirts around what was once Londinium. The western part of Old Street was widened in the 1870s. Some domestic buildings from the 18th and early 19th centuries still stand. The eastern half of the road forms part of the London Inner Ring Road and the congestion charge zone boundary. Old Street and its surrounding area are known for nightlife and have attracted IT and tech companies, leading to the nickname "Silicon Roundabout" for Old Street Roundabout. Old Street station is located there.

Borough Green & Wrotham railway station is located in Borough Green in Kent, England. It is 29 miles 46 chains (47.6 km) down the line from London Victoria. Train services are provided by Southeastern.
Martyrs Bus Service is a bus operator in Melbourne, Australia. It operates one route under contract to Public Transport Victoria. It is a subsidiary of the Dineen Group.

The Cadets were an American doo-wop group, formed in Los Angeles, California, United States. The group began as a gospel group, the Santa Monica Soul Seekers, in the late 1940s. The members were Lloyd McCraw, Willie Davis, Austin "Ted" Taylor, Aaron Collins, Glendon Kingsby, and Will "Dub" Jones. In 1955, the group auditioned for Modern Records, and were accepted. The group decided to switch to the popular R&B style, with the exception of Kingsby, who left to continue in gospel music.

Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) is an analytical technique used to visualize the spatial distribution of molecules within a sample. It was first introduced over 50 years ago, but it was the work of Richard Caprioli and colleagues in the late 1990s that led to its widespread use. MSI involves ionizing molecules from a sample's surface and then using a mass spectrometer to identify them based on their mass-to-charge ratio. The abundance of each ion is then mapped to its spatial location, creating an image that shows the distribution of different molecules.

Several ionization techniques can be used in MSI, including secondary ion mass spectrometry (SIMS), matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI), and desorption electrospray ionization (DESI). Each technique has its own advantages and disadvantages in terms of spatial resolution, sensitivity, and the types of molecules that can be analyzed. MSI can be performed using either a microprobe approach, where a focused beam is scanned across the sample, or a microscope approach, where a 2D detector is used to measure the spatial origin of ions. MSI data can be combined with other imaging techniques, such as fluorescence microscopy and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), to obtain a more comprehensive understanding of the sample. The imzML format is used to exchange data in a standardized XML file. MSI has a wide range of applications, including clinical research, pharmacological research, and materials science.

James David Bouknight (/ˈbʊknaɪt/; born September 18, 2000) is an American professional basketball player for the Charlotte Hornets of the National Basketball Association (NBA). He played college basketball for the UConn Huskies.

A chase gun (or chaser), usually distinguished as bow chaser and stern chaser, was a cannon mounted in the bow (aiming forward) or stern (aiming backward) of a sailing ship. They were used to attempt to slow down an enemy ship either chasing (pursuing) or being chased, when the ship's broadside could not be brought to bear. Typically, the chasers were used to attempt to damage the rigging and thereby cause the target to lose performance.

Bow chasers could be regular guns brought up from the gundeck and aimed through specially cut-out ports on either side of the bowsprit, or dedicated weapons made with an unusually long bore and a relatively light ball, and mounted in the bow. Stern chasers could also be improvised, or left permanently in the cabins at the stern, covered up and used as part of the furniture.

Multiple complex developmental disorder (MCDD) is a research category, proposed to involve several neurological and psychological symptoms where at least some symptoms are first noticed during early childhood and persist throughout life. It was originally suggested to be a subtype of autistic spectrum disorders (PDD) with co-morbid schizophrenia or another psychotic disorder; however, there is some controversy that not everyone with MCDD meets criteria for both PDD and psychosis. The term multiplex developmental disorder was coined by Donald J. Cohen in 1986.

Collective motion refers to the coordinated movement of self-propelled particles, mirroring biological systems. These particles, such as micromotors and nanomotors, respond to external gradients, exhibiting behaviors like schooling and exclusion. For instance, silver chloride micromotors form schools under UV light, while silver orthophosphate microparticles transition between schooling and exclusion based on chemical stimuli, enabling the design of logic gates.

These motors also display chemotaxis, moving towards higher concentrations of specific chemicals. Gold-platinum nanorods move towards hydrogen peroxide sources, and enzyme nanomotors migrate towards their substrates. This chemotactic behavior has applications in separating active enzymes, studying metabolon formation, and directing motion at the microscale for drug delivery, sensing, and lab-on-a-chip devices.
Stockland Bristol (formerly Stockland Gaunts) is a village and civil parish in the Sedgemoor district of Somerset, between Bridgwater and the Steart Peninsula.

An Allied Commission was a control body established by the Allies after World War II to oversee Axis powers. These commissions, such as those in Italy, Romania, Finland, Bulgaria, Hungary, Germany, Austria, and Japan, ensured compliance with armistice terms and treaties. The commissions varied in composition and influence, with some dominated by the Soviet Union and others by the United States.

"Eu Quero Tchu, Eu Quero Tcha" is a 2012 sertanejo/pop single by Brazilian duo João Lucas & Marcelo. Its soccer-themed music video features Brazilian footballer Neymar, who also performs the song's dance. The song appeared on the soundtrack of the telenovela *Avenida Brasil*. Pitbull and Jay & El Punto have sampled the song, and Flavel & Neto popularized it in Europe.

The Hong Kong motion picture rating system was established on November 10, 1988, under the Movie Screening Ordinance Cap.392. The ratings are assessed by the Office for Film, Newspaper and Article Administration, and films must be screened by the Office before public release. The system has been amended since its creation.

In computer architecture, the instruction cycle (also known as the fetch–decode–execute cycle) is the basic operational process of a computer. It consists of fetching an instruction from memory, decoding it, and executing it.

The program counter (PC) holds the memory address of the next instruction. During the fetch stage, this address is copied to the memory address register (MAR), and the PC is incremented. The instruction is then fetched from memory into the memory data register (MDR) and copied to the current instruction register (CIR) for decoding by the control unit (CU). The CU then sends signals to other components to execute the instruction, potentially using the arithmetic logic unit (ALU) or floating point unit (FPU).
Oberhünigen is a municipality in the Bern-Mittelland administrative district in the canton of Bern, Switzerland.

*Eucalyptus largiflorens*, commonly known as the **black box**, is a tree in the family Myrtaceae that is endemic to Australia. It grows to 20 meters and has rough bark. Its flowers are arranged in groups of seven to eleven and it flowers mostly in autumn and spring.

The Leader of Alderney is the highest civil figure in Alderney. Currently, the President is elected by all of Alderney for a four-year term and chairs the States of Alderney, voting only to break ties. Historically, the role has evolved from Lord of Alderney to Governor, Judge of Alderney, and even German officials during WWII occupation. In 1948, Alderney rejoined the Bailiwick of Guernsey, leading to the establishment of a democratically elected President as the Leader of Alderney.

Rangi Ruru Girls' School is an independent girls' school in Christchurch, New Zealand, founded in 1889. Originally "Miss Gibson's Private School for Girls", it was renamed Rangi Ruru, meaning "wide sky-shelter". In 1946, the Presbyterian Church purchased the school.

Bertrand Ndongo (born c. 1990) is a Cameroonian activist for the Spanish political party Vox. He has been nicknamed in the media and in self-reference as "El Negro de Vox" (Vox's Black Guy). Since 2019, he has been a consultant to Rocío Monasterio, the party's leader in the Community of Madrid.

Harry P. Deuel (born in Clarkson, New York) was an American businessman and public official. He moved to Omaha, Nebraska, in 1859 to work for the Porter and Deuel Steamboat Agency. He later became an agent for multiple railroads, including the Kansas City, St. Joseph, & Council Bluffs Railroad, the Burlington and Missouri River Railroad, and the Union Pacific Railroad. In 1896, he became superintendent of the new Burlington Station in Omaha, a position he held until 1901. In 1901, Deuel was elected registrar of deeds. He was also a prominent Freemason, serving as Grand Master of the Nebraska Grand Lodge in 1869.

Margaret Louise Cuninggim (1914–1986) was an American university professor and dean. She taught at several institutions, including the University of Montevallo, Ripon College, and Tennessee Technological University. Cuninggim served as Dean of Women at the University of Tennessee from 1957 to 1966, and at Vanderbilt University from 1966 to 1973. The Margaret Cuninggim Women's Center and the annual Cuninggim Lecture at Vanderbilt are named in her honor.

The Bora–Witoto languages are a proposed language family spoken in the northwestern Amazon. It includes the Boran and Witotoan languages, and sometimes the Andoque language. The relationship between these languages is disputed.

Robert James "Bobby" Voelker (born April 26, 1979) is an American mixed martial artist. A professional since 2006, Voelker has competed in Strikeforce, the Ultimate Fighting Championship, and Bellator MMA.

Royalty Exchange is an online marketplace for buying and selling royalties. Founded in 2011 in Raleigh, North Carolina, it was acquired in 2015 and relocated to Denver, Colorado. In 2017, the company launched Royalty Flow to acquire royalty streams, beginning with a portion of Eminem's catalog, but the IPO was later canceled. Royalty Exchange also offers Private Syndicate investments, allowing accredited investors to buy shares in music catalogs. In 2021, Anthony Martini was named CEO. The company has also conducted multi-unit auctions, such as one featuring royalties for 3 Doors Down tracks.

Tariq Fancy (born 1978) is a Canadian businessman known for his work in sustainable investing and for founding The Rumie Initiative. He holds degrees from Brown University, the University of Oxford, Sciences Po, and INSEAD.

Fancy gained prominence as the former Chief Investment Officer of Sustainable Investing at BlackRock. In 2021, he publicly criticized the financial services industry, asserting that it was misleading the public with "pro-environment, sustainable investing practices." He argued that ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) investing lacked evidence of social impact and that Wall Street was "greenwashing" the financial world, using sustainable investing as a PR tactic that distracted from addressing climate change. Fancy elaborated on these points in a three-part article published by Medium, detailing his experiences and interactions within the sustainable investment sector while at BlackRock.
Maria-Luise Rainer (born April 23, 1959) is an Italian luger who won medals at the World and European championships and competed in four Olympics.

The 47th Walker Cup Match was played 7 and 8 September 2019 at Royal Liverpool Golf Club, Hoylake, Merseyside, England. Royal Liverpool hosted the 1983 Walker Cup. The United States won by 151⁄2 points to 101⁄2. They trailed 71⁄2 to 81⁄2 coming in to the final day singles but won 8 of the 10 matches to win convincingly.
Drexel-Alvernon was a census-designated place (CDP) in Pima County, Arizona, United States. The population was 4,192 at the 2000 census.

In computing, a wireless intrusion prevention system (WIPS) is a network device that monitors the radio spectrum for the presence of unauthorized access points (intrusion detection), and can automatically take countermeasures (intrusion prevention).

The *Spichern* was a German tanker that began its career as the Norwegian oil tanker *Krossfonn*, built in 1935. In 1940, the German auxiliary cruiser *Widder* captured *Krossfonn*, renamed her *Spichern*, and converted her into a naval oiler. Allied forces badly damaged *Spichern* in 1944, and German forces sank her as a blockship. The wreck was raised and rebuilt, and in 1949, she returned to Norwegian merchant service as *Ringfjell*. She was scrapped in 1964.

Sanan Suleymanov is an Azerbaijani Greco-Roman wrestler. He won the silver medal in the 77 kg event at the 2021 World Wrestling Championships held in Oslo, Norway. In 2020, he won the gold medal in the 77 kg event at the European Wrestling Championships held in Rome, Italy.

Anne Ebbs (née Sinnott born 1940 or 1941) is a retired Irish Paralympic table tennis medallist. Outside of her table tennis career, Ebbs founded the Paralympic Council of Ireland in 1987 and was the organization's secretary general until 2008. Ebbs was awarded the Paralympic Order in 2010.

Charles Jackson "Bud" Wildman (born June 3, 1946) is a Canadian politician. He was an Ontario New Democratic Party (NDP) member of the Legislative Assembly of Ontario for Algoma from 1975 to 1999. He served as a cabinet minister in the government of Bob Rae and as interim party leader in 1996.

Charlotte Deane MBE (born May 6, 1975) is a Professor of Structural Bioinformatics at the University of Oxford and Deputy Executive Chair of the Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC).

Alexis Cohen (October 17, 1983 – July 5, 2009) was a two-time reality TV show contestant and singer on American Idol who directed an expletive-filled televised rant at the show's judges after comparing her singing style to vocalists Grace Slick, Janis Joplin, and Pat Benatar. She was called "Glitter Girl" in the media.

Sam's Strip was a humorous comic strip created and produced by Mort Walker and Jerry Dumas. It was distributed by King Features Syndicate from October 2, 1961 to June 1, 1963. The series depended heavily on metahumor and appearances by famous comic-strip characters.
Helmut Zilk (9 June 1927, Vienna – 24 October 2008, Vienna) was an Austrian journalist and politician in the Austrian Social Democratic Party. He served as mayor of Vienna between 1984 and 1994.

Mike Mineo is an American singer-songwriter from Hollywood, Florida. He released his first album, *Eccentricity*, in 2010, and toured the U.S. and Canada. In 2011, Mineo formed the group MINEO and released the album *Beach Season*. He released his fourth album, *1*, in 2016.

In the People's Republic of China, "new areas" (新区) are special economic-development zones. State-level new areas are supported by the central government with preferential policies to encourage development. Provincial-level and prefectural-level new areas are governed by the corresponding provincial or prefectural government.

In typography, a tie is a symbol that joins letters or words. It can appear above, below, or between the elements it connects. The tie has various applications, including transliteration, phonetic alphabets, musical notation, and proofreading. In the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA), different tie symbols represent double articulation or linking. Unicode provides several characters to represent different forms of the tie.
*Extracts from Music for White Cube* is a 1997 ambient album by Brian Eno, created for an installation at the White Cube gallery in London.

Aboriginal South Australians are the Indigenous peoples of South Australia. Several groups' lands lie at least partly within South Australian territory. Despite initial intentions, equality under the law was rarely enforced as settlement spread. Conflicts and massacres occurred. Some areas are now subject to native title. Modern Aboriginal people live in diverse settings.
Come was an American alternative rock band formed in Boston, Massachusetts in 1990. The band consisted of Thalia Zedek, Chris Brokaw, Arthur Johnson, and Sean O'Brien.

The Catskill Mountain House, which opened in 1824, was a famous hotel near Palenville, New York, and in the Catskill Mountains overlooking the Hudson River Valley. In its prime, from the 1850s to the turn of the century, it was visited by three U.S. presidents (U.S. Grant, Chester A. Arthur, and Theodore Roosevelt) and the power elite of the day.

Margaret Ransone Murray (November 16, 1901 – July 13, 1986) was an American scientist known primarily for her work on methods to establish cultures of neuronal cells. Her in vitro studies in cellular neurobiology shed light on both nerve- muscle relationships and axon myelination

Otto Kirchheimer (1905–1965) was a German jurist, political scientist, and sociologist associated with the Frankfurt School. He emigrated to Paris in 1934 and then to the United States in 1937. Kirchheimer worked for the OSS during World War II and later taught at the New School and Columbia University. He is known for the concept of the "catch-all party".

The *Joseph Conrad* is a full-rigged ship, originally named *Georg Stage*. Built in Denmark in 1882, she served as a training vessel. Renamed in 1934, she circumnavigated the globe. Later, she served as a U.S. Merchant Marine training ship during World War II. Since 1947, she has been a museum ship at Mystic Seaport.

"Take Me to the Pilot" is a song written by Elton John and Bernie Taupin, released in 1970 as the B-side to "Your Song" from John's self-titled album. The lyrics of the song have been described as cryptic and incomprehensible, even by John and Taupin themselves.

Despite the ambiguity of its lyrics, "Take Me to the Pilot" became a staple of John's live performances and has been featured on numerous concert recordings. The song has also been covered by a wide range of artists, including José Feliciano, Ben E. King, Al Jarreau and Gloria Loring, and Rick Wakeman. It was also featured in the soundtrack of the 2018 Showtime limited series *Escape at Dannemora*.

The Paris Gregory (BnF Grec 510) is an illuminated manuscript of the Homilies of Gregory of Nazianzus commissioned in Constantinople by Patriarch Photios I as a commemoration to the Emperor Basil I between 879 and 883. The illustrations from the manuscript are held today in the Bibliothèque nationale de France in Paris as part of their collection of Greek manuscripts.
Matthijs van Nieuwkerk (born September 8, 1960) is a Dutch journalist and television presenter. He is known for hosting *De Wereld Draait Door*, *Holland Sport*, *Popquiz*, and *Top 2000 à Go-Go*.

Mark Maryboy (born December 10, 1955) is a retired American politician for San Juan County, Utah, and a former Navajo Nation Council Delegate for the Utah Navajo Section of the Navajo Tribe. He is the brother of Kenneth Maryboy who currently serves as the Navejo Nation Council Delegate, in the positions he once stood. Mark is of the Navajo Indian Tribe.

Mark Maryboy was also the San Juan County, Utah Commissioner for District Three. He made history when he was elected in 1986 as the first Native American county commissioner in Utah's history.

Rebecca M. Benally along with the former Navajo Nation Council Delegate Mark Maryboy and protesters from the Aneth Chapter Blocked the main ExxonMobil corporate office for three days in Aneth, Utah.

The Oregon State University Foundation is a fundraising organization for Oregon State University, established in 1947. Its mission is to enhance the university's excellence and impact through community engagement and investment.
Terry George (born 1965) is an English entrepreneur known for running gay nightclubs in Leeds. He and his husband featured in the documentary *Two Queens and a Castle*.
Dottle is the remaining plug of unburnt tobacco and ashes left in the bottom of a tobacco pipe when it has been smoked.

The Palm Court, also known at other times as the Franco-Italian Dining Room, the Grand Ballroom and the Continental Room, is a ballroom at the Hotel Alexandria in downtown Los Angeles, California. In its heyday from 1911 to 1922, it was the scene of speeches by U.S. Presidents William Howard Taft and Woodrow Wilson and Gen. John J. Pershing. It is also the room where Paul Whiteman, later known as the "Jazz King", got his start as a bandleader in 1919, where Rudolph Valentino danced with movie starlets, and where Hollywood held its most significant balls during the early days of the motion picture business. Known for its history and its stained-glass Tiffany skylight, noted Los Angeles columnist Jack Smith called it "surely the most beautiful room in Los Angeles". The Palm Court was designated as a City of Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument (HCM#80) in 1971.

Wickrema Weerasooria (died December 4, 2018) was a Sri Lankan academic, lawyer, and diplomat. He served as the High Commissioner to Australia and New Zealand from 1986 to 1990. Weerasooria also lectured at multiple universities and published over fourteen texts.

*Circumstance* is a 2011 drama film written and directed by Maryam Keshavarz, starring Nikohl Boosheri and Sarah Kazemy as two teenage girls in Iran exploring their sexuality. It won the Audience Award at the 2011 Sundance Film Festival.

Matthew Tindal (1657 – 16 August 1733) was an eminent English deist author. His works, highly influential at the dawn of the Enlightenment, caused great controversy and challenged the Christian consensus of his time.

*Maximum PC* is an American magazine devoted to computer technology. Published monthly by Future US, it debuted in September 1998 as a rebranding of the magazine *boot*. The magazine is based in San Francisco and is written in English. As of 2011, it had a total circulation of over 192,000.

*Maximum PC* is known for its product reviews, which are scored on a scale of 1 to 10. Exceptional products may receive a "Kick Ass" award. The reviews also include a "Pros and Cons" section, often with humorous notations and wordplay related to the product.

The magazine maintains an archive of back issues in PDF format on its website, dating back to December 2003, though no new issues have been published since October 2014. From 2010 to 2011, the editors also produced a quarterly magazine called *Maximum Tech* focusing on consumer technology. An Italian edition of *Maximum PC* was launched in 2004 but ceased publication after six issues.

Annette Krauss is an artist known for collaborative projects. These include *Read the Masks. Tradition is Not Given*, which critiques the Dutch Zwarte Piet tradition; *Hidden Curriculum*, exploring unofficial learning in schools; and *Sites for Unlearning*, experimental gatherings focused on collaborative unlearning. Krauss is also a co-founder of the cultural collective Read-in.

Menachem Mendel Monsohn (October 13, 1895 – September 3, 1953) was a rabbi born in Jerusalem, Ottoman Syria. His wife was Zipporah Yehudit (Silberman) Monsohn, and they had five children: Esther (Schwartz), Shmuel [Samuel Stanford] Manson, Shimon [Simon] Manson, Chaya Masha Gittel [Marsha] (Bunis), and Raytse [Rose] (Aronson).

Monsohn demonstrated intellectual giftedness early in life, receiving rabbinical ordination at the age of 16 from Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook, the first Ashkenazic chief rabbi of British Mandatory Palestine. Rabbi Kook also gave Monsohn an approbation to be published in his Mi-Peninei Ha-Rambam.

After working at his family's lithographic press in Jerusalem, Monsohn established himself as a rabbi in Brooklyn, New York. He brought his wife and Jerusalem-born children to Brooklyn, where they were later joined by a daughter born in the United States. In Brooklyn, Monsohn dedicated most of his time to working on and printing his *Mi-Penine Ha-Rambam*, utilizing skills he had learned at his father's press. He published multiple editions of the book in Hebrew and English, noting in each that he was born in Jerusalem.

The February 2016 Ankara bombing killed at least 30 people and injured 60 in the capital of Turkey. According to Turkish authorities, the attack targeted a convoy of vehicles carrying both civilian and military personnel working at the military headquarters during the evening rush hour as the vehicles were stopped at traffic lights at an intersection with İsmet İnönü Boulevard close to Kızılay neighborhood. Several ministries, the headquarters of the army and the Turkish Parliament are located in the neighbourhood where the attack occurred. The Kurdistan Freedom Hawks (TAK) took responsibility for the attack and said they targeted security forces. Censorship monitoring organization Turkey Blocks reported nationwide internet restrictions beginning approximately one hour after the blast pursuant to an administrative order. The attack killed 14 military personnel, 14 civilian employees of the military, and a civilian (as well as the perpetrator).

Bitter Tears: Ballads of the American Indian is a 1964 concept album, the twentieth album released by singer Johnny Cash on Columbia Records. It is one of several Americana records by Cash. This one focuses on the history of Native Americans in the United States and their problems. Cash believed that his ancestry included Cherokee, which partly inspired his work on this recording. The songs in this album address the harsh and unfair treatment of the indigenous peoples of North America by Europeans in the United States. Two deal with 20th-century issues affecting the Seneca and Pima peoples. It was considered controversial and rejected by some radio stations and fans.

In 2014 a tribute album, Look Again to the Wind: Johnny Cash's Bitter Tears Revisited, was released with contributions by Gillian Welch, Dave Rawlings, Emmylou Harris, Bill Miller, and others. This was also the name of a documentary film about the suppression of Cash's Native American-themed album in the 1960s. This aired on PBS in February and November 2016.

Democratic centralism is a political principle primarily associated with Vladimir Lenin and communist parties. It involves open discussion of policy within the party, followed by a binding decision that all members are expected to uphold. Lenin described it as "freedom of discussion, unity of action". The principle was formalized in 1917 by the Russian Social-Democratic Labour Party (Bolsheviks). It later became a core organizational tenet in the Soviet Union and other communist states, including China and Vietnam. In practice, it often led to centralized control by the party leadership.

The Divine Wings of Tragedy is the third studio album by progressive metal band Symphony X, released in November 1996 through Zero Corporation (Japan) and March 1997 through Inside Out Music (Europe). A remastered edition was reissued on September 13, 2004, through Inside Out, and again in September 17, 2012, as part of a double-LP vinyl release.

The Sun Fast 1/2 Ton, also called the Jeanneau 1/2 Ton, is a French sailboat that was designed by Tony Castro as an International Offshore Rule Half Ton class racer and first built in 1984.

The Sun Fast 1/2 Ton's prototype was named Sun Fast and the design was the first boat in the Sun Fast sailboat range.

The even though only ten boats were built, the design was developed into the Arcadia 30 and the Sun Way 28.

Mesa/Boogie (also known as Mesa Engineering) is an American company in Petaluma, California, that manufactures amplifiers and other accessories for guitars and basses. It has been in operation since 1969.

Mesa was started by Randall Smith as a small repair shop which modified Fender amplifiers, particularly the diminutive Fender Princeton. Smith's modifications gave the small amps much more input gain, making them much louder as well as creating a high-gain, distorted guitar tone. Prominent early customers included Carlos Santana, and Ron Wood and Keith Richards of The Rolling Stones. Exposure from these top players helped to establish Mesa/Boogie's position on the market, and it is frequently referred to as the first manufacturer of boutique amplifiers.

Vena, currently DNA Latin Group, is an American bachata group formed by members Lenny Santos and Max Santos from Aventura alongside ex-vocalist Steven Tejada from Xtreme. They are known for their hits such as "Sangre de Mis Venas", "Corazon de Hierro (featuring Teodoro Reyes & Frank Reyes)", "Señora", "Dile a El", "Por Mentiras", among others. VENA alongside Larry Hernandez were nominated for a Billboard Premious Musica Mexicana award in 2012.

Josh Whitman (born August 5, 1978) is the athletic director for the Illinois Fighting Illini. Prior to this, he served as athletic director for Wisconsin–La Crosse and Washington University in St. Louis. Before his career in athletic administration, Whitman played tight end at Illinois and briefly in the NFL.

Jessica Ashley Karpov (born July 10, 1992), better known as Harloe (stylized in all caps), is an American singer and songwriter, based in Los Angeles. In addition to her career as a recording artist, Karpov co-wrote and co-produced four songs on Kelly Clarkson's 2017 album Meaning of Life, including the singles "I Don't Think About You"and"Heat". She has also written for Britney Spears, Charli XCX, Celine Dion, Zayn, K/DA, Sabrina Claudio, and Olivia Holt.

*Myrica cerifera*, also known as Southern wax myrtle, is a shrub or small tree native to Central America, the Caribbean, Bermuda, and the southeastern United States. Adaptable to diverse habitats, it grows up to 14m tall and is used in herbalism, soapmaking, and as a source of wax for bayberry candles.

*Dance of the Tiger* is a 1978 novel by Björn Kurtén set in Scandinavia 35,000 years ago. It follows Tiger, a Cro-Magnon, as he seeks to defeat Shelk, a Neanderthal/Cro-Magnon hybrid who killed his father. The novel is known for its plausibility and depiction of interactions between Cro-Magnons and Neanderthals.

The major prion protein (PrP) is a protein highly conserved among mammals, with its gene, PRNP, located on chromosome 20 in humans and chromosome 2 in mice. The protein exists in two isoforms: PrPC (normal cellular) and PrPSc (scrapie). PrPC is found on the outer surface of the cell membrane and is possibly involved in transporting ionic copper to cells. PrPSc is a misfolded isoform that accumulates in neural tissue, forming protease-resistant aggregates.

The conversion of PrPC to PrPSc is implicated in transmissible spongiform encephalopathies (TSEs), fatal neurodegenerative diseases. Mutations in the PRNP gene are associated with inherited prion diseases. PrP also interacts with other proteins, such as Hop/STI1, and has been identified as a receptor for amyloid beta oligomers, implicated in Alzheimer's disease. Research on PrP includes investigating its role in the nervous system, memory, neurons, synapses, the immune system, and cellular processes.

Jatavarman Sundara I, also known as Sadayavarman Sundara Pandyan, was a emperor of the Pandyan dynasty who ruled regions of Tamilakkam (present day South India) between 1250–1268 CE. He is remembered for his patronage of the arts and tamil architecture, along with refurbishment and decoration of many Kovils (temple) in the Tamil continent. He oversaw a massive economic growth of the Pandyan empire. On the eve of his death in 1268 CE, the second Pandyan empire's power and territorial extent had risen to its zenith.

Yana Peel (born Yana Mirkin in June 1974) is a Canadian arts administrator and businesswoman. Born in Leningrad, USSR (now Russia), she emigrated to Canada in 1978 and grew up in Toronto. She studied at McGill University and the London School of Economics.

Peel began her career at Goldman Sachs before co-founding the Outset Contemporary Art Fund in 2003 and Intelligence Squared Asia in 2009. She served as CEO of the Serpentine Galleries from 2016 to 2019. Peel is currently the Global Head of Arts and Culture at Chanel. She is also involved with Para Site, the Victoria and Albert Museum's design fund, and the NSPCC. She is married to Stephen Peel and has two children.

Jemima Anne Morrell (7 March 1832 – 13 October 1909) was an English traveller and illustrator. Morrell was born into a middle class family in Selby, Yorkshire and was a member of the Junior United Alpine Club, a club with majority women members that organised annual holiday trips. Morrell was one of the tourists who in 1863 partook in the first ever guided tour of Switzerland, led and conducted by Thomas Cook, making her one of the first modern international tourists. Her account of the journey was published in 1963 under the title Miss Jemima's Swiss Journal: The First Conducted Tour of Switzerland.

The economy of governments covers the systems for setting levels of taxation, government budgets, the money supply and interest rates as well as the labour market, national ownership, and many other areas of government interventions into the economy.

Most factors of economic policy can be divided into either fiscal policy, which deals with government actions regarding taxation and spending, or monetary policy, which deals with central banking actions regarding the money supply and interest rates.

Such policies are often influenced by international institutions like the International Monetary Fund or World Bank as well as political beliefs and the consequent policies of parties.

Odd Reidar Humlegård (born c. 1960) is a Norwegian police officer. He joined the police force in 1984, later graduating from law school in 1992. He headed the National Mobile Police Service from 2003, and the National Criminal Investigation Service from 2009. In 2012, he was appointed Police Commissioner.

James McLachlan Turnbull (born 23 May 1882) was a Scottish footballer who played as a forward, most notably for Manchester United, with whom he won the Football League and the FA Charity Shield in 1908 and the FA Cup in 1909. He was the first player to net a hat-trick in a Charity Shield game.

Gemeinhardt is an American manufacturer of flutes and piccolos, headquartered in Elkhart, Indiana. Founded in 1948 by Kurt Gemeinhardt, a fourth-generation flute-maker from Germany, the company initially produced handmade flutes before expanding to student models. Since 2011, Gemeinhardt has been owned by Angel Industries Co. Ltd. of Taiwan, with flute components manufactured in the United States and assembled overseas.

Navajo music is music made by Navajos, mostly hailing from the Four Corners region of the Southwestern United States and the territory of the Navajo Nation. While it traditionally takes the shape of ceremonial chants and echoes themes found in Diné Bahaneʼ, contemporary Navajo music includes a wide range of genres, ranging from country music to rock and rap, performed in both English and Navajo.

Freienfeld (Italian: Campo di Trens) is a comune (municipality) in South Tyrol in northern Italy, located in the Eisacktal. As of 30 November 2010, it had a population of 2,668 and an area of 95.3 square kilometres (36.8 sq mi).

The Finse Tunnel (Norwegian: Finsetunnelen) is a 10.3-kilometer (6.4 mi) railway tunnel on the Bergen Line in Norway, located near Finse. Situated at an elevation of 1,237 meters (4,058 ft) above sea level, it is the highest point on the Norwegian railway network. The single-track tunnel opened on May 16, 1993, replacing a challenging section of track frequently blocked by snow.

The decision to build the tunnel arose from persistent operational disruptions and high costs associated with maintaining the Bergen Line through the Finse area. Construction began in 1990, and upon completion, the tunnel reduced the line length by 4.5 kilometers (2.8 mi) and travel time by 8–10 minutes. The project also included upgrades to the adjacent line, allowing for increased speeds of up to 170 kilometers per hour (110 mph). While the tunnel has improved the reliability of rail transport, safety concerns have been raised regarding emergency access and evacuation procedures in the event of a fire.
James J. Carrigan (born April 25, 1941) is an American politician, attorney, and teacher from Massachusetts. He served in the state legislature.

Collar × Malice is a visual novel video game developed and published by Idea Factory under their Otomate brand. It was released for PlayStation Vita on August 18, 2016, in Japan, and by Aksys Games in North America and Europe on July 28, 2017. A Nintendo Switch port was also released in 2020. The game was well received by critics, and sold well in Japan for a niche game of its genre and platform. A fan disc, Collar × Malice: Unlimited, was released on July 26, 2018. An anime film adaptation is set to premiere in 2023.

Oksana Aleksandrovna Kostina (15 April 1972 – 11 February 1993) was a Russian rhythmic gymnast. She won the all-around gold medal at the 1992 World Championships. Kostina died in a car crash at the age of 20.
Alfred Edward Bailey (14 March 1871 – 1 August 1950) was an English cricketer who played for Somerset between 1900 and 1911. He was a left-arm orthodox spin bowler.

The Coaldale Big Green were an American football team based in Coaldale, Pennsylvania, from 1912 to 1933. Founded as Coaldale Football in 1912, the team was renamed the Coaldale Big Green in 1917 after adopting green jerseys. Managed by James H. Gildea for 21 seasons, the Big Green primarily featured local talent, including James "Blue" Bonner and Jack "Honeyboy" Evans. The team won the Curran Cup in 1921, 1922, and 1923. In 1924, the team was a founding member of the Anthracite League, but left after one season. The team folded around 1933.
**C. Harrie Gooden** was a South Australian civil servant, artist, and arts administrator.

*Rajmohan's Wife* is a novella by Indian writer Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay, published in 1864. Written in English, it tells the story of a love triangle and a family feud in rural Bengal. The plot involves Rajmohan, his wife Matangini, and Madhav, who is in love with Matangini. The story explores themes of love, betrayal, and social conflict within a zamindar family.

The 2020–21 Nepal Tri-Nation Series was a Twenty20 international cricket tournament that took place in April 2021 in Nepal. The participating teams were Nepal, Malaysia and the Netherlands. The matches were all played at the Tribhuvan University International Cricket Ground in Kirtipur. The tournament was played in a double round-robin format, followed by a final between the top two sides.

The penultimate round-robin match, between Malaysia and the Netherlands, ended in a tie. It was a rain-curtailed game with no Super Over taking place due to the lost time. This result meant that the Netherlands and Nepal advanced to the final. Nepal defeated the Netherlands in the final by 142 runs to win the series.

The Mississippi Wing of Civil Air Patrol (CAP) is the highest echelon of Civil Air Patrol in the state of Mississippi. Mississippi Wing headquarters are located in Jackson, Mississippi. The Mississippi Wing consists of over 500 cadet and adult members at over 16 locations across the state of Mississippi.

Piero di Cosimo (2 January 1462 – 12 April 1522) was an Italian Renaissance painter known for his eccentric personality and distinctive artistic style. Born in Florence as Piero di Lorenzo, he apprenticed under Cosimo Rosseli, assisting him in the Sistine Chapel in 1481, from whom he gained his moniker.

Piero's early work was influenced by the Netherlandish naturalism of Hugo van der Goes, evident in his detailed landscapes and depictions of flora and fauna. He painted mythological scenes, such as Venus, Mars, and Cupid, The Death of Procris, and the Perseus and Andromeda series, displaying hybrid forms of men and animals, inspired by Vitruvius' account of human evolution.

Giorgio Vasari's biography of Piero in his Lives of the Artists, portrays him as an eccentric figure, fearful of thunderstorms and averse to cooking and cleaning. In his later years, influenced by Girolamo Savonarola, he returned to religious themes, as seen in The Immaculate Conception with Saints and Holy Family.

Piero was also a renowned portrait painter, most famously painting Simonetta Vespucci. He designed pageants and triumphal processions. Piero's pupils included Albertinelli, Bartolomeo della Porta, and Andrea del Sarto. He died in Florence on April 12, 1522, of the plague.

Janice Charette (born in Ottawa, Ontario) is a Canadian civil servant and diplomat. She has served as Clerk of the Privy Council and Secretary to the Cabinet twice, first from 2014 to 2016, and again beginning in 2021, initially in an interim role. Charette also served as the Canadian High Commissioner to the United Kingdom from 2016 to 2021.

The 2015–16 Pittsburgh Penguins season was the 49th season for the National Hockey League (NHL) franchise. The Penguins finished the regular season with a record of 48–26–8, placing second in the Metropolitan Division and second in the Eastern Conference. The team's captain was Sidney Crosby, with alternate captains Chris Kunitz and Evgeni Malkin.

Coached initially by Mike Johnston, who was replaced by Mike Sullivan in December, the Penguins played their home games at PPG Paints Arena, with an average attendance of 18,550. The team scored 245 goals and allowed 203. Sidney Crosby led the team in goals (36) and points (85), while Kris Letang led in assists (51). Marc-Andre Fleury led in wins (35), and Matt Murray led in goals against average (2.00).

The Penguins wore their alternate jerseys for all home games during the Stanley Cup Playoffs. The team was involved in several transactions throughout the season, and participated in the 2015 NHL Entry Draft.

Ultimately, the Penguins won the Stanley Cup, their fourth in franchise history, and also were crowned champions of the Eastern Conference.
Terry George (born 14 March 1965) is a British businessman known primarily for his appearances in television programmes. He was born and raised in Leeds, West Yorkshire.

The Rodeo–Chediski Fire was a wildfire that burned in east-central Arizona beginning on June 18, 2002, and was not controlled until July 7. It was the worst forest fire in Arizona's recorded history until June 14, 2011 when the Wallow Fire surpassed it. Several local communities, including Show Low, Pinetop-Lakeside, Heber-Overgaard Clay Springs and Pinedale, were threatened and had to be evacuated.

"I'd Go to Jail" is a song recorded by Canadian country artist Dean Brody, released in October 2021 as the second single from his album *Boys*. It reached number five on the Canada Country chart and number 73 on the Canadian Hot 100.

Therese Ann Markow is a geneticist and ecologist known for her work with Drosophila genetics. She has been a professor at Arizona State University, the University of Arizona, and the University of California, San Diego. Markow is a recipient of the Presidential Early Career Award for Scientists and Engineers and the Genetics Society of America George Beadle Award. Her research has focused on using Drosophila species to understand public health problems.

USS Romulus (ARL-22) was laid down as a United States Navy LST-542-class tank landing ship but converted to one of 39 Achelous-class repair ships that were used for repairing landing craft during World War II. Named for Romulus (along with Remus, one of the legendary twin sons of Mars and the Vestal Rhea Silvia), she was the only US Naval vessel to bear the name.

I've Sound is a Japanese music production group founded in 1998 in Hokkaido by members of Takase Fight. They initially produced music for karaoke and adult game brands, including two tracks for the Dancemania series that were later featured in Dance Dance Revolution.

I've Sound gained prominence through their work on the eroge *Kanon* in 1999, which led to widespread recognition among eroge gamers and later use in the anime adaptation. They released their first compilation album, *Regret*, in December 1999. In 2002, they produced music for the anime series *Please Teacher!*, expanding their international exposure. Several vocalists, including Kotoko, Mami Kawada and Eiko Shimamiya, later made major debuts. I've Sound held their first concert at Nippon Budokan in 2005. The group has seen numerous changes in membership over the years, with some members retiring and others joining. In 2019, Airi Kirishima left the group.
Scrabble variants include Anagrab, Clabbers, Duplicate Scrabble, Ecological Scrabble, Solitaire Scrabble, Speed Scrabble, and more.

Richard Stöhr (born Richard Stern; June 11, 1874 – December 11, 1967) was an Austrian composer, music theorist, and author. Born in Vienna to Jewish parents, he initially pursued a medical degree before dedicating himself to music. He studied composition with Robert Fuchs at the Vienna Academy of Music, where he later taught.

In 1938, Stöhr was dismissed from the Academy due to the Nazi annexation of Austria. He emigrated to the United States in 1939, where he taught at the Curtis Institute of Music and Saint Michael's College. Although he continued to compose prolifically, his later works remained unpublished. Stöhr's compositions, rooted in the 19th-century tonal tradition, include orchestral works, chamber music, operas, and choral pieces.

The Kwai Tsing Theatre (Chinese: 葵青劇院) is a performing arts venue in Kwai Chung, Hong Kong. It was designed by the Architectural Services Department, engineered by Ove Arup & Partners, and constructed by China International Water & Electric Corporation, opening on November 18, 1999. In 2008, the exhibition gallery was converted into a black box theatre.

South Venice is a census-designated place (CDP) in Sarasota County, Florida, United States. The population was 13,949 at the 2010 census. It is part of the Bradenton–Sarasota–Venice Metropolitan Statistical Area.

*Collar × Malice* is a visual novel developed by Idea Factory, as part of their Otomate brand, and published by Aksys Games. It was released on August 18, 2016, in Japan for the PlayStation Vita, and worldwide on July 28, 2017. A Nintendo Switch version was released on June 25, 2020. The story follows Ichika Hoshino, a rookie police officer in Shinjuku, Tokyo, who is collared with poison and forced to investigate a series of murders by a terrorist group. A fan disc, *Collar × Malice: Unlimited*, was released in Japan in 2018. An anime film adaptation is set to premiere in 2023.
Lugazi University was a private university in Lugazi, Uganda, active from 2007 to 2010, when it was converted to a military school.
Roger-Pierre Shah (born November 29, 1972), also known as Roger Shah, is a German DJ, producer, and radio show host/DJ of Balearic trance.

Wilpena Pound – also known by its Adnyamathanha name of Ikara, meaning "meeting place" – is a natural amphitheatre of mountains located 429 kilometres (267 mi) north of Adelaide, South Australia, Australia in the heart of the Ikara-Flinders Ranges National Park. It is accessible via a sealed road which continues on to the northern Flinders Ranges town of Blinman and to the south, Hawker.

Attempts at farming the Pound failed during the early 20th century. Following this, the tourism potential was recognised in 1945.
Keep Britain Tidy is a UK-based independent environmental charity. The organisation campaigns to reduce litter, improve local places and prevent waste. It has offices in Wigan and London.
The Museum of Musical Instruments is a museum in Céret, France, established in 2013. It holds 2,500 instruments, including a large oboe collection.

1,3,2,4-Dithiadiphosphetane 2,4-disulfides are a class of organophosphorus, four-membered ring compounds which contain a P2S2 ring, many of these compounds are able to act as sources of the dithiophosphine ylides. The most well known example of this class of compound is Lawesson's reagent.

Other examples of this class of compound have been made; many inorganic chemists are now using Fc2P2S4 (Fc = ferrocene) as a starting material in reactions investigating the general chemistry of the 1,3,2,4-dithiadiphosphetane 2,4-disulfides, one reaction for this is that the Fc2P2S4 compound and all its derivatives are red which make column chromatography of the products more easy. Also the ferrocenyl groups provide an electrochemical handle which provide another means of investigating the properties of the products.
Yambio Airport (HSYA) is a public airport serving Yambio, South Sudan.
Southfield Football Club, occasionally referred to as Birmingham Southfield, was an English football club based in Birmingham, then in Warwickshire.

The 1979 Memorial Cup was a junior ice hockey tournament held from May 6–13, 1979. The tournament was hosted in Quebec with games played in Sherbrooke, Trois-Rivières, and Verdun. The Peterborough Petes won the tournament, earning their first title. They represented the Ontario Major Junior Hockey League (OMJHL). The Brandon Wheat Kings represented the Western Hockey League (WHL), and the Trois-Rivières Draveurs represented the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League (QMJHL) as the host team.

WUFO (1080 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station licensed to Amherst, New York, and serving the Buffalo metropolitan area. It is owned by the Visions Multi Media Group headed by Sheila. L. Brown and it broadcasts a hybrid Urban AC and Classic Hip Hop radio format. Its studios are on Broadway Avenue in Buffalo.

WUFO transmits 1,000 watts non-directional. WUFO is a daytimer station because 1080 AM is a clear-channel frequency reserved for Class A stations WTIC Hartford and KRLD Dallas, so WUFO must sign off at sunset to avoid interference. The transmitter is on Genesee Street in Cheektowaga. Programming is heard around the clock on 35-watt FM translator W243DX at 96.5 MHz. It uses the FM translator frequency in its moniker, "Power 96.5."

Nerima Wako-Ojiwa (née Nerima Wako), is a Kenyan political analyst, who serves as the executive director of Siasa Place, an organization, led by young people that engages young Kenyans, educating them about the constitution, governance and electoral processes, through community engagements and social media.

*Persicaria minor*, also known as *Polygonum minus*, is a plant species native to South East Asia. It is an edible herb, commonly known as "kesum" in Malaysia and Singapore, where it is used in salads and as a spice in dishes.

The 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship Division I was an international inline hockey tournament run by the International Ice Hockey Federation. The Division I tournament ran alongside the 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship tournament and took place between 25 June and 1 July 2017 in Bratislava, Slovakia at the Ondrej Nepela Arena Rink 2 and Ondrej Nepela Arena. The tournament was won by Slovenia who upon winning gained promotion to the 2019 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship. While New Zealand and Brazil were relegated to the Qualifications after losing their placement round games along with Hungary who lost the relegation game against Argentina.

The Space Force Association is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization founded on October 29, 2019, in Colorado Springs, Colorado. It provides research and support for the United States Space Force and its veterans.

Eucalyptus largiflorens, or black box or river box, is a tree that is endemic to Australia. It has rough, fibrous or flaky bark, dull greenish-grey, lance-shaped leaves, oval to club-shaped green to yellow flower buds, white flowers and hemispherical, cup-shaped or barrel-shaped fruit.
John Duncan (1794–1881) was a British weaver and self-taught botanist, herbalist, and astronomer.
Ruth Cameron is an American record producer and jazz vocalist.
Caesium bisulfate (CsHSO4) is an inorganic compound. Above 141 °C, it becomes a superionic conductor due to high proton activity. It has potential applications in electrochemical devices.

Save Our Waterways Now (SOWN) is an Australian environmental organization focused on habitat restoration and tree planting in the Enoggera catchment in Brisbane. It began in 1994 as a project of Men of the Trees, initiated by Brisbane City Council Alderman Brian Hallinan, bringing together environmental activists and local residents.

SOWN quickly grew into a major organization, utilizing National Heritage Trust funding and the efforts of hundreds of volunteers. By the late 1990s, volunteers had contributed tens of thousands of hours and planted thousands of native trees and grasses. In 2007, SOWN merged with the Brisbane Rainforest Action and Information Network (BRAIN). In 2008, SOWN launched Ithaca Intact, a project to restore the entire length of Ithaca Creek.

*The Sleeping Tiger* is a 1954 British film noir directed by Joseph Losey and starring Alexis Smith, Alexander Knox, and Dirk Bogarde. Based on a novel by Maurice Moiseiwitsch, the film centers on a psychiatrist, Dr. Clive Esmond (Knox), who takes in a young delinquent, Frank Clemmons (Bogarde), as a psychoanalytic experiment. Tensions rise when Esmond's wife, Glenda (Smith), becomes attracted to Frank.

Boane District is a district of Maputo Province in Mozambique. It has an area of 815 km<sup>2</sup> (315 sq mi) and a population of 98,964 as of 2007. The district is divided into two postos, Boane and Matola Rio. The main rivers are the Umbeluzi, Tembe, and Matola Rivers. The climate is subtropical humid. The district has a road network and is served by a railway station on the Goba railway. The main agricultural products are corn, cassava, cowpea, peanut, and sweet potato.
Mouse-Warming is a 1952 Warner Bros. Looney Tunes short directed by Chuck Jones. The cartoon was released on September 8, 1952 and stars Claude Cat in his final solo cartoon.

"Casa Tomada" (English: "House Taken Over") is a 1946 short story by Argentine writer Julio Cortázar. It was originally published in Los anales de Buenos Aires, a literary magazine edited by Jorge Luis Borges, and later included in his volume of stories Bestiario.

It tells the story of a brother and sister living together in their ancestral home which is being "taken over" by unknown entities. It starts in a realist manner and it slowly introduces a scene in which natural laws are distorted. The mystery that revolves around what those entities are is largely left up to interpretation, allowing the genre of the story to vary from fantasy to psychological fiction to magic realism, among others. Among the resources that are frequent in Cortázar's story, graphic signs (such as parenthesis) are used to reflect censorship. The writer based the house on one located in the city of Chivilcoy in the Province of Buenos Aires, which can still be found in the streets Suipacha and Necochea.

Michael Cobbins (born August 9, 1992) is an American professional basketball player for Basket Brescia Leonessa of the Italian Lega Basket Serie A. He played college basketball for Oklahoma State University.

Hans Klumbach (26 April 1904 – 14 December 1992) was a German archaeologist and expert on Roman helmets. He studied at the University of Tübingen and Heidelberg University, earning a Ph.D. in 1927. Klumbach was a curator (1950) and director (1954) at the Romano-Germanic Central Museum, and a professor at Johannes Gutenberg University Mainz (1952).

Petaluma and Santa Rosa Railroad was a 600 volt DC electric interurban railway in Sonoma County, California, United States. It operated between the cities of Petaluma, Sebastopol, Forestville, and Santa Rosa. Company-owned steamboats provided service between Petaluma and San Francisco.

Portions of the original right-of-way have been acquired by Sonoma County government for the West County Trail and Joe Rodota Trail, managed by the Sonoma County Regional Parks Department. A portion of the original right-of-way can be found along the waterfront in Petaluma. Efforts have been made by volunteers to re-activate this line to become a trolley line once again.

Pheona Nabasa Gladys Wall is a Ugandan lawyer and student leader. She is known for her work in politics and leadership. Wall was elected president of the Uganda Law Society in 2020, and has served in various legal and public relations roles.

Four Lane Ends is a Tyne and Wear Metro station in Longbenton, North Tyneside, England, opened in 1980. It serves as a transport interchange with bus services and a large car park. The station features public art installations and provides step-free access.

Eastoft is a village and civil parish in North Lincolnshire, England. It is situated within the Isle of Axholme, 3 miles (5 km) north-east from Crowle, and on the A161 road.

The 2001 census recorded a parish population of 378, increasing to 431 at the 2011 census.
**Dame Thea King DBE FRCM FGSM (1925–2007) was a British clarinetist and professor at the Royal College of Music.**

Ruaraka is a neighborhood located approximately 8 kilometers (5 mi) northeast of Nairobi's central business district, within the sub-county of Kasarani. It is known as a major industrial hub in Nairobi.
Hanauer Straße in Munich is an arterial road about one and a half kilometers long extending from south to north in the Moosach district. It was named after the Hesse city of Hanau.

Nikaho (にかほ市, Nikaho-shi) is a city located in Akita Prefecture, Japan. As of 31 December 2019, the city had an estimated population of 24,291 in 9,371 households, and a population density of 100 persons per km2. The total area of the city is 241.13 square kilometres (93.10 sq mi).

Jacob "Jake" Blankenship (born March 15, 1994) is an American track and field athlete who competes in the pole vault. He holds a personal record of 5.80 m (19 ft 1⁄4 in) for the event, set in 2015. He placed third at the USA Outdoor Track and Field Championships in 2015.

Blankenship won medals at the World Youth Championships in Athletics in 2011 and NACAC Under-23 Championships in Athletics in 2014. He represents Tennessee Volunteers team collegiately and has had top three finishes both indoors and outdoors in NCAA competition, including winning the 2016 Outdoor Championships.

Carfilzomib, sold under the brand name Kyprolis, is an anti-cancer medication acting as a selective proteasome inhibitor. Chemically, it is a tetrapeptide epoxyketone and an analog of epoxomicin. It was developed by Onyx Pharmaceuticals.

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved it on 20 July 2012, for use in people with multiple myeloma who have received at least two prior therapies, including treatment with bortezomib and an immunomodulatory therapy (such as lenalidomide) and have demonstrated disease progression on or within 60 days of completion of the last therapy. Initial approval was based on response rate. Data demonstrating an Overall Survival (OS) benefit was later demonstrated in the ENDEAVOR trial and approved by the FDA.

The abbreviation CFZ is common for referring to carfilzomib, but abbreviating drug names is not best practice in medicine.
James Harold Bradley (Mar 4, 1906 – Nov. 10, 1984), was a pianist and the Founder and Principal of the Bradley Institute for Music Education Research.
Bharatpur, West Bengal Assembly constituency is an assembly constituency in Murshidabad district in the Indian state of West Bengal.
Archibald Corbett, 1st Baron Rowallan (1856 – 1933) was a Scottish Liberal and Liberal Unionist politician who sat in the House of Commons.

Dennis Knight (born December 26, 1968) is an American chef and retired professional wrestler. He is best known for his appearances with World Championship Wrestling between 1992 and 1994 under the ring name Tex Slazenger and with the World Wrestling Federation (WWF, now WWE) between 1996 and 2001 under the ring names Phineas I. Godwinn and Mideon, as well as his real name.

Therese Ann Markow is the Amylin Chair in Life Sciences at the University of California, San Diego. Her research involves the use of genetics and ecology to study the insects of the Sonoran Desert. She was awarded the Presidential Early Career Award for Scientists and Engineers in 2001 and the Genetics Society of America George Beadle Award in 2012. Her research received widespread attention for its alleged misuse of Native American genetic data.

Wang Gi (王祈; 4 June 1149 – fl. 1170), known posthumously as Crown Prince Hyoryeong, was a Korean royal of the Goryeo dynasty. He was the only son of King Uijong and was appointed Crown Prince in 1153. In 1170, he was deposed and exiled following a coup.

Alexis Erica Cohen (October 17, 1983 – July 5, 2009) was an American student and singer, best known for her two unsuccessful auditions on *American Idol*. She died at age 25 after being struck by a hit-and-run driver. The driver was later arrested and charged in connection with her death.

Kerry O'Brien (born 1951) is an Australian politician who was a Senator for Tasmania from 1996 to 2011, representing the Australian Labor Party. He was also a trade union official and held shadow ministerial positions.

The PKP class ET22 is a Polish six-axle electric freight locomotive. PKP bought 1,183 locomotives from 1971 to 1990, making it the most numerous standard gauge electric locomotive built in Europe. A modernized version, ET22-2000, was produced in 2004.

Potamia (Greek: Ποταμιά, Turkish: Potamya, Bodamya and Dereliköy) is a village in the north-east of Cyprus in the district of Nicosia, close to the Green Line separating it from the area of the breakaway, unrecognised Northern Cyprus. Together with Pyla and Rizokarpaso, Potamia forms one of the few remaining ethnically mixed communities in Cyprus, made up of Greek and Turkish Cypriots.

Birna Arnbjörnsdóttir (born 1952) is an Icelandic linguist specializing in second-language acquisition. She has taught at Notre Dame College and the University of Iceland, and is known for her research on language contact, bilingualism, and Icelandic as a heritage language.

The 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship Division I was an international inline hockey tournament held in Slovakia from June 25 to July 1, 2017. Eight teams participated, with Slovenia winning the championship and earning promotion to the 2019 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship. Latvia finished as the runner-up, and Australia took third place. Qualification tournaments in Asia/Oceania and Europe determined the final participants, with New Zealand and Latvia securing promotion, respectively. The tournament featured a preliminary round followed by a playoff round, with teams seeded based on previous results.

Messier 83 (M83), also known as the Southern Pinwheel Galaxy, is a barred spiral galaxy in the constellation Hydra. Classified as SAB(s)c, it exhibits a weak bar, pure spiral structure, and loosely wound arms. Located 14.7 million light-years away, M83 is a massive, grand design spiral galaxy and a member of the Centaurus A/M83 Group. Star formation is notably high along the leading edges of its spiral arms. Six supernovae have been observed in M83.

The geoduck (*Panopea generosa*) is a species of very large, edible, saltwater clam in the family Hiatellidae. The name "geoduck" is derived from a Lushootseed word, *gwídəq*, which may refer to the clam's shape or the act of digging deep. It is also known as a mud duck, king clam, or elephant-trunk clam. Native to the Pacific Northwest coast of North America, it is the largest burrowing clam in the world, with specimens weighing over 7 kilograms (15 lb) and living up to 140 years.

Geoducks are filter feeders, consuming plankton through their long siphons. They have few natural predators, contributing to their longevity. While a female geoduck can produce billions of eggs, populations are slow to rebound due to low recruitment rates.

The high demand for geoduck, particularly in China, has created an $80-million industry. Geoduck farming practices have raised environmental concerns, leading to ongoing studies and regulations. The clam is prized for its savory flavor and crunchy texture, and is used in various cuisines, including Chinese hot pot, Korean dishes, and Japanese sashimi.

The University of St. Thomas School of Law is a private law school located in downtown Minneapolis, Minnesota. Established in 1923, it closed in 1933 due to the Great Depression, then reopened in 1999. It was accredited by the American Bar Association in 2006. The school is known for its mentor externship program, which has received national recognition. St. Thomas Law offers Juris Doctor, master's, and LL.M. degrees, with a focus on practical training and social justice. As of 2022, U.S. News & World Report ranked it 126th among law schools.

North Hollywood High School is a public high school located in North Hollywood, California, established in 1927. Part of the Los Angeles Unified School District, it serves grades 9-12 with an enrollment of over 2,500 students. The school is known for its three magnet programs: the Highly Gifted Magnet, the Zoo Magnet, and the STEM Magnet. North Hollywood High School's mascot is the Huskies.
Mount Gould is a peak in the Sierra Nevada, on the Fresno/Inyo county line.

Aleksei Fedorovich Losev (Lang-ru; 23 September 1893 – 24 May 1988) was a Russian philosopher, philologist and culturologist, one of the most prominent figures in Russian philosophical and religious thought of the 20th century.
Alimi Ballard (born October 17, 1977) is an American actor. He is known for roles in *Numb3rs*, *Sabrina the Teenage Witch*, and *The City*.

SMS *Leopard* was a *Panther*-class cruiser built for the Austro-Hungarian Navy in the mid-1880s. Laid down in January 1885, launched in September of the same year, and completed in March 1886, the ship was armed with two 12 cm (4.7 in) guns, four 47 mm (1.9 in) quick-firing guns, six 47 mm revolver cannon, and four 14 in (356 mm) torpedo tubes.

*Leopard* participated in fleet maneuvers and represented Austria-Hungary at the Barcelona Universal Exposition in 1888. After running aground in 1888, she underwent repairs and spent periods in reserve and with the active squadron throughout the 1890s. In 1897–1898, she served in the International Squadron off Crete during the Greek uprising. From 1900 to 1901, *Leopard* toured the Pacific Ocean, including visits to Australia, Polynesia, and East Asia, and was temporarily assigned to the East Asia squadron. She made another deployment to East Asia from 1907 to 1909.

From 1910 to 1913, *Leopard* was placed in reserve and rearmed. During World War I, she served as a harbor guard ship in Pola. In 1920, she was ceded to Britain as a war prize but was ultimately sold to ship breakers in Italy and scrapped.

Mesa/Boogie is an American musical instrument company specializing in amplifiers. It was founded by Randall Smith in 1969 in Petaluma, California, where it is headquartered. Smith started out repairing amplifiers and hot-rodding Fender Princeton amps, one of which led to the company's name after Carlos Santana exclaimed that "This thing boogies!".

In 1972, Mesa/Boogie released the Mark I, the first high-gain amplifier. The company continued to develop new models, including the Rectifier series in the 1990s, which became a staple of modern rock guitar tone. In 2021, Mesa/Boogie was acquired by Gibson, with Smith joining Gibson as "Master Designer and Pioneer of Mesa/Boogie and beyond".

UFOria is a science fiction comedy film written and directed by John Binder and starring Fred Ward, Harry Dean Stanton, Harry Carey, Jr. and Cindy Williams. The film includes small appearances by Peggy McKay, Joe Unger, Hank Worden, Charlotte Stewart, and Alan Beckwith. Filming was completed in 1981, but the film was not theatrically released until 1985. It was released only on VHS in 1987 by MCA Home Video, now known as Universal Studios Home Entertainment.
Kristine Jensen (born 1956) is a Danish architect known for landscape architecture and urban revival projects.
Chuck Wichgers (born 1965) is an American politician and Republican member of the Wisconsin State Assembly.

Lucius Julius Iulus was a Roman politician, who was consular tribune in 401 BC. His colleagues were Lucius Valerius Potitus, Marcus Furius Camillus, Manius Aemilius Mamercinus, Gnaeus Cornelius Cossus, and Caeso Fabius Ambustus. He was consular tribune again in 397 BC.

Alan Edgar Bristow, OBE, FRAeS (3 September 1923 – 26 April 2009) founded one of the world's largest helicopter service companies, Bristow Helicopters Ltd, which prospered primarily in the international oil and mineral exploration and extraction industries, but also spread into search and rescue, peacekeeping and other fields.

*American Zombie* is a 2007 American mockumentary film directed by Grace Lee. It follows a documentary crew investigating the zombie subculture in Los Angeles, exploring themes of identity politics and social satire.

Charles Jackson "Bud" Wildman (born June 3, 1946) is a Canadian politician.
