He served in the Legislative Assembly of Ontario as a New Democratic Party Member of Provincial Parliament (MPP) from 1975 to 1999, representing the riding of Algoma, and was a cabinet minister in the government of Bob Rae.

San Miguel High School is a private Catholic and Lasallian college and college preparatory school located on the south side of Tucson, Arizona, United States. It is a member of the Cristo Rey Network of work-study schools.

Nancy Duff Campbell is an American lawyer and a founder and co-president of the National Women's Law Center. Campbell has focused on women's law and public policy issues and has participated in the development of legislative initiatives and litigation regarding women's rights, emphasizing issues affecting low‐income women, and has authored articles on women's legal issues.

*Tajemnica Westerplatte* (English titles include *The Secret of Westerplatte* and *1939 Battle of Westerplatte*) is a 2013 Polish-Lithuanian war film directed by Paweł Chochlew. The film depicts the Battle of Westerplatte, a key event in the opening of World War II. It generated controversy, with some critics alleging an "anti-Polish" and "unpatriotic" revisionist attitude toward the battle's heroic myth.
Kate Addo (born July 23, 1972) is a Ghanaian journalist, broadcaster, and public relations practitioner. She is the Director of Public Affairs of the Parliament of Ghana.

Hyme House is a villa on Fitzjohn's Avenue in Hampstead, London, built in 1886 in the Arts and Crafts style. From 1921 to 1937, it was the residence and studio of portrait painter Philip de László, who hosted royal figures such as Princess Elizabeth there.

Substance Church is an Evangelical multi-site megachurch founded in 2004 by Pastor Peter Haas. Known for its youthful congregation and contemporary worship style incorporating rap and electronic dance music, Substance has permanent facilities in Spring Lake Park and Minneapolis. The church emphasizes cell church methodology and church planting, assisting various denominational and non-denominational churches.
*Perla* is a Mexican telenovela produced by TV Azteca starring Silvia Navarro and Leonardo Garcia. It aired in 1998 with 230 episodes.
Julie Baroh (born August 4, 1970) is an American fantasy artist.

Mafdet (also Mefdet, Maftet) was a goddess in the ancient Egyptian religion. She was often depicted wearing a skin of a cheetah, and protected against the bite of snakes and scorpions. She is part of the ancient Egyptian deities during the First Dynasty of Egypt. She was prominent during the reign of pharaoh Den whose image appears on stone vessel fragments from his tomb and is mentioned in a dedicatory entry in the Palermo Stone. Mafdet was the deification of legal justice, or possibly of capital punishment. She was associated with the protection of the king's chambers and other sacred places, and with protection against venomous animals, which were seen as transgressors against Maat. In the Pyramid Texts of the Old Kingdom of Egypt, she is mentioned as protecting the sun god Ra from venomous snakes.
Al-Majd (Arabic: خربة المجد) is a Palestinian village located in the Hebron Governorate in the southern West Bank. The population in 2007 was 1,925.

*Ghatak: Lethal* is a 1996 Indian Hindi-language action drama film directed by Rajkumar Santoshi, starring Sunny Deol, Meenakshi Seshadri, and Danny Denzongpa. The film revolves around Kashi (Deol), a wrestler who moves to Mumbai with his father for medical treatment and confronts the tyrannical gangster Katya (Denzongpa).

The soundtrack was primarily composed by R. D. Burman, with one song by Anu Malik. *Ghatak* was a major commercial success, grossing ₹ 44 crore against a budget of ₹ 6.25 crore.

The 1957 Alberta liquor plebiscite was a province-wide plebiscite conducted in Alberta, Canada The plebiscite asked voters if they were in favour of adding extra ALCB outlets in their district. It was held on October 30, 1957. At the time, the Alberta Liquor Control Board operated only a few stores from which liquor could be legally purchased.

A vote among egg producers was also held on the question of an egg marketing board.

An additional vote was held regarding mixed drinking in the major cities, also on October 30, 1957.

The 1957 votes were not held in conjunction with an Alberta election.

Herald Scholarly Open Access is a publisher of various academic journals. It has a postal address in Herndon, Virginia, United States, but is actually based in Hyderabad, India. Herald Scholarly Open Access has been included on Beall's List of potential predatory open-access publishers, and has faced other criticisms of its publishing practices.

Wild Jack is a 1974 young adult science fiction novel written by the English prize-winning author John Christopher. Set in twenty-third century England, the plot concerns the young aristocrat Clive Anderson, who lives in decadent isolation within one of many walled-in hierarchal city-states, whose walls keep out the dangerous wilderness of the Outlands, and the barbaric savages known to hide there. When Clive is falsely accused of questioning the status quo, he is sent off to a brutal prison island. Faced with hunger and cruelty, he begins to truly question the good of his world.
Franziska Junge (born 1981 in Zschopau, East Germany) is a German actress for theatre, film, television as well as a singer.
The inSpiral Lounge was a vegetarian restaurant, music venue, and online retailer in London. Founded in 2007, it focused on mindful choices and community spirit. It closed in 2016.

Andrée Geulen-Herscovici (6 September 1921 – 31 May 2022) was a Belgian teacher and member of the resistance during the German occupation of Belgium during World War II. After becoming aware of the persecution of Belgian Jews through her employment, she became involved in the Committee for the Defence of Jews (Comité de Défense des Juifs, or CDJ; Joods Verdedigingscomité, JVC) in 1943 and actively assisted with the organisation and protection of "hidden children" amid the Holocaust in Belgium. In the aftermath of the war, she was recognised as Righteous among the Nations by the Israeli institute Yad Vashem and received several other honours in Belgium and Israel.

The Anatolian diagonal is a theoretical dividing line which runs diagonally across central and eastern Turkey from the northeastern corner of the Mediterranean Sea to the southeastern corner of the Black Sea, and roughly cuts across part of the course of the upper Euphrates River within Turkey's borders.
Ari is a given name in many languages and cultures, for both men and women. It also may be a nickname for a wide variety of unrelated names.
Remnantz is a historic house near Marlow, Buckinghamshire, built around 1720. It housed the Royal Military Academy from 1802 to 1811.
Gerhard vom Rath (20 August 1830 – 23 April 1888), was a German mineralogist, born at Duisburg in Prussia.
Frederick William Fairholt (1814 – 3 April 1866) was an English antiquary and wood-engraver.

The Zec de la Rivière-Blanche (or ZEC of White River, in English) is a zone d'exploitation contrôlée ('controlled harvesting zone') (ZEC), located in the unorganized territory of Lac-Blanc, Quebec, in Portneuf Regional County Municipality, in Upper Batiscanie, in the administrative area of the Capitale-Nationale, on the north shore of St. Lawrence River, Quebec, Canada.

Abdulai Conteh (born August 5, 1945) is a Sierra Leonean and Belizean jurist and politician. He served as the Chief Justice of Belize from 2000 to 2010. Prior to this, he held several ministerial positions in Sierra Leone, including Minister of Foreign Affairs, Minister of Finance, and First Vice-President.

In molecular biology, this protein domain belongs to the terpene synthase family (TPS). Its role is to synthesize terpenes, which are part of primary metabolism, such as sterols and carotene, and also part of the secondary metabolism. This entry will focus on the C terminal domain of the TPS protein.
*Mouse-Warming* is a 1952 *Merrie Melodies* cartoon directed by Chuck Jones and written by Michael Maltese, about a young mouse who falls in love with the girl next door.

David John Lodge CBE (born 28 January 1935) is an English author and critic. A literature professor at the University of Birmingham until 1987, some of his novels satirise academic life, notably the "Campus Trilogy" – Changing Places: A Tale of Two Campuses (1975), Small World: An Academic Romance (1984) and Nice Work (1988). The second two were shortlisted for the Booker Prize. Another theme is Roman Catholicism, beginning from his first published novel The Picturegoers (1960). Lodge has also written television screenplays and three stage plays. After retiring, he continued to publish literary criticism. His edition of Twentieth Century Literary Criticism (1972) includes essays on 20th-century writers such as T. S. Eliot.

Emerich Vogl (12 August 1905 – 29 October 1971) was a Romanian football player and manager. He played as a defender and midfielder for Chinezul Timișoara and Juventus București, winning five Liga I titles with the former. Vogl also played for the Romania national team, participating in the 1930 and 1934 FIFA World Cups.

Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) is a technique used in mass spectrometry to visualize the spatial distribution of molecules, as biomarkers, metabolites, peptides or proteins by their molecular masses. After collecting a mass spectrum at one spot, the sample is moved to reach another region, and so on, until the entire sample is scanned. By choosing a peak in the resulting spectra that corresponds to the compound of interest, the MS data is used to map its distribution across the sample. This results in pictures of the spatially resolved distribution of a compound pixel by pixel. Each data set contains a veritable gallery of pictures because any peak in each spectrum can be spatially mapped. Despite the fact that MSI has been generally considered a qualitative method, the signal generated by this technique is proportional to the relative abundance of the analyte. Therefore, quantification is possible, when its challenges are overcome. Although widely used traditional methodologies like radiochemistry and immunohistochemistry achieve the same goal as MSI, they are limited in their abilities to analyze multiple samples at once, and can prove to be lacking if researchers do not have prior knowledge of the samples being studied. Most common ionization technologies in the field of MSI are DESI imaging, MALDI imaging and secondary ion mass spectrometry imaging (SIMS imaging).

*The Show Must Go On* is a 2007 South Korean film directed by Han Jae-rim, starring Song Kang-ho. The film is about In-gu, a gangster who struggles to balance his criminal life with his family life. It was released on April 5, 2007, and won Best Film and Best Actor at the 28th Blue Dragon Film Awards.
John Mortimer Boudreaux, Jr. (December 10, 1936 – January 14, 2017) was an American rhythm and blues drummer. He is best known for his prolific studio work in New Orleans.
Robert "Robbie" Thorpe is an Aboriginal Australian activist and presenter of Fire First, a program on community radio station 3CR in Melbourne.

Araxá (IPA-pt) is a municipality in Western Minas Gerais state, Brazil. Its estimated population by IBGE (Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics) in 2020 is 107,337 inhabitants and the area of the municipality is 1,283 square kilometres (495 sq mi), with 3.45 square kilometres (1.33 sq mi) making up the urban perimeter.

Lewis James Emanuel (born 4 October 1983) is an English former professional footballer who played as a left-back or left-winger. He began his career with Bradford City in 2001, playing for them until 2006. He also played for Luton Town, including a loan spell at Brentford. In December 2013, Emanuel was sentenced to eight years in prison for robbery and possession of an imitation firearm.

The Codex Boturini, also known as Tira de la Peregrinación de los Mexica, is an Aztec codex created in the early 16th century. Named after Lorenzo Boturini Benaduci, an 18th-century collector, it is a 549 cm-long amate sheet folded into 21.5 pages.

The codex, likely by a single tlacuilo familiar with the Aztec writing system, appears unfinished, using only red ink to link date blocks. It contains 24 Nahuatl glosses added after its creation. The codex depicts the Mexica migration from Aztlán from 1168 to 1355 AD, their alliance with Colhuacan, and ends just before the founding of Tenochtitlan. Housed in Mexico's National Museum of Anthropology, a digitized version is available online since 2015.

The Bronze Fonz is a public artwork by American artist Gerald P. Sawyer, located on the Milwaukee Riverwalk in downtown Milwaukee, Wisconsin. The Bronze Fonz depicts Henry Winkler as "The Fonz," a character in the 1970s television series Happy Days, which was set in Milwaukee.

Burlington Street is a partially at grade and elevated roadway in Hamilton, Ontario, stretching along the south shore of Hamilton Harbour in Lake Ontario. Burlington Street has four different statuses along the route. In its western terminus, it is a collector route ending at Bay Street North. Upon the intersection at Wellington Street, the road becomes an arterial route with four lanes. The Street ends at Parkdale Avenue. On October 14, 2015, city council approved the renaming of the upper portion of Burlington Street from 165 meters east of Ottawa St to the QEW to Nikola Tesla Boulevard.

Nikola Tesla Boulevard is the official exit #90 from QEW. The signage along the QEW for Burlington Street was eventually replaced between October 22-23, 2016 to reflect the name change. After this change, the westbound lanes of Burlington street begin from the first exit on Nikola Tesla Boulevard, heading westbound.
William Frost (28 May 1848 – March 1935) was a Welsh designer of an early flying machine, the Frost Airship Glider.

Katja Hessel (born May 5, 1972) is a German politician of the Free Democratic Party (FDP). She has been a member of the Bundestag since 2017, representing the Nuremberg North district. Hessel was previously a State Secretary in the Bavarian State Ministry of Economic Affairs from 2008 to 2013.

Qualified teacher status (QTS) or Qualified teacher learning and skills status (QTLS) is required in England and Wales to work as a teacher of children in state schools under local authority control, and in special education schools. A similar status exists under a different name in Scotland and Northern Ireland.

High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is a type of cancer that primarily affects the peritoneum, the serous membrane lining the abdominopelvic cavity. HGSC is the most common type of ovarian cancer. Risk factors include the number of ovulatory cycles, menopausal hormone therapy, and genetic mutations in BRCA1, BRCA2, and TP53. Symptoms include persistent bloating, postmenopausal bleeding, and appetite loss.

Diagnosis involves transvaginal ultrasonography, CA125 level analysis, and surgical staging with tissue biopsies. Prevention strategies include salpingo-oophorectomy (removal of the ovaries and fallopian tubes), salpingectomy (removal of the fallopian tubes), and tubal ligation. Treatment typically involves cytoreductive surgery followed by platinum-based chemotherapy. PARP inhibitors are another treatment option, particularly for individuals with BRCA1/2 mutations. Survival rates for ovarian cancer have improved since 1975, but racial disparities in mortality exist.

Bakhtyuras Besikbayev (1920 – June 26, 1941) was a Kazakh Junior Sergeant in the Soviet Air Force. He served as a rear gunner and radio operator. He was killed in action during the Battle of Białystok–Minsk and posthumously awarded Hero of Kazakhstan and Hero of the Russian Federation for his bravery.

Cipriano di Michele Piccolpasso (1524 – 21 November 1579) was a member of an Italian patrician family of Bologna that had been settled since the mid-fifteenth century in Castel Durante, which was an important center for the manufacture of maiolica. Today he is remembered for writing Li tre libri dell'arte del vasajo ("The three books of the potter's art"), which are a storehouse of information on the techniques of maiolica from the choice of clays and their refinement, the shaping of the body, the composition of the glazes, to the preparation of the colors. The work"is now widely accepted as the first comprehensive account of the manufacture of any kind of pottery ever produced in Europe".

His brother operated a maiolica workshop, but it is not clear how much hands-on potting experience Cipriano himself had. He mentions that he had never used lustreware pigments, which might imply that he had used other types. His account of workshop techniques is in places unclear and hard to follow, either because he did not understand the process himself, or had difficulty forming a written description. The date of the book is uncertain, but most writers follow Bernard Rackham who suggested it (or at least the manuscript in the Victoria and Albert Museum) was written between around 1556 and 1559.
Newman O'Neal was the mayor of Hobson City, Alabama, until death threats forced him into exile in July 1919.

Allison Jones (born July 13, 1955) is an American casting director. Since 1982, she has worked on numerous television shows and films, including *Freaks and Geeks*, *Parks and Recreation*, *The Office*, *The 40-Year-Old Virgin*, and *Booksmart*. Jones won an Emmy Award for her work on *Freaks and Geeks*, and was nominated for her work on *Arrested Development*.

Susan Jean Silver (born July 17, 1958) is an American music manager, best known for managing Seattle rock bands such as Soundgarden, Alice in Chains and Screaming Trees. Silver also owns the company Susan Silver Management, and co-owns the club The Crocodile in Seattle. Silver was named "the most powerful figure in local rock management" by The Seattle Times in 1991.

Susan Jean Silver (born July 17, 1958) is an American music manager and entrepreneur. Active since 1983, she is known for managing Soundgarden and Alice in Chains during their rise to fame in the grunge era. Silver co-owns The Crocodile club in Seattle. She was married to Chris Cornell from 1990 to 2004.

Birna Arnbjörnsdóttir (born 1952) is an Icelandic linguist. She is Professor of Second Language Studies at the University of Iceland, Director of the Vigdís Finnbogadóttir Institute of Foreign Languages and Dean of the Faculty of Languages and Cultures.

Vahida Maglajlić (17 April 1907 – 1 April 1943) was a Bosnian communist and Partisan fighter during World War II, posthumously awarded the Order of the People's Hero. Born in Banja Luka to a prominent Bosniak family, she became involved in the labor movement and women's rights activism, defying social norms by cutting her hair short and refusing marriage offers that demanded she abandon her activism.

Following the Axis invasion of Yugoslavia and the establishment of the Independent State of Croatia in 1941, Maglajlić joined the Communist Party and organized resistance efforts in Banja Luka. She used her family's reputation to shelter communists, gather supplies, and mobilize support for the Partisans, encouraging other Muslims to join the cause. After escaping from Ustaše prison in December 1941, she joined the Partisans and engaged in political work, particularly among women in the Kozara, Grmeč and Cazin regions. She was elected to the Central Committee of the Women's Antifascist Front of Bosnia and Herzegovina in December 1942. Maglajlić was killed in action in Mala Krupska Rujiška in April 1943, during the Fourth Enemy Offensive. She is the only Bosnian Muslim woman to be named a People's Hero of Yugoslavia.

The Commissioner of the Delhi Police heads the Delhi Police since the adoption of the commissioner system in 1978, replacing the Inspector General of Police. The current commissioner is Sanjay Arora, appointed in 2022.

Persicaria minor is species of herb in the family Polygonaceae. Common names include pygmy smartweed, small water pepper and swamp willow weed. This herb is native to Asia, but distributed widely in Europe and Australia. It is used in South East Asian cooking.
Lorenzo Abel Faravelli (born 29 March 1993) is an Argentine footballer who plays as a central midfielder for Ecuadorian club Independiente del Valle.

The Queen Sirikit Museum of Textiles is a history and textile museum located within the Grand Palace in Bangkok, Thailand. Originally established in 1873 as the Ratsadakorn-bhibhathana Building, it was intended to centralize Siam's taxing system. In 2003, Queen Sirikit requested permission to renovate the vacant building into a textile museum.

Diana Lee Inosanto (born in Torrance, California) is an American actress, martial artist, choreographer, film director, and author. She is the daughter of Dan Inosanto and is married to Ron Balicki. Inosanto is known for her role as Magistrate Morgan Elsbeth in *The Mandalorian*.

Benedicto "Bong" Ducat (born May 6, 1957) is a Filipino painter. He is known for his paintings of nature. Ducat graduated from the University of Santo Tomas College of Fine Arts and Design and earned a master's degree at University of the Philippines, Diliman. He is currently a professor at the University of Santo Tomas.

Contour crafting is a building printing technology being researched by Behrokh Khoshnevis of the University of Southern California's Information Sciences Institute (in the Viterbi School of Engineering) that uses a computer-controlled crane or gantry to build edifices rapidly and efficiently with substantially less manual labor. It was originally conceived as a method to construct molds for industrial parts. Khoshnevis decided to adapt the technology for rapid home construction as a way to rebuild after natural disasters, like the devastating earthquakes that have plagued his native Iran.

Using a quick-setting, concrete-like material, contour crafting forms the house's walls layer by layer until topped off by floors and ceilings set in place by the crane. The notional concept calls for the insertion of structural components, plumbing, wiring, utilities, and even consumer devices like audiovisual systems as the layers are built.

The Oregon State University Foundation is an American private nonprofit corporation dedicated to enhancing the mission of Oregon State University in Corvallis, Oregon. The OSU Foundation is governed by a volunteer Board of Trustees.

"The Crocodile" is the fourth episode of the second season of the American ABC fantasy/drama television series Once Upon a Time, and the show's 26th episode overall, which aired on October 21, 2012.

It was co-written by David H. Goodman and Robert Hull, while being directed by David Solomon.

In this episode, Mr. Gold, David, and Ruby go searching for a missing Belle, while flashbacks show Rumpelstiltskin's past with Captain Hook.

The 1945 College Football All-America team is composed of college football players who were selected as All-Americans by various organizations and writers that chose College Football All-America Teams in 1945. The nine selectors recognized by the NCAA as "official" for the 1945 season are (1) Collier's Weekly, as selected by Grantland Rice, (2) the Associated Press, (3) the United Press, (4) the All-America Board, (5) the American Football Coaches Association (AFCA), (6) the Football Writers Association of America (FWAA), (7) the International News Service (INS), (8) Look magazine, (9) the Newspaper Enterprise Association (NEA) and (10) the Sporting News.

*The 1900 House* is a historical reenactment reality television series that originated in the United Kingdom and also had a U.S. version. The UK version, broadcast on Channel 4, consists of 10 episodes, while the U.S. version, broadcast on PBS, has 5 episodes. The series places a modern family in a late Victorian-era home, stripped of modern conveniences like electricity, central heating, and indoor plumbing. Located at 50 Elliscombe Road, Charlton, London, the house was refitted with period fixtures, including gas lighting, a cast-iron oven, and fireplaces. The family was assisted by a consultant historian, Daru Rooke, and initially hired a maid-of-all-work, though she was later dismissed. The show's success led to other related productions.

Clyde Lee Chambliss Jr. (born April 16, 1969) is an American Republican politician who has served in the Alabama Senate from the 30th district since 2014. He completed his degree in Civil Engineering from the University of Alabama in 1992, after which he started his civil engineering firm. Chambliss and his wife Tara Herring Chambliss have three daughters, Kristen, Lauren, and Madison.

"Bizarre Love Triangle" is a song by the English rock band New Order, released as a single on November 5, 1986. It is from their fourth studio album, *Brotherhood*. The song is synth-pop and alternative dance, and was produced by New Order.

The single mix features more electronics than the album version. A music video, directed by Robert Longo, was released in November 1986 and features imagery based on Longo's "Men in the Cities" series.

"Bizarre Love Triangle" has received critical acclaim since its release. It has been described as a "synth-pop masterpiece" by *Billboard* and New Order's "finest pop moment" by *NME*. It was ranked No. 204 in *Rolling Stone*'s list of "The 500 Greatest Songs of All Time" in 2004.

The Aeroprakt A-22 Foxbat is a Ukrainian two-seat, high-wing, single-engine ultralight aircraft designed by Aeroprakt in 1990, with the first prototype flying in 1996. A German-certified version entered production in 1999.

The A-22 is primarily metal in construction, with fabric-covered wings and control surfaces. It features extensive glazing for excellent visibility, including convex doors. It utilizes a 3-axis control system and flaperons, resulting in a low stall speed. The aircraft is typically powered by a Rotax 912UL or 912ULS engine, with the Jabiru 2200 also being an option.
Frank Simmons Leavitt (June 30, 1891 – May 29, 1953) was an American professional wrestler of the early 1900s, known by the ring name Man Mountain Dean.
Herbert Willoughby Ellis (born in Woodville, Burton-upon-Trent) was a British entomologist and collector.

An ultrasonic motor is a type of piezoelectric motor powered by the ultrasonic vibration of a component, the stator, placed against another component, the rotor or slider depending on the scheme of operation (rotation or linear translation). Ultrasonic motors differ from other piezoelectric motors in several ways, though both typically use some form of piezoelectric material, most often lead zirconate titanate and occasionally lithium niobate or other single-crystal materials. The most obvious difference is the use of resonance to amplify the vibration of the stator in contact with the rotor in ultrasonic motors. Ultrasonic motors also offer arbitrarily large rotation or sliding distances, while piezoelectric actuators are limited by the static strain that may be induced in the piezoelectric element.

One common application of ultrasonic motors is in camera lenses where they are used to move lens elements as part of the auto-focus system. Ultrasonic motors replace the noisier and often slower micro-motor in this application.

The 1964 Nyasaland general election was announced on Radio Malawi on November 17, 1963. Voters were registered on a general roll for Africans, Asians, and mixed-race people, and a special roll for Europeans. Ultimately, 1,871,790 people registered on the general roll. The initial plan was for the general roll to elect 50 members and the special roll five, but the number of special roll seats was reduced to three. The Malawi Congress Party (MCP) announced its 50 candidates in March 1964 and worked to ensure it would face no opposition in the elections.

South Burnie is a locality in the City of Burnie, Tasmania, Australia. It is situated 2.5 km (2 mi) southeast of Burnie. At the 2016 census, South Burnie had a population of 331.

The municipal area of which South Burnie is part was proclaimed a city in 1988, having previously been named Emu Bay Municipality. Burnie, and thus South Burnie, was named for William Burnie, a director of the Van Diemen's Land Company. The C112 route (Massy-Greene Drive) terminates at the Bass Highway in South Burnie.
Eileen Dennes (1 February 1898 – 22 January 1991) was an Irish-born actress of the silent era.

Muhammad Said Al-Attar (1927 – 2005) was a Yemeni economist, politician, and diplomat. He held various ministerial positions in Yemen, including Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Economy. Al-Attar also served as the Executive Secretary of the United Nations Economic Commission for Western Asia (ESCWA) and as Yemen's Permanent Representative to the United Nations.

Theodorick Bland (December 2, 1708 – June 1, 1784) was a Virginia planter, politician, and militia colonel. He represented Isle of Wight, Surry, and Prince George counties in the Virginia Senate from 1776 to 1779. Bland owned the Cawsons and Kippax plantations.

**Donkeyboy** is a Norwegian synthpop band from Drammen, formed in 2005. They signed with Warner Music after being discovered on MySpace. Their debut single, "Ambitions," topped the Norwegian charts for twelve weeks in 2009.

Gold Hill, also known as Goldhill, Gold Mine, or Gold Ridge, is an unincorporated community north-centrally located in Lee County, Alabama, United States, just a few hundred feet south of the Chambers County line. It is part of the Columbus, Georgia-Alabama Metropolitan Area. Today, Gold Hill lies mostly in the corporate limits of Auburn.

NBC Nightside (also known as NBC News Nightside) is an American overnight news broadcasting program on NBC, that aired from 1991 to 1998. The program was produced in three half-hour segments. It usually aired live seven nights a week, and was fed to NBC stations beginning at 2:00 am ET Sunday through Friday (immediately following Later) and 2:30 am ET on Saturdays (after Friday Night Videos), and looped until the next morning. (NBC News at Sunrise followed Nightside on weekdays.)

Economic policy encompasses government actions influencing a nation's economy. It aims to achieve goals like controlling inflation, unemployment, and promoting economic growth, using tools such as interest rates, taxes, and regulations. Governments balance competing goals, choosing between discretionary policies for flexibility and rule-based policies for credibility. Historically, economic policy evolved from resource acquisition through taxation and coinage to modern fiscal policy and capital markets. The business cycle emerged as a key concern, with debates on interventionist versus limited government roles. Evidence-based policy, a recent trend, uses empirical evidence to justify economic decisions.

FC Stal Kamianske (Lang-uk) was a professional Ukrainian football club based in Kamianske, Dnipropetrovsk Oblast, Ukraine. After being relegated to the Ukrainian First League after the 2017–18 Ukrainian Premier League season, the club re-registered to Bucha and changed its name to PFC Feniks Bucha in June 2018. The club didn't play any game under the new name and was dissolved on 9 July 2018.

The club was sponsored by the Dnieper Metallurgical Combine (DMK) which is a member of the Industrial Union of Donbas (ISD).

Gerard Alsteens (born 3 August 1940, in Auderghem) is a Belgian political cartoonist, graphic artist and painter who works under the pseudonym "GAL". which, artistically speaking, share a closer resemblance to paintings than regularly drawn cartoons. Throughout the years his work has received several prizes and awards.

Publius Sulpicius Quirinius was a Roman aristocrat who served as governor of Syria from 6 to 12 AD. He is known for conducting a census of Judea, which sparked a revolt. Historical and archaeological sources confirm his life and career, including his consulship in 12 BC and his role in Armenia.

The Black Country derby is the name given to football matches between West Bromwich Albion and Wolverhampton Wanderers, the two major clubs in the Black Country. With both clubs being founder members of the Football League in 1888, the derby is one of the oldest in league football. The first match was played in 1883, with Albion winning 4–2.

Tom Gorman (born January 19, 1946) is an American former tennis player. He reached the semifinals at Wimbledon (1971), the US Open (1972), and the French Open (1973). Gorman was a member of the winning U.S. Davis Cup team in 1972, and captain-coach of the winning teams in 1990 and 1992.
*Space Under Sun* is the second studio album by Ari Gold, released in 2004.

George Washington's reception at Trenton was a celebration hosted by the Ladies of Trenton social club on April 21, 1789, in Trenton, New Jersey, as George Washington, then president-elect, journeyed from his home at Mount Vernon to his first inauguration in the then capital of the United States, New York City. A ceremonial triumphal arch was erected on the bridge over the Assunpink Creek to commemorate his two victories here, the Battle of Trenton on December 26, 1776 and the Battle of the Assunpink Creek on January 2, 1777.

Conrad Albert 'Connie' Lau (February 8, 1921 – April 18, 1964) was an American aeronautical engineer, inventor, and executive. Lau led or contributed to the development of a number of important aircraft and spacecraft projects.

*The Holy Science* is a book by Sri Yukteswar Giri comparing Sanskrit *slokas* to passages from the New Testament, especially the Book of Revelation. It is divided into four sections corresponding to the development of knowledge. The book also presents Sri Yukteswar's Yuga Cycle theory, which posits that the earth is currently in Dwapara Yuga.

Future Strategic Tanker Aircraft (FSTA) is a British project to procure Airbus A330 Multi Role Tanker Transport aerial refuelling (AR) and air transport (AT) aircraft for the Royal Air Force, to replace older models such as the VC10s and TriStars.

After evaluation of bids the RAF selected the AirTanker consortium, owned by Cobham plc, EADS, Rolls-Royce plc, Thales UK and VT Group plc, in 2004 offering the Airbus A330 MRTT.

Tales of the Heroes: Twin Brave is an action game in the Tales series of games. published by Bandai Namco Games. It is considered a spinoff, and plays similarly to the Dynasty Warriors series. The game was released for the PlayStation Portable in Japan on February 23, 2012.

*A Walk in the Sun* is a 1945 American war film directed by Lewis Milestone, based on the 1944 novel of the same name by Harry Brown. The film stars Dana Andrews, Richard Conte, and Lloyd Bridges, and features Burgess Meredith as the narrator. Set during the Allied invasion of Italy in September 1943, it follows a platoon from the Texas Division as they approach a heavily defended farmhouse near Salerno. The film is known for its realistic portrayal of soldiers and its anti-war message.

Collective motion is defined as the spontaneous emergence of ordered movement in a system consisting of many self-propelled agents. It can be observed in everyday life, for example in flocks of birds, schools of fish, herds of animals and also in crowds and car traffic. It also appears at the microscopic level: in colonies of bacteria, motility assays and artificial self-propelled particles. The scientific community is trying to understand the universality of this phenomenon. In particular it is intensively investigated in statistical physics and in the field of active matter. Experiments on animals, biological and synthesized self-propelled particles, simulations and theories are conducted in parallel to study these phenomena. One of the most famous models that describes such behavior is the Vicsek model introduced by Tamás Vicsek et al. in 1995.

USS *Housatonic* (SP-1697) was a United States Navy minelayer in commission from 1918 to 1919. Before and after her naval service, she was the commercial cargo ship *El Rio*, serving with the Morgan Line and Southern Pacific Steamship Company.

*El Rio* was built in 1899 by Newport News Shipbuilding. In 1917, the U.S. Navy acquired the ship and converted her into a minelayer, commissioning her as USS *Housatonic*. During World War I, she operated out of Scotland and laid thousands of mines in the North Sea. After the war, she was decommissioned and returned to her original owners, reverting to the name *El Rio*. She was renamed *Brazos* in 1925 and served until sinking in a collision in 1942.

Kwai Tsing Theatre (Chinese: 葵青劇院) is a major performance venue in Kwai Chung, New Territories, Hong Kong. It is located in Kwai Fong, near Kwai Fong MTR station. It was built by the Regional Council and opened on 18 November 1999 to provide a medium-size venue for performances. It has an 899-seat auditorium, a black box theatre, and various smaller function rooms.

The *Proserpine* was a Dutch frigate launched in 1801. The British captured her in 1804 and renamed her HMS *Amsterdam*. As *Amsterdam*, she served in the Royal Navy, including time as a storeship and guardship. She was sold in 1815.

Eleanor Reissa is an American actress, singer, theatre director, playwright, librettist, choreographer, translator, and author based in New York City. She works and performs in English and Yiddish speaking stages, and also interprets and performs Yiddish theatre and songs.

Convoy HX 156 was the 156th of the numbered series of World War II HX convoys of merchant ships from Halifax, Nova Scotia to Liverpool. Forty-three ships departed Halifax on 22 October 1941, and were met two days later by United States Navy Task Unit 4.1.3 consisting of Gleaves-class destroyer Niblack, Clemson-class destroyer Reuben James, Wickes-class destroyer Tarbell, and Benson-class destroyers Benson and Hilary P. Jones.

1,3,2,4-Dithiadiphosphetane 2,4-disulfides are a class of organosulfur compounds commonly synthesized through the reaction of arenes or thiols with P4S10. A notable example is Lawesson's reagent, widely used in organic synthesis. These compounds participate in reactions where nucleophiles attack the phosphorus atom, leading to the formation of phosphorus-oxygen bonds, useful in creating metal-binding agents and insecticides. Electrophiles can also react with these compounds, forming sulfur-carbon and phosphorus-halide bonds. Lawesson's reagent can also act as a dehydrating reagent, converting β-aminoamides into imidazolines, and transforming 1,4-diketones into thiophene rings. The dithiophosphine ylides can react with strained alkenes to form a PSC2 ring.

Grimethorpe Hall is a Grade II* listed building located in Grimethorpe, South Yorkshire, England. Constructed around 1670 for Robert Seaton, the hall's design is believed to be influenced by the work of Robert Trollope, evident in its similarities to Norton Conyers House. The building features a square plan with a two-story front elevation and a three-story rear elevation, incorporating a half-basement.

The hall's architecture includes red brick laid in English Bond with stone dressing on the front and sides, while the rear is built of sandstone blocks with herringbone patterns. Alterations were made in the 18th, 19th, and 20th centuries, including replacing the original windows with sash windows and modifications to the parapet brickwork.

Uninhabited since the 1960s, Grimethorpe Hall faced potential demolition in 1981, which was successfully opposed. Ownership has since passed through various hands, including the Crown Estate, leading to its current state of disrepair. Despite community efforts and concerns raised by organizations like SAVE Britain's Heritage, the hall remains without a buyer and in need of significant repairs.

Pierre Galet (1921 – December 31, 2019) was a French ampelographer and viticulturist. He spent most of his life in southern France. From 1946 to 1989, he taught at the University of Montpellier, where he advanced the study of ampelography and mentored many leading ampelographers.

Galet traveled extensively to wine regions worldwide, identifying grape varieties and resolving legal disputes. He identified over 100 Pinot varieties and discovered misidentified vines, such as Pinot blanc in California that was actually Melon de Bourgogne.

Galet authored several influential works, including *Cépages et vignoble de France* and *Dictionnaire encyclopédique des cépages*, a comprehensive catalog of grape varieties. He was made an Officier de l'Ordre du Mérite Agricole and received a special recognition prize from the OIV for his contributions to viticulture and ampelography.

The Portuguese Faculdade de Ciências Sociais e Humanas (Faculty of Social and Human Sciences - FCSH) is an organic unit of the Universidade NOVA de Lisboa (NOVA). According to its statutes, “the Faculty of Social and Human Sciences of NOVA University of Lisbon is an institution dedicated to education, scientific research and cultural creation". The Faculty's own identity stems from the coexistence of social sciences with humanities, allowing an unusual interdisciplinarity in the Portuguese higher education panorama.

Software was a German electronic music duo consisting of Peter Mergener and Michael Weisser. They released music on the Innovative Communication label from the 1980s until their split in 1999. Weisser revived the name in 2017.

Australia's National Reserve System (NRS) is a network of more than 10,000 Commonwealth plus state and territory protected areas which, in combination, on a national scale, protect more than 137 million hectares (340×10^6 acres), greater than 17% of the continent, of unique biodiversity and most significant ecological landscapes for future generations. The aim of the NRS is protect the diversity of all native landscapes, flora and fauna across Australia through strategic habitat protection. It consists of public, indigenous and privately protected areas of land and inland freshwaters.

As part of the National Reserve System Cooperative Program, the Interim Biogeographic Regionalisation for Australia framework was developed as a planning tool to assist in identifying areas of priority.

The City Summit is an annual speaker summit, award show, and fundraising event founded by Ryan Long often held on the evening of the Grammy Awards or the Academy Awards. The ceremony honors the most successful people in various industries, including science, entertainment, business, and philanthropy. The City Gala's purpose is to support and raise awareness about solving the world's global grand challenges. The Gala's purpose is to work with startup non-profit charitable organizations.
Lady Juliana (also known as the Lady Julian), was launched at Whitby in 1777. She transported convicts in 1789 from England to Australia.
Claude Frédéric t'Serclaes, Count of Tilly (1648–1723) was a field marshal in the Dutch States Army.
Andrea Stinson (born November 25, 1967) is a retired professional basketball player from the United States, playing from 1998 to 2005 for the Charlotte Sting and the Detroit Shock.

Society for the Rise of Kurdistan (Lang-ku) also known as the Society for the Advancement of Kurdistan (SAK), was secretly established in Constantinople on 6 November 1917 and officially announced organization formed on the 17 December 1918. It was headquartered in Istanbul, with the aim of creating an independent Kurdish state in eastern Turkey. The Society based its statements for an independent or autonomous Kurdistan on the Treaty of Sèvres and the Fourteen Points stipulated by Woodrow Wilson. The society formed many local dependencies in the eastern provinces of Turkey.
Deerfoot (1828 – after 1863), also known as Lewis Bennett or Hut-goh-so-do-neh, was a Seneca runner who gained international fame in the 1860s.

An eruption column is a cloud of hot volcanic ash, tephra, and gases emitted during an explosive volcanic eruption. The ash and tephra are ejected at high speeds and can rise several kilometers into the atmosphere due to convection. The height of the column depends on factors like vent diameter, gas content, and atmospheric conditions.

Eruption columns pose significant hazards. If a column becomes too dense, it can collapse, creating dangerous pyroclastic flows. Volcanic ash can also severely damage aircraft, as demonstrated by several incidents where planes encountered eruption columns, leading to engine failure and other problems. Volcanic Ash Advisory Centers monitor eruption columns to mitigate these risks. Large eruptions can inject material into the stratosphere, affecting global temperatures.

Hyundai Chungun High School (Korean: 현대청운고등학교) is a private, boarding high school located in Ulsan, South Korea. Established in 1981 by Chung Ju-yung, the school is known for its specialized education programs aimed at gifted students. As of 2024, the principal is Hyun Yung-gil, there are 61 faculty members, and the enrollment is 530 students. The school's flower is the rose and its tree is the bamboo.

Hyundai Chungun High School operates an "Elite Leadership Education" program, including Advanced Placement (AP) courses, and was registered as an official AP test center by the College Board in 2007. The school also runs the Hyundai Chungun Global Leader Program (HCGLP), an international class focused on preparing students for admission to top foreign universities. The school also offers programs in Korean traditional music and arts.

Ben James Herbert "Bert" Burridge (11 March 1898 – 22 December 1977) was an English footballer and manager. He played as a half-back for Darlington, Sheffield Wednesday, Oldham Athletic, and in non-league football for Macclesfield, Hyde United and Hurst. He was part of the Sheffield Wednesday team that won the Football League title in both 1928–29 and 1929–30.

NFL Blitz is a series of American football themed video games originally released by Midway featuring National Football League (NFL) teams. It began as a 1997 arcade game NFL Blitz that was ported to home consoles and spawned a series of sequels. Rather than being designed as a realistic interpretation of the sport of football, like Madden NFL or NFL 2K, the Blitz series was created as an over-the-top, exaggerated version of the sport, inspired by Midway's own NBA Jam basketball games.

In 2005, after losing the NFL license, Midway relaunched the Blitz series as Blitz: The League, depicting fictional players and teams in a fictional league with slightly more realistic (though still exaggerated) on-field play and a focus on the seedy behind-the-scenes lives of the players.

Following the dissolution of Midway, EA Sports acquired the rights to the Blitz name and relaunched the series on PlayStation Network and Xbox Live with a new entry in the series simply titled NFL Blitz. The game took the series back to the original style of Blitz, but removed the late hits due to input by the NFL.

Transbaikal, Trans-Baikal, Transbaikalia (Russian: Забайка́лье, romanized: Zabaykalye, IPA: [zəbɐjˈkaljjɪ]), or Dauria (Даурия, Dauriya) is a mountainous region to the east of or "beyond" (trans-) Lake Baikal in Far Eastern Russia.

The steppe and wetland landscapes of Dauria are protected by the Daurian Nature Reserve, which forms part of a World Heritage Site named "The Landscapes of Dauria".

Connah's Quay Power Station in Flintshire, Wales, is a 1,420 MW natural gas-fired power station. Originally a coal-fired station opened in 1954, it was replaced by a CCGT station in 1996, now owned by Uniper.

*Fatal Deviation* is a 1998 Irish martial arts film directed by Simon Linscheid, Shay Casserley, and James Bennett. The film stars James Bennett as Jimmy, a young man who returns home after a stay in reform school and becomes embroiled in a conflict with a local gang. He trains with a group of monks and enters an underground fighting tournament. Despite its low budget, the film has gained notoriety as one of the worst films ever made. A sequel was announced in 2020, but was replaced by *Triple Deviation*.

Walter G. Alexander (1880–1953) was an American physician and politician. He was the first African American to serve in the New Jersey Legislature, elected to the General Assembly in 1920. He was also active in the NAACP and the National Medical Association.

Dan Beach Bradley (18 July 1804 – 23 June 1873) was an American Protestant missionary to Siam from 1835 until his death. He is credited with numerous firsts, including, bringing the first Thai-script printing press to Siam, publishing the first Thai newspaper and monolingual Thai dictionary, performing the first surgery in Siam, and introducing Western medicine and technology.
**Stockland Bristol** is a village and civil parish in the Sedgemoor district of Somerset, England, with a population of 165 in 2011.

General elections were due to be held for the Legislative Council in Nyasaland on 28 April 1964, and would have been the first in the country under universal suffrage. However, there were no opposition candidates to either the Malawi Congress Party in the general roll seats (the Nyasaland Asian Convention had dissolved itself and declared its support for the MCP), or the Nyasaland Constitutional Party in the special roll seats, resulting in all 53 candidates winning without votes being cast.

MCP leader Hastings Banda remained as Prime Minister, leading the country to independence on 6 July that year.
Louis François Auguste Cauchois-Lemaire (August 28, 1789 in Paris – August 9, 1861 in Paris) was a French journalist.

A toxic leader is someone who has traits and behaviors that negatively affect individuals, organizations, and even those outside their immediate circle. These traits can include being insular, intemperate, callous, inept, or aggressive. Toxic leaders often prioritize their own needs, micro-manage, and make poor decisions. Jean Lipman-Blumen argues that people follow toxic leaders due to their own psychological needs, such as feeling safe or special. Barbara Kellerman identifies seven types of bad leadership, while Terry Price suggests that ethical failures in leaders can stem from cognitive errors rather than simply a lack of willpower.

Otto Kirchheimer (IPA-de; 11 November 1905, Heilbronn – 22 November 1965, Washington, D.C.) was a German jurist of Jewish ancestry and political scientist of the Frankfurt School whose work essentially covered the state and its constitution.

Kirchheimer worked as a research analyst at the Office of Strategic Services (OSS), the forerunner of the CIA, starting in World War II and continuing to 1952.

Hannah Mary Bardell (born 1 June 1983) is a Scottish politician. She was elected as the Scottish National Party (SNP) Member of Parliament (MP) for Livingston in 2015. Bardell served as the SNP Spokesperson for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport from 2018 to 2019 and for Work and Pensions in 2015.

The Oetker Collection is a hospitality company headquartered in Baden-Baden, Germany, founded in 1870. Managed by the Oetker family, it operates hotels and country estates under the Oetker Collection brand. Brenners Park-Hotel & Spa was the first hotel operated by the family. In 2008, the company began an expansion. Timo Gruenert is the current CEO.

May Song Vang (February 5, 1951 – August 5, 2013) was a Laotian-American leader within the Hmong community. She was the youngest wife of General Vang Pao, and after his death in 2011, she took on a more prominent role in Hmong American affairs, mediating disputes and encouraging education. Vang was instrumental in establishing Vang Pao Elementary School in Fresno, California, and founded the General Vang Pao Foundation.
Perla is a Mexican telenovela produced by TV Azteca and starred Silvia Navarro and Leonardo Garcia as protagonists.
Frederic Maugham, 1st Viscount Maugham (1866–1958), was a British lawyer who served as Lord Chancellor from 1938 to 1939. He was also a rower and president of the Cambridge Union Society.

Banjara Hills is an urban commercial centre and one of the most affluent neighbourhoods in Hyderabad, Telangana, India. This is an upmarket locality close to Jubilee Hills. This area was a hilly forest and was least inhabited in the past. Only few royal members of the Nizam's dynasty lived here, which was a hunting ground for them. Even with its history and status, this area now has completely been transformed to an urban commercial centre consisting of an array of high-end hotels, restaurants, night clubs and office buildings of global corporations. Banjara Hills is segregated by its road numbers, with each road having its own importance: the numbers start from 1 and end at 14.

Banjara Hills is considered the most expensive zip code in India according to Economic Times magazine and along with Jubilee Hills, is the most prestigious borough/city in the greater Hyderabad area to live in. Economic Times estimated that properties in Banjara Hills were worth "a whopping Rs 96,000 crore", an equivalent to US$20.7 billion (As of 8 September 2011).

The much neglected Banjara Lake is also located here.
Louis François Auguste Cauchois-Lemaire (1789 – 1861) was a French journalist and historian known for his liberal activism during the Restoration.

The Martin PBM Mariner was an American patrol bomber flying boat of World War II and the early Cold War era. It was designed to complement the Consolidated PBY Catalina and PB2Y Coronado in service. A total of 1,366 PBMs were built, with the first example flying on 18 February 1939 and the type entering service in September 1940, with the last of the type being retired in 1964.

KushCo Holdings (formerly Kush Bottles) is a public company founded in 2010. It supplies packaging and other products to the cannabis industry. The company went public in December 2015 and trades on the OTCQB marketplace under the symbol KSHB.

Stonestown station is a light rail station on the Muni Metro M Ocean View line, located in the median of 19th Avenue adjacent to Stonestown Galleria and the Lakeside neigborhood in San Francisco, California. It opened in 1925 with the first phase of the line and was rebuilt with a high-level island platform in 1993.

Valhallaorden was a Finnish society originating from a Swedish quasi-masonic group, La Constance. Based in the Sveaborg fortress, it drew symbology from ancient Scandinavian roots. Though nominally loyal to the Swedish constitution, it became a forum for officers with grievances against Gustav III, possibly discussing Finnish independence.

Sander Kaasjager (IPA-nl; born 21 June 1985), who plays under the pseudonym Vo0 (pronunciation: 'vu:), is a Dutch professional player of the first-person shooter games Painkiller, Quake II, Quake III, Quake 4, Quake Live and Quake Champions, and also World of Warcraft. He has won more titles and prize money—over US$250,000—at professional Painkiller tournaments than any other player.

Kurihara is a city located in Miyagi Prefecture, in the Tōhoku region of Japan. As of April 2020, the city had an estimated population of 66,565. The economy of Kurihara is largely based on agriculture, especially rice production. The city is known for its natural environment, including Mount Kurikoma, Lake Izunuma, and numerous hot spring resorts.

The Spurling test is a medical maneuver used to assess nerve root pain (also known as radicular pain). The examiner turns the patient's head to the affected side while extending and applying downward pressure to the top of the patient's head. A positive Spurling's sign is when the pain arising in the neck radiates in the direction of the corresponding dermatome ipsilaterally. It is a type of cervical compression test.

Patients with a positive Spurling's sign can present with a variety of symptoms, including pain, numbness and weakness. In addition to the clinical history, the neurological examination may show signs suggesting a cervical radiculopathy.
Frederick Valentine Harrison (born May 21, 1876), known as Barry Ono, was a British comedian and songwriter. He was known for his "The Old Time Music Hall" routine. He married Dorinda Hill in 1932.

Sergio Llull (born November 15, 1987) is a Spanish professional basketball player for Real Madrid of the Liga ACB and the EuroLeague. Primarily a point guard, he can also play as a shooting guard. He was drafted by the Denver Nuggets in the 2009 NBA draft, and his rights were later traded to the Houston Rockets and then the New York Knicks.

Llull has won two EuroLeague titles (2015, 2018), earning the EuroLeague MVP award in 2017. He has also won seven Liga ACB championships and six Copa del Rey titles with Real Madrid. With the Spanish national team, he has won multiple medals, including gold at the 2019 FIBA World Cup.
Diplomatic relations between Cuba and Suriname were established on 23 March 1979. Suriname has an embassy in Havana since 2003. Cuba has an embassy in Paramaribo since 1981.

Grace Yun Yi is a professor of the University of Western Ontario where she currently holds a Tier I Canada Research Chair in Data Science. She was a professor at the University of Waterloo, Canada, where she holds a University Research Chair in Statistical and Actuarial Science. Her research concerns event history analysis with missing data and its applications in medicine, engineering, and social science.

Red Obsession is a 2013 Australian documentary film which collects interviews with winemakers and wine lovers across the world. The film is narrated by Russell Crowe. Red Obsession was co-directed and co-written by David Roach and Warwick Ross.

David John Lodge, CBE (born January 28, 1935) is a British author and literary critic. Born in Brockley, London, he attended University College, London, and later taught English at the University of Birmingham from 1960 to 1987. Lodge is known for his comic and satirical novels, many of which explore themes of Catholicism, academia, and post-war England. His notable works include *The Picturegoers* (1960), *Changing Places* (1975), and *Nice Work* (1988). Lodge's work gained wider recognition in 1975 when he won the Hawthornden Prize for *Changing Places*.

Germinated wheat (Lang-fa) or sprouted wheat, wheat sprout is a product of germinating wheat seeds in a wet and relatively warm environment in a process called sprouting. It is sometimes used instead of barley in the form of malt (early stage sprout) for making beer.

It is used in numerous Persian pastries, dishes, and desserts. Also, it has been used traditionally in the New Year celebration's Haft-Seen table decoration, in the form of Sabzeh (wheat grass), and a dessert called Samanu, as symbols of blessings from Ahura-Mazda. This custom has been observed by most of the people on Iranian Plateau and some of the populations in beyond (e.g. some parts of India, Yemen, Lebanon, Russia, and China) for thousands of years maybe as old as emerging of Zoroaster.

One of the confections called Sohan is made up of mainly wheat sprout flour. It is a traditional Persian saffron brittle toffee and made in the holy city of Qom.
The Jack Rabbit Trading Post is a convenience store and curio shop located on former U.S. Route 66, five miles west of Joseph City, Arizona city limits off of Exit 269 on Interstate 40.

WNSN (101.5 FM), branded as "Sunny 101.5," is an adult contemporary radio station serving the Michiana area from South Bend, Indiana. WNSN is owned by Mid-West Family Broadcasting, along with sister stations WSBT, WQLQ, and WZOC.

The station first went on the air August 1, 1962, as WSBT-FM, originally simulcasting its AM sister station. It later broadcast beautiful music as WWJY, then adult contemporary as WTHQ. In 1984, the call letters changed to WNSN. Previously owned by Schurz Communications, WNSN was sold to Gray Television in 2015, then quickly resold to Mid-West Family Broadcasting.

The 1900 House is a historical reenactment reality television series made by Wall to Wall/Channel 4 in 1999. The program features a modern family attempting to live in the way of the late Victorians for three months in a modified house. It was first broadcast on Channel 4 in the United Kingdom and PBS in America (with American commentary).

The series was accompanied by the book 1900 House: Featuring Extracts from the Personal Diaries of Joyce and Paul Bowler and Their Family by Mark McCrum and Matthew Sturgis. It won a Peabody Award in 2000 for being "an often humorous, always perceptive, series about the realities of life in 1900 that reveals themes of perseverance, human adaptation and family dynamics."

Joseph Tanke is an academic specializing in aesthetics and philosophy. He was the Chalsty Professor of Aesthetics and Philosophy at California College of the Arts from 2007 to 2011, and in 2012, he joined the Philosophy Department at the University of Hawaii at Manoa. In 2014, he was the Critical Studies Fellow at the Cranbrook Academy of Art. Tanke is the editor of the Global Aesthetic Research book series and the director of the International Cultural Studies Graduate Certificate Program at the University of Hawaii at Manoa. He is the author and editor of books on Foucault, Rancière, and aesthetics.

Sergio Llull Melià (IPA-es; born November 15, 1987) is a Spanish professional basketball player and the team captain for Real Madrid of the Spanish Liga ACB and the EuroLeague. He is a 6 ft 3 in (1.91 m) tall point guard and shooting guard.

Llull is a two−time All-EuroLeague Team selection, and was the EuroLeague MVP of the 2016–17 season. As a regular member of the senior Spain national team, he has won a FIBA World Cup title, three EuroBasket gold medals, as well as a bronze. He also won two Summer Olympics medals, a silver in 2012, and a bronze in 2016.

"I Am Real" is a 2011 dance-pop and EDM single performed by Simon van Kempen, an Australian hotelier known for his appearances on *The Real Housewives of New York City*. Released on April 21, 2011, through JSM Music, the song was written by Allison Beth Simon, Jon Jason Appleton, and Koki Saito, and produced by Joel Simon, Ross Hopman, and Saito. A remix, the "Thug in a Club mix", followed on May 12, 2011.

Van Kempen, who grew up with a musical background, recorded the song at JSM Music Studios in New York City on March 31, 2011. The lyrics, described as tongue-in-cheek and self-deprecating, address van Kempen's "haters", his views on class, and his experiences on *The Real Housewives*, including rumors about his sexuality and his interactions with other cast members. The song also incorporates the line "Thug in a cocktail dress," originally uttered by his wife, Alex McCord, on the show.

"I Am Real" received mixed to negative reviews, with some critics deeming it the worst song in *The Real Housewives* franchise. Despite this, one reviewer called it one of the better *Real Housewives* singles. Van Kempen promoted the song with performances on *Watch What Happens Live with Andy Cohen* and at Manhattan's "Fashion Night Out" event. It sold approximately 1,000 digital copies in the United States.

Red God (1954 – date of death unknown) was an American-bred, British-trained Thoroughbred racehorse. As a two-year-old, he won the Richmond Stakes. Later in the United States, his best result was a win in the Roseben Handicap. As a stallion, he sired 10 stakes winners.
Edward Franc Jones (June 3, 1828 – August 14, 1913) was an American merchant, manufacturer, soldier, author and politician from New York.

Taptu was a UK-based social media company, headquartered in Cambridge, that was founded in 2007. Its initial product was a mobile search engine, offered as a website and through dedicated apps, but it was discontinued in 2011. Taptu's other product was My Taptu, a mobile social news aggregator app for iPhone and Android. My Taptu aimed to solve information overload by presenting personalized "streams" of content. The company is now defunct.
Tõnis Kõiv (born 1971) is an Estonian politician and lawyer. He was a member of the Riigikogu from 2005 to 2015 and mayor of Paide from 1996 to 2004.

The Philadelphia Jewish Film Festival is an annual film festival in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, founded in 1981 by Archie Perlmutter. Hosted by the Gershman Y, it is the second-oldest Jewish film festival in the United States.

Archbishop Boghos Lévon Zékiyan (Lang-hy; born October 21, 1943 in Constantinople) is an Armenologist, philosopher, Professor of Armenian Language and Literature at Ca' Foscari University of Venice, Pontifical Oriental Institute of Rome and Istanbul University, a member of the Academy of Venice, Foreign member of Armenian National Academy of Sciences, Corresponding member of the Istituto Veneto di Scienze, Lettere ed Arti (1992). He is the Armenian Catholic Archeparch of Istanbul. Zekiyan is the Founding President of the Associazione "Padus-Araxes", the director of Summer Intensive Course of the Armenian Language and Culture at the University of Venice (since 1986), and former editor of Hye Endanik (1974–82) and Bazmavep (1980-1985) periodicals.
Ikhtiyar al-Din Ai-Taq was a Seljuk Ghulam who took advantage of Sanjar's overthrow in 1153 to establish influence in the western regions of Sanjar's empire.

Civil parishes in Cambridgeshire are the lowest tier of local government. They arose from Church of England divisions that acquired civil administration powers over time. The Local Government Act 1894 established elected parish councils and aligned parish boundaries. Recent legislation has encouraged the formation of town and parish councils in unparished areas. A parish council can become a town council by resolution.
Domingos Duarte (born 1995) is a Portuguese professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Getafe CF and the Portugal national team.
Future of the Left are a Welsh alternative rock band from Cardiff, formed in 2005. The band was formed after the breakup of Mclusky and Jarcrew.

Dardanella was a touring theatre company founded in 1926 by Willy A. Piedro in the Dutch East Indies. Departing from earlier forms, Dardanella adopted a European style known as toneel, emphasizing spoken dialogue over music. The troupe gained popularity for its adaptations of Hollywood productions and original plays, attracting figures like Andjar Asmara. After a failed film venture in India, the troupe disbanded in 1936. Several members later entered the Indonesian film industry. Dardanella was praised for its performances and contribution to a unified Indonesian identity.
Alexandra Pringle Hon. FRSL (born 1952 or 1953) is a British publisher and editor-in-chief of Bloomsbury Publishing since 1999. She previously worked at Virago and Hamish Hamilton.

Money Jungle is a studio album by pianist Duke Ellington with double bassist Charles Mingus and drummer Max Roach. It was recorded on September 17, 1962, and released in February 1963 by United Artists Jazz. All but one of the compositions were written by Ellington, with four of the seven on the original LP being recorded for the first time on this album. Later releases on CD added eight tracks from the same recording session.

The album was reviewed positively at the time of its release and subsequent reviews have remained highly favorable. Negative comments have concentrated on differences in playing style among the three musicians, brought about by the generational gap between Ellington and the others, and an argument that led to Mingus leaving the studio mid-session. Hundreds of musicians have been influenced by the recording, in particular by the freedom of individual expression within a small-group setting.
The struggle for women's right to vote in Mexico dates back to the nineteenth century, with the right being achieved in 1953.

Nippu Ravva (transl. Fire Spark) is a 1993 Telugu-language action film, produced by M.V.Srinivas Prasad on Yuvarathna Arts banner and directed by A. Kodandarami Reddy. The film stars Nandamuri Balakrishna, Vijayashanti, and music composed by Bappi Lahiri & Raj–Koti with A. R. Rahman's background score. Nippu Ravva and Bangaru Bullodu, also starring Balakrishna, were released on the same day and both ran for over 100 days. It was dubbed into Tamil and Hindi as Suriya and Mawaali Raaj, respectively.

In New York State, Regents Examinations are statewide standardized examinations in core high school subjects. Students are required to pass these exams to earn a Regents Diploma. To graduate, students are required to have earned appropriate credits in a number of specific subjects by passing year-long or half-year courses, after which they must pass at least five Regents examinations in some of the subject areas. For higher-achieving students, a Regents with Advanced designation and an Honors designation are also offered. Students with disabilities or enrolled in an English as a Second Language program are able to earn a local diploma.

The Regents Examinations are developed and administered by the New York State Education Department (NYSED) under the authority of the Board of Regents of the University of the State of New York. Regents exams are prepared by a conference of selected New York teachers of each test's specific discipline who assemble a test map that highlights the skills and knowledge required from the specific discipline's learning standards. The conferences meet and design the tests three years before the tests' issuance, which includes time for field testing and evaluating testing questions.

Fredric Sheldon Brandt (June 26, 1949 – April 5, 2015) was an American physician and author known for his work in cosmetic dermatology and innovative skin care. He graduated from Rutgers University and obtained his medical degree from Hahnemann Medical College. He completed residencies at New York University and the University of Miami.

Brandt established a dermatology practice in Miami in 1982 and later opened a New York City practice in 1998. He also ran the Dermatology Research Institute in Miami, where he conducted clinical research. Brandt authored two books on skin aging. He died by suicide at the age of 65.
Gilad Efrat (Hebrew: גלעד אפרת; born 1969) is a leading contemporary Israeli painter and a professor at the Shenkar College of Engineering and Design, Israel.

Thrilling is an online marketplace for vintage clothing, apparel and home goods. The company partners with independent vintage and secondhand clothing stores that use the platform to list, sell, and ship inventory. Thrilling was founded by Shilla Kim-Parker and Brad Mallow in 2018, In 2021, the company received $8.47 million in series A funding.

Thrilling has partnered with Banana Republic, Academy Award nominated costume designer Ruth E. Carter, and offers a studio service that uses the company's network of stores to find authentic period clothing for film and television productions. In 2020, Thrilling partnered with stylist Zerina Akers of "Black Owned Everything" to curate an online collection of vintage items from black-owned stores.

As of January 2022, Thrilling hosted listings from more than 900 vintage stores in approximately 200 cities and planned to use series A funding to improve technology such as partner store analytics, partially automating the item listing process, and adding personalization for customers shopping on the site.

In May 2022, Thrilling introduced a home goods section that allows thrift shop owners to list and sell vintage home decor items.

Nakae Chōmin (1847–1901) was a Japanese journalist, politician, and philosopher. He was born in Kōchi Prefecture. Nakae is known for his role in developing liberalism in Japan. He translated works by Jean-Jacques Rousseau into Japanese and propagated Western democratic ideas through his newspapers, including the "Oriental Free Press." Nakae also served in the Diet of Japan.

Michelle Stephenson (born January 3, 1977) is an English singer and presenter. She was briefly a member of Touch, the group that would become the Spice Girls, before being replaced by Emma Bunton due to commitment issues.

Jefferson Joseph DeBlanc (February 15, 1921 – November 22, 2007) was a United States Marine Corps officer and a recipient of the Medal of Honor for his actions as a fighter pilot in World War II. On January 31, 1943, he shot down five Japanese aircraft in a single mission over Kolombangara Island.

DeBlanc enlisted in the U.S. Naval Reserve in 1941 before joining the Marine Corps in 1942. He served in the Pacific Theater, flying F4F Wildcats. Beyond the Medal of Honor, DeBlanc received the Distinguished Flying Cross, Purple Heart, and Air Medal with four gold stars. He continued to serve in the Marine Corps Reserve, retiring as a colonel in 1972. After the war, he earned multiple degrees and taught math and physics. DeBlanc was featured in the History Channel series *Dogfights*, and he died in 2007, the last surviving WWII Medal of Honor recipient from Louisiana.

*Day of Defeat* is a multiplayer first-person shooter set in World War II, developed by Valve and published by Activision in 2003. It simulates squad-level infantry combat between the Allies (United States or Great Britain) and the Axis powers (Germany) in the European Theater. Gameplay revolves around teams achieving objectives within historical scenarios, emphasizing realistic weaponry and teamwork.

Originally a *Half-Life* modification in 2000, *Day of Defeat* was purchased by Valve, who hired the modding team to create a standalone retail version. The retail version featured improved graphics, new maps, and UI enhancements. In 2013, Valve released versions of the game for Mac OS X and Linux.

*Day of Defeat* received generally favorable reviews, praised for its intense pacing and promotion of teamwork, though criticized for its dated visuals. It was followed by a remake, *Day of Defeat: Source*, in 2005.

Lieutenant Colonel Thomas Bernard Hackett VC (15 June 1836 – 5 October 1880) was born in Riverstown, County Tipperary and was an Irish recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest and most prestigious award for gallantry in the face of the enemy that can be awarded to British and Commonwealth forces.
Yuda was a town located in Waga District, Iwate Prefecture, Japan.
Republic Township is a civil township within Marquette County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 1,060 at the 2010 census. The Township is administered from the community of Republic.
*Beatrijs* was a Catholic women's magazine in the Netherlands, founded in 1939. It was shut down during World War II and later merged with *Libelle*.

The American Association of Colleges of Nursing (AACN) is a national organization of nurses in the United States that is dedicated to advancing nursing education. It was established in 1969, and represents nursing schools at 840 universities and colleges in the United States.

**Disarmament, demobilization and reintegration (DDR)** refers to the removal of weapons, disbanding of armed groups, and reintegrating ex-combatants into civilian society. Trust between groups is essential for successful DDR.

Kogiidae is a family of whales, belonging to the superfamily Physeteroidea, which also includes sperm whales. The family includes the extant pygmy sperm whale and dwarf sperm whale, as well as fossil species. Kogiids are found in all oceans except polar waters. They are deep-diving whales that use suction feeding to eat squid. Threats to kogiids include entanglement in fishing lines, plastic consumption, and commercial fishing.

In medicine, an avulsion is an injury in which a body structure is torn off by either trauma or surgery (from the Latin avellere, meaning "to tear off"). The term most commonly refers to a surface trauma where all layers of the skin have been torn away, exposing the underlying structures (i.e., subcutaneous tissue, muscle, tendons, or bone). This is similar to an abrasion but more severe, as body parts such as an eyelid or an ear can be partially or fully detached from the body.

Jemima Anne Morrell (7 March 1832 – 13 October 1909) was an English tourist known for her journal, *Miss Jemima's Swiss Journal: The First Conducted Tour of Switzerland*, which documented her experiences on the first guided tour of Switzerland, led by Thomas Cook in 1863. The journal, published in 1963 after being discovered in a warehouse after the Blitz, provides insights into early international tourism and Victorian travel. Morrell was later identified as the author through cross-referencing letters and travel records. She married John Broadley Greenwood in 1867 and had one child.

Longwood House is a mansion in St. Helena and the final residence of Napoleon Bonaparte, the former Emperor of the French, during his exile on the island of Saint Helena, from 10 December 1815 until his death on 5 May 1821.

Tom Gorman (born January 19, 1946) is a retired ATP tour American tennis player and coach. He won 7 singles and 9 doubles titles and reached semi-finals in the 3 of the 4 ATP tour grand slam events. His ATP ranking peaked at 8 in 1973.

CollabNet is a software company founded in 1999 by Tim O'Reilly, Brian Behlendorf, and Bill Portelli. Headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, it provides tools for agile management, DevOps, and value stream management. In 2017, CollabNet merged with VersionOne, becoming CollabNet VersionOne. In 2020, CollabNet VersionOne merged with XebiaLabs and Arxan to become Digital.ai.

*Going Blank Again* is the second studio album by the British shoegaze band Ride, released on March 9, 1992. It followed their debut album *Nowhere*. The album spawned the singles "Leave Them All Behind" and "Twisterella". It has been re-released several times, including a 20th-anniversary edition with a bonus DVD.

The Coaldale Big Green was an early professional football team based in Coaldale, Pennsylvania. The club played as an independent until joining the Anthracite League in 1924. After leaving the league in 1924, the team spent its 1925 season as an independent, then joined the short-lived Eastern League of Professional Football in 1926. While the most well known Anthracite League team is the Pottsville Maroons, which jumped to the National Football League in 1925 and immediately established itself as a championship contender, the most consistently successful club in the coal region was the Big Green. Coaldale won the coal region's Curran Cup in 1921, 1922 and 1923.
Stephen Soter Ortynsky (1866–1916) was the first bishop for Ukrainian Greek Catholics in the United States, serving from 1907 until his death.

Diana Lee Inosanto is an American actress, director, stuntwoman, and martial artist. She also wrote and directed the film The Sensei (2008), as well as having written a children's book titled The Curious Mind of Sebastian in 2020.

Force Majeure (IPA-fr; Lang-sv, "tourist") is a 2014 internationally co-produced black comedy film written and directed by Ruben Östlund. It follows the marital tension resulting from an apparent avalanche in the French Alps, during which the husband prioritizes his own escape over the safety of his wife and two children. The title used for the film in some English-speaking countries comes from force majeure, a contractual clause freeing both parties from liability in the event of unexpected disasters.

Force Majeure was acclaimed upon release, with critics praising its script and cinematography. It won the Best Film award at the 50th Guldbagge Awards, and was named one of the best films of 2014 by various publications.

"Kashmiri Song" is a popular song that has been recorded by numerous artists, including Peter Dawson, Frank Titterton, John McCormack, and Richard Tauber.
