The lyrics reference "beside the Shalimar," which may refer to either the Shalimar Gardens in Kashmir or the Shalimar Gardens in Lahore. The song has had a varied presence in popular culture.

The pluperfect (shortening of plusquamperfect), usually called past perfect in English, is a type of verb form, generally treated as a grammatical tense in certain languages, relating to an action that occurred prior to an aforementioned time in the past. Examples in English are: "we had arrived"; "they had written".

The word derives from the Latin plus quam perfectum, "more than perfect". The word "perfect" in this sense means "completed"; it contrasts with the "imperfect", which denotes uncompleted actions or states.

In English grammar, the pluperfect (e.g. "had written") is now usually called the past perfect, since it combines past tense with perfect aspect. (The same term is sometimes used in relation to the grammar of other languages.) English also has a past perfect progressive (or past perfect continuous) form: "had been writing".
**John Patrick** (born February 29, 1968) is an American basketball coach and former player.
Oberhünigen is a municipality in the Bern-Mittelland administrative district in the canton of Bern in Switzerland.

The 2022 al-Shabaab invasion of Ethiopia was a military incursion by the Somali militant group al-Shabaab into Ethiopia's Somali Region, starting on 20 July 2022. The invasion is part of the ongoing Somali Civil War, the Ethiopian–Somali conflict, and the Ethiopian civil conflict.

Al-Shabaab forces crossed the border and attacked several settlements, capturing Hulhul before being encircled. A smaller contingent reached the Bale Mountains, the invasion's main target. Ethiopia claimed to have destroyed most of the invading force, inflicting heavy casualties. Somali Region officials reported heavy losses and captured administrators. The Ethiopian government framed the invasion as a major victory, while analysts suggest al-Shabaab aimed to open a new frontline or exploit local tensions.

The Commissioner of Police, Delhi or Delhi Police Commissioner is the head of the Delhi Police, the Law enforcement agency of the 15 police districts (as of January 2019) of National Capital of India, Delhi.

The New York World was a newspaper published in New York City from 1860 until 1931. The paper played a major role in the history of American newspapers. It was a leading national voice of the Democratic Party. From 1883 to 1911 under publisher Joseph Pulitzer, it was a pioneer in yellow journalism, capturing readers' attention with sensation, sports, sex and scandal and pushing its daily circulation to the one-million mark. It was sold in 1930 and merged into the New York World-Telegram.
The Mercedes-Benz S-Series (W06) was a successful line of sports cars produced from 1927-33 that bore the nickname, the "white elephants."

Wickrema Weerasooria was a Sri Lankan lawyer, civil servant, diplomat and an academic. He was the Permanent Secretary to the Ministry of Plan Implementation, former Sri Lankan High Commissioner to Australia and New Zealand. He served as Sri Lanka's first Insurance Ombudsman.

O RLY? is an Internet phenomenon, typically presented as an image macro featuring a snowy owl. The phrase "O RLY?", an abbreviated form of "Oh, really?", is popularly used in Internet forums in a sarcastic manner, often in response to an obvious, predictable, or blatantly false statement. Similar owl image macros followed the original to present different views, including images with the phrases "YA RLY" (Yeah, really.), "NO WAI!!" (No way!), and NO RLY. (Not really.)

A phantom border (Lang-de) is an informal delineation following the approximate course of an abolished political border, associated with demographic differences on each side as a continuing legacy of historical division, despite official geopolitical union. Not all former political borders are today phantom borders. Factors that may increase the likelihood of a political border becoming a phantom border upon dissolution, include: short time elapsed since the border's dissolution, long existence of the former border, imporousity of the former border, and divergent characteristics of the political entity formerly governing one side of the border.

Phantom borders have many different implications: in Ukraine they are associated with conflict, while in countries such as Romania they play an important part in relations with neighboring countries.

"Loti" (IPA-sq; ‹See RfD› transl. Tear) is a song by Albanian singer and songwriter Elvana Gjata. The song was composed and written by Gjata, while produced and arranged by Jumpa and Lex Barkey, respectively. Loudcom under exclusive license from East Music Matters released it as a single for digital download and streaming on 10 June 2021. Musically, it is an Albanian-language pop, R&B and urban song fusing folk, trap and rock elements, with the lyrics telling a tale of a woman, who loses the intimacy of her loved one. Music critics applauded "Loti", with praise concentrated on its appeal, music video as well as Gjata's musical versatility. Commercially, the song peaked atop the ranking in Albania and reached the top 60 in Switzerland. An accompanying music video was uploaded to Gjata's YouTube channel on 10 June 2021 and was filmed at the Divjakë-Karavasta National Park, Albania.

A temporary crown is a provisional tooth-shaped cap used in dentistry to protect a prepared tooth while a permanent crown is being fabricated. They serve to maintain function and aesthetics. Temporary crowns can be preformed (plastic, composite, or metal-based) or custom-formed using resin. Cementing requires materials that offer retention but allow easy removal, avoiding strong adhesives. Oral hygiene is important to prevent inflammation.

Vincent (sometimes referred to as East Irwindale) is a census-designated place (CDP) in the central San Gabriel Valley, in Los Angeles County, California, United States. The city name and zipcode for addresses south of Arrow Highway is Covina and 91722, respectively, and Azusa and 91702 for addresses north of Arrow Highway. The population was 15,922 at the 2010 census, up from 15,097 at the 2000 census. Vincent is named after Vincent Avenue, which passes through the community.
Giorgos Fountas (1924 – 2010) was a Greek actor who appeared in film, television, and theatre between 1944 and 1997.

Kentucky Route 8 (KY 8) is a state highway in Kentucky. It is split into two segments, totaling 114.202 miles (183.790 km) in length, maintained by the Kentucky Transportation Cabinet (KYTC). The longer, western segment runs for 78.819 miles (126.847 km), beginning near Francisville at KY 237 and running parallel to the Ohio River, passing through cities such as Covington, Newport, and Augusta, before terminating at US 62 in Maysville.

A portion of the original route in Fort Thomas was rerouted due to a landslide, with the former route replaced by KY 445 and KY 6335. The eastern segment spans 35.383 miles (56.943 km), from KY 57 and KY 3550 in Concord to US 23 in South Portsmouth. This segment was formerly part of Kentucky Route 10 until the late 1990s, when KY 10 was rerouted.

In Hindustani music, the *gharana* system is a tradition of musical apprenticeship and lineage, rooted in the *guru–shishya* tradition. It was greatly influenced by the decline of the Mughal Empire, which caused musicians to migrate to princely states.

Different *khyal gharanas* have distinct styles of presenting *khyal*, including variations in enunciation, improvisation, and emphasis on rhythm. There are ten prominent *khyal gharanas*. The *dhrupad* tradition includes four original styles. *Thumri gharanas* include Benares, Lucknow and Patiala. There are six widely accepted *tabla gharanas*. In Kathak, performers draw lineage from four major schools: Jaipur-Atrauli, Lucknow, Benares, and Raigarh.

Proserpine was launched at Amsterdam in 1801 as a 32-gun frigate. The Royal Navy captured her in May 1804 at the capture of Surinam and took her into service as HMS Amsterdam. She sailed to England where she became a guard and storeship at Cork. She was sold in 1815.
The Drammen Museum of Art and Cultural History is a museum in Drammen, Norway, founded in 1996 and centered on Marienlyst manor.
Prakash Vir Shastri (1923–1977) was an Indian politician. He was an Independent member of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, and a devotee of the Arya Samaj movement.
The 1960 Spanish Guinea by-election elected the first representatives to the Cortes Españolas.

WNED-FM (94.5 MHz) is a non-commercial radio station licensed to Buffalo, New York. WNED-FM offers a classical music radio format. It is owned by the Western New York Public Broadcasting Association (formerly the Western New York Educational TV Association), doing business as Buffalo Toronto Public Media. The organization also operates PBS network affiliate Channel 17 WNED-TV and FM 88.7 WBFO (which offers a news/talk format and programming from NPR). While WNED-FM airs no commercials, it does conduct periodic pledge drives on the air to seek donations for the station. WNED-FM has local hosts in most day-parts, including weekends.

Programming on WNED-FM is simulcast on WNJA 89.7 FM in Jamestown, New York for listeners in Southwestern New York and some parts of Pennsylvania. WNED-FM's studios and offices are at Horizons Plaza on Lower Terrace in Buffalo, and the transmitter is off Zimmerman Road in Hamburg, New York. WNED-FM and TV also maintain an office in Toronto for listeners and contributors in Canada.

An economic calendar is a resource that lists economic events and indicators, such as a country's gross domestic product, and monetary policy announcements. These indicators are released by governments, international organizations, and private research firms. The calendar also indicates the event's volatility level, which forecasts the likelihood that a specific event will impact the markets. Events are released at different intervals, such as weekly, monthly, and quarterly.

"Take Me to the Pilot" is a song written by English musician Elton John and songwriter Bernie Taupin, and performed by John. It was originally released on John's eponymous second album in 1970.

It was recorded at Trident Studios in London in January 1970 and released in the United States in October 1970 as the A-side of a single, with "Your Song" as the B-side. Both songs received airplay, but "Your Song" was preferred by disc jockeys, becoming the singer's first hit (on both sides of the Atlantic) and rendering "Take Me To The Pilot" as the B-side.

The country duo Brothers Osborne recorded a remake of the song for the 2018 tribute album Restoration: Reimagining the Songs of Elton John and Bernie Taupin.

Robert Žák (born 6 May 1966) is a Czech football manager and former player. As a player, he was a midfielder who played in the Czechoslovak First League for Slavia Prague, Hradec Králové and České Budějovice. As a manager, he has led České Budějovice, Most, Bohemians Prague, and Litvínov.

The history of the mandolin is a story of instrument development from ancient roots to its modern forms. Early stringed instruments, such as the musical bow, evolved into harps, lyres, and eventually the lute. The lute traveled from Mesopotamia to Europe via Islamic Iberia and Sicily, influencing instrument making in Italy and Germany.

A distinct European lute tradition emerged by the 13th century, leading to the gittern and mandore. In Italy, the mandore evolved into the mandolino, which was redesigned in Northern Italy. The modern mandolin emerged in Naples, Italy, with the Vinaccia family credited with developing metal-string mandolins. Pasquale Vinaccia modernized the instrument around 1835, creating the Neapolitan mandolin.

The mandolin's popularity surged in the late 19th century, known as the "Golden Age," with mandolin orchestras forming worldwide. After World War I, its popularity declined, but the instrument adapted to new musical genres, leading to the creation of resonator, flatback, carved-top, mandolin-banjo, and electric mandolins.

Anula Karunathilaka (born January 23, 1946) is a Sri Lankan actress. Active from 1960 to 2010, she is known for character acting. Karunathilaka's notable role was as Dhammi in *Golu Hadawatha* (1968), for which she won a Sarasaviya Award. She was married to Daya Ranaweera.
Autumn Exhibition of Leningrad artists of 1978 (Lang-ru) opened in Exhibition Halls of the Leningrad Union of Soviet Artists and became one of the largest art exhibitions of 1978 in the USSR.
Newman O'Neal was the mayor of Hobson City, Alabama, until he faced death threats and was assaulted forcing him to flee.

Vena, also known as D'Element DNA Latin Group, was a bachata group formed in the Bronx, New York, in 2011 by Lenny Santos and Max Santos after the separation of Aventura. The group, initially named D'Element, became Vena with the addition of Steve Styles. After Styles and Santos left, Lenny continued with J Love under the name DNA Latin Group. The group was active until 2019.
Charlotte Mary Deane MBE (born 1975) is an English Professor of Structural Bioinformatics and the former Head of the Department of Statistics at the University of Oxford.

Maximum PC, formerly known as boot, is an American magazine and website published by Future US. It focuses on cutting-edge PC hardware, with an emphasis on product reviews, step-by-step tutorials, and in-depth technical briefs. Component coverage areas include CPUs, motherboards, core-logic chipsets, memory, videocards, mechanical hard drives, solid-state drives, optical drives, cases, component cooling, and anything else to do with recent tech news. Additional hardware coverage is directed at smartphones, tablet computers, cameras and other consumer electronic devices that interface with consumer PCs. Software coverage focuses on games, anti-virus suites, content-editing programs, and other consumer-level applications.

Prior to September 1998, the magazine was called boot. boot and sister magazine MacAddict (now Mac|Life) launched in September 1996, when Future US shut down CD-ROM Today.

In March 2016, Future US announced that the Maximum PC website would be merged with PCGamer.com, appearing as the hardware section of the website from that point forward. The magazine was not affected by this change. As of July 2, 2018, browsing to MaximumPC.com no longer forwards to the Hardware section of PCGamer.com

Maitreya-nātha (c. 270–350 CE) is a name whose use was pioneered by Buddhist scholars Erich Frauwallner, Giuseppe Tucci, and Hakuju Ui to distinguish one of the three founders of the Yogacara school of Buddhist philosophy, along with Asanga and Vasubandhu. Some scholars believe this Maitreya to be a historical person in India. The traditions themselves have held that it is referring to Maitreya, the future buddha.
Sofya Skya (born August 12, 1987) is a Russian actress, ballerina, acting coach, and director. She won the Mrs. World pageant in 2006.
Apollonius Dyscolus (2nd century) was an Alexandrian grammarian, considered the founder of scientific syntax. His surviving works concern syntax, adverbs, conjunctions, and pronouns.

The Jack Rabbit Trading Post is a Route 66 trading post in Arizona, founded in 1949. It is known for its billboards featuring a jackrabbit silhouette and the slogan "HERE IT IS," and a large fiberglass jackrabbit. It was featured in the 2006 film *Cars*.

Stepan Petrovich Krasheninnikov (November 11, 1711 – March 8, 1755) was a Russian naturalist and geographer known for his explorations of Kamchatka and Siberia as part of the Second Kamchatka Expedition. He studied the local plants, animals, minerals, history, and geography, and published his observations in *History of Kamtschatka*.

Joshua Nyamweya Oigara (born 1975) is Kenyan businessman, accountant and corporate executive who is the immediate former Group chief executive officer of the Kenya Commercial Bank Group. He served in that position from 1 January 2013. until 25 May 2022.

Before that, he was the chief financial officer and Member of the board of directors of both KCB Group and KCB Bank Kenya, between January 2012 and January 2013. At age 37, his appointment in November 2012 to replace the outgoing CEO Martin Oduor-Otieno made him the youngest CEO of a publicly traded bank at the Nairobi Securities Exchange.

The Materfer CMM 400-2 is a diesel multiple unit (DMU) manufactured by Materfer in Argentina. They are used on a variety of urban and inter-urban services in Argentina on 1,000 mm, 1,435 mm, and 1,676 mm gauge tracks. Each car seats 70 passengers.

*Top Chef Masters* season 2 aired on Bravo from April 7 to June 9, 2010, with 22 chefs competing in 19 tasks over 10 episodes. Each episode consisted of a Quickfire and Elimination challenge, with chefs winning money for their designated charities. Marcus Samuelsson won the season and $100,000 for his charity.

The *Testamentum Domini* is a 4th or 5th-century Church Order presented as the testament of Jesus Christ to his apostles. Written in Greek, the original is mostly lost, but it survives in Syriac, Ge'ez, and Latin fragments. The text emphasizes asceticism, exorcism, the importance of virgins, and the role of women in the church.

Audubon Aquarium of the Americas is an aquarium in New Orleans, Louisiana, United States.

It is run by the Audubon Nature Institute, which also supervises Audubon Zoo, Audubon Louisiana Nature Center, Freeport-McMoRan Audubon Species Survival Center, Audubon Center for Research of Endangered Species (ACRES), Coastal Wildlife Network, Audubon Wilderness Park, and Audubon Park. It is located along the banks of the Mississippi River by the edge of the historic French Quarter off Canal Street, at the upper end of Woldenberg Park. It opened on September 1, 1990.

New Zealand has 1157 described spider species, with an estimated total fauna of 2000 species. Over 97 per cent are endemic, and the rest have been introduced through human activities or were natural wind-borne introductions.

The New Zealand spider with the largest leg span is the Nelson cave spider (Spelungula cavernicola), with a leg span of up to 13 centimetres (5.1 in) and a 3 centimetres (1.2 in) body length.

The Australian white-tailed spider, first recorded in New Zealand in 1886, has been falsely attributed as the cause of many necrotising spider bites.

The flat huntsman spider (Delena cancerides), also from Australia, and called the Avondale spider in New Zealand, was accidentally introduced in the early 1920s, possibly in shipments of hardwood logs used for railway sleepers. The huntsman spiders, which are considered harmless to humans, have been collected for use in at least two films.

Very few New Zealand spiders have bites that can cause significant injury to humans, and of these, only one – the katipō – is endemic. Katipō bites have been known to cause systemic effects, such as hypertension, seizure, or coma, though no deaths as a result of katipō bites have been recorded for over 200 years. Its more dangerous close relative, the venomous Australian redback spider, has established a foothold in some parts of New Zealand, notably in Taranaki and Central Otago.

Agnieszka Brustman (born in Warsaw) is a Polish chess player who holds the title of Woman Grandmaster (WGM). She won the Polish women's championship four times and won the 1980 European junior girls' championship and the 1982 world junior girls' championship.

City Tower (formerly Sunley House) is a 30-storey skyscraper situated in the Piccadilly Gardens area of Manchester city centre in England. As of 2022, it is the tallest office building in Manchester and outside London, and the eighth-tallest building in Manchester, with a roof height of 107 metres (351 ft).

Giant skippers are medium-sized butterflies belonging to the subfamily Megathyminae within the skipper family Hesperiidae. They are larger than other skippers, with thick bodies and typically brown coloration with yellow markings. Males have long, hairlike scales on their upperwings and exhibit territorial behavior. Adult giant skippers rarely feed on flowers. Their caterpillars create silk-lined tunnels within host plants, where they feed and pupate. The subfamily includes the genera *Agathymus*, *Megathymus*, and *Stallingsia*.
Child mortality in Ghana, also known as under-5 mortality, has historically been a concern, though improvements have been made through various initiatives.

The Medical University of South Carolina (MUSC) College of Dental Medicine, established in 1953, is a public dental school in Charleston, South Carolina. It was renamed the James B. Edwards College of Dental Medicine in 2010.

The Sportpalast speech (Lang-de) or Total War speech was a speech delivered by German Propaganda Minister Joseph Goebbels at the Berlin Sportpalast to a large, carefully selected audience on 18 February 1943, as the tide of World War II was turning against Nazi Germany and its Axis allies. The speech is particularly notable as Goebbels almost mentions the Holocaust, when he begins saying "Ausrotten" (using the German word for extermination), but quickly changes it to Ausschaltung (i.e. exclusion). This was the same word Heinrich Himmler used on 18 December 1941, when he recorded the outcome of his discussion with Adolf Hitler on the Final Solution, wherein he wrote "als Partisanen auszurotten" ("exterminate them as partisans").

It is considered the most famous of Joseph Goebbels's speeches. The speech was the first public admission by the Nazi leadership that Germany faced serious dangers. Goebbels called for a total war (German: totalen Krieg) to secure victory over the Allies, and exhorted the German people to continue the war even though it would be long and difficult because—as he asserted—both Germany's survival and the survival of a non-Bolshevist Europe were at stake.

Annette Krauss (born 1971) works as artist, writer and educator. Krauss is a member of the Read-in collective and her projects include Read the Masks. Tradition is Not Given, Hidden Curriculum, Sites for Unlearning, and Spaces of Commoning. Currently, she is course leader of the Master Fine Arts at the HKU and Elise-Richter-Peek Post-Doc researcher at Academy of Fine Arts Vienna.

William Henry Seaman (1842–1915) was a United States Circuit Judge of the Seventh Circuit, a United States District Judge for the Eastern District of Wisconsin, and the 22nd Mayor of Sheboygan, Wisconsin. He also served in the Union Army during the American Civil War.

San José Castle (Spanish: *Castillo de San José*) was a military fort in Guatemala City. Construction began in 1843 and finished in 1846. It was built on the Buena Vista hill, which gave it a military advantage over the surrounding valley. In 1847, it became the most important ammunition warehouse in Guatemala. By 1852, it was also used as a prison. The fort was the site of heavy fighting during the revolt of April 1920, when forces loyal to Manuel Estrada Cabrera were defeated.

Velocity Tower is a mixed use development next to the Inner Ring Road in Sheffield City Centre, South Yorkshire, England, overlooking Ecclesall Road. The build is a continuation of a previous design to be built by Cala, titled 'Eclipse'.

Roll for the Galaxy is a dice game of building space empires for 2–5 players. Your dice represent your populace, whom you direct to develop new technologies, settle worlds, and ship goods. The player who best manages his workers and builds the most prosperous empire wins!

This dice version of Race for the Galaxy takes players on a new journey through the Galaxy, but with the feel of the original game.

Scott L. Thoele (born 1958) was a major general in the National Guard of the United States. His final assignment was as Deputy Commanding General for the Reserve Component of United States Army Forces Command. Thoele previously served as Deputy Commanding General, Army National Guard, United States Army Combined Arms Center, Fort Leavenworth. He retired on 30 September 2015 after 14 years of service in the U.S. Army and 21 years of service in the Illinois Army National Guard.

2011 MD is a bright micro-asteroid, classified as near-Earth object of the Apollo and Amor group, respectively. On 27 June 2011, at around 17:00 UTC (13:00 EDT), the object passed exceptionally close to Earth's surface at a distance of approximately 12,000 kilometers (7,500 mi), roughly the diameter of the Earth.

The United States Space Force's 4th Electromagnetic Warfare Squadron (4 EWS) is an offensive space electromagnetic warfare unit formerly located at Holloman Air Force Base, New Mexico, stood up operations July 1, 2014 at Peterson Space Force Base, Colorado.

Jack Ross (born 6 November 1962 in Auckland), is a New Zealand poet, novelist, translator, editor and lecturer in creative writing. A trilogy of novels has been published by two different publishers, Alan Brunton's Bumper Books (Volume 1) and Titus Books.

Fluorenone is an aromatic organic compound with the chemical formula C13H8O. It is bright fluorescent yellow in color and is a solid at room temperature.

It can be synthesised from fluorene with the addition of glacial acetic acid and sodium hypochlorite solution, undergoing an oxidation reaction.

It is used to make antimalaria drugs. It is used as a fragrance or odor agent in candles.

The laminar flamelet model is a combustion model that simplifies turbulent flames by representing them as an aggregate of locally one-dimensional, laminar flame structures. These flamelets are often modeled using counterflow diffusion flames, where fuel and oxidizer jets oppose each other. The model relies on mixture fraction and scalar dissipation to determine species mass fraction and temperature. Steady Laminar Flamelet Models (SLFM) assume a balance between chemical reactions and diffusion, but neglect curvature and transient effects. Transient laminar flamelet models (TLFM) have been proposed to improve SLFM. The model is useful for showing coupling between chemical reactions and molecular transport.

The 1944 explosion in Aarhus was a devastating event that occurred on July 4, 1944, at 13:47 local time in the Port of Aarhus, Denmark. A barge anchored in basin III, laden with 150 tonnes of ammunition, detonated, creating a massive explosion. The force of the blast sent a column of water a kilometer high into the air and was felt and heard up to 20 kilometers away.

The explosion resulted in widespread destruction in the harbor area. Buildings and cranes collapsed, and fires broke out in nearby storehouses. Debris rained down on the city, and another German ship loaded with ammunition caught fire but was saved.

The disaster resulted in the deaths of 39 people, primarily Danish dock workers, and left 250 injured. German authorities attributed the explosion to communist sabotage, a claim disputed by Danish labor unions. A memorial service was held in Aarhus Cathedral, and a monument was erected in Vestre Cemetery after the war to commemorate the victims, some of whom remained unidentified and were buried in a mass grave.
George Henson Estes Jr. (1873–1969) was a Brigadier General in the United States Army. He served from 1894 to 1937 and fought in multiple wars.

The compaction of ceramic powders is a crucial step in the production of both traditional and advanced ceramics. This process involves mechanically densifying a granular material into a cohesive solid. The quality of the resulting "green body" significantly impacts the final product's properties after sintering. Challenges in this process include minimizing defects and optimizing manufacturing technologies to reduce waste and improve the final product's quality.
This article lists the squads for each of the ten teams competing in the women's field hockey tournament at the 2010 Commonwealth Games.
Herbert Willoughby Ellis FRES FGS (28 June 1869-15 October 1943) was a British entomologist.

Pheona Nabasa Gladys Wall, commonly known as Pheona Wall, is a Ugandan lawyer, Public Relation Officer and serves as the president of Uganda Law Society since September 2020. She served as a lecturer of Law at Kampala International University.
George Ware (1924–2010) was an American dendrologist and Research Director of the Morton Arboretum. He is known for his elm research and the hybrid oak *Quercus × warei*, named in his honor.
Flight Sergeant William Thomas Smith DCM, MM (21 October 1896 – October 1994) was a British World War I flying ace credited with five aerial victories.

Cine City (originally named the Scala Cinema) was a cinema in Withington, Manchester, England located at 494 Wilmslow Road, Withington, Manchester, M20 3BG. It opened in 1912 as The Scala, and was the third cinema to open in Britain. When the popularity of picture houses reached its peak in the 1930s, The Scala was one of 109 cinemas in Manchester.

During the Second World War, the cinema escaped with minor damage when the road outside was hit by a small bomb in 1940. After the war, television led to a decline in cinema attendances, and by 1965, only 40 cinemas remained in Manchester. Cine City closed in July 2001, making it the third-longest running cinema in England.

By 2005 the building was in a bad state of repair, and was threatened with demolition. Although heritage groups won a stay of execution, the cinema was demolished in spring 2008. A new residential building has been constructed on the site.
Auguste-Henri-Victor Grandjean de Montigny (15 July 1776 – 2 March 1850) was a French architect who had considerable influence on the development of architecture in Brazil.
Arthur "Art" Quimby (1933–2010) was an American college basketball player for the UConn Huskies, where he set numerous rebounding records.
Patrizio Bianchi (born 1952) is an Italian economist, professor, and politician. He served as Minister of Education from 2021 to 2022.

Elizabeth Marian Swaney OLY (born 30 July 1984) is an American born-and-raised freestyle skier who competed for Hungary in the 2018 Winter Olympics in the women's halfpipe based on her Hungarian ancestry. She was unable to qualify for the 2014 Winter Olympics for Venezuela in both skeleton and freestyle skiing, and qualified for the 2018 Winter Olympics in the women's halfpipe, in which she placed last.

The 1983 Barcelona City Council election, also the 1983 Barcelona municipal election, was held on Sunday, 8 May 1983, to elect the 2nd City Council of the municipality of Barcelona. All 43 seats in the City Council were up for election. The election was held simultaneously with regional elections in thirteen autonomous communities and local elections all throughout Spain.

The University of Silesia in Katowice (Lang-pl) is an autonomous state-run university in Silesia Province, Katowice, Poland.

The university offers higher education and research facilities. It offers undergraduate, masters, and PhD degree programs, as well as postgraduate, postdoctoral research, habilitation, and continuous education and training programs.

Kingman is a city in, and the county seat of, Mohave County, Arizona, United States. It is named after Lewis Kingman, an engineer for the Atlantic and Pacific Railroad. It is located 105 miles (169 km) southeast of Las Vegas, Nevada, and 180 miles (290 km) northwest of Arizona's state capital, Phoenix.

Sheddrick Tobias "Buck" Gurley (born April 7, 1978) is a former American football defensive tackle. Gurley played college football for Florida. He played professionally in the NFL for the Miami Dolphins, Chicago Bears, and Tampa Bay Buccaneers and was part of the 2002 Buccaneers team that won Super Bowl XXXVII.
Beau Mirchoff (born January 13, 1989) is an American-Canadian actor. He is known for his roles in *Scary Movie 4*, *Desperate Housewives*, *Awkward.*, *The Fosters*, and *Good Trouble*.

Swallow Music Awards (Lang-hy, Tsitsernak yerazhshtakan mrcanakabashkhutiun) is an annual national music awards ceremony first held in Yerevan, Armenia, on 25 March 2017. The awards are created and sponsored by RA Ministry of Culture to appreciate and spur the activities of individuals and creators of various genres of Armenian musical art, as well as to display the significant achievements in the field of music to the broader layers of society. The awards consist of four parts: achievements in classical genre, folk genre, mass pop genre, and special/other awards. During the ceremony artists are given the awards designed by Armenian painter and designer Arman Nur. Each year, the award ceremony airs live on Public Television company of Armenia.

Matthew Asen Kantakouzenos or Cantacuzenus (Greek: Ματθαῖος Ἀσάνης Καντακουζηνός, Matthaios Asanēs Kantakouzēnos, c. 1325 – 1383 or 1391) was Byzantine Emperor from 1353 to 1357 and later Despot of the Morea from 1380 to 1383.

Tidbinbilla Nature Reserve is a protected area located approximately 40 km (25 mi) southwest of Canberra in the Australian Capital Territory, Australia. Established in 1971, the reserve spans 54.5 square kilometers (21.0 sq mi) and is managed by Territory and Municipal Services. It is listed on the Australian National Heritage List as part of the Australian Alps National Parks and Reserves.

The area has a rich Indigenous history, with evidence of Aboriginal occupation dating back 25,000 years, including rock paintings at Gibraltar Peak. The name "Tidbinbilla" is derived from the Aboriginal word 'Jedbinbilla', meaning "a place where boys become men." European history is also present, with remnants of 19th-century pastoral settlements like Nil Desperandum and Rock Valley Homestead.

Tidbinbilla offers a variety of walking trails showcasing native Australian fauna such as kangaroos, wallabies, koalas, and platypuses. The reserve suffered significant damage during the 2003 Canberra bushfires, but has since recovered. It is also involved in breeding programs for endangered species like the Northern Corroboree Frog and the Southern Brush-tailed Rock-wallaby. The reserve is located near the Canberra Deep Space Communication Complex and was the site of a live international TV broadcast in 1988.

The National Reserve System (NRS) is a group of protected areas across Australia, established in 1992. It was created in response to the international Convention on Biological Diversity. Managed by the Australian Government's Department of the Environment, it includes over 137 million hectares, incorporating both government-owned and privately protected areas.

The system initially expanded rapidly with federal funding and cooperation from states and territories, including the establishment of Indigenous Protected Areas. Tasmania pioneered partnerships to create protected areas on private land. The NRS is considered a national priority, with ongoing investment targeting under-protected areas and a long-term strategy for biodiversity conservation. The WWF Australia and CSIRO have praised the NRS as a cost-effective conservation measure and an important tool to combat the effects of climate change.

Arenys de Mar (IPA-ca, arenys being Catalan for "sands (of a seasonal creek)", and de mar for "by the sea" as opposed to Arenys de Munt, "sands up hill") is one of the main municipalities of the comarca of Maresme, Barcelona Province, Catalonia, Spain. It is situated on the coast between Caldes d'Estrac and Canet de Mar, about 40 kilometres (25 miles) northeast of Barcelona. As of 2010, it had a population of 14,688.

Arenys' harbour was for many years the only existing port on the north coast of Barcelona. The beaches, framed by hills which run right down to the sea, give a preview of the neighbouring Costa Brava.
Oliver Adolphus Samuels (born November 4, 1948) is a Jamaican actor and comedian, best known for his television series *Oliver at Large*.

The Republic of Benin was a short-lived puppet state proclaimed on September 19, 1967, by Biafran forces occupying Nigeria's Mid-Western Region during the Nigerian Civil War. The republic's capital was Benin City, and it had an estimated population of 3 million.

Led by Albert Okonkwo, the republic was an attempt to distance the region from Biafra and garner support from non-Igbo residents, though this ultimately failed. The declaration of the Republic of Benin occurred as Biafran forces retreated from advancing Nigerian federal troops. The republic ceased to exist on September 20, 1967, a day after its proclamation, when Nigerian forces recaptured Benin City. The Biafran occupation and the creation of the Republic of Benin damaged support for Biafra and escalated the conflict into a full-scale war.

Francisco de Holanda (September 6, 1517 – June 19, 1585) was a Portuguese Renaissance painter, architect, sculptor, essayist, and art theorist. Born in Lisbon, he initially worked as an illuminator, following in the footsteps of his father, António de Holanda.

Between 1538 and 1547, Francisco studied in Italy, where he was exposed to the High Renaissance and Classicism. He became acquainted with prominent figures such as Vittoria Colonna, Parmigianino, Giambologna, and Michelangelo, who greatly influenced his artistic development.

Upon his return to Portugal at the age of 30, he secured commissions from the Portuguese King, the Cardinal-Archbishop of Évora, King John III, and King Sebastian. Francisco embraced Renaissance aesthetic values, emphasizing personal originality and the connection between nature and classical art. His treatise, "On Ancient Painting" (1548), reflects his passion for Classicism and includes dialogues with Michelangelo.

Francisco is also known for his drawings of Italian antiquities, commissioned by King João III, and for designing the façade of the Church of Our Lady of Grace in Évora. He wrote the first essay on urbanism in the Iberian Peninsula, "On the construction lacking to the city of Lisbon".

GABA transporter type 2 (GAT2) is a protein that, in humans, is encoded by the SLC6A13 gene. It is a GABA transporter, but it also transports taurine.

GAT2 is expressed in the brain, liver, and kidneys. In the brain, deletion of the GAT2 gene in mice results in a slight elevation of taurine levels. In the liver, GAT2 appears to be the major taurine transporter. In the kidneys, GAT2 is present in proximal tubules, but its function is unknown.

The clinical significance of GAT2 is currently undetermined. While some anticonvulsants act upon GABA transporters, GAT1 and GAT3 are more likely to have anticonvulsant effects due to their higher expression levels in the brain.

The Redan Football Netball Club (nicknamed the Lions) is an Australian rules football and netball club located in the city of Ballarat, Victoria. The football team currently competes in the Ballarat Football League. Redan has a history of 59 Premierships including 11 Senior Football Premierships and was named the Victorian Country Football League Most Disciplined Club in 2004 & 2005.

Redan players have also won the Henderson Medal (Ballarat Football League's best player award) on 14 occasions with two players, Michael Smith (1994, 1994) and Jarrod Edwards (2006, 2007, 2008 and 2009) winning the award in consecutive years. Edwards is the first player in BFL history to win four consecutive Henderson Medals.

The club also has four winners of the Tony Lockett Medal, for the leading goalscorer in the Ballarat Football League. Redan currently play their home matches at City Oval in Ballarat and play in a predominantly maroon strip with a maroon top band and a lion logo on the chest.

The first Redan game was reported in The Ballarat Star Saturday September 2, 1871 against the Ballarat Football Club. The Redan team was made up from miners from the Band of Hope mine and their average weight was 14 Stone (90 kg). The Star reported that the Redan team were everywhere, kicking and tripping regardless of the consequences.

Former Adelaide Crows coach, the late Phil Walsh, played for Redan prior to be being recruited by Collingwood.

Joel Spitzer is an American smoking cessation educator known for advocating quitting "cold turkey" and for his book, *Never Take Another Puff*. He began presenting smoking prevention seminars in 1971 and has conducted hundreds of stop-smoking clinics.

The crested servaline genet (Genetta cristata), also known as the crested genet, is a genet species endemic to Nigeria and Cameroon. As the population has declined due to loss of habitat, it is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. It was first recorded in the Mamfe Division in Cameroon and initially considered a subspecies of the servaline genet (Genetta servalina). But now it is regarded as a distinct species.

Annamarie Tendler (born June 9, 1985) is an American make-up artist, writer, and designer. She gained recognition as an early online beauty guru and has worked on various television series, comedy specials, and Broadway shows. Tendler owns Silk Parlor, handcrafting Victorian-style lampshades. She exhibited artwork at The Other Art Fair in 2021.

Joseph Alexander Heller (born April 10, 1941 in Sydney, Australia) is an Israeli zoologist-malacologist. Heller is an Associate Professor Emeritus at the Department of Ecology, Evolution and Behavior in the Silverman Institute of Life Sciences, Faculty of Science, at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem.

Heller immigrated to Israel in 1949. He received his B.Sc. in Life Science; Zoology and Microbiology in 1965, MSc in Zoology in 1968 and his PhD in 1972 at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. His dissertation thesis is titled "Studies on the systematics distribution and ecology of the landsnail Buliminus in Israel".

Heller was a visiting scientist at the University of Liverpool, University of Bristol, University of Cape Town, and the State University of New York at Stony Brook. He published 4 books and over 100 articles. His books are the first ever on the land snails of Israel and on marine molluscs of the land of Israel.

Matt Seers (born 28 June 1974) is an Australian former professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s. He played for the North Sydney Bears, Wests Tigers and Wakefield Trinity Wildcats, as a fullback, centre or five-eighth.

The Kooralbyn International School (TKIS) is an independent, co-educational, boarding and day school, located in Kooralbyn, about 64 km south of Brisbane, Queensland, Australia. TKIS is the only school in Australia to have produced two Young Australian of the Year recipients (Cathy Freeman and Scott Hocknull).

Mieczyslaw "Mickey Doyle" Kuzik is a fictional character in the HBO TV series Boardwalk Empire. He is played by Paul Sparks. Mickey Doyle is loosely based on Polish American mobster Mickey Duffy.

Mickey is a gangster in Prohibition-era Atlantic City. For the first two seasons, he is the main rival of harlem liquor kingpin Chalky White. He is known for his distinctive, nasal laugh, and serves as the series' comic relief.
Park Sol-mi (born Park Hye-jeong on January 3, 1978) is a South Korean actress.

Whiteboard animation is a presentation style that involves recording drawings on a whiteboard in sequence and playing them back as an animation, creating a time-lapse or stop-motion effect. Also known as "video scribing" or "animated doodling," it is used by businesses and educators.

The production process includes topic selection, scriptwriting, and creating animation drafts. Whiteboard animation has been used in commercials, online videos, and for internal training. While popular for educational purposes, a 2016 study found little evidence that it is more effective than other learning methods. The Royal Society of Arts gained popularity using whiteboard animations of public events.

The American Association of Colleges of Nursing (AACN) is a national organization founded in 1969. It represents over 840 member schools of nursing at baccalaureate and graduate levels. AACN also provides scholarship opportunities to nursing students. Its headquarters are located in Washington, D.C.

Margaret Cuninggim (October 15, 1914 in Nashville, Tennessee – July 4, 1986 in St. Petersburg, Florida) was an American university professor and administrator. She served as Dean of Women at the University of Tennessee from 1957 to 1966 and at Vanderbilt University from 1966 to 1973. Additionally, she served as the President of the Tennessee Association of Women's Deans from 1958 to 1960. The Margaret Cuninggim Women's Center at Vanderbilt University in named in her honor.

Chandler School is an independent, co-ed day school for students in kindergarten through grade eight located in Pasadena, California United States, founded in 1950. School enrollment is 450 students. The 4.5-acre (1.8 ha) campus overlooks the Pasadena Rose Bowl and the Arroyo Seco.
Heber Springs is a city in and the county seat of Cleburne County, Arkansas, United States. The population was 7,165 at the 2010 census.

Hazel Hawke AO (née Masterson; 20 July 1929 – 23 May 2013) was an Australian community worker, writer, and pianist. She was the wife of Bob Hawke, the Prime Minister of Australia from 1983 to 1991; she was married to him from 1956 until their divorce in 1995. Hawke revealed in 2003 that she had Alzheimer's disease, from which she died in 2013.

Nagorik Natya Sampradaya is a Bangladeshi theatre group founded in 1968. After Bangladesh gained independence, the troupe staged their first stage production in 1972. In April 2018, Nagorik celebrated its Golden Jubilee with a three-day event in Dhaka. Some of Bangladesh's biggest cultural names have been involved with the troupe.
Animal Control Products is a New Zealand state-owned enterprise founded in 1991. It manufactures and distributes pest control products throughout New Zealand.

Araxá is a municipality in the state of Minas Gerais, Brazil. In 2020, the population was estimated at 107,337. The name comes from the indigenous Araxá people and means "the place from where the sun is seen first". The city is known for its spa with medicinal mud and mineral waters.

The InSpiral Lounge was a vegetarian restaurant, café and events venue in Camden Lock, Camden, London, England, overlooking the Regent's Canal and Camden Market until its closure in 2016. It was the trading name of Ekopia Ltd.

El Camino High School is one of two high schools of the Oceanside Unified School District, located at 400 Rancho Del Oro Drive in Oceanside, California. Declared a California Distinguished School in the early 2000s, it was originally named "Oceanside High School East", and officially became El Camino High School in 1976. El Camino's Truax Theatre was built in the early 1980s and houses a large performance venue, an adjacent classroom with stage and large music and drama rooms. It also recently built a new Science and Technology Building and is undergoing constant construction to clean up the campus. El Camino maintains a close athletic rivalry with Oceanside High School. The ECHS mascot is the "Wildcat" and the school colors are brown and gold.
Fatma Nur Serter (born 10 November 1948) is a female Turkish academic and politician.
Scrabble variants are games created by changing the normal Scrabble rules or equipment.

*Terrace House: Closing Door* is a 2015 Japanese film directed by Masato Maeda, serving as a continuation of the reality television series *Terrace House: Boys × Girls Next Door*. The film follows the lives of six young adults living together in a shared house, focusing on their relationships and personal growth. The cast includes both new and returning members from the series, such as Tetsuya Sugaya and Yuiko Matsukawa. Jin Otabe, an editor, plans to write a book about Terrace House and interviews former house members. The film explores themes of love, friendship, and ambition as the housemates navigate their lives and relationships. *Terrace House: Closing Door* was a commercial success, opening at number one at the Japanese box office and grossing ¥1.3 billion.

Marley Sianto Sikawa (born c. 1990), is a Kenyan communications and marketing professional, who serves as the tourism marketing coordinator for Narok County, one of the 47 administrative Kenyan counties.

Kerry Williams Kelso O'Brien (born 19 July 1951), is a former Australian politician. O'Brien was a member of the Australian Senate for the state of Tasmania from September 1996 until June 2011, representing the Australian Labor Party.

Banjara Hills is an affluent urban neighborhood in Hyderabad, Telangana, India. With a population around 150,000, it is known for its upscale hotels, restaurants, shopping malls, and commercial establishments.

The area's development began in 1927 when Nawab Mehdi Nawaz Jung, a minister to the Nizam, acquired the land and built his residence, Banjara Bhavan. Despite the Nizam's suggestion to name the area after him, the Nawab chose to honor the original inhabitants, the Banjaras. Banjara Bhavan was visited by prominent figures like Jawaharlal Nehru and Rabindranath Tagore.

Key features of Banjara Hills include star hotels like Taj Krishna, various restaurants offering international cuisines, and shopping malls such as GVK One and City Center. The Laxmi Cyber Center is the tallest building in the area. It also houses the Jalagam Vengal Rao Park, Muffakham Jah College of Engineering and Technology, KBR Park, Lamakaan cultural center, and the Svayambhu Sri Lakshmi Narasimha Swamy Temple. Banjara Hills is well-connected to other parts of Hyderabad by TSRTC buses and has a good road network.

Abubakar Umar Tutare is a Nigerian politician who was elected Senator for the Taraba Central Senatorial District of Taraba State, Nigeria in the April 2011 general elections, running on the People's Democratic Party (PDP) platform.
Frank Simmons Leavitt (1891–1953), known as Man Mountain Dean, was an American professional wrestler, football player, actor, and soldier. He retired from wrestling in 1940.
Yambio Airport is an airport serving Yambio in South Sudan.
Maestrale was the lead ship of her class of four destroyers built for the Regia Marina (Royal Italian Navy) in the early 1930s. Completed in 1934, she served in World War II.
Malcolm Lockyer (born in Greenwich, London) was a British composer and conductor for film, television, and radio.
*ACE*, or *Advanced Computer Entertainment*, was a British video game magazine published from 1987 to 1992, covering multiple platforms and including demo cassettes/disks.

In ecology, rarefaction is a statistical technique used to standardize species richness data, allowing for meaningful comparisons between samples with varying sizes. Developed in 1968 by Howard Sanders to analyze marine ecosystems, rarefaction addresses the problem of comparing species richness when sample sizes differ. The technique involves creating rarefaction curves, which plot the number of species as a function of the number of individuals or samples.

Rarefaction has undergone several refinements since its initial development, with researchers like Stuart Hurlbert and Daniel Simberloff addressing issues such as overestimation of species numbers. Today, it is used to understand diversity at various taxonomic levels and is applied in diverse fields, including microbiome research and community ecology studies. While useful for estimating species richness and facilitating data comparison, rarefaction has limitations. It assumes random species distribution, is sensitive to uneven species abundance, and does not account for species composition or provide estimates of asymptotic richness.

Faces of Ground Zero: A Photographic Tribute to America's Heroes was a traveling photo exhibition about the September 11 attacks. It was shown at several major cities in the United States, aiming to educate the public about the impact of modern urban terrorism. Faces of Ground Zero was one of the most widely seen exhibits about 9/11 and its aftermath.

The exhibit consisted of life-size photographs (9 ft × 4 ft framed images) of emergency workers, survivors, and relatives of victims of the attacks; some 272 people in all. (Some of the portraits included two or three subjects.) The aim was to capture the sense of loss, pain, and bravery of the time.

The touring exhibit was open to the public, free of charge. Made by photographer Joe McNally, in the Moby C Studio, a few blocks from the "Ground Zero" World Trade Center site in the immediate aftermath of 9/11, with "Moby C", the world's largest one-of-a-kind instant camera.

A hardcover companion photobook, Faces of Ground Zero: Portraits of the Heroes of September 11, 2001 (New York: Little, Brown & Co., 2002) was published to commemorate the project with a foreword by Rudy Giuliani and an original essay by McNally. A large percentage of the proceeds went to 9/11 charities.

Navajo music is primarily vocal, traditionally used in ceremonies to restore balance and harmony. Instruments like drums, rattles, and flutes accompany specific songs. These sacred songs, part of Navajo spiritual literature, contrast with popular music for recreation and work. Contemporary Navajo music includes genres like rock, metal, hip hop, and country.

Vlașca County is a former first-order administrative division of the Kingdom of Romania, in southern Muntenia, located between Bucharest and the Danube, which is now mostly the county of Giurgiu. The county seat was Giurgiu.

The county was located in the southern part of Greater Romania, in the southern part of the historical region of Muntenia. The county was bordered on the west by Teleorman County, on the northwest by Argeș County, on the north by Dâmbovița County, on the east by Ilfov County, and to the south by the Kingdom of Bulgaria. Its surface coincides in large part with that of the present Giurgiu County, but there are small parts of it that are included in today's Teleorman County.

The Show Must Go On (Korean: 우아한 세계; RR: Uahan segye; lit: Elegant World) is a 2007 South Korean action film written and directed by Han Jae-rim, starring Song Kang-ho in the lead role. The film revolves around a full-time gangster who's aspiring to be a full-time husband and dad.

The film released theatrically in South Korea on 5 April 2007.

*Mysterious Girlfriend X* (謎の彼女X, *Nazo no Kanojo Ekkusu*) is a Japanese romantic comedy manga series written and illustrated by Riichi Ueshiba. It was serialized in *Monthly Afternoon* from 2006 to 2014, and tells the story of Akira Tsubaki and Mikoto Urabe, focusing on their unusual relationship that begins with Tsubaki's addiction to Urabe's drool. An anime adaptation aired in 2012, with an original video animation released later that year.

The olfactory tract is a bundle of nerve fibers in the brain that connects the olfactory bulb to several regions including the olfactory tubercle, anterior olfactory nucleus, and amygdala. It divides into medial and lateral striae. Damage to the olfactory tract can cause anosmia, and the depth of the olfactory sulcus can indicate congenital anosmia.

*NFL Blitz* is a series of American football video games that originated in 1997. Initially developed by Midway Games, the *Blitz* titles are known for their fast-paced, arcade-style gameplay that deviates from the rules of the National Football League (NFL) to encourage more aggressive play. Key differences include having only seven players per side (later increased), the allowance of late hits and excessive celebrations, and the absence of timeouts. The gameplay also features exaggerated animations and wrestling-style moves.

Midway released new *Blitz* titles annually until 2004. After losing the NFL license, Midway created *Blitz: The League* in 2005, featuring fictional teams and a mature storyline with explicit content. Following Midway's bankruptcy, Electronic Arts (EA Sports) acquired the *NFL Blitz* rights and released a new title, *NFL Blitz*, in 2012. In 2000, Midway also released a spinoff based on the Arena Football League, titled *Kurt Warner's Arena Football Unleashed*. *Blitz: The League* received mostly positive reviews, with critics praising its return to the over-the-top style of the original games.
Black Noise: Rap Music and Black Culture in Contemporary America is a 1994 book by Tricia Rose. It was released in hardback on April 29, 1994 through Wesleyan University Press.

Essert-Pittet (IPA-fr) is a former municipality in the district of Jura-Nord Vaudois of the canton of Vaud in Switzerland. In 2017 the former municipalities of Essert-Pittet and Corcelles-sur-Chavornay merged into the municipality of Chavornay.
Police impersonation is the act of falsely portraying oneself as a member of the police for the purpose of deception.

An institute is an organisational body created for a certain purpose. They are often research organisations (research institutes) created to do research on specific topics, or can also be a professional body.

In some countries, institutes can be part of a university or other institutions of higher education, either as a group of departments or an autonomous educational institution without a traditional university status such as a "university institute" (see Institute of Technology). In some countries, such as South Korea and India, private schools are sometimes referred to as institutes, and in Spain, secondary schools are referred to as institutes.

Historically, in some countries institutes were educational units imparting vocational training and often incorporating libraries, also known as mechanics' institutes.

The word "institute" comes from a Latin word institutum meaning "facility" or "habit"; from instituere meaning "build", "create", "raise" or "educate".

Christian Malford is a village and civil parish in Wiltshire, England, near Chippenham. The population of the parish was 705 in 2011.

The village is known to paleontologists for a rediscovered Lagerstätte, a site of remarkably preserved fossils in Middle Jurassic Oxford clay. A chance discovery in the 19th century uncovered thousands of exquisitely preserved ammonites, fish, and crustaceans.

The Church of England parish church of All Saints dates from c. 1300 and is a Grade I listed building. A Congregational chapel, named Shecaniah, was built in 1836; it closed in 1998 and was converted for residential use.

Christian Malford is near a crossing point of the River Avon. The M4 motorway, built in 1971, passes less than a mile north of the village centre. A railway station serving the village operated from 1926 to 1965; the nearest station is now at Chippenham. The village has a primary school, a village hall, and a pub.

Hyundai Chungun High School (Korean: 현대청운고등학교; HCU), is a private boarding high school located in Dong-gu, Ulsan, South Korea. It is one of the six independent private high schools (자율형 사립고) in South Korea that require an application of nationwide scale and admissions test to the applicant students. Since students who want to get in HCU usually get straight A when they are at middle school and take a test to get in HCU, they usually get rank 1 or rank 2 for their grades of CSAT(대학수학능력시험)College Scholastic Ability Test statistically. For example, there was a research for those who took CSAT in 2016(2017학년도 대학수학능력시험), and HCU placed the 4th in nationwide for the average CSAT grade for Korean, Mathematics, and English. The percentage of rank 1 and rank 2 is 4%, 7% respectively. The aggregate percentile for rank 2 is 11%. Academic grading in South Korea 72.6% of students at HCU in 2016 got rank 1 or rank 2 for their CSAT grade.
Ruby is a color that is a representation of the color of the cut and polished ruby gemstone and is a shade of red or pink.

*Bitter Tears: Ballads of the American Indian* is a concept album by Johnny Cash, released in 1964 on Columbia Records. The album focuses on the history of and contemporary issues facing Native Americans in the United States, a subject that Cash became interested in due to his belief that he had some Cherokee ancestry. Five of the songs were written by Peter La Farge, two by Cash, and one by Cash and Johnny Horton. The album's single, "The Ballad of Ira Hayes," reached No. 3 on the Billboard Hot Country Singles chart.

The album and single initially met with resistance and censorship from some radio stations, DJs, and fans due to the controversial social issues they addressed. Cash responded by taking out an advertisement in Billboard magazine criticizing those who refused to play "The Ballad of Ira Hayes." In 2014, a re-recording of the album, *Look Again To The Wind: Johnny Cash’s Bitter Tears Revisited*, was released, along with a documentary film, *We're Still Here: Johnny Cash's Bitter Tears Revisited*.

Cardea or Carda was the ancient Roman goddess of the hinge (Latin cardo, cardinis), Roman doors being hung on pivot hinges. The Augustan poet Ovid conflates her with another archaic goddess named Carna, whose festival was celebrated on the Kalends of June and for whom he gives the alternative name Cranê or Cranea, a nymph. Ovid's conflation of the goddesses is likely to have been his poetic invention, but it has also been conjectured that Carna was a contracted form of Cardina, and at minimum Ovid was observing that their traditions were congruent.

Conrad Albert Lau (1921–1964) was an aeronautical engineer at Chance Vought, later Ling-Temco-Vought. He contributed to the F4U Corsair, F7U Cutlass, F8U Crusader, and A-7 Corsair II aircraft programs. He also contributed to early mission studies for the Apollo program.

"Audi" is a song by American rapper Smokepurpp, released on May 17, 2017, as part of the album *Deadstar*. The music video was released the following day and has amassed over 60 million views. A sequel, "Audi II", was released in 2019.

Paul Speratus (13 December 1484 – 12 August 1551) was a Swabian Catholic priest who became a Protestant preacher, reformer and hymn-writer. In 1523, he helped Martin Luther to create the First Lutheran hymnal, published in 1524 and called Achtliederbuch.
Eileen Dennes (1898–1991) was an Irish silent film actress who began her career on stage in the early 1910s.
The *Maestrale* was an Italian *Maestrale*-class destroyer built for the Royal Italian Navy in the early 1930s. *Maestrale* was sunk on September 9, 1943, due to sabotage.

John Zachary Young FRS (18 March 1907 – 4 July 1997), generally known as "JZ" or "JZY", was an English zoologist and neurophysiologist, described as "one of the most influential biologists of the 20th century".

Blackwell Farm is the name of one of the estates situated on the Hog’s Back, on the West side of Guildford. Located in the parish of Compton, the historical farm is currently a controversial issue as part of the Guildford Borough Council’s Local Plan consultation.

The new areas or new districts of the People's Republic of China are new urban districts that are given special economic and development support by the Chinese Central Government or regional government. New areas are divided into two varieties: administrative or management and further divided into levels: state-level, provincial-level, and prefectural-level.

David John Lee (born 26 November 1969) is an English football coach and former professional player.

Lee played as a defender from 1988 until 2004, notably played in the Premier League for Chelsea and in the Australian top flight for Parramatta Power. He also played in the Football League for Reading, Plymouth Argyle, Portsmouth, Sheffield United, Bristol Rovers, Crystal Palace, Colchester United and Exeter City. He then went on to play in Non-league for Havant & Waterlooville, Forest Green Rovers, Weston-super-Mare, Yate Town and Mangotsfield United. He was capped ten times by England U21.

After retiring became a scout for Bristol City and later assistant manager of Northampton Town where he briefly managed in a caretaker role.

Edith Gertrude Clements (née Schwartz; 1874–1971) was an American botanist, plant ecologist, and botanical artist. She was the first woman to earn a Ph.D. from the University of Nebraska in 1904. Clements is known for her research and illustrations in plant ecology and her collaboration with her husband, Frederic Clements.

She taught at the University of Nevada and the University of Minnesota, and she was a field assistant for the Carnegie Institution of Washington. Clements co-founded the Alpine Laboratory with her husband in Colorado, training botanists and ecologists for four decades. She illustrated their joint publications, including *Rocky Mountain Flowers*. In her memoir, *Adventures in Ecology*, she described her contributions to their expeditions. The Edith S. and Frederic E. Clements Papers are archived at the University of Wyoming.

Colonel Sir John Wallace Pringle, CB, FRGS (23 May 1863 – 16 July 1938) was a British engineer who was Chief Inspecting Officer of the Railways Inspectorate of the Ministry of Transport from 1916 to 1929. As such he was in charge of investigations into a number of serious railway accidents in the UK.

WNED-FM is a non-commercial, classical music radio station broadcasting at 94.5 MHz in Buffalo, New York. Owned by Buffalo Toronto Public Media, it is a sister station to WBFO and WNED-TV. WNED-FM operates with 94,000 watts, making it a grandfathered "Superpower" Class B FM station whose signal reaches into Toronto and the Niagara Peninsula of Ontario.

The station first signed on in 1960 as WEBR-FM, a commercial station. It went through several call sign changes before the Western New York Educational TV Association acquired it in 1975 and converted it to non-commercial operation. In 1976, it became WNED-FM and adopted its classical music format. WNED-FM produces local programming, drawing from its extensive music library and collaborating with local ensembles such as the Buffalo Philharmonic Orchestra. WNED-FM broadcasts in HD Radio, with its HD2 subchannel carrying the "JazzWorks" network feed.

Randy Kaplan (b. Randall Leigh Kaplan) is an American songwriter, playwright, poet, and performer. He gained renown for his “not-JUST-for-kids” music in the mid-aughts, first in Brooklyn, New York, and later nationwide. Dedicated members of his fan base call themselves FORKs (Fans of Randy Kaplan). He was signed with myKaZoo Music for his CD dedicated to Country Blues and Ragtime music (Mr. Diddie Wah Diddie), which Universal Music released.

Randy has released six not-JUST-for-kids CDs (with his seventh, Shake It and Break It, released on May 10, 2019). Previous "kids" records include Five Cent Piece (2006), Loquat Rooftop (2008), The Kids Are All Id (2010), Mr. Diddie Wah Diddie (2012), Jam on Rye (2014), and Trippin'Round the Mitten (2017). Previous not-REALLY-for-kids records include Boyish Hips (1997), Reborn As Bees (1999), Lake Champions (1999), Miraculous Dissolving Cures (2001), Perfect Gentleman (2004), Ancient Ruins (2008), Durango (2008), and Songs For Old Lovers (2011). Randy has also released several digital singles, including "Don't Fill Up On Chips,""Nagasaki,""On the Phone on the Toilet,"and"Hugs for My Family, High Fives for My Friends."

Randy's songs have been included on many compilations, including those for Putumayo World Music, Fast Folk, and Park Slope Parents.

La Grosse Décharge Est is a tributary of the north shore of La Grosse Décharge Ouest, flowing entirely in the town of Baie-Saint-Paul, in the Charlevoix Regional County Municipality, in the administrative region of Capitale-Nationale, in the province of Quebec, in Canada.

This valley is mainly served by secondary forest roads which connect to the Chemin du Séminaire which runs along the south side of La Grosse Décharge Ouest. Forestry is the main economic activity in this valley; recreational tourism, second.

The surface of La Grosse Décharge Est is generally frozen from the beginning of December until the beginning of April; however, safe circulation on the ice is generally done from mid-December to the end of March. The water level of the river varies with the seasons and the precipitation; the spring flood generally occurs in April.

South Burnie is a locality and suburb of Burnie in the local government area of City of Burnie, in the North West region of Tasmania. It is located about 2.5 kilometres (1.6 mi) south-east of the town of Burnie. The Bass Highway passes from south-east to north-west along the shore of Bass Strait, which forms part of the northern boundary. The Emu River forms part of the eastern boundary. The 2016 census determined a population of 331 for the state suburb of South Burnie.

"Tight Connection to My Heart (Has Anybody Seen My Love)" is a song by Bob Dylan, released in 1985 on the album *Empire Burlesque*. It was released as a single, reaching the Top 10 in New Zealand. Paul Schrader directed a music video for the song set in Tokyo. An early version, titled "Someone's Got a Hold of My Heart", was recorded in 1983. The song makes references to Humphrey Bogart films.

NBA Street Vol. 2 is a basketball video game, published by Electronic Arts under the EA Sports BIG label and developed by NuFX and EA Canada. It is the sequel to NBA Street and the second game in the NBA Street series. It was released on April 29, 2003 for the PlayStation 2, GameCube, and, for the first time in the series, on the Xbox. Japan was only able to see a PlayStation 2 release of this game. The GameCube version was also planned to be released in that region, but it was canceled for unknown reasons.

Sapporo Television Broadcasting Co., Ltd. (札幌テレビ放送株式会社, Sapporo Terebi Hōsō kabushiki gaisha), also known as STV, is a Japanese private television broadcasting station headquartered in Sapporo, Hokkaido. It was founded on April 8, 1958, and serves Hokkaido Prefecture. Toyoaki Negishi is the company's current president.

STV began broadcasting on April 1, 1959, initially as a dual affiliate of NNN and FNN. In 1962, it launched STV Radio, becoming the only broadcaster in Japan to start TV broadcasts before radio broadcasts. STV was also involved in broadcasting the Sapporo Winter Olympics in 1972. The network has consistently achieved high TV ratings, holding the number one position for 12 years as of 2019 and for 27 years in all-day ratings since 1993.
**Andrea Batista Schlesinger** (born October 27, 1976) is an American political writer and campaigner. She directed the Drum Major Institute from 2002 to 2009.

Clyde Lee Chambliss Jr. (born April 16, 1969) is an American politician and Republican member of the Alabama Senate, representing the 30th district since 2014. He is a graduate of the University of Alabama. In 2019, Chambliss sponsored the Human Life Protection Act, which bans all abortions except in cases of endangerment to the mother's health.
*Vijenac* is a Croatian biweekly magazine for literature, arts, and science, published in Zagreb by Matica hrvatska. It is considered a successor to the 19th-century literary magazine *Vienac*.
Tõnis Kõiv (born 29 October 1970, in Paide) is an Estonian politician and lawyer who represented the Estonian Reform Party in the Riigikogu from 2005 to 2015.
**Ruth Cameron was a singer, manager, and record producer.**

Dan Erickson (born January 26, 1984) is an American television screenwriter, showrunner, and producer. He created the thriller television show Severance, starring Adam Scott and directed by Ben Stiller, which premiered on the Apple TV+ streaming service in February 2022.
Joan Maie Freeman AO (1918–1998) was an Australian nuclear physicist and radar researcher.

*Millennium Fantasy* is a composition for solo piano and orchestra by the American composer Ellen Taaffe Zwilich. The piece is in two movements and has a duration of approximately 20 minutes. Its thematic material is derived from the folk song "Come All You Fair and Tender Ladies." *Millennium Fantasy* was well-received at its world premiere. A recording of the work performed by Jeffrey Biegel and the Florida State University Symphony Orchestra was released in 2010.

Soyuz TMA-11 was a Roskosmos Soyuz TMA spacecraft mission to the International Space Station (ISS). It launched on October 10, 2007, carrying cosmonauts Yuri Malenchenko and Peggy Whitson, along with spaceflight participant Sheikh Muszaphar Shukor.

The mission docked with the ISS on October 12, 2007, and undocked on April 19, 2008. The landing in Kazakhstan was a ballistic reentry, resulting in the spacecraft landing 475 km from the intended landing point. Yi So-yeon, who landed with the crew, was hospitalized due to injuries sustained during the rough landing. An investigation attributed the unusual reentry to the failure of a pyro-bolt, potentially caused by long-term exposure to the electrical environment of the ISS. The mission lasted 191 days, 19 hours, and 17 minutes.

The Virginia Constitutional Convention of 1864 convened in Alexandria from February 13 to April 11. It aimed to support the Union war effort and determine the future governance of Virginia. The convention voted to abolish slavery immediately without compensation and introduced balloting for state officials. The new constitution was enforceable only in Union-controlled areas.

The Camp Beauregard Memorial, outside Water Valley, Kentucky on Kentucky state road 2422 northeast of town, marks the site of a Confederate States Army encampment named for General P. G. T. Beauregard. The camp was situated to protect the right flank of the Confederate encampment at Columbus, Kentucky.
FC Ingolstadt 04 II is the reserve team of German association football club FC Ingolstadt 04. The team plays in the Bayernliga Süd, the fifth tier of the German football league system.

Erdinç Tekir (born November 17, 1966) is a Turkish national known for his involvement in the Black Sea hostage crisis in 1996 and his activity with the IHH (İnsani Yardım Vakfı) humanitarian organization. He participated in conflicts in Abkhazia and attempted to join the Chechen War. He was aboard the Mavi Marmara during the Gaza flotilla raid.

USS *Romulus* (ARL-22) was a *LST-542*-class tank landing ship converted to an *Achelous*-class landing craft repair ship. She was commissioned in 1944, saw service in World War II and the Korean War, and was decommissioned in 1956. In 1961, she was transferred to the Philippine Navy and renamed BRP *Aklan* (AR-67). She was renamed BRP *Kamagong* in 1975 and served until 1989.
Wodan Timbur Coaster is a wooden roller coaster, located in the Europa-Park in Rust, Baden-Württemberg. The coaster opened on March 31, 2012.

The Port of Gaza is a small port near the Rimal district of Gaza City. It is the home port of Palestinian fishing-boats and the base of the Palestinian Naval Police, a branch of the Palestinian National Security Forces. Under the Oslo II Accord, the activities of the Palestinian Naval Police are restricted to 6 nautical miles from the coast. Since 2007, the Port of Gaza has been under an Israeli-imposed naval blockade as part of a blockade of the Gaza Strip, and activities at the port have been restricted to small-scale fishing.

Clotilde von Derp (1892–1974) was a German expressionist dancer. She performed from 1910 to 1956, and was known for her striking beauty and crossdressing costumes. With her husband Alexander Sakharoff, she toured internationally and taught dance in Rome.
The Merchant Street Historic District in Honolulu, Hawaii, was the city's earliest commercial center.

Nomads Rugby Football Club is a Hong Kong rugby union club founded in 1989. Originally established to develop local Chinese players, the club now fields three teams in the G4S League and maintains ties with the Flying Kukris RFC for youth development.

A Fazenda 4 was the fourth season of the Brazilian reality television series A Fazenda which premiered Tuesday, July 19, 2011 at 11:15 p.m. on RecordTV.

This season was confirmed on late December 2010, before the finale of the third season. Britto Junior and Chris Couto reprise their hosting stints for the show. Tina Roma makes her debut as the show's new special correspondent.

For the first time ever, four women reached the finals, making this the first reality show in Brazilian television history with public voting to do so. No Limite 1 (2000) and Aprendiz 6 − Universitário (2009) also managed to create all female final fours but there was no public vote involved.

On October 12, 2011, personal trainer Joana Machado won the competition with 48% of the public vote over TV host Monique Evans (44%) and DJ Raquel Pacheco (8%).

The crested servaline genet (Genetta cristata) is a genet species endemic to Nigeria and Cameroon. It is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List because it is threatened by habitat loss. It has khaki colored fur with dark spots, a long tail with black and whitish bands, and a crest on the back of its neck. It inhabits scrub and forests at elevations up to 1,000 m (3,300 ft).

The POW/MIA flag is an American flag designed as a symbol of national concern and commitment to resolving the fates of prisoners of war (POW) and those missing in action (MIA). It was created in 1972 by Newt Heisley for the National League of Families of American Prisoners and Missing in Southeast Asia during the Vietnam War. Federal law requires it to be flown at certain federal properties.

North Hollywood High School (NHHS) is a public high school in the North Hollywood neighborhood of Los Angeles, California, United States. It is located in the San Fernando Valley and enrolls approximately 2,500 students. Several neighborhoods, including most of North Hollywood, Valley Village, Studio City and Sun Valley, send students to this school. NHHS is accredited by the Western Association of Schools and Colleges. The school principal is Ricardo Rosales.

Gemeinhardt Co. is a manufacturer of flutes and piccolos. These musical instruments are developed by this company for all levels of musicians, beginners to professionals. It is owned by its major supplier, Angel Industries Co. Ltd of Taiwan, widely acknowledged as the premier manufacturer of woodwind musical instruments. The Gemeinhardt Company is headquartered in Elkhart, Indiana, where many of its instruments are still made.

*A Fazenda 4* was the fourth season of the Brazilian reality television show *A Fazenda*, which premiered on Rede Record in 2011. Due to the 2011 Pan American Games, the season returned to its original broadcast schedule during the South American winter–spring. The season lasted 87 days and featured 16 contestants, including three former contestants who returned for another chance to win the grand prize of R$2,000,000. The winner of the season was Joana Machado, with Monique Evans as the runner-up. The season was filmed and broadcast in high-definition. One notable event during the season was the ejection of Duda Yankovich due to violent behavior, and she was replaced by Dani Bolina.

*Tales of the Heroes: Twin Brave* is a 2012 action beat 'em up game developed by Alfa System and published by Bandai Namco Games for the PlayStation Portable. It features characters from the *Tales* series and gameplay similar to the *Dynasty Warriors* series. It topped the Japanese charts in its first week.

April Carrión (born April 12, 1989) is a Puerto Rican drag performer, who competed on the sixth season of *RuPaul's Drag Race*. Carrión has also appeared in a Gloria Estefan music video and the documentary *Mala Mala*.

An ultrasonic motor is a type of electric motor powered by the ultrasonic vibration of a component, the stator. Ultrasonic motors differ from conventional electric motors in several aspects.
