For example, the torque is produced via dry friction of components in contact, and the ultrasonic vibration induced in the stator is used both to impart motion to the rotor and to modulate the frictional forces present at the interface.

Two methods are used to control the friction along the stator-rotor contact interface: traveling-wave vibration and standing-wave vibration. The first practical motors used standing-wave vibration, while later designs used traveling-wave vibration to obtain bi-directional motion.

Canon was one of the pioneers of the ultrasonic motor, incorporating it into its autofocus lenses in the late 1980s. The ultrasonic motor is now used in many consumer and office electronics requiring precision rotations over long periods.

Edmonton Public Schools (legally Edmonton School Division) is the largest public school division in Edmonton, the second largest in Alberta, and the sixth largest in Canada. The division offers a variety of alternative and special needs programs, and many are offered in multiple locations to improve accessibility for students. As a public school division, Edmonton Public Schools accepts all students who meet age and residency requirements set out in provincial legislation.
Sangita Patel (born January 2, 1979) is a Canadian television personality. She is a co-host on Entertainment Tonight Canada.

The Reformed Christian Church in Slovakia dates back to the 1520s. It was formed from congregations of the Reformed Church in Hungary. As of 2001, the church had 110,000 members in 204 parishes. It is a member of the World Communion of Reformed Churches.

El Valle de Arroyo Seco is a census-designated place (CDP) in Santa Fe County, New Mexico, United States. It is part of the Santa Fe, New Mexico Metropolitan Statistical Area. The population was 1,440 at the 2010 census.

The Ma Tante River is a tributary of the Franquelin River, flowing in the township of Franquelin, in the municipality of Franquelin, in the Manicouagan Regional County Municipality, in the administrative region of Côte-Nord, in the province of Quebec, in Canada.

Forestry is the main economic activity in this valley; recreational tourism, second.

The surface of this Middle North Shore river is usually frozen from the beginning of November to the end of April, except the rapids; however, safe circulation on the ice is generally from late November to early April.

Gomer Hill is a mountain located west-northwest of Turin in Lewis County, New York. Its summit is 2,106 feet (642 m) in elevation.

In 1940, a 67.5-foot-tall steel fire lookout tower was built on the hill by the Civilian Conservation Corps. The tower was staffed until 1988, after which it was closed and now serves as an antenna support structure. The tower site remains open to the public.

The Homilies of Gregory is a Byzantine illuminated manuscript commissioned by Patriarch Photios I as a gift for Emperor Basil I. Created after Iconoclasm, it celebrates the triumph of Orthodoxy and praises Basil's reign. The manuscript contains homilies by St. Gregory of Nazianzus and elaborate illustrations designed to draw parallels between historical events and the enduring faith of Greek Orthodoxy.

The Federation of State Medical Boards (FSMB) of the United States is a national non-profit organization that represents the 71 state medical and osteopathic boards of the United States and its territories and co-sponsors the United States Medical Licensing Examination. Medical boards license physicians, investigate complaints, discipline those who violate the law, conduct physician evaluations, and facilitate the rehabilitation of physicians where appropriate. The FSMB's mission calls for "continual improvement in the quality, safety and integrity of health care through the development and promotion of high standards for physician licensure and practice."

Based in Euless, Texas and Washington, D.C., the FSMB serves as the national voice for its member boards and is a recognized authority throughout the United States and internationally on issues related to medical licensure and discipline.

The United States Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE) is a three-step (three-part) professional examination for medical licensure that is sponsored by the FSMB and the National Board of Medical Examiners (NBME).

"Me or Sum" is a song by Nardo Wick featuring Lil Baby and Future, released on November 29, 2021, from the album *Who Is Nardo Wick?* The song is about the artists' luxurious lifestyles and experiences. The music video, released in 2022, is set in the Roaring Twenties.

Ma Zhou (601–648), courtesy name Binwang, was a politician during the Tang dynasty. After being discovered by Emperor Taizong for his talent in drafting policy suggestions, Ma served in several important governmental positions. He was known for his insightful advice and was highly regarded by Emperor Taizong, who relied on him for his logical thinking and alertness. Ma suffered from diabetes and died in 648.
Grantham and Stamford is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament by Gareth Davies, a Conservative.

Pottawattamie Park is a town in LaPorte County, Indiana, United States. It was incorporated in 1936 on land once inhabited by the Potawatomi Indians and is completely surrounded by Michigan City. The population was 242 at the 2020 census.
William Tempest (1682–1761) was an English barrister and Fellow of the Royal Society.
John Birkbeck (born in Settle) was a banker, justice of the peace, potholer, and mountaineer.

CCM is a Canadian bicycle manufacturer that was founded in 1899 in Weston, Ontario. It was formed through the amalgamation of four major Canadian bicycle manufacturers, accounting for 85% of the country's cycle production. The company later diversified into motorcar production.

Paul H. Thomson (1916–2008) was an American botanist and horticulturalist known for his work with rare fruits and succulents. He experimented with growing tropical fruits in Southern California and co-founded the California Rare Fruit Growers Association (CRFG) in 1968. Thomson also privately published the *Dudleya and Hassenthaus Handbook* in 1993, a book dedicated to the *Dudleya* genus of succulents native to California and Baja California.

Albert Henri Bourderon (26 November 1858 – 2 April 1930) was a French cooper (barrel maker) and syndicalist who became a leading socialist. During World War I he supported a pacifist position in line with internationalist principles.

Margaret Bernadine Hall (10 March 1863 – 2 January 1910) was an English painter who spent most of her career in Paris. Few of her works have survived, but she is notable for her 1886 painting Fantine, which hangs in the Walker Art Gallery, Liverpool, England. The subject of the painting is Fantine, a character in Victor Hugo's 1862 novel Les Misérables.
Drexel-Alvernon, Arizona, was a former census-designated place (CDP) in Pima County, Arizona, United States. The population was 4,192 at the 2000 census.

I Even Met Happy Gypsies is a 1967 Yugoslav film by Serbian director Aleksandar Petrović. Its original Serbian title is Skupljači perja, which means The Feather Gatherers. The film is centered on Romani people's life in a village in northern Vojvodina, but it also deals with subtler themes such as love, ethnic and social relationships. Beside Bekim Fehmiu, Olivera Vučo, Bata Živojinović and Mija Aleksić, film features a cast of Romani actors speaking the Romani language. I Even Met Happy Gypsies is considered one of the best films of the Black Wave in Yugoslav cinema.

Piero di Cosimo (2 January 1462 – 12 April 1522), also known as Piero di Lorenzo, was an Italian painter of the Renaissance.

He is most famous for the mythological and allegorical subjects he painted in the late Quattrocento; he is said to have abandoned these to return to religious subjects under the influence of Savonarola, the preacher who exercised a huge sway in Florence in the 1490s, and had a similar effect on Botticelli. The High Renaissance style of the new century had little influence on him, and he retained the straightforward realism of his figures, which combines with an often whimsical treatment of his subjects to create the distinctive mood of his works.

Vasari has many stories of his eccentricity, and the mythological subjects have an individual and quirky fascination. He trained under Cosimo Rosselli, whose daughter he married, and assisted him in his Sistine Chapel frescos.

He was also influenced by Early Netherlandish painting, and busy landscapes feature in many works, often forests seen close at hand. Several of his most striking secular works are in the long "landscape" format used for paintings inset into cassone wedding chests or spalliera headboards or panelling. He was apparently famous for designing the temporary decorations for Carnival and other festivities.

Aussie Gold Hunters is an Australian factual television show which follows crews of gold prospectors in Australia. The series is produced by Electric Pictures and began on the Discovery Channel on 15 September 2016. The series has been a ratings success and gone on to air in 125 countries including the United Kingdom.

In August 2022 the series was renewed for a eighth and ninth season, with season eight to premiere in 2023. Season seven concluded on 13 October 2022.

The karbovanets was a currency of Ukraine in three distinct periods. The first karbovanets was introduced by the Ukrainian People's Republic in 1917. A second karbovanets was issued by the German occupying government during World War II (1942–1945). The third karbovanets (ISO 4217 code: UAK) replaced the Soviet ruble in 1992 and was itself replaced by the hryvnia in 1996 due to hyperinflation, at a rate of 100,000 karbovantsiv to 1 hryvnia.

The 9th Marine Regiment is an infantry regiment of the United States Marine Corps, nicknamed the "Striking Ninth." Active from 1917 to 2015 in several periods, it was part of the 3rd Marine Division and III Marine Expeditionary Force. The regiment fought in World War II battles such as Bougainville, Guam, and Iwo Jima, as well as in the Vietnam War, including Operation Dewey Canyon.

Uckfield Town Council is the local authority for the town of Uckfield in East Sussex, England. It is composed of fifteen councillors elected from five wards. As of 2023, the council is led by Mayor Jackie Love and Deputy Mayor Donna French. The council's political composition includes TRUST Independent, Labour, Conservative, and Liberal Democrat councillors. Elections are held every four years, with the most recent election in 2019. The council meets at the Civic Centre.
Lars Eric Mattsson is a Finnish musician, composer, and record producer.

The *Murakumo*-class destroyers were a class of six destroyers of the Imperial Japanese Navy. Ordered from John I. Thornycroft & Company of England, they were the first destroyers procured by Japan. All six ships saw action in the Russo-Japanese War and World War I.

Georgy Vasilyevich Pigulevsky (Lang-ru; 10 December [O.S. 28 November] 1888 – 19 September 1964) was a soviet organic chemist, specializing in natural product chemistry. He studied essential oils and resins of plants, as well as terpene compounds extracted from them.

*Wild Jack* is a 1974 science fiction novel by John Christopher. Set in a post-apocalyptic twenty-third century England, the story follows Clive Anderson, a privileged youth from the city-state of London. After being imprisoned on a reform island, Clive escapes and encounters Wild Jack, an outlaw leader in the wilderness known as the Outlands. Through his experiences, Clive questions the social structures of his world and learns the meaning of freedom. The novel explores themes of freedom, societal collapse, and the conflict between civilization and wilderness.

Argentina was one of 23 nations to compete at the 1960 Summer Paralympics in Rome, Italy. The Argentinian delegation consisted of five athletes, all of whom competed in swimming. This was the first Summer Paralympics, and Argentina has participated in every edition since. Argentinian athletes won a total of six medals, two gold, three silver, and one bronze, placing them tenth in the medal table. All five Argentinian athletes won at least one medal. The most successful was Sznitowski, who won a gold and a silver medal.

Shikantaza (只管打坐) is Dogen's Japanese translation of the Chinese phrase zhǐguǎn dǎzuò (只管打坐 / 祇管 打坐), "just sitting." The phrase was used by his teacher Rujing, a monk of the Caodong school of Zen Buddhism, to refer to the meditation-practice called "Silent Illumination" (Chinese: 默照禅), or "Serene Reflection," taught by the Caodong master Hongzhi Zhengjue (1091-1157). In Japan, it is associated with the Soto school. In shikantaza one does not focus attention on a specific object (such as the breath); instead, practitioners "just sit" in a state of conscious awareness.
Geometrics are an English five piece indie rock band from Hertfordshire, England.

"Take Me for a Ride" is a song recorded by British group Steps, released in July 2021 as the second single from their seventh studio album, What the Future Holds Pt. 2 (2021). The single peaked at number 27 on UK Download chart.

The Future Strategic Tanker Aircraft (FSTA) project was a program to replace the Royal Air Force's (RAF) aging fleet of Vickers VC10 and Lockheed TriStar air-to-air refueling aircraft. AirTanker was selected to provide 14 converted Airbus A330 aircraft, known as Voyager in RAF service, under a Private Finance Initiative. The first Voyager arrived in 2011 and achieved operational certification in 2013.

Phoebe Forrester was a fictional character on the CBS soap opera *The Bold and the Beautiful*. She was portrayed by child actors before Brynne McNabb (2002-2003), Chandler Mella (2004), Keaton Tyndall (2004), Addison Hoover (2005), and MacKenzie Mauzy (2006-2008).

A central storyline involved Phoebe's romance with Rick Forrester, her legal uncle. Mauzy and co-star Kyle Lowder, both singers, performed on the show, and Phoebe later worked with record producer Constantine Parros. Phoebe died in a car crash on December 9, 2008, following an argument with Rick. In 2019, her twin sister Steffy named her adoptive daughter after Phoebe.

Roll for the Galaxy is a dice game for 2 to 5 players where each player develops a galactic civilization by recruiting workers to settle worlds and build developments. Each round, players secretly roll dice to determine what actions their workers will take. The player who best manages their workers to create the greatest space empire wins by accumulating victory points. The game ends when a player has 12 or more tile squares or all victory point chips have been earned.

Messier 83 or M83, also known as the Southern Pinwheel Galaxy and NGC 5236, is a barred spiral galaxy approximately 15 million light-years away in the constellation borders of Hydra and Centaurus. Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille discovered M83 on 23 February 1752 at the Cape of Good Hope. Charles Messier added it to his catalogue of nebulous objects (now known as the Messier Catalogue) in March 1781. It is one of the closest and brightest barred spiral galaxies in the sky, and is visible with binoculars. Its nickname of the Southern Pinwheel derives from its resemblance to the Pinwheel Galaxy (M101).

Scott L. Thoele is an officer in the United States Army, commissioned in 1980. He served in the Gulf War, the Iraq War, and the War in Afghanistan. His awards include the Legion of Merit and the Bronze Star Medal with oak leaf cluster.

As a civilian, Thoele is vice president and credit analyst of First Bankers Trust Company in Quincy, Illinois. He received his education at Quincy University and the University of Wisconsin-Madison.

Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani (1922 – November 25, 1977) was the Emir of Qatar from 1960 to 1972. He acceded to the throne on October 24, 1960, after his father's abdication, and oversaw Qatar's independence from Great Britain in 1971. On February 22, 1972, he was deposed in a bloodless coup by his cousin Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani while on a hunting trip in Iran. Following his deposition, he lived in exile in Dubai until his death.

Barley yellow mosaic virus (BaYMV) is a plant virus of the genus *Bymovirus* that affects barley (*Hordeum vulgare*). The virus is transmitted by the soil-borne protist *Polymyxa graminis*, and is found primarily in Eastern Asia and Western Europe.

Infected plants exhibit yellowing and mosaic-like flecking of the leaves, along with leaf curl, stunting, and necrosis. BaYMV can cause significant yield losses, reaching up to 50% in the United Kingdom and 80% in Japan. There is no known cure, so control relies on preventative measures, primarily the use of resistant cultivars, though new strains of the virus are constantly emerging.
Frederick Eugene Crawford (July 27, 1910 – March 5, 1974) was an American football player.

Jhajhar is a village, situated in the erstwhile province of Shekhawati of Rajasthan, India. It is located in the district of Jhunjhunu, approximately 7 km from Nawalgarh. Formally it was the part of Pentalisa of Bhojyana.

Chenani is a town in the Udhampur district of Jammu and Kashmir, India. It is located 90 km from Jammu. The town had a population of 2,620 in 2011 and a literacy rate of 88.91%. Until 1947, Chenani was ruled by Chandel Rajput clan.

Nancy Duff Campbell is an American attorney and law professor. She graduated from Barnard College in 1965 and New York University School of Law in 1968. Campbell co-founded the National Women's Law Center in 1981. She has successfully litigated cases before the Supreme Court and has been involved in tax reform advocacy.

Ian Simmonds (born 1966) is a Welsh disc jockey, music producer, and singer. Active since 1987, he also records under the name Juryman. In the late 1980s, Simmonds formed the London-based collective Sandals, which organized club nights. He began releasing his own music in 1995, and his debut album, *Last States of Nature*, was released in 1999. Since the mid-1990s, Simmonds has collaborated with artists such as Leftfield and Goldfrapp.
Hanna Yussuf Atik (born 7 February 1959) is a Lebanese politician, and veteran of the Lebanese Civil War.

Daniel "Dan" Earling Erickson (born January 26, 1984) is an American television screenwriter, producer, and showrunner. He is best known as the creator and writer of the Apple TV+ series *Severance* (2022–present), for which he was named to the TIME100 Next list.

*The Love of Hypnosis* (also known as *Records of the Southern Mist House*) is a 2018 Chinese fantasy period drama series starring Liu Yifei and Jing Boran. Based on a novel, it tells the story of a woman with unique abilities during the late Qing Dynasty.
Thierry Escaich (born 1965) is a French composer and organist, teaching at the Conservatoire de Paris.

Arenys de Mar is a coastal town in Catalonia, Spain, located in the province of Barcelona and the comarca of Maresme. As of 2018, the municipality had a population of 15,533. The town's economy is largely based on fishing and related industries, centered around its harbor, the most important in the Maresme.

Main attractions include the parish church of Santa Maria, the Marés Museum of Lace-making, the Mollfulleda Museum of Mineralogy, and the Cementiri de Sinera, a marine cemetery. Arenys de Mar is well-connected to Barcelona and other nearby cities by road and rail.
Howard M. Radzely (born 1970) is an American lawyer and government official. He served as acting United States Secretary of Labor in 2009.

Tech4 Carmen Contreras-Bozak, (December 31, 1919 – January 30, 2017) was the first Puerto Rican woman to serve in the U.S. Women's Army Corps (WAC) where she served as an interpreter and in numerous administrative positions.
Bethuel Muzeu (born 2 February 2000), also known as Muzeu Muzeu, is a Namibian footballer who plays for National First Division club Black Leopards and the Namibia national team.
Mario Adrián Vázquez (born June 15, 1977) is an American singer from The Bronx, New York City. He competed on American Idol's fourth season, which aired on the FOX network in early 2005.

Democratic centralism is a practice in which political decisions reached by voting processes are binding upon all members of the political party. It is mainly associated with Leninism, wherein the party's political vanguard of professional revolutionaries practised democratic centralism to elect leaders and officers, determine policy through free discussion, and decisively realise it through united action. Democratic centralism has also been practised by social democratic and democratic socialist parties as well. Scholars have disputed whether democratic centralism was implemented in practice in the Soviet Union and China, pointing to violent power struggles, backhanded political maneuvering, historical antagonisms and the politics of personal prestige in those regimes.

Lilian Mary Elizabeth Wyles (1885–1975) was one of the first women to become a police officer in London. She joined the Metropolitan Women Police Patrols in 1919 and was instrumental in gaining them attested status in 1923.

Alan Bristow OBE (1923–2009) was a British helicopter pilot and entrepreneur. He served in World War II and later founded Bristow Helicopters, which expanded globally. Bristow was involved in the Westland Affair and had other interests, including equestrianism and inventing.

The Cello Concerto is a composition for cello and orchestra by the Finnish composer Esa-Pekka Salonen. The work was co-commissioned by the Chicago Symphony Orchestra, the New York Philharmonic, the Barbican Centre, and the Elbphilharmonie. It was completed in 2017 and was first performed by the cellist Yo-Yo Ma and the Chicago Symphony Orchestra under the direction of Salonen on March 9, 2017. The piece is dedicated to Yo-Yo Ma.

Declo is a city in Cassia County, Idaho, United States. The population was estimated at 369 in 2019. It was founded as Marshfield, but renamed Declo in 1916. The J.R. Simplot company was founded near Declo in 1923.
Bharatpur is an assembly constituency in Murshidabad district in the Indian state of West Bengal.

Svalikhita-jivani is an autobiography written in 1896 by Kedarnatha Datta (also known as Bhaktivinoda Thakur), a prominent thinker of Bengali Renaissance and a leading philosopher, savant and spiritual reformer of Gaudiya Vaishnavism who effected its resurgence in India in late 19th and early 20th century and was hailed by contemporary scholars as the most influential Gaudiya Vaisnava leader of his time.

Written in Bengali on the request of Bhaktivinoda's son Lalita Prasad, Svalikhita-jivani gives a detailed autobiographical account that spanned most of his life from his birth in 1838 until retirement in 1894. The book was published by Lalita Prasad in 1916, after Bhaktivinoda's death.
Bent Sæther (born February 18, 1969) is a Norwegian musician, composer, and producer. He is a multi-instrumentalist and vocalist, best known as a founding member of the rock band Motorpsycho.
Marvel Super Heroes: The Heroic Role-Playing Game is a role-playing game boxed set published by TSR in 1984 for the Marvel Super Heroes role-playing game, and was the debut product for the game.

In computational geometry, an optimal bitonic tour is a bitonic tour of minimum total length. Dynamic programming can construct the optimal bitonic tour in polynomial time. The optimal bitonic tour has no self-crossings and minimizes horizontal motion.

Joanna Mytkowska (born 1970) is a Polish curator, art critic, and museum director. She curated the Artur Żmijewski exhibition at the 51st Venice Biennale in 2005 and organized exhibits for the Centre Georges Pompidou. In 2018, she received the Igor Zabel Award.
Software was a German electronic duo active from 1984 to 1999, originally composed of Peter Mergener [de] and Michael Weisser [de]. The duo formerly used the name Mergener & Weisser.

The International Pool Tour (IPT) was a professional pool tour founded by Kevin Trudeau. In an effort to improve the image of pool, the IPT re-established a dress code requiring players to wear suits, dress shirts, and leather shoes.

The IPT refused to follow some of the rules normally required by pool's governing bodies, in particular the policy that all prize money be held in escrow. Despite this, both the Women's Professional Billiards Association and the U.S. Professional Pool Players Association allowed their players to join the IPT.

In September 2006, Trudeau announced that the IPT was being sold due to lack of funding after a deal with Stanley Ho fell through. Afterward, the IPT shifted its strategy to live streaming matches.

Anna Maria Rückerschöld (1725–1805) was a Swedish writer known for her books on cooking and household management. She advocated for household education for women, arguing for its priority over social skills. Her publications include *En Liten Hushålls-Bok* ("A Small Household Book") and *Den Nya och Fullständiga Kok-Boken* ("The New and Complete Cookbook"). *Fattig Mans Wisthus och Kök* ("Poor Man's Larder and Kitchen") earned her a silver medal from Patriotiska Sällskapet for being the first cookbook written for poorer households.

Supersingular isogeny Diffie–Hellman key exchange (SIDH or SIKE) is an insecure proposal for a post-quantum cryptographic algorithm to establish a secret key between two parties over an untrusted communications channel. It is analogous to the Diffie–Hellman key exchange, but is based on walks in a supersingular isogeny graph and was designed to resist cryptanalytic attack by an adversary in possession of a quantum computer. Before it was broken, SIDH boasted one of the smallest key sizes of all post-quantum key exchanges; with compression, SIDH used 2688-bit public keys at a 128-bit quantum security level. SIDH also distinguishes itself from similar systems such as NTRU and Ring-LWE by supporting perfect forward secrecy, a property that prevents compromised long-term keys from compromising the confidentiality of old communication sessions. These properties seemed to make SIDH a natural candidate to replace Diffie–Hellman (DHE) and elliptic curve Diffie–Hellman (ECDHE), which are widely used in Internet communication. However, SIDH is vulnerable to a devastating key-recovery attack published in July 2022 and is therefore insecure.

Allison Jones (born 1955) is an American casting director who is credited for helping bring together realistic ensemble casts for such popular television shows as The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air (1990–1996), Freaks and Geeks (1999–2000), Curb Your Enthusiasm (2000–), Undeclared (2001–2002), Arrested Development (2003–2006), the American version of The Office (2005–2013), United States of Tara (2009–2011), Parks and Recreation (2009–2015), Brooklyn Nine-Nine (2013–2021), The Good Place (2016–2020), and What We Do in the Shadows (2019–).

Sam Walker (born 16 June 2002) is a professional rugby league footballer who plays as a halfback & five-eighth for the Sydney Roosters in the NRL.

He has played for the Prime Minister's XIII at representative level.

Conrad Richardt Strauss (born 29 January 1986) is a South African-born former rugby union player who played as a hooker. He won the Under 19 Rugby World Championship in 2005 with South Africa. From 2009 he played for Leinster Rugby, winning the Heineken Cup in 2011.

Strauss was diagnosed with a heart condition in 2013 but made an early return to the sport. He retired from professional rugby at the end of the 2017/18 season. Strauss became eligible to play for Ireland in 2012 and was named in their squad for the 2015 Rugby World Cup. His brother Andries has played for the Springboks.

Woodall services is a motorway service station on the M1 motorway in Rotherham close to Sheffield in England. It lies between junctions 30 and 31. It was opened in 1968 by Trust House Forte but was renamed Welcome Break after the takeover of the company. It takes its name from the nearby village of Woodall.

Barasat Junction railway station, located in Barasat, North 24 Parganas, West Bengal, is a Kolkata Suburban Railway junction station operated by Eastern Railway. Opened in 1906 and electrified in 1972, it features 5 platforms and 6 tracks. The station provides a reservations counter and smart card booths for ticketing.

Historically, it was part of the Barasat Basirhat Railway (BBR), a narrow-gauge line that operated from 1914 to 1955. The route was later converted to broad gauge by Indian Railways.

A planned extension of the Kolkata Metro from Noapara to Barasat aims to reduce pressure on the Sealdah–Barasat section. However, the project has faced setbacks due to encroachment issues, leading to construction delays.
Hudson Township is a civil township within Charlevoix County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 691 at the 2010 census.
Samuel Lord (born in Saddleworth, Yorkshire) was the co-founder of the department store Lord & Taylor. He retired in 1862 and died in England in 1889.

Four Freedoms Plaza is a fictional structure appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics. It is depicted as being located in the Manhattan of the Marvel Universe; it served as the replacement headquarters for the Fantastic Four when their original dwelling, the Baxter Building, was destroyed by Kristoff Vernard, the adoptive son of Doctor Doom. It is located at 42nd Street and Madison Avenue in New York City. The title of the building comes from a Franklin D. Roosevelt speech urging the Congress of the United States to enter World War II. In it Roosevelt outlined the four freedoms the world would enjoy if it united together to defeat the Axis Power.

GLtron is an open-source snake game, initially a private project by Andreas Umbach in 1998. Released in 1999 under the GPL-2.0-or-later license, it features gameplay where players aim to be the last one riding light cycles. The game has been ported to multiple platforms.

Yemodish Bekele (born 1960) is an Ethiopian author and crime journalist. Born in Addis Ababa, she grew up reading police publications due to her father's work as a police officer.

In 1979, she won a competition that led to the publication of twelve of her poems in "Abyotawi Gitmoch". In 1990, she published her first book of short stories, "ye bakene gize". Yemodish worked for 26 years at Polis Ena Ermijaw, the newspaper of the Federal Police in Ethiopia, eventually becoming the first female head editor. She is also a supporter of Ethiopian poets and is involved in women's rights organizations, currently serving as President of the Ethiopian Women Writers’ Association and chair of the Network for Ethiopian Women's Associations.

Ultimate Canada is a not-for-profit organization that serves as the governing body of the sport of Ultimate (also known as "Ultimate Frisbee") in Canada. It runs the Canadian Ultimate Championships (CUC) and Canadian University Ultimate Championship (CUUC) series.

Canadian Ultimate Championships (CUC)

Each August, teams from across the country travel to the Canadian Ultimate Championships (CUC) to compete for the national title in 7 different divisions: mixed, open, women's, junior open, junior women, masters open and masters women. Teams compete at this seven-day tournament not only to determine the national champion, but also to determine who will represent Canada at the next world championships.

From 2016 to 2019, the mixed divisions were held as a separate event from the remaining divisions.

In 2022, the format was changed again with the three Masters divisions at their own event, the three senior divisions at their own event, and the two Junior divisions at their own event.

Canadian University Ultimate Championships (CUUC)

The CUUC started in 1995 and brings university teams from across the country to compete in the open & the women's division. Each fall Ultimate Canada operates two competitions for university Ultimate teams in Canada: the Canadian University Ultimate Championships (CUUC) and the Canadian Eastern University Ultimate Championships (CEUUC). The CEUUC began in 1998 and brings university teams primarily from Ontario and Quebec together to compete in the open & the women's division.

Odd Reidar Humlegård (born 9 May 1961, in Porsgrunn) is a Norwegian lawyer, civil servant and the current National Police Commissioner. Humlegård previously served as head of the National Criminal Investigation Service, and was appointed as director to the National Police Directorate in August 2012 after the controversial resignation of Øystein Mæland.
Nashipur is a village in the Murshidabad-Jiaganj CD block in the Lalbag subdivision of Murshidabad district in West Bengal, India.

Hans Klumbach (26 April 1904 – 14 December 1992) was a German archaeologist and scholar of classical and provincial Roman studies. He served as a curator at, and later the director of, the Romano-Germanic Central Museum. His 1973 work Spätrömische Gardehelme laid bare the relationship between the late Roman ridge helmets and the later Scandinavian and Anglo-Saxon crested helmets.

Titus Haterius Nepos was a Roman equestrian who served as prefect of Egypt from 120 to an unknown date. His career is primarily known from an inscription found in Fulginiae. Prior to governing Egypt, Nepos held a series of military and administrative posts, including a posting in Roman Britain and procurator of Roman Armenia. While governor of Egypt, he visited the Colossi of Memnon and heard the statue sing.

Jagadishpur is a census town in Bally Jagachha CD Block of Howrah Sadar subdivision in Howrah district in the Indian state of West Bengal. It is a part of Kolkata Urban Agglomeration.

Jagadishpur is under the jurisdiction of Liluah Police Station of Howrah City Police.

Connah's Quay Power Station is a 1,420 MW gas-fired power station to the west of Connah's Quay in Flintshire in North Wales. It is next to the A548, being tightly situated between the road and the south bank of the River Dee.

King Mongkut's Institute of Technology Ladkrabang (KMITL) is a public research university in Bangkok, Thailand, established in 1960. It began as a telecommunications training center with Japanese government support. KMITL offers programs in engineering, architecture, science, and technology. Carnegie Mellon University established a joint institute with KMITL in 2017 called CMKL University.

Melanie Susan Oxley (born in Kingscliff, New South Wales) is an Australian musician, singer-songwriter, and primary school teacher. She is the sister of Peter and Jeremy Oxley, founders of the band The Sunnyboys. Oxley performed with Paris Green and The Sparklers before forming a duo with Chris Abrahams in 1989. Melanie Oxley & Chris Abrahams have released four studio albums, including *Welcome to Violet*, which was nominated for Best Independent Release at the ARIA Music Awards of 1993.

Exabyte Corporation was a public company that developed magnetic tape data storage products, including automation solutions and VXA. It was founded in 1985 by Juan Rodriguez, Harry Hinz, and Kelly Beavers, along with a group of former StorageTek engineers. Exabyte introduced the Data8 format in 1987. The company went public on the NASDAQ in 1989 under the symbol EXBT. In 2001, Exabyte merged with Ecrix, a company founded by two of Exabyte's founders, giving Exabyte access to Ecrix's VXA Packet Technology tape drive format. Exabyte was acquired by Tandberg Data in November 2006.
The North Bengal State Library in Cooch Behar, India, was established in 1870. It holds over 75,000 books and many ancient manuscripts.

The Anthony Massad Three-Decker is a historic triple-decker house in Worcester, Massachusetts. Built around 1912 in the Colonial Revival style, it is located in the Brittan Square area. The house features an asymmetrical facade with a stack of porches on one side and a polygonal window bay on the other. Anthony Massad, the first owner, was the proprietor of the Exchange Hotel. The building was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1990.

Nikaho is a city located in the southwest corner of Akita Prefecture, Japan, bordered by the Sea of Japan and Yamagata Prefecture. As of December 2019, the city had an estimated population of 24,291. Part of Nikaho is within the Chōkai Quasi-National Park, and Mount Chōkai is a popular destination for recreation.
Hudson Township is a civil township of Charlevoix County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 691 at the 2010 census.

_Lord Forbes_ was launched in 1803. Initially armed with 20 guns, she served as an armed defense ship protecting Britain's coasts. She rescued the crew of HMS _Imogen_ in 1805. Between 1811 and 1814 she made two voyages to India for the British East India Company (EIC). She was condemned and sold for breaking up in Bengal in 1817.

The qualification for the 2023 BAL season, also known as Road to BAL 2023, is the 3rd edition of the qualifying tournaments for the Basketball Africa League. The tournaments are organised by FIBA Africa and are held from 10 October to 27 November 2022.

Code R is an American action-adventure television series that aired on CBS from January 21 to June 10, 1977. Code R focuses on the emergency services (police, fire, and ocean rescue) of the California Channel Islands. The series starred James Houghton, Martin Kove and Tom Simcox and ran for a single season of thirteen episodes.

Spichern was a motor tanker that was built in Denmark for Norwegian owners and launched in 1935 as Krossfonn. In 1940 the German navy captured her, renamed her Spichern and converted her into an oiler. In 1944 she was damaged in an air raid and then scuttled.

Spichern was raised in 1947, rebuilt, renamed Ringfjell and returned to Norwegian merchant service in 1949. She was converted into a bulk carrier in 1955, renamed Ringsaker in 1961 and scrapped in 1964.

Springfield Central railway station is an elevated station on the Springfield line in Queensland, Australia, serving the suburb of Springfield Central. Opened on December 2, 2013, it was part of the Darra to Springfield Transport Corridor project. The station has an island platform, bus bays, and parking facilities.

The former Reading Municipal Building is a historic building at 49 Pleasant Street in Reading, Massachusetts. Built in 1885, this two-story brick building was the town's first municipal structure, housing the town offices, jail, and fire station. In 1918 all functions except fire services moved out of the building. It now serves as Reading's Pleasant Street Senior Center. The building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1984.

Maitreya-nātha is the name used to refer to the figure to whom the Five Dharmas of Maitreya were revealed. Buddhist tradition holds that Asaṅga received these texts directly from the Bodhisattva Maitreya in the Tuṣita Heaven. However, some scholars believe Maitreya-nātha may have been a historical human teacher of Asaṅga.

Alison Mary Fiske (2 August 1943 – 26 July 2020) was an English actress, who won Actress of the Year in a New Play at the 1977 Laurence Olivier Awards for playing Fish in Dusa, Fish, Stats and Vi. She was also nominated in the 1979 Laurence Olivier Awards for Best Actress in a Supporting Role for playing Evie in For Services Rendered, and she won awards for her television performance in Helen: A Woman of Today.

Rafael "Ralfi" Pagán (September 11, 1946 – November 1978) was an American singer of Latin soul, boogaloo, salsa, disco, and Latin pop. Beginning his career in 1966, Pagán initially recorded under the name Ray Paige. He gained prominence in 1969 with his self-titled album on Fania Records, becoming popular among young Hispanic Americans, particularly in urban centers like Los Angeles and New York.

In 1971, Pagán achieved national recognition with his Latin cover of "Make It With You," which charted on Billboard's R&B chart and led to an appearance on Soul Train. He continued to release albums, including "With Love" and "I Can See," solidifying his status within the Chicano community. By 1976, Pagán was based in Los Angeles, performing regularly on the local club circuit. He maintained a dual career as a salsa and Latin disco singer, releasing albums like "El Flaco De Oro" and "Girl From The Mountain." While touring in Colombia in 1978, Pagán was murdered. His music has experienced a resurgence in popularity since the mid-1990s.

The Great Lakes Depression (Lang-mn, Ikh Nuuruudyn Khotgor), also called the Great Lakes' Hollow, is a large semi-arid depression in Mongolia that covers parts of the Uvs, Khovd, Bayan-Ölgii, Zavkhan and Govi-Altai aimags. Bounded by the Altai in the West, Khangai in the East and Tannu-Ola Mountains in the North, it covers the area of over 100,000 km2 (39,000 sq mi) with elevations from 750 to 2,000 m (2,460–6,560 ft).

Small northern parts of the depression are part of Russia.

The depression is named so because it contains six major Mongolian lakes: saline Uvs Nuur, Khyargas Nuur and Dörgön Nuur; and freshwater Khar-Us Nuur, Khar Nuur and Airag Nuur, as well as a number of smaller ones. In addition, it includes 14,000 km2 (5,400 sq mi) of solonchaks and large sandy areas. Northern parts are dominated by arid steppes, and southern by semideserts or deserts. The major rivers are Khovd River, Zavkhan Gol, and Tesiin Gol.

The Additional Civil Lord of the Admiralty was a civilian member of the British Board of Admiralty. The position was created in 1882 for George Wightwick Rendel, a civil engineer. The post was abolished in 1885, then re-established from 1912 to 1919. Responsibilities included dockyard organization, ship construction and repair, and tenders for ship hulls, machinery, and armaments. From 1917, additional civil lords were appointed with different titles until the office was again abolished in 1919.

Trichomonas gallinae is a cosmopolitan parasite of birds including finches, pigeons, doves, turkeys, chickens, parrots, raptors (hawks, golden eagle, etc.). The condition in birds of prey is called frounce. It is believed to be an ancient pathogen causing frounce-like symptoms in theropod dinosaurs. The same condition in pigeons is commonly called canker.

The protozoa are small (5 to 20 μm size) oval or spherical flagellates with four free flagella found on the anterior aspect of the parasite. They also have an axostyle found on the posterior end as well as an undulating membrane on one side. An important diagnostic feature is the lack of a free posterior flagellum.
George Hall (1795–1868) was the first and 10th Mayor of Brooklyn, New York, serving 1834–1835 and 1855–1856.

Ä is a letter in several alphabets, including Finnish, Swedish, German, and Estonian. It typically represents a front vowel sound, such as /æ/ or /ɛ/. It originated from A with a small e written above.

Oak is a discontinued programming language created by James Gosling in 1989, initially for Sun Microsystems' set-top box project. The language later evolved to become Java.

The name Oak was used by Gosling after an oak tree that stood outside his office.
*CBS Playhouse* was an anthology drama television series that aired on CBS from 1967 to 1970. The show broadcast twelve teleplays over three seasons and won a Peabody Award in 1967.

The New York Regents Examinations are statewide standardized exams in specific subject areas, typically taken by high school students in New York State. First administered in 1866, they were expanded in 1878 to assess secondary school curricula and serve as end-of-course high school exams. Over time, the number and types of Regents Exams have evolved to reflect changes in high school curriculum, with vocational exams discontinued and a shift towards comprehensive examinations.

Passing scores on Regents Exams are a requirement for earning a New York State Regents Diploma, with additional requirements for Advanced Designation. The exams are typically three hours long, with the exception of Earth Science, which includes a laboratory component. Most exams are administered in January, June, and August, though some are only offered in June.

The Board of Regents has modified the curriculum and exams over the years, including changes to the mathematics curriculum in the 2000s and the incorporation of Common Core Standards in the 2010s. Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, several exam administrations were canceled in 2020 and 2021, and some requirements were temporarily waived. Local Diplomas are offered by New York State school districts for students who did not or cannot pass the Regents Diploma requirements.

Sander Kaasjager (born June 21, 1985), known as Vo0, is a Dutch professional gamer. He gained popularity with *Challenge ProMode Arena* and won two world championships in *Painkiller* in 2004. In 2017, he returned from retirement to finish second at the Quake World Championship.
Alfred Edward Bailey (14 March 1871 – 1 August 1950) was a professional cricketer who played first-class cricket for Somerset County Cricket Club from 1900 to 1911 as slow left-arm spin bowler.
Gatillazo is a Spanish punk band formed in the Basque Country in 2005 by the former La Polla Records singer Evaristo Páramos.

Qualified Teacher Status (QTS) is a qualification in England and Wales required to teach in state-maintained and special schools. It is typically obtained through a degree and a teacher training program like a Postgraduate Certificate in Education (PGCE). Teachers must also pass an induction period to be fully qualified.

"Tsunagu" is a single by the Japanese boy band Arashi, released on June 28, 2017, through J Storm. The song was used as the theme song for the film *Shinobi no Kuni* starring Arashi member Satoshi Ohno. The single topped both the Oricon and Billboard Japan charts and was certified platinum by RIAJ.

Red God (1954–1979) was a Thoroughbred race horse foaled in Kentucky who competed in England and the United States but who is best known as the sire of Blushing Groom who prominent turfman Edward L. Bowen calls one of the great international sires of the 20th century.
The Gibson Dove is a flattop steel-string acoustic guitar made by the Gibson Guitar Corporation since 1962.
Boussens is a municipality in the district of Gros-de-Vaud in the canton of Vaud in Switzerland.

Kamaha Castle (鎌刃城, Kamaha jō) was a Sengoku period yamashiro-style Japanese castle located in the Banba neighborhood of the city of Maibara, Shiga Prefecture, in the Kansai region of Japan. Its ruins have been protected as a National Historic Site since 2005.

"Charles" is a short story by Shirley Jackson, first published in Mademoiselle in July 1948. It was later included in her 1949 collection, The Lottery and Other Stories, and her 1953 novel, Life Among the Savages.

East Rennell is the southern portion of Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands which is designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Rennell is the largest raised coral atoll in the world and the area in East Rennell surrounding Lake Tegano contains many endemic species.

In 2013, the World Heritage Committee added East Rennell to the List of World Heritage in Danger because of the threat of logging activities to the site's outstanding universal value.

In Hindustani music (North Indian classical music), a gharānā is a system of social organisation in the Indian subcontinent, linking musicians or dancers by lineage or apprenticeship, and more importantly by adherence to a particular musical style.

The word gharana comes from the Hindi word 'ghar' which is derived from the Sanskrit word Griha, which means 'house'. It typically refers to the place where the musical ideology originated; for example, some of the gharanas well known for singing khyals are: Dilli(Delhi), Agra, Gwalior, Indore, Atrauli-Jaipur, Kirana and Patiala. Four famous kathak gharanas are: Lucknow, Atrauli-Jaipur, Benares and Raigarh.
William Tempest (1682–1761) was an English barrister, Prothonotary, and Fellow of the Royal Society.

Hambleden Lock is a lock with a long weir situated on the River Thames in England, about 2 miles downstream of Henley Bridge. The lock is on the Berkshire bank between Aston and Remenham. Built by the Thames Navigation Commission in 1773, the lock is named after the village of Hambleden, a mile (1.5 km) to the north.

The great weir is impressive and there are walkways over it from the lock to the small village of Mill End on the Buckinghamshire bank. Here is situated the picturesque Hambleden Mill, and the site of a Roman villa is nearby.

The mandolin is a modern member of the lute family, dating back to Italy in the 18th century. The instrument was played across Europe but then disappeared after the Napoleonic Wars. Credit for creating the modern bowlback version of the instrument goes to the Vinaccia family of Naples. The deep bowled mandolin, especially the Neapolitan form, became common in the 19th century, following the appearance of an international hit, the Spanish Students. They toured Europe and America, and their performances created a stir that helped the mandolin to become widely popular.

Although the modern instruments date to the 18th century, ancestral instruments of similar construction and range, the mandore and gittern, were used across Europe (including Spain, Italy, England, France, Germany and Poland) centuries earlier. These instruments developed from short-handled lutes that entered Christian Europe from Muslim Sicily and Spain. Muslims picked these instruments in Central Asia, calling them barbat and oud. Residents of Asia were playing them as far back as the 2nd century A.D.

An amnesty bin is a container for the disposal of items without risk of penalty. They are used at airports to prevent the spread of invasive species or to allow travelers to discard cannabis. They are also used at music venues for illegal drugs and in Amazon fulfillment centers for damaged or unscannable items.
Keep Britain Tidy is a charitable organisation founded in 1955. It runs programmes such as Eco-Schools, the Green Flag Award, and the Blue Flag Award.

King Arthur's Disasters is a British animated series which first aired on CITV. Co-created by Paul Parkes and Will Ashurst, the series follows and depicts attempts by King Arthur, assisted by the wizard Merlin, to woo the beautiful self-obsessed Princess Guinevere. Due to the popularity of the show, it was picked up for a second series which began transmission on CITV from 6 November 2005. Both were executive-produced by Genevieve Dexter at cake entertainment

King Arthur's Disasters was the highest-rated new CITV show during spring 2005. It regularly achieved an audience share of over 20% of children and it regularly won its time slot against CBBC. In 2006 the show was nominated for a children's BAFTA for Best Animation; however, it lost to The Amazing Adrenalini Brothers. It airs on Pop in the UK.
Air Vice-Marshal Paul Douglas Luker, CB, OBE, AFC, DL is a former Royal Air Force officer who served as Commander of Joint Helicopter Command from 2002 to 2005.

Design studies can refer to any design-oriented studies but more formally is an academic discipline or field of study that pursues, through both theoretical and practical modes of inquiry, a critical understanding of design practice and its effects in society.

Saga (born 1975) is a Swedish white nationalist singer-songwriter. She started as the vocalist for Symphony of Sorrow, but has since become known for her tribute CDs to the band Skrewdriver and her solo projects, promoting white nationalism and anti-communism. She is one of the most successful Swedish white nationalist singers.

Joel Spitzer (born c. 1957) is an American smoking cessation educator. He currently serves as technical advisor at WhyQuit.com, a free nicotine dependence recovery website. All of his videos and writings on WhyQuit.com end with his advice, "Never Take Another Puff!"
Gatillazo is a Spanish punk rock band formed in 2005 by Evaristo, former singer of La Polla Records, along with Txiki and Tripi.

Islam in Brazil is believed to have begun with the importation of African slaves, with scholars claiming Brazil received more enslaved Muslims than anywhere else in the Americas. A notable event was the 1835 Malê Revolt in Bahia, a significant slave rebellion led by African Muslims.

Later, Muslim immigration from the Middle East and Southeast Asia influenced the country. According to the 2010 census, Brazil has over 35,000 Muslims, primarily concentrated in São Paulo and Paraná. There's a growing trend of conversions to Islam among non-Arab Brazilians. The country has over 150 mosques, with foreign assistance playing a role in their sustainability.

The Children's Museum of the Arts (“CMA”) is located at 103 Charlton Street, Manhattan, New York, United States in the South Village district. Founded by Kathleen Schneider in 1988, CMA opened its new, 10,000-square-foot space in October 2011.

At CMA, kids ages 0–15 work with teaching artists to create art, including drawings, sculpture, sound art, textiles, and stop-motion animation. As part of its mission to promote art for all children, CMA features programs and partnerships designed for children with learning disabilities, children in foster care, and children in homeless families.

The museum maintains a collection of over 2,000 pieces of international children's art dating back to the 1930s from over 50 different countries.
Space Under Sun is the second album by American singer, Ari Gold.

Kathryn Michelle "Katy" Kinard (born September 16, 1980) is an American Christian musician and pianist, who primarily plays a style of contemporary worship music and Christian pop music. She has released four musical works, Headed Back (2005), You're Still Better (2007), Lullaby Hymns (2010), and God of Fireflies (2016).

"Rollin' in My 5.0" is a single by Vanilla Ice, released on March 1, 1991, from the album *Extremely Live*. The song is a rap ballad about cruising around Miami looking for girls while street racing. A music video was produced to promote the single and album. The song is referenced in Limp Bizkit's "Rollin'" and Vanilla Ice's "Cruisin' in My 6'4".

Srikanth Nammalwar Kidambi (born 7 February 1993) is an Indian badminton player. In 2014, he won the China Open Super Series Premier, the first Indian to do so. In 2022, he was part of the Indian team that won the Thomas Cup. As of September 2022, he is ranked 11th in the world.

*Sam's Strip* was a metafictional comic strip by Mort Walker and Jerry Dumas, which ran from October 2, 1961, to June 1, 1963. The strip starred Sam, who operated his own comic strip, and featured appearances by characters from other comic strips.

Josef Kiefel (1909–1988) was a German communist and Stasi officer. He fled to the Soviet Union during the Nazi era and fought against the Nazis during World War II. After the war, he helped establish East Germany's state security and led Stasi's counterintelligence from 1953 to 1960.

Day of Defeat is a team-based multiplayer first-person shooter video game set in the European theatre of World War II on the Western front. Originally a modification of the 1998 game Half-Life, the rights of the modification were purchased by Valve and released as a full retail title in 2003.

Set in the midst of World War II, Day of Defeat includes no single-player campaign, with focus left only on the game's multiplayer aspects. The game favors teamwork and features objective-based gameplay in combination with its system of classes. Maps are primarily made up of narrow paths, all of which typically lead to a few key locations. An official remake of the game, Day of Defeat: Source, was released by Valve in 2005.
Sianto Sikawa (born 1990) is a Kenyan tourism coordinator for Narok County, fashion model, and philanthropist. She was named among the Top 40 Under 40 Women in Kenya in 2018.

Qin Shengxiang (Chinese: 秦生祥; born February 1957) is an admiral of the Chinese People's Liberation Army Navy (PLAN) who was Political Commissar of the PLA Navy from September 2017 to January 2022. He formerly served as Director of the General Office of the Central Military Commission.

Ralfi Pagán (born Rafael Pagán; 1946–1978) was a Bronx, New York-based Latin soul and salsa singer of Puerto Rican and Cuban parentage who was active from the mid-1960s until his death in 1978. He specialized in soul ballads sung both in Spanish and English and released five albums during the 1970s. He is well known for the duet with Sylvia Robinson entitled Soul Je T'aime and for his solo reworking of Make It With You previously recorded by David Gates and Bread. Ralfi toured with legendary music producer and promoter Eddie Torres for several years throughout the Southwest United States. Prior to leaving on tour Ralfi visited Eddie’s home to share he was leaving to Colombia. Eddie told Ralfi he had a bad feeling about the trip. Eddie advised Ralfi not to go. Ralfi went to Colombia against Torres’ advise. In 1978, he was murdered while on a promotional tour in Colombia, South America. His murder remains unsolved.
Karl Hermann Heinrich Benda (1748–1812) was a German violinist, composer and music teacher. He was the son of Franz Benda, from a noted musical family.

Stefi Baum (born December 11, 1958) is an American astronomer and academic administrator. She obtained a BA in physics from Harvard University and a PhD in astronomy from the University of Maryland. Baum is currently the dean of the Faculty of Science and professor of physics and astronomy at the University of Manitoba.

Albert Henri Bourderon (1858–1930) was a French cooper and pacifist. He founded the coopers unions in 1903 and became a member of the General Confederation of Labour (CGT). During World War I he was involved in the Zimmerwald movement.
**Europe** is a Paris Métro station on Line 3 in the 8th arrondissement of Paris. In 2018, it was renamed **Europe – Simone Veil**.

Victor L. Shapiro (1926 – death date unknown) was an American mathematician. He received his Ph.D. from the University of Chicago in 1952, under Antoni Zygmund. He was a professor at multiple universities and the author of over 80 articles. Shapiro was also a Fellow of the AAAS and AMS.

Uzel Holding (also known as Uzel Group and Uzel Corporation) was one of Turkey's oldest and largest manufacturing companies. Its main sector was agricultural machinery, although it eventually branched out into the automotive industry and real estate through its venture capital arm, Argentum. The company was founded in 1937. However, its roots went back to the middle of the nineteenth century. Uzel Holding collapsed in 2010 and was declared bankrupt in July 2012.

John Dinham (1359–1428) of Nutwell, Devon, was a member of the Devon gentry. He inherited extensive estates from his father, Sir John Dinham (1318–1383), and his mother, Muriel Courtenay. Dinham was known for his violent nature, including killing one of his father's murderers who had sought sanctuary in Exeter Cathedral, for which he was ordered to perform penance. He married three times and was survived by his third wife, Philippa Lovel, and his son and heir, Sir John Dinham (1406–1458). His chest tomb survives in St Mary's Church, Kingskerswell.

Disarmament, demobilisation and reintegration (DDR), or disarmament, demobilisation, repatriation, reintegration and resettlement (DDRRR) are strategies used as a component of peace processes, and is generally the strategy employed by all UN Peacekeeping Operations following civil wars.

A chase gun was a naval artillery piece primarily used during ship chases in the Age of Sail. Due to the length of chases, which could last for hours or days, the accuracy of these guns was crucial. Chase guns were often made of brass to improve accuracy, and skilled gunners would aim carefully at the enemy's rigging to disable their sails. By the late 18th century, the Royal Navy emphasized artillery training for chases and modified cannons for greater range and elevation. From 1799, Royal Navy frigates were typically equipped with two bow and two stern chase guns, as frigates were most often involved in chasing enemy vessels.

*Bathothauma lyromma* is a species of glass squid with a worldwide distribution in tropical and subtropical oceans. The paralarva has eyes mounted on long stalks, a feature reabsorbed as it matures. It is currently the only species in the genus *Bathothauma*.

KushCo Holdings is an American company that sells packaging, containers, and other ancillary products for the cannabis industry. It is based in Garden Grove, California and is publicly traded on the New York OTCQB marketplace under the stock ticker symbol, KSHB.

Cécile Rol-Tanguy (born Cécile Le Bihan; 10 April 1919 – 8 May 2020) was a French communist and Resistance member. During the Occupation of France in World War II, she served as a liaison officer and secretary for the French Forces of the Interior.

Festac '77, also known as the Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture (the first was in Dakar, 1966), was a major international festival held in Lagos, Nigeria, from 15 January 1977 to 12 February 1977. The month-long event celebrated African culture and showcased to the world African music, fine art, literature, drama, dance and religion. About 16,000 participants, representing 56 African nations and countries of the African Diaspora, performed at the event. Artists who performed at the festival included Stevie Wonder from United States, Gilberto Gil from Brazil, Bembeya Jazz National from Guinea, Mighty Sparrow from Trinidad and Tobago, Les Ballets Africains, South African Miriam Makeba, and Franco Luambo Makiadi. At the time it was held, it was the largest pan-African gathering to ever take place.

The official emblem of the festival was a replica crafted by Erhabor Emokpae of the royal ivory mask of Benin. The hosting of the festival led to the establishment of the Nigerian National Council of Arts and Culture, Festac Village and the National Theatre, Iganmu, Lagos. Most of the events were held in four main venues: the National Theatre, National Stadium, Surulere, Lagos City Hall and Tafawa Balewa Square.

Janie Dee (born 20 June 1962) is an English actress, singer, and dancer, known for her versatility in both musical theatre and dramatic roles. Dee began her career as a dancer before transitioning to acting. She trained at the Arts Educational School in Chiswick, London.

Dee's extensive stage work includes award-winning performances in productions such as *Carousel*, *Comic Potential*, and *Follies*. She has also appeared in numerous television dramas and radio productions, showcasing her diverse talents. Dee is involved with several charities and organized the London Concert For Peace in 2003 and the London Climate Change Festival in 2020.
*Other Voices* was a Canadian current affairs television series hosted by Don Francks and broadcast on CBC Television in 1964 and 1965.

The Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture (FESTAC 77) was a month-long international event held in Lagos, Nigeria, from January 15 to February 12, 1977. It celebrated African culture and showcased artistic and cultural achievements from across the African continent and the African diaspora. The festival's origins can be traced to the Négritude movement and Pan-African ideals.

FESTAC 77 involved participants from 48 countries. A new housing estate, Festac Village, was constructed to accommodate approximately 17,000 participants, and a state-of-the-art National Theatre was built to host performances, exhibitions, and a colloquium. The colloquium was a central component of the festival, featuring lectures and discussions by writers, artists, and scholars on topics related to African arts and culture. Other events included a Durbar festival in Kaduna and a boat regatta in Lagos.

Following the festival, Nigeria established the Center for Black and African Arts and Civilization (CBAAC) to preserve the artifacts and legacy of FESTAC 77. The photographic documentation of the festival has also been preserved and exhibited, providing valuable insights into this significant cultural event.
William Frost (1848–1935) was a Welsh inventor who designed the "Frost Airship Glider" in the 1890s, a flying machine.
Hanauer Straße is a street in Munich, Germany, branching from Dachauer Straße. It is known for being the location of the Munich attack in 2016 and the former Moosach gasworks.

The bullace is a variety of plum. It bears edible fruit similar to those of the damson, and like the damson is considered to be a strain of the insititia subspecies of Prunus domestica. Although the term has regionally been applied to several different kinds of "wild plum" found in the United Kingdom, it is usually taken to refer to varieties with a spherical shape, as opposed to the oval damsons.

Unlike nearly all damsons, bullaces may be either "white" (i.e. yellow or green) or "black" (i.e. blue or purple) in colour, and ripen up to six weeks later in the year. Though smaller than most damsons, bullaces are much larger than the closely related sloe. Their flavour is usually rather acidic until fully ripe.
The history of the Jews in South Africa has been marked by periods of official and unofficial antisemitism.

Kachche Heere is a 1982 Bollywood Hindi action film directed by Narender Bedi, starring Feroz Khan, Reena Roy, Danny Denzongpa, Aruna Irani and Shakti Kapoor. The movie was produced by Netra Pal Singh and the music was composed by Rahul Dev Burman. The film was somewhat a sequel to the 1974 Blockbuster Khote Sikkay. The film had the same star cast and almost the exact story line. Among few changes were that Ranjeet was replaced by Shakti Kapoor. The film was released on 12 February 1982.

Charles Van Dell Johnson (August 25, 1916 – December 12, 2008), known professionally as Van Johnson, was an American actor, dancer, and singer. He began his career in Broadway revues and musicals before signing with Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer (MGM).

During World War II, Johnson's all-American image and good looks made him a popular star in war dramas and musicals like *A Guy Named Joe* and *Two Girls and a Sailor*. He was one of the top box office stars of the 1940s. After his time at MGM, Johnson continued to appear in films, television, and stage productions, including *The Caine Mutiny*, *Brigadoon*, and *La Cage aux Folles*.

Harewood House (/ˈhɑːrwʊd/ HAR-wuud, /ˈhɛər-/ HAIR-) is a country house in Harewood, West Yorkshire, England. Designed by architects John Carr and Robert Adam, it was built, between 1759 and 1771, for Edwin Lascelles, 1st Baron Harewood, a wealthy West Indian plantation and slave-owner. The landscape was designed by Lancelot "Capability" Brown and spans 1,000 acres (400 ha) at Harewood.

Still home to the Lascelles family, Harewood House is a member of the Treasure Houses of England, a marketing consortium for ten of the foremost historic homes in the country. The house is a Grade I listed building and a number of features in the grounds and courtyard have been listed as Grade I, II* and II.

William Le Baron Jenney (1832–1907) was an American architect and engineer. He graduated from École Centrale Paris and served as an engineer in the Civil War. Jenney is known for designing the Home Insurance Building in Chicago, the first skyscraper.

Josh Whitman (born August 5, 1978) is a university administrator, a lawyer, and a former American football player. He is currently the athletic director at the University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign. Whitman served as the athletic director at University of Wisconsin–La Crosse from 2011 to 2014, and Washington University in St. Louis from 2014 to 2016.

The White Obelisk is a large, four-sided pillar of white limestone discovered in Nineveh, Iraq, in 1853. It dates to 1050-1031 BC and is now located in the British Museum. The obelisk is decorated with relief carvings on all sides, depicting military campaigns, recreational activities like hunting, and scenes of an Assyrian king receiving tribute. The king is believed to be either Ashurnasirpal I, Tiglath-Pileser II, or Ashurnasirpal II. The inscription describes the king as a conqueror bringing booty and prisoners to Assur. One scene shows the king pouring a libation to the goddess Ishtar.

The Oetker Hotel Management GmbH, which also appears under the brand name Oetker Collection (OHC), is a German hotel management company based in Baden-Baden. The Oetker Collection is managed by the Oetker family. Company hospitality business first started in 1870 when Villa Soleil in Cap d'Antibes opened as a writers' retreat and later become the celebrated Hotel du Cap-Eden-Roc.
Li Shuai (simplified Chinese: 李帅; traditional Chinese: 李帥; pinyin: Li Shuài, born 18 August 1982) is a Chinese former footballer who played as a goalkeeper.

John Ricus Couperus (24 February 1816 – 13 October 1902) was a member of the Council of Justice in Padang and of the High Military Court of the Dutch East Indies. He was also active as a composer in his youth and later in his life.

The 1st Battalion, 160th Field Artillery Regiment is headquartered in Chandler, Oklahoma. It is a part of the 45th Infantry Brigade Combat Team, Oklahoma Army National Guard.

The 160th FA saw action during World War II and the Korean War as part of the 45th Infantry Division and again in Afghanistan and Iraq as part of the 45th Infantry Brigade.
Michael N. Delagi (1879-1957) was a New York lawyer, politician, judge, and New York State Assembly member (Bronx County, 7th District).

"Iced Out Audemars" is a drill song by American rapper Pop Smoke featuring Dafi Woo, released on July 20, 2020, as a track on the deluxe version of Pop Smoke's posthumous debut album, *Shoot for the Stars, Aim for the Moon*. The song was written by Pop Smoke, Dafi Woo, L3gion, and Rico Beats, with production handled solely by Rico Beats and additional production by L3gion.

A remix of the song featuring Lil Wayne surfaced online in October 2020 and was released later that month. The remix was met with generally positive reviews, with critics praising Lil Wayne's energy, bars, and tribute to Pop Smoke, who had been murdered earlier that year. Lil Wayne's verse includes a melodic approach that shifts to a rapid-fire flow, with lyrics referencing Pop Smoke and Wu-Tang Clan.

Dennis Knight (born December 26, 1968) is an American chef and retired professional wrestler. He is best known for his appearances with the World Wrestling Federation (WWF) under the names Phineas I. Godwinn and Mideon. Knight also wrestled as Tex Slazenger in World Championship Wrestling (WCW) and the United States Wrestling Association (USWA).
Papua New Guinea competed at the 2016 Summer Paralympics in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, from 7 September to 18 September 2016.

Vagif Mustafazadeh (Lang-az; March 16, 1940 – December 16, 1979), also known as Vaqif Mustafa-Zadeh, was a Soviet-Azerbaijani jazz pianist and composer, acclaimed for fusing jazz and the traditional Azerbaijani folk music known as mugham. According to many world famous jazz musicians, Mustafazadeh is one of the pioneers and "the architect of jazz in Azerbaijan".

Velocity Tower is a residential building located in Sheffield, South Yorkshire. Construction began in 2006, and the building opened to tenants in December 2008, though unfinished. The tower currently stands at 22 of the planned 30 stories.

Rajkumar Singhajit Singh, (born 1 May 1935) is a leading exponent, choreographer and a guru of Indian classical dance form of Manipuri, including the Pung cholom and Raslila. He was awarded with the Sangeet Natak Akademi Award in 1984 and the Padma Shri in 1986 for his contribution to the Manipuri dance. In the year 2011, Sangeet Natak Akademi, India's The National Academy for Music, Dance and Drama, awarded him with its highest award, the Sangeet Natak Akademi Fellowship for his contribution to Indian Dance. In 2014 he was also conferred with the Tagore Award.

Guru Singhajit Singh and his wife Charu Sija Mathur, who is also a Sangeet Natak Akademi Award recipient, have established, Manipuri Nrityashram, a Manipuri dance school, in New Delhi.

Crescent Baguma (born February 23, 1993), most famously known as Beenie Gunter, is a Ugandan reggae and dancehall musician. Active since 2013, Gunter has released numerous singles and collaborated with artists such as Skales, Eddy Kenzo, and Sheebah Karungi. In 2019, he released his debut album, *No Fear*.

The Togliattiazot Affair is a series of events connected to criminal proceedings against the owners and executives of the Russian firm Togliattiazot (Toaz) and their foreign partners. The case spanned 14 years, from 2005 to 2019, and resulted in the owners and executives receiving prison sentences of between to 8.5 and 9 years. The activity of the involved parties was described as "a threat to [Russia’s] economic activity" by the Chairman of the Investigative Committee of the Russian Federation, Alexander Bastrykin.

Togliattiazot is the largest ammonia producer in Russia, with an estimated global market share of 7.6%.

Milnrow Cricket Club, based in Milnrow, an area of the Metropolitan Borough of Rochdale, Greater Manchester, are an English cricket team who as of 2022 play in the Greater Manchester Cricket League (GMCL).

The club is over 150 years old and celebrated its centenary in 1957 with the publication of a club history. The first professional player for the club was James Rigby, in 1884, but the club has never had a great deal of sporting success when compared to its local rivals. It was a founder member of the Central Lancashire League in 1892.

In 2010 the club received a £57,000 grant from the England & Wales Cricket Board. This was towards the costs of providing artificial practice areas for coaching purposes. Earlier in that year it had also received a smaller grant from Sport England. It has also received funding from, for example, the Co-op.

The club has a junior section and has in the past developed players who have been selected for the Academy of Lancashire Cricket Club, which exists to further develop young talent with the aim of seeing its members become professional players.

For the 2018 and 2019 seasons Milnrow played in the Lancashire League.

The *Breac Maodhóg* is an 11th-century Irish house-shaped reliquary shrine. It is made of bronze figures attached to a wooden base and measures approximately 7 inches in height, 8 inches in width, and 3 inches in depth. The shrine is currently located at the National Museum of Ireland in Dublin.

The shrine likely functioned as a battle standard, similar to the *Cathach of St. Columba*, and would have been carried into battle by a cleric to protect troops and potentially ensure victory. The figures on the *Breac Maodhóg*, mostly clerics, saints, and apostles, are finely molded with individualistic features, expressions, and poses, resembling wood carvings. One notable figure is a musician playing what is believed to be the oldest visual representation of a harp.

The shrine is thought to have originated at Drumlane monastery in County Cavan, although a 15th-century text describes its donation to Rossinver, County Leitrim. The O'Farrelly family were its hereditary keepers when it was rediscovered. Due to its age, the *Breac Maodhóg* is in poor condition.

Terrace House: Closing Door (Japanese: テラスハウス クロージング・ドア, Hepburn: Terasu Hausu Kurōjingu Doa) is a 2015 Japanese reality show theatrical film that acts as a continuation and ending to the Fuji Television show Terrace House: Boys × Girls Next Door. It was released by Toho on February 14, 2015. Tetsuya Sugaya and Seina Shimabukuro continue as residents from the show, with four new members joining them. One of whom is writing a book on Terrace House and briefly interviews previous members. Studio commentators You, Reina Triendl, Yoshimi Tokui, Azusa Babazono, Ryota Yamasato and Hiroomi Tosaka also return.

Closing Door is the last installment in the Terrace House franchise before Netflix became a co-producer later in the year with Terrace House: Boys & Girls in the City.

Borscht Corporation is a Miami-based arts collective and production company known for commissioning short films by local artists. Their productions have been featured in major media outlets and screened at film festivals worldwide, including Sundance and SXSW, with several winning jury prizes. Borscht Corp. also hosts the Borscht Film Festival in Miami, recognized for its unique and experimental programming. The organization and its members have received grants and accolades from institutions like the Knight Foundation and Filmmaker Magazine.
Alimi Ballard (born October 17, 1977) is an American television actor. He is best known for his role as FBI agent David Sinclair on the CBS police procedural Numb3rs (2005–2010).

The Subnetwork Access Protocol (SNAP) is a mechanism for multiplexing protocols on networks using IEEE 802.2 Logical Link Control (LLC). It allows more protocols than can be distinguished by the 8-bit 802.2 Service Access Point (SAP) fields. SNAP supports identifying protocols by Ethernet type field values and vendor-private protocol identifier spaces. It is used with IEEE 802.3, IEEE 802.11 and other IEEE 802 physical network layers, as well as with non-IEEE 802 physical network layers such as FDDI that use 802.2 LLC. The 5-octet SNAP header follows the 802.2 LLC header if the destination SAP (DSAP) and the source SAP (SSAP) contain hexadecimal values of AA or AB.
Lilian Staple Mead (30 June 1865 – unknown) was an Australian suffragette and children's book author. She was the only female student ever educated at Adelaide's Prince Alfred College.

*The Highwomen* is the debut studio album by the Highwomen, released on September 6, 2019. It was produced by Dave Cobb and features the singles "Redesigning Women", "Crowded Table", and "Highwomen". The album received positive reviews from critics.
Iberia Líneas Aéreas de España, S.A. (Iberia Airlines of Spain in English), usually shortened to Iberia, is the largest airline of Spain, based in Madrid.

The 152 mm howitzer-gun M1937 (ML-20) was a Soviet artillery piece produced from 1937 to 1947. Officially classified as a howitzer-gun, it combined characteristics of both weapons, achieving versatility through a wide range of elevation angles and separate loading with 13 propellant loads. Designed by F. F. Petrov's bureau, 6,884 were built and saw action in the Winter War and World War II. The ML-20 was also mounted on the SU-152 and ISU-152 self-propelled guns. Captured ML-20s were used by the Wehrmacht and Finnish Army. After the war, it was widely exported and saw action in various conflicts.

"D.A.N.C.E." is a song by French electronic music duo Justice, released on April 23, 2007, as the second single from their debut album, *†*. A nu-disco track dedicated to Michael Jackson, it interpolates Britney Spears's "Me Against the Music". The song's music video, directed by Jonas & François, won an MTV Europe Music Award.
Gamawan Fauzi is an Indonesian politician and former Minister.

An acoustic torpedo is a torpedo that aims itself by listening for characteristic sounds of its target or by searching for it using sonar (acoustic homing). Acoustic torpedoes are usually designed for medium-range use, and often fired from a submarine.

The first passive acoustic torpedoes were developed nearly simultaneously by the United States Navy and the Germans during World War II.
