The Germans developed the G7e/T4 Falke, which was first deployed by the submarines U-603, U-758 and U-221 in March 1943. Few of these torpedoes were actually used and quickly phased out of service in favor of the T4's successor, the G7es T5 Zaunkönig torpedo in August 1943. The T5 first saw widespread use in September 1943 against North Atlantic escort vessels and merchant ships in convoys.

On the Allied side, the US Navy developed the Mark 24 mine, which was actually an aircraft launched, anti-submarine passive acoustic homing torpedo. The first production Mk. 24s were delivered to the U.S. Navy in March 1943, and it scored its first verified combat kills in May 1943. About 204 torpedoes were launched against submarine targets, with 37 Axis submarines being sunk and a further 18 damaged.

Since its introduction, the acoustic torpedo has proven to be an effective weapon against surface ships as well as serving as an anti-submarine weapon. Today, acoustic torpedoes are mostly used against submarines.

Larry Keel (born November 25, 1968 in Manassas, Virginia) is a bluegrass musician "reared in" Southwest Virginia who's been performing "seriously" since 1976. He was a founding member of Magraw Gap in 1990 with Danny Knicely and Will Lee. He performs with his wife, bassist Jenny Keel, in The Larry Keel Experience – and with other musicians in other groups. He has released over a dozen albums, the latest being Experienced in 2016, which he recorded with his group, Larry Keel Experience. He is married to his bass player, Jenny Keel.

Transbaikal, also known as Dauria, is a region stretching from Lake Baikal to the Shilka and Argun Rivers' confluence. Historically inhabited by the Daur people, it was the Transbaikal Oblast in Imperial Russia and later part of the Far Eastern Republic. The region is known for its unique fauna and flora, with several species named after it.

Carfilzomib, sold under the trade name Kyprolis, is an anti-cancer medication used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. It is derived from epoxomicin, a natural product that inhibits the proteasome. Carfilzomib covalently binds to and inhibits the chymotrypsin-like activity of the 20S proteasome, an enzyme that degrades unwanted cellular proteins.

The FDA granted carfilzomib fast-track status in January 2011 and approved it based on the results of a Phase II trial. Clinical trials have shown carfilzomib to be effective in patients with relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma, both as a single agent and in combination with other drugs. Common adverse events include hematologic toxicity, pneumonia, fatigue, and hyponatremia. Cardiovascular toxicity may also occur.

Medelpad is a historical province (landskap) in northern Sweden, in the southern part of Norrland. It roughly corresponds to the southern portion of Västernorrland County. While the provinces of Sweden no longer serve administrative functions, Medelpad remains a cultural entity. The province is situated between the rivers Ljungan and Indalsälven. Sundsvall is the only city with historical city status and the largest population center. The "High Coast" along the Gulf of Bothnia is a UNESCO World Heritage Site due to post-glacial rebound.

Akhtarul Wasey (born September 1, 1951) is an Indian professor emeritus of Islamic studies. He taught at Jamia Millia Islamia from 1980 to 2016. In 2014, he was appointed Commissioner for Linguistic Minorities in India. He was awarded the Padma Shri in 2013.
Martyrs Bus Service is an Australian bus operator in eastern Melbourne. Founded in 1927, it was purchased by the Dineen Group in 2011 and operates 42 vehicles as of November 2022.

W. David Arnett (born 1940) is an American astrophysicist known for his work in nuclear astrophysics and supernovae. He received his PhD in physics from Yale University in 1965. Arnett has served on the faculties of multiple universities, including the University of Chicago as the B. and E. Sunny Distinguished Service Professor and the University of Arizona as Regents Professor. He was awarded the Hans Bethe Prize in 2009 and the Henry Norris Russell Lectureship.

Ribadesella is a municipality in the autonomous community of Asturias, Spain. Located on the coast, it is known for the Tito Bustillo Cave, which contains prehistoric wall paintings dating back approximately 29,000 years and is part of the Paleolithic Cave Art of Northern Spain UNESCO World Heritage Site. The first written references to the settlement date to the first century BC.

The town of Ribadesella is characterized by its two main streets, the Gran Vía de Agustin Argüelles and the Calle Comercio, as well as the Casco Antiguo (old quarter) and the Paseo Marítimo del Puerto (Maritime Avenue of the Port). A major cultural event is the annual International Descent of the Sella, a kayak race accompanied by the Fiesta de Les Piragües. Numerous other festivals celebrate local traditions, food, and religious figures.

*Primitive Love* is a studio album by Miami Sound Machine, released on August 13, 1985, by Epic Records and CBS Discos International. It was produced by Emilio Estefan Jr. The album followed up on the band's previous releases in its music, rhythms, and Gloria Estefan's vocals. *Primitive Love* marked the band's first appearance on the American albums chart, reaching number 23 on the Billboard 200. Three singles from the album reached the Top 10 of the Billboard Hot 100: "Conga", "Bad Boy", and "Words Get in the Way".

Taptu was a social media and technology company that built platforms, tools and applications that enabled content on touch screen mobile devices, including phones running iOS and Android. Taptu was a privately held company that was founded in Cambridge in 2007 and was funded by DFJ Esprit and Sofinnova. The company was based in Cambridge and Denver, Colorado.

In September 2012, Taptu was acquired by Mediafed Ltd. The Taptu service shut down March 31, 2015, a year before Mediafed went into administration.

The 500-series format is a standardized format for a modular signal processor system consisting of a combination of 500-series modules installed within a 500-series chassis (or rack), with the chassis providing power and audio connections for the individual modules. Originally invented by Automated Processes, Inc. co-founder Saul Walker, today numerous companies manufacture 500-series format products.

The modular nature of the 500-series format allows individual modules to be combined to create a customized signal processing chain, with individual modules added or changed as budget allows. Many 500-series chassis also offer portability, allowing a recording engineer to bring their desired signal processing chain to any recording session.

SMS Leopard was a torpedo cruiser (Torpedoschiff) of the Austro-Hungarian Navy. She and her sister ship, SMS Panther, were part of a program to build up Austria-Hungary's fleet of torpedo craft in the 1880s. Both ships, the only members of the Panther class, were built in Britain at the Armstrong shipyard in Elswick. Leopard was laid down in January 1885, launched in September 1885, and completed in March 1886. She was armed with a battery of two 12 cm (4.7 in) guns and ten 47 mm (1.9 in) guns, along with four 356 mm (14.0 in) torpedo tubes.

Leopard spent much of her career in the main Austro-Hungarian fleet. During this period, she visited Spain for the 1888 Barcelona Universal Exposition, and took part in an international naval demonstration off Crete in 1897 in an attempt to limit the Greco-Turkish War. From 1900, Leopard made two major overseas deployments, including a tour of the Pacific Ocean in 1900–1901, and a stint in the East Asia squadron in 1907–1909. Upon returning to Austria-Hungary, the ship was modernized in 1910 and then decommissioned in May 1914, shortly before the start of World War I in July. The ship was immediately reactivated and stationed in Pola as a guard ship, where she remained for the duration of the conflict. After Austria-Hungary's defeat, Leopard was ceded to Britain as a war prize and sold to ship breakers in Italy in 1920.

The Murakumo-class destroyers (叢雲型駆逐艦, Murakumo-gata kuchikukan) ("Gathering Clouds") were a class of six torpedo boat destroyers (TBDs) of the Imperial Japanese Navy, built in Britain in 1897–99. The class is also sometimes referred to as the Shinonome-class destroyers (東雲駆逐艦, Shinonome-gata kuchikukan)("Daybreak"). All were named after celestial phenomena.

A toxic leader is a person who has responsibility for a group of people or an organization, and who abuses the leader–follower relationship by leaving the group or organization in a worse condition than it was in. Marcia Lynn Whicker popularized the term "toxic leader" in 1996;

In his 1994 journal article "Petty Tyranny in Organizations" Blake Ashforth discussed potentially destructive sides of leadership and identified what he referred to as "petty tyrants", i.e. leaders who exercise a tyrannical style of management, resulting in a climate of fear in the workplace.

The E3 Tug Project is a joint R&D project between Smit International, Damen Shipyards Gorinchem and Alewijnse Marine Technology. The goal of the project is to create an energy efficient tugboat engine to be integrated into future tugboat designs. The purpose of which is to alleviate unnecessary ship pollution by studying the operational patterns of harbor tug use. The Port of Rotterdam is spearheading the project with a goal that by the 2020 it will have reduced its air pollution by ten percent.

Societetsskolan, also known as Hall School, was a school in Gothenburg, Sweden, inaugurated on November 1, 1787. Founded with the Moravian ideal of equal education for males and females, it quickly gained popularity among Gothenburg's wealthy merchant families. While initially intended for children of the Moravian Church, it welcomed non-members and even offered separate classes for boys between 1799 and 1814.

The school provided a structured secondary education for girls, including subjects like German, French, English, and Mathematics, which was innovative for Swedish girls' schools at the time. Educators such as Helena Eldrup and Cecilia Fryxell taught there, and notable students included Emily Nonnen and Mathilda Hall. Although not technically the first girls' school in Sweden, Societetsskolan is recognized for its serious secondary education for girls, setting it apart from other finishing schools.

Edmonton Public Schools is a public school board in Edmonton, Alberta, operating 212 schools and serving over 105,000 students. With a budget of $1.21 billion (2021), it is governed by nine elected trustees. The district pioneered site-based decision making in Canada, giving schools more autonomy. Its archives and museum are located in the historic McKay Avenue School, the site of Alberta's first legislative sessions.

The White Obelisk is a large stone monolith found at the ancient Assyrian settlement of Nineveh, northern Iraq. Excavated by the British archaeologist Hormuzd Rassam in 1853, it is one of only two intact obelisks to survive from the Assyrian empire, the other being the Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III. Both are now preserved in the British Museum. The White Obelisk dates to the beginning of the Neo-Assyrian Empire and has been variously ascribed to the reigns of Ashurnasirpal I (c. 1040 BC), Tiglath-Pileser II (c. 950 BC) or Ashurnasirpal II (c. 870 BC).

Klinga Church (Norwegian: Klinga kirke) is a parish church in Namsos, Trøndelag, Norway. Likely built in the 14th century, the original stave church was replaced in 1705, and then again in 1866 when the current church was consecrated. The present wooden long church was designed by Ernst Kulaas and Jacob Wilhelm Nordan. The church tower was rebuilt in 1948 after being dismantled during World War II. The church is listed as a Norwegian Cultural Heritage Site.

"Tsunagu" is the 52nd single by Japanese boy band Arashi. It was released on June 28, 2017 under their record label J Storm. "Tsunagu" is the theme song for the film Shinobi no Kuni starring Arashi member Satoshi Ohno. The single sold over 380,000 copies in its first week and topped the weekly Oricon Singles Chart. The single was certified Platinum by the Recording Industry Association of Japan (RIAJ).

The Palm Court is a ballroom located within the Alexandria Hotel in Los Angeles, California. It was part of an addition to the hotel designed by Parkinson and Bergstrom in 1911. From 1911 to 1923, it was the city's most prestigious ballroom, hosting events for figures such as President Woodrow Wilson and silent film stars including Rudolph Valentino.

After 1923, with the opening of the Biltmore Hotel, the Alexandria declined in prominence. By the 1930s, the hotel went bankrupt, and the Palm Court was later used as a boxing training venue. The Palm Court was designated a Historic Cultural Monument in 1971. A restoration in the 1980s led to praise of its beauty. In 2009, the Blankenship Ballet Company established its artistic residence at the Alexandria Hotel with the goal of revitalizing the Palm Court. As of 2008, the Alexandria had been converted to apartments.

The laminar flamelet model is one of the methods of modelling turbulent combustions apart from SCRS, eddy flamelet model and others. Combustion is a very important thermochemical process with significant material and aerodynamic implications and thus CFD modeling of combustion has become indispensable. The laminar flamelet model is basically for non pre-mixed fuel (the system in which the fuel and oxygen is supplied from two different pipes).

The concept of ensemble of laminar flamelets was first introduced by Forman A. Williams in 1975, while the theoretical foundation was developed by Norbert Peters in the early 80s.

Tobin Stokes (born 1966) is a Canadian composer from North Vancouver, British Columbia. He studied piano from age three and was a member of the Powell River Boy's Choir. Stokes holds degrees from the University of Victoria and the University of British Columbia. He has been composer-in-residence with the Victoria Symphony and at festivals in Powell River, British Columbia, and Örebro, Sweden. Stokes has composed the operas *Fallujah*, *Pauline*, *The Vinedressers*, and *Rattenbury*.

The Reinhardswald is a large, heavily forested Bunter sandstone plateau in North Hesse, Germany. Rising to 472.2 m above sea level, it is one of Germany's largest forests and most sparsely populated regions, covering over 200 km². Beech and oak trees thrive here, and wood pastures are common. The forest was once an imperial forest and later used as a hunting ground. Sababurg Castle, known as the "Sleeping Beauty" castle, is a popular destination.

The Störtebeker Festival (Lang-de) is an yearly open-air theatre festival in Germany. It is based on stories around the medieval German privateer Klaus Störtebeker and his Victual Brothers, who later turned to pirates.

Founded in 1959 as part of an East German cultural initiative, the festival has become Germany's most successful open-air theatre event, and is broadcast by public television network NDR. It is held in the small town of Ralswiek on the isle of Rügen.

The Citroën Rosalie is a range of three models – the 8CV, 10CV, and 15CV – that were produced by Citroën from 1932 to 1938. Replacing the Citroën C4 and C6, the Rosalie represented a move upmarket for Citroën. The Rosalies were more modern in appearance than their predecessors and were competitively priced due to Citroën's advanced assembly line manufacturing.

In 1934, all Rosalie models received a facelift, becoming known as the NH or B-series. Production of the Rosalie continued even after the introduction of its revolutionary successor, the Traction Avant, in 1934. In total, 88,090 four-cylinder and 7,230 six-cylinder Rosalies were built. The 8CV featured a 1,452 cc four-cylinder engine, while the 10CV had a 1,767 cc engine. The 15CV was equipped with a 2,650 cc six-cylinder engine. For the last four model years, the 8 and 10 were replaced by the 7UA and 11UA.

Alison Fiske (2 August 1943 – 26 July 2020) was a British actress. She trained at the Central School of Speech and Drama and was a founding student of the Drama Centre. Fiske made her television debut in 1965 and joined the Royal Shakespeare Company in 1971. She won a Laurence Olivier Award in 1977. Fiske retired from acting in 2008 and died of cancer in 2020.

Jose Vasquez Aguilar (March 23, 1900 - January 31, 1980) is the first Filipino recipient of the Ramon Magsaysay Award (Asian equivalent of Nobel Prize) and also the first awardee of the said award for Government Service (Ramon Magsaysay Award for Government Service). He is also a recipient in the government service category of the said award which he shared with Chintaman Deshmukh in 1959. He is also recognized as the Father of the Community School Movement in the Philippines, pioneering reforms to ensure that community life is integrated in the education system. His community school model banks on the partnership between teachers, parents, and community with the goal of ensuring a practical education using the vernacular as a medium of instruction.

The 1944 explosion in Aarhus or the 4th of July Disaster (Lang-da) was an explosion in the city of Aarhus, Denmark when a barge loaded with ammunition exploded in the harbor, killing 39 people and injuring another 250.

The explosion occurred in conjunction with the occupation of Denmark during the Second World War. Aarhus had become an increasingly important transport hub for German supplies and troops to occupied Norway, by virtue of a large port in the Kattegat and a railway connection to Germany. Supplies arrived by rail from Germany and was loaded from rail cars to barges in the harbor by Danish dock workers, often by hand. Officials from the Aarhus municipal government had prior to 4 July approached German authorities with safety concerns since accidents with ammunition had previously occurred in Norway; specifically the 1943 Filipstad explosion and the 1944 explosion in Bergen. Discussions were underway about the possibility to move transhipment to a less populated area but by July 1944 no decision had yet been made.
**Andrey Zayakin** (born February 23, 1981) is a Russian physicist, journalist for *Novaya Gazeta*, and co-founder of Dissernet, known for exposing plagiarism in dissertations.

Ernest Dichmann Peek (1878–1950) was a major general in the United States Army. He served in the Philippine–American War, World War I, and World War II, and received the Distinguished Service Medal and Silver Star.

Baasskap (IPA-af) (also spelled baaskap), literally "boss-ship" or "boss-hood", is an Afrikaans term that was used during apartheid to describe the social, political and economic domination of South Africa by its minority white population generally and by Afrikaners in particular. The term is sometimes translated to the English-language term "white supremacy" and functioned either as a description or an endorsement of white minority rule in South Africa.

Bertrand Ndongo (born c. 1990) is a consultant to Rocío Monasterio, Vox's leader in the Community of Madrid, who built up a following on YouTube. He supports limiting migration to Spain. In December 2019, he criticised King Felipe VI. He has been suspended from Twitter for spreading fake news.
The *Dartmouth Jack-O-Lantern* is Dartmouth College's humor magazine, founded in 1908. It publishes quarterly and maintains an active online presence.

The Jamaica Premier League is the top professional football league in Jamaica. Founded in 1973, it is contested by 12 clubs. The league has produced four Caribbean champions, with Portmore United winning the most championships (7 titles). Cavalier F.C. are the current champions (2021). The league season typically runs from August to May.
John Duncan (19 December 1794 – 9 August 1881) was a Scottish weaver and botanist.

*Trichomonas gallinae* is a parasitic protozoan responsible for avian trichomoniasis, a disease primarily affecting young birds. The parasite is typically found in the upper digestive and respiratory tracts, multiplying through binary fission. Transmission occurs via contaminated food, water, crop milk, or through predation.

The severity of infection varies, with symptoms ranging from mild lesions to fatal necrotic masses in the mouth and throat. Diagnosis involves microscopic examination of oral excretions. Treatment typically involves nitroimidazole antibiotics. Prevention includes culling carrier birds and maintaining clean food and water sources. *T. gallinae* is found worldwide, excluding Antarctica, Greenland, and far northern regions. The parasite is not known to infect humans.

*This Is Orson Welles* is a 1992 book comprising a series of interviews between Orson Welles and Peter Bogdanovich. The book, published by HarperCollins, explores Welles's life and career through extensive conversations recorded over several years in the United States, Mexico, and Europe.

The project originated in 1968 after Welles expressed interest in collaborating with Bogdanovich on a book about his life, following a favorable impression of Bogdanovich's monograph on Welles's films. The interviews were structured loosely, mirroring the natural flow of their conversations, rather than adhering to a strict chronological order. Welles actively participated in the editing process, revising and rewriting chapters.

The book's progress was intermittent, interrupted by Welles's other commitments, including a separate offer to write his memoirs. After Welles's death in 1985, Oja Kodar enlisted Bogdanovich's help to prepare the book for publication. Bogdanovich transcribed the extensive materials, which were then edited by Jonathan Rosenbaum into the final published form.
Kaintuck' (Kentucky) is a 1935 symphonic poem for piano and orchestra by American composer William Grant Still.

Joanna Mytkowska (1970) is a Polish curator and art critic. She has been the director of the Museum of Modern Art, Warsaw since 2007. She is the co-founder of the Foksal Gallery Foundation, which since 2001 has been operating independently from the Foksal Gallery.

The Stockade Building System is a construction method patented in 1927 by Buckminster Fuller and his father-in-law, James Hewlett. It used fibrous blocks coated with plaster, molded with holes for concrete or beams to create a weight-bearing structure. It was evaluated at a residence in Florida in 1926.
Idan Roll (born April 27, 1984) is an Israeli politician. He was first elected to the Knesset in 2019 as part of the Blue and White alliance, and later joined Yesh Atid. He is married to Harel Skaat.

Marco Pinotti (born 25 February 1976 in Osio Sotto, Lombardy) is an Italian former road racing cyclist, who competed as a professional between 1999 and 2013. An individual time trial specialist, Pinotti was a six-time Italian Time Trial Champion (2005, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010 and 2013).

TVR Cultural (IPA-ro) was the cultural channel of Romania's government-funded television network Televiziunea Română. It provided cultural news, documentaries about the arts, as well as various shows, musicals and theatrical pieces. It was closed in September 2012.

The Children's Museum of the Arts (CMA) is a children's art museum. CMA's inaugural exhibition in its new facility is “Make Art (in) Public,” a survey of art in the public realm, which consists of sketches, photographs, models and pieces by Keith Haring, Christo and Jeanne-Claude, Friedensreich Hundertwasser, Tranqui Yanqui, and Swoon. Past exhibitions include “Beyond the Refrigerator Door,” “The Black Book of Colors,” Ian Berry's 'Secret Garden' and “Art Within Reach: From WPA to the Present”. The CMA provides art and creative expression programs for several groups of children and their families, including those with autism, families who are receiving preventive services, in foster care working toward reunification. These programs take place both at the museum and as outreach programs.

The slender-billed gull (Chroicocephalus genei) is a mid-sized gull which breeds very locally around the Mediterranean and the north of the western Indian Ocean (e.g. Pakistan) on islands and coastal lagoons. Most of the population is somewhat migratory, wintering further south to as far as North Africa and India. A few birds have wandered to western Europe. A vagrant individual was reportedly seen on Antigua, April 24, 1976 (AOU, 2000).

The genus name Chroicocephalus is from Ancient Greek khroizo, "to colour", and kephale, "head". The specific genei commemorates Italian naturalist Giuseppe Gené.

The **Bronze Fonz** is a bronze sculpture of Henry Winkler as Arthur Fonzarelli (The Fonz) from the TV show *Happy Days*. Created by Gerald P. Sawyer and commissioned by Visit Milwaukee, it was unveiled in 2008 on Milwaukee's Riverwalk. The statue has faced some opposition from local art critics.
Nashipur is a village in Murshidabad district, West Bengal, India, known for the Nashipur Rajbari and Nashipur Akhara.
Michael McClune (born August 22, 1989) is an American tennis player. In 2007, he won the USTA Boys National 18s Tennis Championships, earning a wildcard into the US Open.
Andrea Stinson (born November 25, 1967) is an American former professional basketball player. A guard, she played in the WNBA from 1997 to 2005 and was a three-time All-Star.
Paladin Career and Technical High School is a Minnesota public charter school in Coon Rapids, Minnesota, in the United States. It opened in September 2002 and teaches grades 9–12.

James McLachlan "Jimmy" Turnbull (23 May 1882 – unknown) was a Scottish footballer. He played as a forward for multiple clubs in Scotland and England, including Manchester United, where he won the Football League title, FA Charity Shield, and FA Cup. His brothers Tom and Alex were also footballers.

Jessica Ashley Karpov (born July 10, 1992), known professionally as Harloe, is an American singer, songwriter, and music producer. Active since 2009, she initially performed as Jessica Ashley, releasing the EP *Prelude* in 2012. In 2016, she debuted as Harloe with the single "All in My Feelings." She co-wrote and produced K/DA's "POP/STARS" and in 2019, she was featured on Robin Schulz's "All This Love". Her debut EP, *Rivers Run Dry*, was released in 2020.

Konanakunte, formerly a census town, is a suburb of Bangalore in the Indian state of Karnataka.The MP of this area is DK Suresh (brother of DK Shivakumar, former karnataka minister),mla for this area is M.Krishnappa, Corporator is Shashirekha Jayaram.

Hyme House, at 3 Fitzjohn's Avenue, Hampstead, England, was the London home of society portrait painter Philip de László. He painted many of his portraits at sittings in the studio and gardens of Hyme House. Sitters included royalty, celebrities, businesspeople and politicians from the 1920s and 1930s.

Maria Silva Cruz (1915 – 1936), also known as La Libertaria, was a Spanish anarchist known for her participation in the Casas Viejas incident. She was executed by firing squad during the Spanish Civil War.
Serena Katherine "Violet" Dandridge (1878–1956) was an American scientific illustrator and naturalist. She worked at the Smithsonian Institution and was also a suffragist.
The PKP class Pm36 is a class of Polish steam locomotives. Two prototypes were built in 1937, one of which, Pm36-2 "Beautiful Helen", survives to this day.

Samuel Walker (born 16 June 2002) is an English-born Australian professional rugby league footballer who plays as a halfback or five-eighth for the Sydney Roosters in the National Rugby League (NRL). Walker was named Dally M Rookie of the year in 2021.
Egypt competed at the 2014 Summer Youth Olympics in Nanjing, China, with 83 competitors in 20 sports and 8 medals.

Joseph Lawson Howze (August 30, 1923 – January 9, 2019) was an American prelate of the Catholic Church. He was the first bishop of the Diocese of Biloxi (1977-2001) and the first Black bishop to head a diocese in the United States.

The Cromarty Courthouse is a municipal building in Cromarty, Scotland, completed in 1773. It was designed in the Scottish medieval style and served as a judicial venue and meeting place of the burgh council.
Hugues Alexandre Joseph Meunier (23 November 1758 or 1751 – 9 December 1831) was a French Army general during the French Revolutionary Wars and the Napoleonic Wars.

The Anthony Massad Three-Decker is a historic triple-decker house in Worcester, Massachusetts. Built about 1912, it was cited as a good local example of Colonial Revival styling when it was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1990. Many details, including Tuscan columns on the porch and modillion blocks in the eaves, have been lost or obscured by subsequent exterior changes (see photo).

*Commonwealth v. Pullis*, also known as the Philadelphia Cordwainers' case, was a 1806 conspiracy trial against leaders of the Federal Society of Journeymen Cordwainers. The Philadelphia Mayor's Court found them guilty of conspiring to raise wages, effectively making labor unions illegal conspiracies until *Commonwealth v. Hunt*.

The Martin PBM Mariner was an American twin-engined flying boat patrol aircraft designed in 1937 by the Glenn L. Martin Company. Entering service in 1940, it was used in World War II for anti-submarine patrols, sinking ten U-boats. The United States Coast Guard also used the PBM for search and rescue. It continued service in the Korean War and with the Royal Netherlands Navy until the late 1950s.

Barasat Junction railway station is the primary rail hub in the city of Barasat It is one of the major suburban city railway stations in the district of North 24 Parganas. Generally, the people commuting daily use this station for travelling to Kolkata's Central Business District or other towns, with the busiest times at the station being the morning and evening peak periods. This station provides the facility of separate rest rooms for both male & female passengers. Barasat Junction Railway station consists of 6 permanent platforms (Platform no: 1–6).

Recently, the government-owned Indian Railways corporation approved a proposal for an underground metro railway station connecting Barasat Junction to Noapara metro station. The construction of the project has started in October 2011.

An EMU carshed is situated in the vicinity of Barasat Junction railway station.

La Grosse Décharge Est (The Big East Discharge) is a river in the city of Baie-Saint-Paul, within the Charlevoix Regional County Municipality in the Capitale-Nationale region of Quebec, Canada.

The river is 6.5 kilometers (4.0 mi) long and originates from a small lake at an elevation of 673 meters (2,208 ft). It descends from the mountains, dropping 249 meters (817 ft) before draining into the north shore of La Grosse Décharge Ouest. The water flows into the Rivière des Mares, then the Rivière du Gouffre, which empties into the Saint Lawrence River.

The name "La Grosse Décharge Est" was officially recognized by the Commission de toponymie du Québec on March 25, 1997. The name appeared on a map in 1955.
Jean Garrabé (11 August 1931 – 13 September 2020) was a French psychiatrist.

Annamarie Tendler (born June 9, 1985), known professionally as Anna Marie Tendler, is an American multimedia artist known for her work in photography, makeup and hairstyling, and textile crafts, with a notable specialty in handmade lampshades. She is the author of Pin It!: 20 Fabulous Bobby Pin Hairstyles and The Daily Face: 25 Makeup Looks for Day, Night, and Everything In Between!.

The College of Dental Medicine is the dental school of the Medical University of South Carolina. It is located in the city of Charleston, South Carolina, United States. It is the only dental school in South Carolina.

Saint Vincent Martyr School (SVMS), the oldest Catholic elementary school (established in 1848) in New Jersey, is the coeducational parish school for Saint Vincent Martyr parish in Madison borough, Morris County, New Jersey, United States. SVMS educates approximately 400 students in grades PK-3 through Eighth Grade. The school is named for Vincent of Saragossa. The school nickname is the Eagles.

The school and parish are in the Southeastern Morris Deanery of the Diocese of Paterson of the Province of Newark of the Latin Rite of the Catholic Church.

SVMS was one of three elementary schools in New Jersey, and the only non-public school, recognized by the national Blue Ribbon Schools Program, awarded by the United States Department of Education, for the 2005-06 school year. It is fully accredited by the Middle States Association of Colleges and Schools.

The word *entrée* first appeared in print around 1536, initially referring to the "entrance to the table" or the first stage of a meal. By the 17th century, it evolved to denote the second stage, following the *potage* (soup). Entrées typically featured meats cooked using moist methods like sautés and fricassées, and were always served hot. Over time, distinctions arose, such as *grosses entrées* (large joints of meat) and *entrées ordinaires* (smaller, more elaborate dishes). In modern French cuisine, *entrée* refers to the appetizer or starter course.
**Christa Peters-Lidard** is an American earth scientist at NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center. She is known for her work on land surface modeling.
François Picquet (IPA-fr; 4 December 1708 – 15 July 1781) was a French Sulpician priest who emigrated to Montreal, Canada, in 1734.

"Like a Surgeon" is a song recorded by American recording artist Ciara. Written by The-Dream and produced by Tricky Stewart, the song is included on her third studio album, Fantasy Ride. It was planned to be released as the third single from the album in North America, but its release was cancelled. It still manage to chart on the lower half of US R&B charts.

The song's promotion began on MTV. During a commercial for the album, Ciara sang part of the song in a cappella.

Mongolia competed at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, Greece, from August 13 to August 29, 2004. This was Mongolia's tenth appearance at the Summer Olympics, with the nation having participated in every Summer Olympics since 1964, excluding 1984 due to the Soviet boycott.

The Mongolian National Olympic Committee sent a total of 20 athletes to the Games, 14 men and 6 women, to compete in 7 sports. The flag bearer for the country at the opening ceremony was Damdinsürengiin Nyamkhüü. Mongolia's team in Athens was highlighted by the judoka Khashbaataryn Tsagaanbaatar, who won a bronze medal in the men's 60 kg event. This was Mongolia's only medal at the games. Other sports that Mongolian athletes participated in were athletics, boxing, shooting and weightlifting.
William Patrick "Bill" Twomey Sr. (1899–1977) was an Australian rules footballer who played for Collingwood and Hawthorn, and won the 1924 Stawell Gift.

The 1957 Alberta liquor plebiscite was held after debate in the legislature on loosening liquor restrictions in place since Prohibition. A second plebiscite among egg producers on establishing a compulsory egg marketing board was held at the same time.

Voters were asked whether new liquor store locations should be permitted. In Calgary and Edmonton, a second question asked if "mixed drinking" should be allowed in beer parlours, where men and women had been segregated since 1928. The province-wide question on liquor stores passed, as did the question on mixed drinking in the major cities. Gender-based segregation of bars was abolished province-wide in 1967.
The Secours Populaire Français (SPF), or French Popular Relief, is a French non-profit organization founded in 1945, dedicated to fighting poverty and discrimination in public life.
Harold Bradley (1905 – November 1984) was a Canadian pianist and music educator. He founded the Bradley Institute for Music Education Research.

El Camino High School is a public high school located in Oceanside, California. Established in 1975, it is part of the Oceanside Unified School District. As of 2018–19, the school had an enrollment of 2,958 students and a student-to-teacher ratio of 26:1. The school's colors are brown and gold, its mascot is the Wildcat, and its rival is Oceanside High School.

El Camino High School received media attention in 1986 when students requested the banning of books on sorcery and satanism. In 2008, the school drew national media attention for staging a controversial fake car accident as part of an anti-drunk driving program.
*Securicula gora* is a freshwater fish in the family Danionidae, native to the Ganges and Brahmaputra drainages. It is of Least Concern to conservation.

Ibex Peak is a 2,039-meter (6,690 ft) mountain summit located in British Columbia, Canada. It lies within the North Cascades range. The first ascent of Ibex Peak was made in 1962 by Asger Bentzen, Hamish Mutch, Glenn Woodsworth, and Bob Woodsworth. The easiest route to the summit is a scramble via the northwest slope.

Geologically, the mountain's formation is attributed to volcanic activity and terrane creation millions of years ago, followed by glacial scouring during the Pleistocene period. Ibex Peak experiences a marine west coast climate, characterized by high precipitation, especially snowfall during winter. The most favorable weather for climbing is from July through September.
Dennis Mallonee (born January 22, 1955) is an American writer of comic books.

Pierre Galet (28 January 1921 – 30 December 2019) was a French ampelographer and author who was an influential figure within ampelography in the 20th century and before DNA typing was widely introduced. Beginning in the 1950s, Pierre Galet introduced a system for identifying varieties based on the shape, contours and characteristics of the leaves of the vines, petioles, growing shoots, shoot tips, grape clusters, as well as the colour, size, seed content and flavour of the grapes. The impact and comprehensiveness of his work earned him the consideration as the "father of modern ampelography". He started publishing within ampelography in the 1950s and his Ph.D. thesis was presented in 1967. He has also written popular science books on grape varieties. Galet was active at the École Nationale Supérieure Agronomique de Montpellier.

Fluorenone (9H-Fluoren-9-one) is an aromatic organic compound with the chemical formula C13H8O. It appears as a yellow solid and is soluble in various organic solvents. Azafluorenones, pyridine analogs to fluorenones, have shown antimicrobial activity and other biological effects, leading to research into their synthesis through various reactions.
Lucius Julius L. f Vop. n. Iulus was a member of the patrician house of the Julii at ancient Rome. He was military tribune with consular powers in 401 and 397 BC.
John Skipper is an American television executive, current executive chairman of DAZN Group, and former president of ESPN.

The 2019 Walker Cup was held September 7–8 at Royal Liverpool Golf Club in Hoylake, England. The United States team, captained by Nathaniel Crosby, defeated Great Britain & Ireland 15½ to 10½. Craig Watson was captain of the Great Britain & Ireland team. The U.S. team included Andy Ogletree, John Augenstein, and Cole Hammer.

*End of the World* (French: *La Fin du monde*) is a 1931 French science fiction film directed by Abel Gance. It stars Victor Francen, Colette Darfeuil, and Abel Gance. The film revolves around a comet, Lexell's Comet, on a collision course with Earth, and the reactions of various groups of people to the impending apocalypse.

The plot follows Martial Novalic, who discovers the comet, and his efforts to unite the world in peace. His efforts are opposed by the stock promoter Schomburg, who seeks to profit from the chaos. Genevieve de Murcie, torn between the two men, ultimately joins Martial in his quest for peace.

Originally intended to be a much longer film, *End of the World* was cut down to 105 minutes by its backers, against Gance's wishes. The film was a critical and commercial failure upon its release. A heavily edited version, retitled *Paris After Dark*, was released in the United States in 1934. Gance himself disowned the film, calling it "an abortive work."

The 4th Electromagnetic Warfare Squadron is a United States Space Force unit assigned to Space Delta 3 at Peterson Space Force Base, Colorado. Established in 1990, the squadron delivers offensive counterspace and space situational awareness effects, operating the Counter Communications System.
STM is a Turkish public company founded in 1991. It provides technology, engineering, and consultancy services in the defense industry.

Eastoft is a village and civil parish in North Lincolnshire, England. The village lies along the A161 road between Crowle and Goole. The population of the civil parish was 431 in 2011. The Anglican parish church, dedicated to St Bartholomew, was built in 1855.

Great Wishford is a village and civil parish in the Wylye Valley in Wiltshire, England, about three miles (5 km) north of Wilton and five miles (8 km) northwest of Salisbury. The village lies west of a bend in the River Wylye and has a triangular street layout comprising South Street, West Street and Station Road.

The Rodeo–Chediski Fire was a wildfire in Arizona in June–July 2002. It was the largest wildfire recorded in Arizona history, burning 468,638 acres (732.247 sq mi) in Coconino and Navajo counties. The fire was the result of two separate fires, one arson and one accidental, that merged. It destroyed 426 structures.

Casey Cizikas (born February 27, 1991) is a Canadian professional ice hockey centre for the New York Islanders of the National Hockey League (NHL). He spent four seasons with the Mississauga St. Michaels Majors of the Ontario Hockey League (OHL), where he had two 20-goal seasons. Cizikas was selected by the Islanders in the fourth round, 92nd overall, of the 2009 NHL Entry Draft. He began playing for their minor league affiliate, the American Hockey League (AHL)'s Bridgeport Sound Tigers, two years after the draft, during the 2011–12 season; he was recalled to the Islanders' NHL roster mid-season.

The Reinhardswald (German pronunciation) is a range of hills up to 472.2 m above sea level (NN) and covering an area of over 200 km2 in the Weser Uplands in the district of Kassel, Hesse (Germany). Of this, 183 km2 are part of the unincorporated area known as Gutsbezirk Reinhardswald.

The area is known for its myths and legends, such as Grimms' Fairy Tales, and the "Sleeping Beauty" castle of Sababurg is internationally known.
Paladin Career and Technical High School is a public charter school in Coon Rapids, Minnesota, serving grades 9-12. It serves an at-risk student population.

Dale Harris Van Sickel (November 29, 1907 – January 25, 1977) was an American college football, basketball and baseball player during the 1920s, who later became a Hollywood motion picture actor and stunt performer for over forty years. Van Sickel played college football for the University of Florida, and was recognized as the first-ever first-team All-American in the history of the Florida Gators football program.

Jerry Collins (4 November 1980 – 5 June 2015) was a Samoan-born New Zealand rugby union player. He played for the All Blacks, Wellington, and the Hurricanes. Collins captained the All Blacks in 2006 and at the 2007 Rugby World Cup. After retiring from international rugby in 2008, he played for Toulon, Ospreys, Yamaha Júbilo, and Racing Club Narbonne. Collins died in a car accident in France along with his partner, Alana Madill. His infant daughter survived.

A temporary crown (provisional crown, interim crown) is a temporary (short-term) crown used in dentistry. Like other interim restorations, it serves until a final (definitive) restoration can be inserted. Usually the temporary crown is constructed from acrylic resins (monomethacrylate-based/polymethacrylate-based) or, chemical-cure/light cure composite (dimethacrylate-based), although alternative systems using aluminium crown forms are occasionally used. Temporary crowns function to protect the tooth, prevent teeth shifting, provide cosmetics, shape the gum tissue properly, and prevent sensitivity.
Eric Reed (born 1963 or 1964) is an American horse trainer. He is the trainer of Rich Strike, the winner of the 2022 Kentucky Derby.
A by-election to the Spanish Cortes Españolas was held in Spanish Guinea in 1960.

Dr Abdulai Osman Conteh (born 5 August 1945) is a lawyer and politician from Sierra Leone. He is a former vice president of Sierra Leone, who served under President Joseph Saidu Momoh before he was ousted by the military junta in 1992. Conteh later spent a considerable number of years in Belize, where he served as the country's chief justice before returning to Sierra Leone.

Alliance française or AF (Lang-fr, IPA-fr; The French Alliance), is an international organization that aims to promote the French language and francophone culture around the world. Created in Paris on 21 July 1883 under the name Alliance française pour la propagation de la langue nationale dans les colonies et à l'étranger (French alliance for the propagation of the national language in the colonies and abroad), known now simply as L'Alliance française, its primary goal is teaching French as a second language. Headquartered in Paris, the Alliance had 850 centers in 137 countries on every inhabited continent in 2014.

Joseph Heller (born April 10, 1941) is an Israeli zoologist and malacologist, originally from Sydney, Australia. He is known for his research on the taxonomy, biogeography, and reproductive biology of gastropods, particularly in Israel. His work includes studying the natural history of terrestrial and aquatic gastropods, their resistance to heat and desiccation, and developing a new bio-geographic methodology for modeling faunal responses to climatic gradients.

Heller has also contributed to conservation planning strategies for terrestrial invertebrate faunas. Since 1979, he has been the Academic Curator of the National Molluscs Collection at the National Natural History Collections at the Hebrew University, Jerusalem. He participates in committees at the Academy of the Hebrew Language and the Israel Academy of Sciences and Humanities. Heller was editor in chief of *The Israel Journal of Zoology* and was Head of the Department of Zoology at the Hebrew University. In 2010, he was awarded Honorary Membership in the Zoological Society of Israel.

Convoy HX 156 was a trade convoy of merchant ships during World War II. It sailed from New York on October 22, 1941, and arrived in Liverpool on November 5, 1941. On October 31, the German submarine U-552 torpedoed and sank the USS Reuben James, the first United States warship sunk during World War II. The convoy was also attacked by U-567.

Fungistatics are substances that prevent fungi from growing. They are used in both medicine, such as fluconazole and itraconazole, to treat fungal infections, and as food preservatives, such as sodium benzoate and potassium sorbate.

Henrique Honoré Dumont (1832–1892) was a Brazilian coffee farmer and engineer. Applying his engineering knowledge, he transformed his farm into the most modern in South America. Dumont also helped build the first gasoline car. He is the father of Alberto Santos Dumont.

"Jam" is a song by American singer Michael Jackson, released as the fourth single from his 1991 album *Dangerous* on July 13, 1992. It blends new jack swing, industrial, and funk genres. The song was written by Jackson, René Moore, Bruce Swedien, and Teddy Riley, with production helmed by Jackson, Riley, Moore, and Swedien.

Music critics generally praised "Jam," with some highlighting its funky groove, brassy horns, and Jackson's energetic vocals. One reviewer noted the song's message about escaping worldly issues through music and dance.

The music video, directed by David Kellogg, features Jackson teaching basketball star Michael Jordan how to dance, while Jordan teaches Jackson basketball. It includes cameo appearances by rap groups Kris Kross, Naughty by Nature, and Heavy D. Jackson performed "Jam" as the opening number on his Dangerous World Tour and at the Royal Brunei Concert in 1996. A portion of the song was also performed at the 1993 Super Bowl halftime show.
Puschkinia peshmenii is a bulbous perennial from Turkey and Iran, producing flowers in shades of green in spring.

Santiago de Cuba, also known as Santiago General Senén Casas, is the main railway station of the city of Santiago de Cuba, seat of the homonym province, Cuba. It is owned by the state company Ferrocarriles de Cuba (FFCC) and is located in the central Paseo de Martí, nearby the city harbor.

It is the second most important stations of Cuba and, along with Havana Central, Camagüey and Santa Clara, is a network's divisional headquarter.
Workpoint TV is a Thai television network owned by Workpoint Entertainment, launched in 2011 and on digital in 2014.
*R (Corner House Research) v Director of the Serious Fraud Office* was a UK House of Lords case concerning the rule of law.

Spurling's test is a medical maneuver used during a spinal or neck examination to assess nerve root pain and aid in the diagnosis of cervical radiculopathy. It is typically performed on patients with radicular symptoms to guide further imaging and diagnosis. The test involves applying axial compression to the cervical spine, sometimes with lateral flexion.

While Spurling's test is considered relatively sensitive for diagnosing cervical spondylosis or acute cervical radiculopathy in individuals with abnormal electromyogram results, caution is advised in cases of acute cervical injuries, instability, or other underlying conditions. Studies on the test's accuracy have shown varying results, with sensitivity ranging from 30% to 95% and specificity from 93% to 94%.
Egypt competed at the 2014 Summer Youth Olympics, in Nanjing, China from 16 August to 28 August 2014.

The 2013 Stadium Super Trucks Series was the inaugural season of Stadium Super Trucks competition. The series marked the revival of off-road racing in stadiums, originally created by Mickey Thompson, and reintroduced by former NASCAR driver Robby Gordon. The trucks were one of four classes available, along with Bigfoot Monster Trucks, Super Trophy Karts, and Super Buggys.

The champion was awarded the Mickey Thompson Championship Trophy. Gordon won the championship by seven points over Rob MacCachren.

Ma Zhou (601–648), courtesy name Binwang, formally the Duke of Gaotang (高唐公), was a Chinese politician who served as a chancellor during the reign of Emperor Taizong in the Tang dynasty. He was initially a commoner and a guest of the general Chang He (常何), and after Chang submitted suggestions that Ma drafted, Emperor Taizong was impressed and retained Ma as an imperial official, eventually rising to the post of chancellor.

CCM is a Canadian bicycle manufacturer based in Montreal, Quebec.

"CCM" was originally an initialism for Canada Cycle & Motor Co. Ltd. However, the company would eventually split into two separate entities both maintaining the CCM trademark, one maintaining bicycle manufacturing and the other, CCM (The Hockey Company), producing hockey equipment.

Acts of Parliament of the United Kingdom relating to the European Communities and the European Union include legislation concerning the UK's accession and membership, referendums, the European Economic Area, and European Parliament elections. These acts also cover the European Union Act 2011, accession treaties, and amendments to the European Communities Act 1972. The European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 repealed the 1972 Act, but many provisions remained until the end of the Brexit transition period.

Sanjay National Park is a national park located in the Sidhi district of Madhya Pradesh, India. Established in 1983, it spans 466.657 square kilometers. The park is known for its tropical Sakhua forests and diverse fauna, including Bengal tigers, leopards, deer, and numerous bird species. It forms part of the Sanjay-Dubri Tiger Reserve.

Margaret Dayton (born May 17, 1949) is an American politician and former Republican member of the Utah State Senate, representing the 15th district from 2007 to 2018. Before that, she was a member of the Utah House of Representatives from 1996 to 2007. Dayton is a graduate of Brigham Young University and a former registered nurse. She is known for her conservative stance on abortion and other social issues.

The Cadets were an American doo-wop group from Los Angeles, California, formed in the early 1950s. They were signed to Modern Records, who gave them their name. To avoid competing with themselves, they recorded under the name "The Jacks" on Modern's subsidiary label, RPM Records. The group is known for "Stranded in the Jungle". The Cadets stopped touring in 1957 and broke up at the end of the year. They reformed in the late 1990s and appeared on the PBS special, Doo Wop 51.
Caesium bisulfate or cesium hydrogen sulfate is an inorganic compound with the formula CsHSO4. The caesium salt of bisulfate, it is a colorless solid obtained by combining Cs2SO4 and H2SO4.

*King Arthur's Disasters* is a British children's television series that aired on CITV from April 11, 2005, to February 1, 2006. Starring Rik Mayall, Matt Lucas, Morwenna Banks, and Phil Cornwell, the show ran for two seasons, comprising 26 episodes.

The series revolves around Princess Guinevere's requests for specific items, animals, or beings, which King Arthur seeks in exchange for her hand in marriage. Accompanied by Merlin, Arthur embarks on quests fraught with danger. Despite reaching his goals, something invariably ruins the outcome, leaving Guinevere dissatisfied. Adding to Arthur's troubles are Sir Lancelot and Sir Martyn, two rebellious knights who constantly bicker but unite in their opposition to the king. The show features characters drawn from Arthurian legend, reimagined with a 20th-century sensibility.

Hatton Castle is a castle near Newtyle in Angus, Scotland, built in 1575. Commissioned by Laurence, fourth Lord Oliphant, it incorporates an unusual scale and platt staircase for a construction of its size. After being de-roofed around 1720, it fell into disrepair until being faithfully reconstructed starting in the late 20th century. Today, Hatton Castle is a family home known for its Great Hall with stunning acoustics, hosting regular gatherings of Scottish traditional musicians and house concerts. The gardens have been re-established, featuring traditional dry-stane dyking and an orchard of Scottish apple varieties.

The Catskill Mountain House was a hotel built in 1824 on a plateau in the Catskill Mountains. It became a popular subject for artists and writers, including those of the Hudson River School. The hotel declined in popularity and was burned down by the State of New York in 1963. Today, the site offers sweeping views and is near the North-South Lake campground.

Songs of the Pixies was composed by Samuel Taylor Coleridge during 1793. The poem describes Coleridge's summer vacation and his childhood home. It also incorporates Coleridge's own view of himself as a young poet.

Burlington Street is an 8.5 km (5.3 mi) east–west thoroughfare in Hamilton, Ontario, Canada, running parallel to Hamilton Harbour. Originally named "Industrial Road," it was renamed for its proximity to Burlington Bay (now Hamilton Harbour).

During Hamilton's industrial peak after World War II, Burlington Street was home to the Canadian head offices of major manufacturing companies, including Stelco, Dofasco, Firestone, International Harvester, Procter & Gamble, and Westinghouse. The street was also the location of significant innovations, such as the first building to receive power from the Decew Falls Generating Station in 1898, marking Hamilton's emergence as the "Electric City."

Today, Burlington Street remains a vital industrial corridor, home to the Hamilton Oshawa Port Authority, Lakeport Brewing Company, Bunge oilseed processing plant, and other major industries. The eastern section of the road has been renamed Nikola Tesla Boulevard.

The Republic of Benin was a short-lived unrecognized secessionist state in West Africa that existed for one day in 1967. It was established on 19 September 1967 during the Nigerian Civil War as a puppet state of Biafra, following its occupation of Nigeria's Mid-Western Region, and named after its capital, Benin City, with Albert Nwazu Okonkwo as its head of government.

The new state was an attempt by Biafra to prevent non-Igbo residents of the neighbouring Mid-Western Region from siding with Nigeria following regional ethnic tensions early in the war. The Republic of Benin was officially declared even as the Nigerian federal forces were reconquering the region and ended the following day as they entered Benin City.: 369 The occupation of the Mid-Western Region turned residents against the secessionist cause, and was used by the Nigerian government as justification to escalate the war against Biafra.

Cephaloridine (or cefaloridine) is a first-generation semisynthetic derivative of antibiotic cephalosporin C. It is a Beta lactam antibiotic, like penicillin. Its chemical structure contains 3 cephems, 4 carboxyl groups and three pyridinium methyl groups.

Cephaloridine is mainly used in veterinary practice. It is unique among cephalosporins in that it exists as a zwitterion.
**John Skipper** is an American media executive, currently executive chairman of DAZN Group and co-founder of Meadowlark Media.
Machi (Chinese: 麻吉; pinyin: májí; Pe̍h-ōe-jī: Mâ-kiat) is a Taiwanese hip hop group that has nine active members, with two additional members on a hiatus from group activities.
**Enrico Morin** (1841–1910) was an Italian admiral and politician who served as minister of the Navy.

Sokratis Kokkalis (born May 27, 1939) is a Greek businessman. He is the founder and majority shareholder of Intracom Holdings and chairman and majority shareholder of Intralot S.A. His net worth was estimated at US$1.2 billion in 2015.

"Audi" is a song by American rapper Smokepurpp. It was released on May 17, 2017, as the lead single from his debut mixtape Deadstar. The track is considered one of his breakout singles, along with "Ski Mask".

Cromarty Courthouse, formerly Cromarty Town House, is a municipal building in Church Street, Cromarty, in the Highland area of Scotland. The structure, which is used as a museum, is a Category A listed building.
Prakash Vir Shastri (30 December 1923 – 23 November 1977) was a Member of the Parliament of India (Sansad) and was also a leader in the Arya Samaj movement.

Irving Trust was a commercial bank based in New York City, founded in 1851 as the Irving Bank of the City of New York. It was named after Washington Irving, whose portrait appeared on the bank's notes. Through a series of mergers and acquisitions, it became the Irving Trust Company in 1929. In 1988, Irving Trust was acquired by the Bank of New York in a hostile takeover, creating the 12th largest bank in the United States.

WTRF-TV, virtual channel 7, is a television station licensed to Wheeling, West Virginia, serving the Wheeling–Steubenville, Ohio, market. It is owned by Nexstar Media Group. WTRF-TV's primary affiliation is CBS. The station also carries MyNetworkTV, ABC, and Ion Mystery on its digital subchannels. WTRF-TV began broadcasting on October 24, 1953, and was initially affiliated with NBC and ABC.
George Hall (September 21, 1795 - April 16, 1868) was an American businessman and politician who served as the first Mayor of Brooklyn.

Jhajhar is a village in the Jhunjhunu district of Rajasthan, India. Historically, the Jagir of Jhajhar was granted to Purshottam Singh, son of Thakur Todar Mal. Teej, Gangaur, Holi, and Diwali are among the festivals celebrated in the village.

Mike Mineo is an American singer-songwriter and multi-instrumentalist born in Hollywood, Florida. His musical style encompasses avant-garde, pop, folk, soul, and jazz. He has recorded and self-released four albums on his own independent record label Nevernothing Records, LLC. In March 2016 Mike Mineo moved to Nashville, Tennessee, to further his music career.

The Federation of State Medical Boards (FSMB) is a non-profit organization founded in 1912 that represents the 71 medical boards within the United States and its territories. It facilitates cooperation among these boards and promotes uniform standards for medical regulation. The FSMB is headquartered in Washington, D.C., and is led by President and CEO Humayun Chaudhry, D.O.

The FSMB sponsors the United States Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE), a crucial step for medical graduates seeking licensure in the U.S. It also operates the Federation Physician Data Center (FPDC), which maintains records of disciplinary actions against physicians, and the Federation Credentials Verification Service (FCVS), a repository for physician credentials. Through its foundation, the FSMB supports education and research related to medical regulation. The FSMB publishes the *Journal of Medical Regulation* and other materials to inform its members and the public about medical licensing and healthcare trends.

Karli Coburger (1929–) was a senior officer in the East German Ministry for State Security (Stasi). He joined the Stasi in 1952, rising to head "Section VIII" (Surveillance, Investigations and Arrests) in 1984. Following German reunification, he was convicted in 2000 of complicity in the perversion of justice and received a suspended sentence.
Stephen Soter Ortynsky Hentosh, O.S.B.M. (Lang-uk; 29 January 1866 – 24 March 1916) was the first Bishop of all Greek Catholics in the United States.
Diether Ocampo (born 1974) is a Filipino actor, singer, model, and Philippine Coast Guard Auxiliary officer.
Workpoint TV is Thailand's television station, owned by Workpoint Entertainment, broadcasting on channel 23 of Thailand's digital television.

Baasskap was a form of racial segregation systematically applied in South Africa to preserve white (particularly Afrikaner) dominance. Proponents, the largest faction of apartheid ideologues, were not necessarily opposed to black economic participation, provided it was controlled to maintain Afrikaner economic power. Prime Minister J.G. Strydom and Minister of Justice C.R. Swart were prominent proponents. While Hendrik Verwoerd favored stricter segregation, he legitimized baasskap.
William Thomas Smith (1896–1994) was a British and Australian World War I flying ace and World War II Royal Australian Air Force Flight Sergeant.
Mount Gould is a thirteener on the Sierra crest, just north of the Kearsarge Pass.

Common ownership refers to holding the assets of an organization, enterprise or community indivisibly rather than in the names of the individual members or groups of members as common property.

Forms of common ownership exist in every economic system. Common ownership of the means of production is a central goal of communist political movements as it is seen as a necessary democratic mechanism for the creation and continued function of a communist society. Advocates make a distinction between collective ownership and common property as the former refers to property owned jointly by agreement of a set of colleagues, such as producer cooperatives, whereas the latter refers to assets that are completely open for access, such as a public park freely available to everyone.
Bethuel Muzeu (born February 22, 2000) is a Namibian footballer who plays as a forward for Black Leopards F.C. and the Namibia national team.

Martina Carraro (born June 21, 1993) is an Italian swimmer specializing in breaststroke. She has competed in the Olympics and won medals at the World Championships, European Championships, Universiade, and Mediterranean Games. At the 2019 World Championships, she won a bronze medal in the 100 meter breaststroke.
St John's Rectory is at 3 Vicar's Lane, Chester, Cheshire, England. It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.

TVR Cultural is a Romanian television channel owned by Televiziunea Română. Originally launched in 2002, it ceased broadcasting in 2012 due to financial difficulties, but relaunched in 2022 in both SD and HD.
Doris Mary Stenton, Lady Stenton, FBA (1894–1971) was an English historian of the Middle Ages.
*The Women's Balcony* is a 2016 Israeli comedy-drama film directed by Emil Ben-Shimon.

Carl Froch vs. Glen Johnson was a super middleweight boxing match that took place on June 4, 2011, at Boardwalk Hall in Atlantic City, New Jersey. Froch's WBC Super Middleweight Championship was on the line. Froch defeated Johnson by majority decision. On the Showtime broadcast, Mikkel Kessler defeated Mehdi Bouadla by TKO.

Ruslan Abubakar Said-Khusainovich Edelgeriev (Russian: Русла́н Абубакар Сайд-Хуса́йнович Эдельгери́ев; born 4 December 1974, Akhmat-Yurt, Chechen-Ingush ASSR, RSFSR, Soviet Union) is a Russian politician, currently serving as Advisor to the President of the Russian Federation on Climate Change since 22 June 2018.

Edelgeriev has also served as Chairman of the Government of the Chechen Republic from 24 May 2012 to 25 June 2018. Edelgeriev is a member of United Russia, serving as Secretary of the Chechen Regional Office from July 2012 to December 2019.
The Museum of Russian Impressionism is an art museum in Moscow dedicated to Russian art at the turn of the twentieth century.

Baltoceratidae is an extinct family of orthoconic cephalopods from the Ordovician period. They are characterized by straight, slender shells with a ventral siphuncle. They are ancestral to the Orthoceratidae and Troedsonnellidae.
*Personal and Vehicular Basic Loads and The Morrow Project Role Playing Expansion* is a supplement for *The Morrow Project* role-playing game.

Modesto Marini is an Italian chef and restaurateur based in Malaysia. He is the founder of The Marini's Group, which includes the restaurants Marini's on 57, Marble 8, and M Marini Caffè.

Marini began his culinary career as a teenager and trained as a chef before working at Cecconi's in London. At 21, he moved to Singapore and then to Malaysia in 1995. He founded the Modesto's chain of Italian restaurants, which grew to nine outlets before he sold his shares.

In 2012, Marini opened Marini's on 57, a rooftop bar, restaurant, and lounge in Kuala Lumpur. He followed this with M Marini Caffè in 2014 and Marble 8, a steakhouse. Marini's on 57 has hosted royals and celebrities and was the venue for the after-party of the 2014 Laureus World Sports Awards. Marini has also released a series of chill-out albums called Sunset Hours in collaboration with DJs such as José Padilla. He has received several awards, including the Hospitality Asia Platinum Awards' Icon of the Year award in 2013.

The Vladimir Lenin monument is located in Azov, Rostov Oblast, Russia. It was opened on April 30, 1978. The monument's authors were Yuri Orekhov, Vladimir Peterburzhtsev and Alexander Stepanov. The total height of the monument is 10.8 meters.

The E3 Tug Project is a collaborative effort between Smit International and Damen Shipyards Gorinchem, focusing on designing tugboats that are Efficient, Environmentally friendly, and Economically viable (E3). The project involves three stages: studying existing tugs like the Smit Elbe, designing a new propulsion system for a Damen ASD Tug 2810 model, and creating a future tug design incorporating technologies expected to be available in the coming years. The Technological University of Delft is also involved, assisting with design simulations.

Margaret Dayton (born 1949) is an American politician from Utah. A member of the Republican Party, she served longer in the Utah Legislature than any other woman in Utah history. After serving a decade in the Utah House of Representatives, she unseated an incumbent senator in 2006 and served in that position until June 2018, when she resigned for medical reasons.

She represented the 15th Senate District which includes parts of Provo, Orem, Vineyard, Lindon and Pleasant Grove.

Fight Sports is a Brazilian jiu-jitsu (BJJ) academy founded in Miami in 2011 by Roberto "Cyborg" Abreu. By 2022, Fight Sports had over thirty affiliated schools worldwide. In 2021, Fight Sports partnered with Melqui Galvao's project in Brazil. In 2018, Fight Sports faced controversy when one of its coaches was arrested and charged with sexual assault.

"Eu Quero Tchu, Eu Quero Tcha" (I Want Tchu, I Want Tcha), full title "Eu Quero Tchu Eu Quero Tcha (Tchu Tcha Tcha)" is a single by Brazilian Sertanejo universitário duo João Lucas & Marcelo, released in 2012, and written by Shylton Fernandes. The song was a hit in Brazil, following the success of similar songs such as Michel Teló's "Ai Se Eu Te Pego" and Gusttavo Lima's "Balada", though not as successful in Europe as them.

Polytrauma refers to the condition of a person who has experienced injuries to multiple body parts and organ systems, often resulting from high-impact events. In civilian life, motor vehicle crashes are a common cause, while in military contexts, it frequently results from blast injuries from improvised explosive devices. Polytrauma can involve a combination of traumatic brain injury, limb loss, burns, fractures, blindness, and hearing loss. The U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs operates rehabilitation centers and networks specializing in polytrauma care for veterans, particularly those returning from conflicts like OEF/OIF/OND.

Fungistatics are anti-fungal agents that inhibit the growth of fungus (without killing the fungus). The term fungistatic may be used as both a noun and an adjective. Fungistatics have applications in agriculture, the food industry, the paint industry, and medicine.

Entrenching battalions were temporary units formed in the armies of the British Empire during the First World War. Entrenching Battalions were trained as infantry, but were primarily utilized for manual labour duties such as trench repair, wire laying, road making and assisting tunnellers, pioneers, engineers and signals. The Battalions were also used as pools of men from which drafts of replacements could be drawn by conventional infantry battalions.

The Diocese of Ypres was a Roman Catholic diocese in Belgium, created in 1557 from part of the abolished Diocese of Thérouanne. In 1801, it was incorporated into the Diocese of Ghent. Cornelius Jansen was Bishop of Ypres from 1635 to 1638.

Vladimir Nemukhin (d. 2016) was a Russian artist known for his abstract works, particularly still lifes with playing cards. He played a role in the Moscow art world, negotiating between artists and the state. His works are held in major museums worldwide.

Thrilling is a private, venture-backed online marketplace for vintage clothing. Founded in 2018 by Shilla Kim-Parker and Brad Mallow, the company is headquartered in Los Angeles, California. Kim-Parker serves as CEO, and Mallow as CTO.

Thrilling partners exclusively with brick and mortar thrift and vintage stores, providing them with a platform to list their inventory online. Thrilling handles sales, shipping, and returns, taking a 10% commission on each sale. The platform offers technology to help store owners digitize their inventories, including features that use photography to determine an item's size and recommended price.

Thrilling has raised venture capital in several rounds of funding. These include a $100,000 seed funding in 2018, a $2.1 million seed round in 2019, and an $8.5 million Series A round in 2021 led by Prelude Ventures. Other investors include Elemental Excelerator, Defy, Urban US, Phoenix Rising, Closed Loop, and Congruent Ventures. Thrilling's competitors include Etsy, Depop, Poshmark, and Tradesy.
"Songs of the Pixies" is a poem by Samuel Taylor Coleridge. It was written in 1793 and published in his 1796 collection *Poems on Various Subjects*. The poem makes references to pixies and fairies.

The 1907 Los Angeles Angels were led by pitcher Dolly Gray, who had multiple 20-win seasons for the team. First baseman Pop Dillon led the team with a .304 batting average, while right fielder Gavvy Cravath tied for the league lead with 45 doubles. Left fielder Walter Carlisle led the PCL with 14 home runs.

The Subnetwork Access Protocol (SNAP) is a mechanism for multiplexing, on networks using IEEE 802.2 LLC, more protocols than can be distinguished by the 8-bit 802.2 Service Access Point (SAP) fields. SNAP supports identifying protocols by EtherType field values; it also supports vendor-private protocol identifier spaces. It is used with IEEE 802.3, IEEE 802.4, IEEE 802.5, IEEE 802.11 and other IEEE 802 physical network layers, as well as with non-IEEE 802 physical network layers such as FDDI that use 802.2 LLC.

The SNAP and LSAP fields are added to the packets at the transmitting node in order to allow the receiving node to pass each received frame to an appropriate device driver which understands given protocol.

The Audubon Aquarium of the Americas is an aquarium located in New Orleans, Louisiana, United States. Specializing in aquatic life from throughout the Americas, the aquarium is home to 10,000 animals representing 530 species. It opened on September 1, 1990, and is accredited by the Association of Zoos and Aquariums (AZA). The aquarium's exhibits showcase regions throughout North and South America. The aquarium was affected by Hurricane Katrina in 2005, resulting in the death of most of its animals. It reopened in May 2006, with revamped exhibits.

Sir George Lisle (baptized 10 July 1615 – 28 August 1648) was an English Royalist officer. Born in London, Lisle came from a family with connections to the Villiers family, which facilitated his early military career. He served with the Dutch States Army and later in the Bishops' Wars.

During the First English Civil War, Lisle fought in several major battles, including Edgehill, Chalgrove, and Naseby. He gained a reputation for courage and was eventually knighted and appointed Master of the Household and governor of Faringdon in December 1645. After the surrender of Oxford and Faringdon, Lisle joined the Royalist forces in the Second English Civil War. Following the siege of Colchester, he was executed by firing squad along with Sir Charles Lucas for violating the terms of surrender and for his prior involvement in the capture of Leicester, where civilians were massacred.

Despite the controversial nature of his execution, Lisle was later memorialized as a Royalist martyr, particularly during the Victorian era. His remains were reburied in St Giles Church Colchester after the Stuart Restoration, and a monument was erected in his memory at Colchester Castle.

EC KAC is an Austrian professional ice hockey team based in Klagenfurt, Carinthia. Founded in 1909, the team plays in the ICE Hockey League and has won 32 Austrian Championships, the first of which was in 1934. Their home arena is the Stadthalle Klagenfurt, which has a capacity of 5,500.

City Tower is a 30-story modernist office, communication, and retail building in Manchester, England, completed in 1965 as part of the Piccadilly Plaza complex. It stands 123 meters (404 ft) tall, houses Manchester's main radio transmitting station, and was sold to Schroders in 2014.
Maria-Luise Rainer (born 23 April 1959 in South Tyrol) is an Italian luger who competed between the late 1970s and the late 1980s.

Frank Manumaleuna (May 9, 1956 – June 1, 2022) was an American football linebacker. He played high school football at Phineas Banning High School before playing college football for the UCLA Bruins and the San Jose State Spartans, where he was later inducted into the San Jose State University Sports Hall of Fame.

Manumaleuna was drafted by the Kansas City Chiefs in the 1979 NFL Draft, playing for them from 1979 to 1981. He also played in the USFL for the Oakland Invaders (1983-1984) and the Portland Breakers (1985). His son, Brandon Manumaleuna, also played in the NFL.

Planet Earth is a television and film documentary franchise produced and broadcast by the BBC. The franchise began in 2001 with the success of The Blue Planet. As of 2017, The Blue Planet has spawned 5 series and one feature film.

The Hoosiers are an English pop rock band formed in Indianapolis, Indiana, in 2003. The band is composed of members Irwin Sparkes and Alan Sharland. The name derives from "Hoosier", the colloquial term for a citizen of Indiana.

Sparkes and Sharland, after meeting in the US, returned to London and signed to RCA, releasing their first album, *The Trick to Life*, in 2007. The album featured the singles "Goodbye Mr A", which appeared in *FIFA 08*, "Worst Case Scenario", and "Cops and Robbers". The band later departed from Sony after a dispute and re-released their second album, *The Illusion of Safety*, as *Bumpy Ride*. They have since released multiple albums under their own label, Crab Race.

Katja Hessel (born 5 May 1972) is a German lawyer and politician of the Free Democratic Party (FDP) who has been serving as Parliamentary State Secretary in the Federal Ministry of Finance in the coalition government of Chancellor Olaf Scholz since 2021. She has been serving as a member of the Bundestag from the state of Bavaria since 2017.
Sheila Elias (born in Chicago) is an American artist. Her works have been featured in exhibitions across North America and at the Liberty show at the Louvre Museum in Paris.
Brad Davis (born 30 April 1982) is an Australian former professional rugby league player. He played halfback for the Gold Coast Titans in the National Rugby League.

The Hong Kong motion picture rating system (Chinese: 香港電影分級制度; pinyin: Xiānggǎng diànyǐng fēnjí zhìdù; Jyutping: hoeng1 gong2 din6 jing2 fan1 kap1 zai3 dou6) is a legal system of movie screening and rating. An official government agency issues ratings for any movie that will be shown in Hong Kong cinemas.

The 2014 Mountain Province Bus accident was a bus accident that occurred when a Florida Trans bus fell off a road in Sitio Paggang, Talubin, Bontoc, Mountain Province of the Philippines. The accident occurred on February 7, 2014. Fourteen people were killed and more than 32 people were injured. Comedian Tado and two more artists died in this accident.

Ports North, the trading name of the Far North Queensland Ports Corporation Limited, is a Queensland Government statutory corporation that is responsible for the Cairns Marlin Marina and the Cairns Cityport project and the ports in Cairns, Cape Flattery, Karumba, Mourilyan, Skardon River, Quintell Beach, Thursday Island, Burketown and Cooktown, in Queensland, Australia. Since 2017, the shareholding Ministers are the Honourable Jackie Trad, Deputy Premier, Treasurer and Minister for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Partnerships, and the Honourable Mark Bailey, Minister for Transport and Main Roads.

Formerly known as the Cairns Port Authority, the corporation was responsible for the operation of the Cairns International Airport until the sale of the airport in December 2008 to a private consortium. The former authority was responsible for the Cairns Seaport and in 2009 became responsible for the regional ports of Mourilyan, Cooktown, Cape Flattery, Quintell Beach, Thursday Island, Skardon River, Karumba and Burketown.
Cornelia Livia Orestina (her cognomen is sometimes given in the diminutive form Orestilla) was the second wife of the Roman Emperor Caligula in AD 37 or 38.
